topics.title: Overview of Wireless Networks: Introduction to Wireless LANs, IEEE 802.11 WLANs, Physical Layer, MAC Sublayer, MAC Management Sublayer, Wireless ATM, HIPERLAN, HIPERLAN 2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Wireless Networks: Introduction to Wireless LANs, IEEE 802.11 WLANs, Physical Layer, MAC Sublayer, MAC Management Sublayer, Wireless ATM, HIPERLAN, HIPERLAN 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Wireless LANs (WLANs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) is a flexible data communication system that uses Radio Frequency (RF) or Infrared (IR) technology to transmit and receive data over the air, minimizing the need for wired connections and enabling mobility within a limited geographical area (typically within a building or campus).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a WLAN as an "invisible Ethernet cable" that broadcasts data through the air instead of through physical wires. Just like a radio station sends music to anyone with a receiver within range, a WLAN Access Point (AP) broadcasts data packets to any device (station) equipped with a wireless network interface card (WNIC) within its coverage area ÔÇö known as a **Basic Service Set (BSS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics for WLANs:**
> - **Operating Frequency Bands:** **2.4 GHz** (ISM band), **5 GHz** (UNII band), **6 GHz** (Wi-Fi 6E)
> - **Typical Range:** **30 m (indoors)** to **300 m (outdoors)**
> - **Data Rates:** Legacy 1ÔÇô2 Mbps Ôćĺ Modern Wi-Fi 6/6E reaching **9.6 Gbps**
> - **Standard:** Governed primarily by the **IEEE 802.11 family**

### 1.2 IEEE 802.11 WLANs ÔÇö The Backbone Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The IEEE 802.11 standard, popularly branded as **Wi-Fi**, defines the Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layers of the OSI model for implementing Wireless Local Area Networks. First ratified in **1997**, it has evolved through numerous amendments (a/b/g/n/ac/ax) to meet growing throughput and security demands.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
IEEE 802.11 is like a "universal language" or "traffic rulebook" that allows different wireless devices from different manufacturers to communicate seamlessly. Without it, your laptop, phone, and smart TV would each speak a different "wireless dialect" and fail to interoperate.

### 1.3 Architecture Components of IEEE 802.11

The standard defines several key building blocks:

| Component | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Station (STA)** | Node | End-user device with wireless interface |
| **Access Point (AP)** | Bridge | Connects wireless stations to Distribution System (DS) |
| **Basic Service Set (BSS)** | Cell | Group of stations under one AP |
| **Extended Service Set (ESS)** | Network | Multiple BSSs interconnected via DS |
| **Distribution System (DS)** | Backbone | Wired (usually Ethernet) link between APs |
| **Independent BSS (IBSS)** | Ad-hoc | Direct STA-to-STA communication without AP |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 802.11 architecture uses two network topologies:
> - **Infrastructure Mode** ÔÇö communication via AP (used in homes/offices)
> - **Ad-hoc Mode** ÔÇö direct peer-to-peer (used in emergency/tactical scenarios)

### 1.4 Wireless ATM, HIPERLAN, and HIPERLAN 2 ÔÇö European Counterparts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wireless ATM (W-ATM):** An extension of Asynchronous Transfer Mode (ATM) technology to wireless media, providing Quality of Service (QoS) guarantees for voice, video, and data over a wireless link.
>
> **HIPERLAN (High Performance Radio Local Area Network):** A European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) standard (ETSI RES10), defined in **1991**, operating at **5.15ÔÇô5.30 GHz** and **17.1ÔÇô17.3 GHz**, supporting data rates up to **23.5 Mbps**. It was the European answer to IEEE 802.11.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of HIPERLAN as the "European cousin" of Wi-Fi. While Wi-Fi (American-led IEEE 802.11) dominated globally, HIPERLAN was developed to be inherently faster and QoS-aware, paving the way for **HIPERLAN 2** ÔÇö which later converged with the IEEE 802.11a physical layer specification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 IEEE 802.11 Physical Layer (PHY) ÔÇö Detailed Analysis

The Physical Layer of 802.11 has undergone several generational upgrades. Let us break them down:

#### 2.1.1 Physical Layer Generations

| Amendment | Year | Frequency | Modulation | Max Data Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **802.11 (Legacy)** | 1997 | 2.4 GHz | DSSS, FHSS, IR | 1ÔÇô2 Mbps |
| **802.11a** | 1999 | 5 GHz | OFDM | **54 Mbps** |
| **802.11b** | 1999 | 2.4 GHz | DSSS/CCK | 11 Mbps |
| **802.11g** | 2003 | 2.4 GHz | OFDM/DSSS | 54 Mbps |
| **802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)** | 2009 | 2.4/5 GHz | OFDM + MIMO | 600 Mbps |
| **802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)** | 2013 | 5 GHz | OFDM + MU-MIMO | 6.93 Gbps |
| **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6)** | 2019 | 2.4/5/6 GHz | OFDMA + MU-MIMO | **9.6 Gbps** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why OFDM Matters:** Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM) splits the available spectrum into many narrow orthogonal subcarriers, each carrying a portion of the data. This combats multipath fading and improves spectral efficiency ÔÇö a critical enabler for high-speed WLANs.

#### 2.1.2 Spread Spectrum Techniques (Legacy Layer)

- **DSSS (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum):** Each data bit is multiplied by a higher-rate pseudo-random **chip sequence** (e.g., 11-chip Barker code for 802.11). Spreads signal over wider bandwidth, providing resistance to narrowband interference.
- **FHSS (Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum):** The carrier frequency hops among 79 channels (in 2.4 GHz) according to a pseudo-random pattern. Hops at least **2.5 times/second** in 802.11 FHSS.
- **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing):** Multi-carrier modulation using 52 subcarriers in 802.11a/g (48 data + 4 pilot).

### 2.2 IEEE 802.11 MAC Sublayer ÔÇö The Heart of Wireless Coordination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The MAC Sublayer of IEEE 802.11 manages channel access, addressing, frame formatting, and error control. Its primary innovation is the **CSMA/CA** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) protocol, tailored for the wireless medium where collision detection is impractical.

#### 2.2.1 Why CSMA/CA Instead of CSMA/CD?

| Feature | Wired Ethernet (CSMA/CD) | Wireless 802.11 (CSMA/CA) |
|---|---|---|
| Collision Detection | Yes (via voltage sensing) | No (cannot sense while transmitting) |
| Hidden Node Problem | Not significant | Critical issue |
| Approach | Detect then retransmit | Avoid before transmitting |
| Acknowledgment | Not always required | Mandatory **ACK** frame |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hidden Node Problem:** Station A and C can both reach B (the AP), but A and C cannot hear each other. Both may transmit simultaneously to B, causing a collision at B. Solution: **RTS/CTS** handshake.

#### 2.2.2 CSMA/CA Operational Logic

The MAC operates as follows:
1. **DIFS Sensing:** Station senses the medium. If idle for a **DCF Inter-Frame Space (DIFS)** period, it proceeds.
2. **Backoff Counter:** If the medium is busy, the station waits for DIFS, then enters a random **backoff** window (0 to CW slots).
3. **Slot Decrement:** The backoff counter decrements only when the medium is idle.
4. **Transmission:** When counter reaches zero, the frame is transmitted.
5. **ACK Wait:** Sender waits for an **ACK** after a **Short Inter-Frame Space (SIFS)**.
6. **Retransmit:** If no ACK, the contention window **CW doubles** (up to CW_max).

#### 2.2.3 Inter-Frame Spaces (IFS) Hierarchy

$$\text{SIFS} < \text{PIFS} < \text{DIFS} < \text{EIFS}$$

- **SIFS** (Short IFS) ÔÇö 10 ┬Ás in 802.11b; used for ACK, CTS, fragmentation.
- **PIFS** (PCF IFS) ÔÇö SIFS + 1 slot; used by Point Coordination Function.
- **DIFS** (DCF IFS) ÔÇö SIFS + 2 slots; used for normal data frames.
- **EIFS** (Extended IFS) ÔÇö used when a corrupted frame is received.

#### 2.2.4 The RTS/CTS Handshake (Solving Hidden Node)

To resolve the **hidden node problem**, 802.11 introduces an optional 4-way handshake:

- **RTS (Request To Send):** Sender announces intent to transmit, including duration.
- **CTS (Clear To Send):** Receiver grants permission; all stations hearing CTS defer.
- **DATA Frame:** Sent after SIFS.
- **ACK:** Receiver acknowledges successful receipt.

### 2.3 MAC Management Sublayer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The MAC Management Sublayer sits above the MAC and handles operations such as station association, authentication, power management, synchronization, and scanning ÔÇö essential for mobility and security.

#### 2.3.1 Core Management Functions

1. **Scanning:** Stations discover available networks.
   - **Passive Scanning:** Listen for **Beacon frames** (typically every 100 ms / 102.4 ┬Ás TBTT).
   - **Active Scanning:** Broadcast **Probe Request**, wait for **Probe Response**.
2. **Authentication:** Verify station identity (Open / Shared Key in legacy; WPA2/WPA3 in modern).
3. **Association:** Join a BSS by sending **Association Request** to AP, receiving **Association Response**.
4. **Reassociation:** Roam from one AP to another while maintaining connectivity.
5. **Power Management:** Stations sleep (dozing) and wake for beacon intervals to save battery.
6. **Synchronization:** Timing Synchronization Function (TSF) keeps all stations aligned.
7. **Fragmentation/Reassembly:** Breaks large MSDUs into smaller MPDUs to improve reliability in noisy environments.

### 2.4 Wireless ATM (W-ATM) ÔÇö Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Wireless ATM integrates broadband ATM (designed for B-ISDN) with wireless media, preserving ATM's 53-byte cell structure and QoS classes (CBR, VBR, ABR, UBR) over the air interface.

**Why Wireless ATM?**
- ATM provides **QoS guarantees** essential for voice/video.
- Standard Ethernet over wireless lacks native QoS.
- W-ATM thus bridges **cellular mobility** with **fixed-network broadband quality**.

**Reference Architecture:**
- **Mobile Terminal (MT)** Ôćö **Radio Access Layer** Ôćö **W-ATM Switch** Ôćö **ATM Backbone**

### 2.5 HIPERLAN and HIPERLAN 2

#### 2.5.1 HIPERLAN (Type 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** HIPERLAN Type 1 is an ETSI standard (ETS 300 652) for WLANs operating in the **5.15ÔÇô5.30 GHz** band, supporting data rates of **up to 23.5 Mbps** with a range of **50 m** (indoor) and **800 m** (outdoor).

| Parameter | HIPERLAN 1 |
|---|---|
| Frequency | 5.15ÔÇô5.30 GHz |
| Max Rate | 23.5 Mbps |
| MAC | EY-NPMA (Elimination-Yield Non-Preemptive Priority Multiple Access) |
| Topology | Ad-hoc only (no AP concept) |
| Channel | GMSK modulation |

**EY-NPMA ÔÇö The HIPERLAN 1 MAC Protocol:**
A three-phase prioritization scheme:
- **Elimination Phase:** Only higher-priority stations survive.
- **Yield Phase:** Stations yield if they sense another transmission.
- **Data Phase:** The surviving station transmits.

#### 2.5.2 HIPERLAN 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** HIPERLAN 2 (ETSI TS 100 811) is a second-generation WLAN standard operating in the **5 GHz** band, supporting data rates up to **54 Mbps** using OFDM, with built-in **QoS** and is functionally aligned with IEEE 802.11a PHY but differs in MAC.

| Parameter | HIPERLAN 2 |
|---|---|
| Frequency | 5.15ÔÇô5.35 GHz, 5.470ÔÇô5.725 GHz |
| Max Rate | 54 Mbps |
| PHY | OFDM (52 subcarriers, identical to 802.11a) |
| MAC | TDMA/TDD with centralized control |
| QoS | Native support (4 QoS levels) |
| Security | Built-in AES/3DES support |

**HIPERLAN 2 Architecture:**
- **MT (Mobile Terminal)** Ôćö **AP (Access Point)** Ôćö **Core Network**
- Supports both **Cell-based** (with AP) and **Ad-hoc** (Direct Mode) topologies.
- Connection-oriented MAC using **time slots** allocated by AP.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential equations and metrics for Module 1 examination:**

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Shannon Capacity | $$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$ | Maximum achievable data rate over channel of bandwidth **B** |
| DSSS Spreading Gain | $$G_p = \frac{R_c}{R_b}$$ | Chip rate $R_c$ over data rate $R_b$ (e.g., 11 for Barker) |
| FHSS Hop Rate | $f_{hop} \geq 2.5$ Hz | 802.11 FHSS minimum hopping frequency |
| Beacon Interval | $T_{BI} = 100$ ms | Time between successive beacons (102.4 ms in spec) |
| SIFS Duration (802.11b) | $10 \; \mu s$ | Used for ACK, CTS, fragmented frames |
| DIFS Duration | $\text{SIFS} + 2 \times \text{slot time}$ | Where slot time = 20 ┬Ás (802.11b) |
| CW Evolution | $CW_i = \min(2^{i+4} - 1, CW_{max})$ | Exponential growth up to CW_max = 1023 |
| OFDM Subcarriers (802.11a/g) | $N_{sc} = 52$ | 48 data + 4 pilot |
| Net Throughput | $$\text{Throughput} = \frac{L_{data}}{T_{DATA} + SIFS + T_{ACK} + DIFS + T_{backoff}}$$ | Effective MAC-level data rate |
| Path Loss (Friis) | $$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$ | Free-space propagation loss |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **WLANs** are the foundation of **smart campuses, IoT, and Industry 4.0** automation.
- **802.11 MAC techniques** influence modern protocols (Bluetooth, Zigbee, 5G NR unlicensed).
- **OFDM** principles extend directly to **4G LTE, 5G, and Wi-Fi 6/6E** ÔÇö making 802.11 PHY study directly relevant to 5G NR understanding.
- **W-ATM concepts** inspired **QoS-aware mobile broadband** (LTE QoS classes).
- **HIPERLAN/2** taught the world that **QoS at the MAC layer** is essential for multimedia ÔÇö a lesson fully adopted in **Wi-Fi Multimedia (WMM)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum WLAN Throughput Accounting for Overheads

**Problem:** Compute the maximum MAC throughput of an 802.11b system transmitting 1500-byte MSDUs using RTS/CTS, given:
- Slot time $T_{slot} = 20 \; \mu s$
- SIFS $= 10 \; \mu s$
- DIFS $= 50 \; \mu s$ (i.e., SIFS + 2├ŚTslot)
- RTS $= 20$ bytes, CTS $= 14$ bytes, ACK $= 14$ bytes
- PHY preamble + header: $T_{pre} = 192 \; \mu s$
- Data rate $= 11$ Mbps, Control rate $= 1$ Mbps
- Assume **no backoff** (best-case saturated load, all stations perfectly coordinated)

**Step 1 ÔÇö Compute Transmission Times**

For RTS/CTS, control frames are sent at 1 Mbps, data at 11 Mbps.

$$T_{RTS} = T_{pre} + \frac{20 \times 8}{1 \times 10^6} = 192 + 160 = 352 \; \mu s$$

$$T_{CTS} = 192 + \frac{14 \times 8}{1 \times 10^6} = 192 + 112 = 304 \; \mu s$$

$$T_{DATA} = 192 + \frac{1500 \times 8}{11 \times 10^6} = 192 + 1090.91 = 1282.91 \; \mu s$$

$$T_{ACK} = 192 + \frac{14 \times 8}{1 \times 10^6} = 192 + 112 = 304 \; \mu s$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Compute the Total Cycle Time (one successful data exchange)**

The full sequence is:

$$\text{DIFS} \rightarrow \text{RTS} \rightarrow \text{SIFS} \rightarrow \text{CTS} \rightarrow \text{SIFS} \rightarrow \text{DATA} \rightarrow \text{SIFS} \rightarrow \text{ACK}$$

Substituting:

$$T_{cycle} = T_{DIFS} + T_{RTS} + T_{SIFS} + T_{CTS} + T_{SIFS} + T_{DATA} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK}$$

$$T_{cycle} = 50 + 352 + 10 + 304 + 10 + 1282.91 + 10 + 304$$

$$T_{cycle} = 2322.91 \; \mu s$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Compute Net Throughput**

The data payload is $1500 \times 8 = 12000$ bits. The throughput is:

$$S = \frac{L_{payload}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{12000}{2322.91 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$S \approx 5.166 \times 10^{6} \; \text{bits/s} \approx 5.17 \; \text{Mbps}$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Compute Overhead Fraction**

$$\text{Overhead} = 1 - \frac{5.17}{11} = 1 - 0.470 = 53.0\%$$

So **~53% of channel time is spent on overhead** (preambles, inter-frame spaces, control frames). This is a classic KTU-level analysis showing why raw 802.11b throughput is far less than 11 Mbps.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Wireless LAN Technologies

| Parameter | 802.11b | 802.11a | 802.11g | HIPERLAN 1 | HIPERLAN 2 | Wireless ATM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Year | 1999 | 1999 | 2003 | 1991 | 2000 | 1996 |
| Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 5 GHz | 5 GHz | 2.4/5 GHz |
| Modulation | DSSS/CCK | OFDM | OFDM | GMSK | OFDM | QAM/QPSK |
| Max Rate | 11 Mbps | 54 Mbps | 54 Mbps | 23.5 Mbps | 54 Mbps | 25+ Mbps |
| MAC | CSMA/CA | CSMA/CA | CSMA/CA | EY-NPMA | TDMA/TDD | Reservation-based |
| QoS | No | No | No | Limited | Yes (native) | Yes (native) |
| Topology | Infra+Adhoc | Infra+Adhoc | Infra+Adhoc | Ad-hoc only | Both | Both |
| Range (indoor) | 35 m | 35 m | 38 m | 50 m | 30ÔÇô50 m | Variable |
| Security | WEP | WEP | WPA/WPA2 | Optional | AES/3DES | Cell-level crypto |

### 3.3 Derivation: DSSS Spreading Gain (Barker Code)

**Problem:** In 802.11 DSSS, a 1 Mbps data signal is spread using an 11-chip Barker sequence. Calculate the chip rate, processing gain, and resulting bandwidth.

**Step 1 ÔÇö Chip Rate Calculation**

Each data bit is encoded using 11 chips (Barker code length = 11). So:

$$R_c = R_b \times L = (1 \times 10^6) \times 11 = 11 \times 10^6 \; \text{chips/s} = 11 \; \text{Mcps}$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Processing Gain**

The processing gain is the ratio of chip rate to data rate:

$$G_p = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = 11 \; (\text{or} \; 10 \log_{10}(11) \approx 10.41 \; \text{dB})$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Channel Bandwidth**

Each chip occupies a bandwidth approximately equal to the chip rate. For DSSS:

$$B \approx R_c = 11 \; \text{MHz}$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Interpretation**

The original 1 MHz signal is spread over an 11 MHz channel. This spreading provides:
- **Resistance to narrowband interference** (only ~9% of chips are affected).
- **Low probability of detection (LPD)** ÔÇö important for military/security.
- **Multiple access** when each user uses a unique spreading code (CDMA principle).

### 3.4 Derivation: Effective OFDM Subcarrier Capacity (802.11a)

**Problem:** In IEEE 802.11a OFDM, the channel is divided into 52 subcarriers (48 data + 4 pilot) within a 20 MHz channel. Calculate the per-subcarrier symbol rate and net throughput for 64-QAM with 3/4 coding rate.

**Step 1 ÔÇö OFDM Symbol Duration**

The IFFT window is $T_{FFT} = 3.2 \; \mu s$, with a cyclic prefix of $T_{CP} = 0.8 \; \mu s$. Total OFDM symbol:

$$T_{OFDM} = T_{FFT} + T_{CP} = 3.2 + 0.8 = 4.0 \; \mu s$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Per-Subcarrier Symbol Rate**

$$R_{sym} = \frac{1}{T_{OFDM}} = \frac{1}{4.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 250{,}000 \; \text{symbols/s} = 250 \; \text{kSps}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Bits per Subcarrier (64-QAM)**

64-QAM encodes $\log_2(64) = 6$ bits per symbol.

**Step 4 ÔÇö Net Data Rate for 64-QAM, 3/4 Coding**

$$R_{data} = N_{data} \times R_{sym} \times b \times \eta_c$$

$$R_{data} = 48 \times 250 \times 10^3 \times 6 \times 0.75$$

$$R_{data} = 48 \times 250{,}000 \times 4.5$$

$$R_{data} = 54 \times 10^6 \; \text{bits/s} = 54 \; \text{Mbps}$$

**Step 5 ÔÇö Interpretation**

This matches the maximum 54 Mbps data rate of 802.11a/g, confirming the OFDM design. The **3/4 coding rate** trades error correction strength for throughput.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: WLAN Threat Surface (Forward-Looking for Security Course)

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is a security course, Module 1 first establishes the **attack surface** introduced by these protocols:

| Layer | Vulnerability Introduced | Security Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **PHY** | Eavesdropping (RF capture) | Requires encryption (WPA3, AES) |
| **MAC** | MAC spoofing, deauthentication attacks | Needs MAC filtering + 802.11i |
| **Management** | Rogue APs, beacon spoofing | Needs 802.1X authentication |
| **Hidden Node** | Collision-based DoS | RTS/CTS provides partial mitigation |
| **Open Authentication** | Identity theft | Mandate WPA3-SAE |
| **Ad-hoc Mode** | Direct peer attacks | Disable unless required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IEEE 802.11 Protocol Stack Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Layer HTTPS SSH SMTP] --> B[Network Layer IP]
    B --> C[Logical Link Control LLC 802.2]
    C --> D[MAC Sublayer 802.11 CSMA CA]
    D --> E[MAC Management Sublayer Scanning Auth Association Power]
    D --> F[Physical Layer 802.11 OFDM DSSS FHSS IR]
    F --> G[Wireless Medium Air RF 2.4 5 6 GHz]
    H[Distribution System DS Ethernet] --> I[Access Point AP]
    I --> D
```

### 4.2 BSS and ESS Topological View

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ESS_Extended
        DS_DS[Distribution System Wired Ethernet Backbone]
        AP1[Access Point 1]
        AP2[Access Point 2]
        DS_DS --- AP1
        DS_DS --- AP2
    end
    subgraph BSS_1
        STA1[Station Laptop]
        STA2[Station Phone]
        STA3[Station IoT Device]
        STA1 --- AP1
        STA2 --- AP1
        STA3 --- AP1
    end
    subgraph BSS_2
        STA4[Station Tablet]
        STA5[Station Printer]
        STA4 --- AP2
        STA5 --- AP2
    end
    AP1 -. Roaming Handoff .-> AP2
```

### 4.3 CSMA/CA Flowchart with RTS/CTS

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Station has frame to send] --> B{Channel idle for DIFS}
    B -- Yes --> C[Set backoff counter to random in 0 to CW]
    B -- No --> D[Wait for channel to become idle]
    D --> B
    C --> E{Slot elapsed idle}
    E -- Yes --> F[Decrement backoff counter]
    E -- No --> G[Freeze counter and wait]
    G --> E
    F --> H{Backoff counter is zero}
    H -- No --> E
    H -- Yes --> I{Use RTS CTS mode}
    I -- Yes --> J[Send RTS frame]
    I -- No --> K[Send DATA frame directly]
    J --> L{CTS received within timeout}
    L -- Yes --> K
    L -- No --> M[Double CW and retry]
    K --> N{ACK received within timeout}
    N -- Yes --> O[Successful transmission]
    N -- No --> M
    M --> C
    O --> P[Done]
```

### 4.4 HIPERLAN 2 Architecture and Protocol Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph HIPERLAN2_Stack
        L1[Application Layer User Data Voice Video]
        L2[Convergence Layer Adapts to Core Network ATM IP 3GPP]
        L3[Data Link Control DLC Connection Oriented TDMA TDD]
        L4[Physical Layer OFDM 5 GHz 52 subcarriers]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> Air[(Wireless Air Interface 5 GHz)]
    Air --> AP_H[HIPERLAN 2 Access Point]
    AP_H --> CN[Core Network IP ATM UMTS]
```

### 4.5 Wireless ATM Reference Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    MT1[Mobile Terminal User Equipment] --> RAL[Radio Access Layer Wireless PHY MAC]
    RAL --> WAS[W-ATM Switch]
    WAS --> AB[ATM Backbone]
    AB --> WCN[Wired Core Network]
    MT2[Mobile Terminal 2] --> RAL
    WAS --> QoS[QoS Engine CBR VBR ABR UBR]
    WAS --> MOB[Mobility Manager Handover Location Update]
```

### 4.6 MAC Frame Format (802.11 Standard)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MAC_Frame
        FC[Frame Control 2 bytes] --> D[Duration ID 2 bytes]
        D --> A1[Address 1 6 bytes DA]
        A1 --> A2[Address 2 6 bytes SA]
        A2 --> A3[Address 3 6 bytes BSSID]
        A3 --> SC[Sequence Control 2 bytes]
        SC --> A4[Address 4 6 bytes optional]
        A4 --> BODY[Frame Body 0 to 2312 bytes]
        BODY --> FCS[Frame Check Sequence 4 bytes CRC 32]
    end
    FC -. Contains .- FC_DETAILS[Protocol Version Type Subtype ToDS FromDS More Frag Retry Power Management More Data WEP Order]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Wireless LAN. List the key components of IEEE 802.11 architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) is a data communication system that uses radio frequency (RF) or infrared (IR) transmission to provide network connectivity within a limited geographical area, eliminating the need for physical cabling.

**Key components of IEEE 802.11 architecture:**

1. **Stations (STA):** End-user devices equipped with wireless network interface cards, e.g., laptops, smartphones, IoT devices.
2. **Basic Service Set (BSS):** A group of stations controlled by a single Access Point (AP). The BSS defines the cell of coverage.
3. **Access Point (AP):** A central hub that bridges wireless stations to the wired Distribution System and provides beaconing, authentication, and frame relay functions.
4. **Distribution System (DS):** The wired backbone (typically Ethernet) that interconnects multiple APs to form a larger network.
5. **Extended Service Set (ESS):** A union of two or more BSSs connected through a DS, enabling seamless roaming across APs.
6. **Independent Basic Service Set (IBSS) / Ad-hoc Network:** A peer-to-peer network where stations communicate directly without an AP.

These components collectively enable mobility, scalability, and interoperability in enterprise, home, and public Wi-Fi deployments. *(3 marks ÔÇö full allocation for: definition 1 mark, list of 4ÔÇô6 components with brief explanation 2 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
**Explain the role of the MAC Management Sublayer with respect to scanning and authentication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **MAC Management Sublayer** is a logical component above the MAC that handles all non-data-plane operations required for stations to join, leave, and roam across WLANs. Its two key functions are:

**1. Scanning (Network Discovery):**
- A station performs scanning to discover available 802.11 networks within range.
- **Passive Scanning:** The station listens on each channel for periodic **Beacon frames** broadcast by APs (typically every 100 ms / 102.4 ┬Ás TBTT). The station extracts the SSID, capabilities, and supported rates from the beacon.
- **Active Scanning:** The station transmits a **Probe Request** frame on each channel. APs that hear the probe respond with a **Probe Response** containing the same information as a beacon.

**2. Authentication:**
- Once a network is selected, the station must authenticate itself to the chosen AP.
- **Open System Authentication:** A null authentication exchange (two frames) ÔÇö historically used in legacy WEP but vulnerable to spoofing.
- **Shared Key Authentication:** Uses a 4-way challenge-response handshake with WEP.
- **Modern Methods (WPA2/WPA3):** Authentication is handled via **802.1X / EAP** (for enterprise) or **PSK / SAE** (for personal). These provide mutual authentication and strong cryptographic identity verification.

Thus, scanning and authentication together ensure that only authorized stations with valid identities can join the BSS, forming the first line of defense in wireless security. *(3 marks ÔÇö 1.5 marks for scanning with both types, 1.5 marks for authentication with progressive methods)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full Questions (14 Marks Each ÔÇö Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> For Part B questions on wireless standards comparisons, students often **memorize tables without understanding the design rationale**. Examiners specifically deduct marks for: (1) confusing HIPERLAN 1 and HIPERLAN 2 parameters, (2) writing "Wi-Fi = IEEE 802.11" without specifying which amendment, (3) omitting the **Why** behind design choices (e.g., why OFDM over DSSS, why CSMA/CA over CSMA/CD). Always present answers in a structured table **followed by a 2-line rationale** for the design choice.

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

(a) **Explain the IEEE 802.11 Physical Layer in detail, comparing the modulation techniques of 802.11b, 802.11a, and 802.11g. Highlight the role of OFDM in modern WLANs.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **Describe the CSMA/CA protocol of IEEE 802.11 MAC sublayer. How does the RTS/CTS handshake resolve the hidden node problem?** *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer for (a) ÔÇö 7 marks:**

The IEEE 802.11 Physical Layer defines the wireless transmission characteristics, including frequency band, modulation, and channel access.

**802.11b (1999):**
- Operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** (2.4ÔÇô2.4835 GHz).
- Uses **DSSS** with **CCK (Complementary Code Keying)** modulation, supporting data rates of **1, 2, 5.5, and 11 Mbps**.
- The 11 Mbps rate uses CCK with 8-chip complementary codes.
- **Limitation:** Only 3 non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11) in 2.4 GHz, leading to congestion.

**802.11a (1999):**
- Operates in the **5 GHz UNII band** (5.15ÔÇô5.35 GHz, 5.47ÔÇô5.725 GHz).
- Uses **OFDM** with **52 subcarriers** (48 data + 4 pilot) over a 20 MHz channel.
- Supports data rates of **6, 9, 12, 18, 24, 36, 48, and 54 Mbps** using **BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, and 64-QAM** with various coding rates (1/2, 2/3, 3/4).
- **Advantage:** Less interference than 2.4 GHz; **disadvantage:** Shorter range due to higher propagation loss.

**802.11g (2003):**
- Operates in the **2.4 GHz band** for backward compatibility with 802.11b.
- Uses **OFDM** (same as 802.11a) for 54 Mbps, with **CCK-DSSS** fallback for 11 Mbps interoperability.
- Hence, 802.11g is a **"best of both worlds"** ÔÇö wide coverage of 2.4 GHz + speed of OFDM.

**Role of OFDM in Modern WLANs:**
OFDM splits a wideband channel into many narrowband orthogonal subcarriers, each carrying a small portion of data. This provides:
- **Robustness against multipath fading** (each subcarrier experiences flat fading).
- **High spectral efficiency** due to orthogonality.
- **Ease of equalization** using a single-tap equalizer.
- **Foundation for OFDMA in 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6)** for multi-user scheduling.

OFDM is the de-facto PHY choice from 802.11a/g through 802.11ac/ax and continues in 802.11be (Wi-Fi 7). *(7 marks: 1.5 marks for 802.11b, 1.5 for 802.11a, 1.5 for 802.11g, 2.5 for OFDM explanation with at least 3 bullet points)*

---

**Model Answer for (b) ÔÇö 7 marks:**

The IEEE 802.11 MAC sublayer uses **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** to arbitrate channel access among stations. It is designed for wireless media where collision detection is impossible because a station cannot sense its own transmission while transmitting (the "near-far" effect of radio hardware).

**CSMA/CA Operation ÔÇö Step-by-step:**

1. **Carrier Sense:** A station with a frame to send first senses the channel. If the channel is **idle for a DIFS** (Distributed Inter-Frame Space = 50 ┬Ás in 802.11b), it proceeds; otherwise, it defers.
2. **Backoff:** After DIFS, the station selects a **random backoff counter** uniformly from [0, CW], where CW (Contention Window) starts at 31 (802.11b) and doubles after each failure up to CW_max = 1023.
3. **Slot Decrement:** The counter decrements by 1 for every idle slot (20 ┬Ás); it freezes when the channel becomes busy.
4. **Transmission:** When the counter reaches 0, the station transmits the frame.
5. **Acknowledgment:** The receiver, after a **SIFS** (10 ┬Ás), sends an **ACK**. The sender treats the absence of ACK within the timeout as collision and schedules retransmission.
6. **Retry:** On failure, CW is doubled (Binary Exponential Backoff). After successful ACK, the new frame starts again at CW_min = 31.

**RTS/CTS Handshake ÔÇö Solving the Hidden Node Problem:**

The **hidden node problem** occurs when two stations (A and C) are both within range of AP (B) but **not within range of each other**. They may sense the channel as idle simultaneously and transmit to B, causing a collision at B.

**The Solution:**
- Before sending data, the sender transmits a short **RTS (Request To Send)** frame containing the duration of the upcoming data exchange.
- The receiver responds with **CTS (Clear To Send)** after a SIFS, also advertising the duration.
- **All stations hearing either the RTS or CTS update their NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** and defer channel access for the announced duration. Since stations within range of the receiver hear the CTS, hidden nodes (e.g., A hearing B's CTS) will not transmit, preventing collision.

The RTS/CTS handshake trades a small control overhead for significantly reduced collision probability, especially for long data frames ÔÇö a critical mechanism for enterprise WLANs. *(7 marks: 3 marks for CSMA/CA steps, 2 marks for IFS and backoff math, 2 marks for RTS/CTS hidden node solution)*

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks ÔÇö Alternative Choice]**

(a) **Explain the architecture of HIPERLAN and HIPERLAN 2. Compare HIPERLAN 2 with IEEE 802.11a.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **What is Wireless ATM? Discuss its architecture, advantages, and the challenges it faces. Explain how ATM QoS is preserved over the wireless link.** *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer for (a) ÔÇö 7 marks:**

**HIPERLAN (Type 1):**
HIPERLAN (High Performance Radio LAN) is an ETSI standard (ETS 300 652, 1991) for WLANs. It operates in the **5.15ÔÇô5.30 GHz** band, supports data rates up to **23.5 Mbps** using **GMSK modulation**, and is designed purely for **ad-hoc (peer-to-peer)** topology. Its MAC protocol is **EY-NPMA** (Elimination-Yield Non-Preemptive Priority Multiple Access), a 3-phase prioritization scheme that gives preference to time-sensitive traffic.

**HIPERLAN 2:**
HIPERLAN 2 (ETSI TS 100 811, 2000) is the second-generation standard, operating in the **5.15ÔÇô5.35 GHz and 5.470ÔÇô5.725 GHz** bands, supporting up to **54 Mbps** using **OFDM** (with 52 subcarriers, identical to 802.11a). Key features:
- **Centralized MAC:** Time-slot allocation via AP (TDMA/TDD).
- **Native QoS:** 4 QoS levels (constant bit rate, variable bit rate, best effort, data).
- **Built-in security:** AES, 3DES, no WEP vulnerability.
- **Dual modes:** Cell (with AP) and Direct (ad-hoc) mode.

**Comparison with IEEE 802.11a:**

| Parameter | HIPERLAN 2 | IEEE 802.11a |
|---|---|---|
| PHY | OFDM (same 52 subcarriers) | OFDM (52 subcarriers) |
| Max Rate | 54 Mbps | 54 Mbps |
| MAC | Centralized TDMA/TDD | Distributed CSMA/CA |
| QoS | 4 native classes | None (WMM added later) |
| Security | AES/3DES built-in | WEP (weak), WPA2 later |
| Topology | Cell + Ad-hoc | Cell + Ad-hoc |
| Standard Body | ETSI | IEEE |

**Conclusion:** HIPERLAN 2 and 802.11a share the same PHY but differ fundamentally in MAC philosophy ÔÇö **HIPERLAN 2 favored connection-oriented, QoS-aware centralized control**, while IEEE 802.11a used contention-based distributed access. Despite HIPERLAN 2's technical superiority, IEEE 802.11a won the market due to lower chip cost, broader industry support, and compatibility with 802.11b ecosystems. *(7 marks: 2 marks for HIPERLAN 1, 2 marks for HIPERLAN 2, 3 marks for the comparison table with key differences)*

---

**Model Answer for (b) ÔÇö 7 marks:**

**Definition:** Wireless ATM (W-ATM) is the integration of Asynchronous Transfer Mode (ATM) technology with wireless media, preserving ATM's 53-byte cell structure, QoS classes, and connection-oriented design over a radio link.

**Why W-ATM?**
Standard WLANs (802.11) lack native QoS, making them unsuitable for **real-time voice and video**. ATM, on the other hand, was designed for **B-ISDN** with strict QoS guarantees. W-ATM thus bridges the gap by extending ATM's capabilities to mobile users.

**Architecture of W-ATM:**
The W-ATM architecture consists of:
1. **Mobile Terminal (MT):** User device with W-ATM interface.
2. **Radio Access Layer:** The wireless PHY/MAC that replaces the wired ATM PHY.
3. **W-ATM Switch:** An ATM switch with wireless extension modules.
4. **ATM Backbone:** Standard ATM network connecting multiple W-ATM switches.
5. **Mobility Manager:** Handles **handover** and **location management** (analogous to GSM/VLR/HLR).
6. **QoS Engine:** Maintains CBR, VBR, ABR, UBR service classes over wireless hops.

**How QoS is Preserved:**
- Each connection is established with a **Traffic Contract** (PCR, SCR, MBS for VBR).
- The wireless segment uses **W-ATM MAC with reservation slots** for CBR/VBR traffic.
- The radio scheduler pre-allocates time slots based on traffic class.
- Handover is performed using **PNNI (Private Network-to-Network Interface)** signaling to re-establish the connection at the new AP without dropping QoS.

**Advantages:**
- Native QoS for multimedia.
- Connection-oriented routing for predictable performance.
- Compatibility with B-ISDN infrastructure.

**Challenges:**
- **High overhead:** 48-byte payload per 53-byte cell Ôćĺ ~10% cell tax.
- **Atmospheric effects:** Fading, multipath, interference disrupt strict QoS.
- **Mobility-induced handoffs** can drop connections or violate delay budgets.
- **Cell tax over wireless** reduces efficiency compared to IP/802.11.

Despite these challenges, W-ATM was a key research milestone that influenced 3G and 4G QoS architectures. *(7 marks: 2 marks for definition/why, 2 marks for architecture diagram description, 2 marks for QoS preservation, 1 mark for advantages and challenges)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ÔÇö Module 1**

- **WLAN** = Wireless LAN using RF/IR; bounded range; uses shared medium.
- **IEEE 802.11** is the **Wi-Fi standard**; defines PHY and MAC for WLANs.
- **Architecture:** BSS (one AP + stations) Ôćĺ ESS (multiple BSSs via DS).
- **Ad-hoc mode (IBSS)** allows direct peer-to-peer communication without AP.
- **PHY evolutions:** 802.11 (1ÔÇô2 Mbps) Ôćĺ 802.11b (11 Mbps DSSS) Ôćĺ 802.11a (54 Mbps OFDM) Ôćĺ 802.11g (54 Mbps OFDM in 2.4 GHz) Ôćĺ 802.11n/ac/ax (MIMO, OFDMA, multi-Gbps).
- **DSSS** uses 11-chip Barker code Ôćĺ chip rate 11 Mcps at 1 Mbps.
- **OFDM** uses 52 subcarriers (48 data + 4 pilot) Ôćĺ 54 Mbps at 64-QAM, 3/4 coding.
- **MAC uses CSMA/CA**, not CSMA/CD (collision detection impossible in wireless).
- **IFS hierarchy:** SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS.
- **Hidden node problem** is solved by **RTS/CTS** handshake using NAV.
- **Binary exponential backoff:** CW doubles on each failure, max CW = 1023.
- **MAC Management Sublayer** handles **scanning, authentication, association, roaming, power management, synchronization, fragmentation**.
- **Passive scanning** = listen for beacons (every 100 ms / TBTT).
- **Active scanning** = probe request/response.
- **WEP (insecure)** Ôćĺ **WPA (TKIP)** Ôćĺ **WPA2 (AES-CCMP)** Ôćĺ **WPA3 (SAE)**.
- **Wireless ATM** extends ATM QoS (CBR, VBR, ABR, UBR) to wireless hops; uses 53-byte cells.
- **HIPERLAN 1** = 1991, 5 GHz, 23.5 Mbps, GMSK, ad-hoc, EY-NPMA MAC.
- **HIPERLAN 2** = 2000, 5 GHz, 54 Mbps, OFDM, centralized TDMA, native QoS, AES security.
- **HIPERLAN 2 PHY = IEEE 802.11a PHY** but MAC is different (TDMA vs CSMA/CA).
- **Why CSMA/CA, not CSMA/CD:** Wireless stations cannot sense collisions during transmission (near-far effect + half-duplex radio).
- **Beacon interval** = 100 ms (102.4 ┬Ás in spec, often simplified to 100 ms).
- **Slot time** = 20 ┬Ás (802.11b DSSS), 9 ┬Ás (802.11a OFDM).
- **Net throughput < Max rate** due to DIFS, backoff, SIFS, RTS/CTS, ACK overheads (~50% of 11 Mbps in 802.11b).
- **HIPERLAN 2 lost to 802.11a** primarily due to ecosystem cost, not technical merit.
- **W-ATM introduced mobile QoS** concepts that became foundational for 3G/4G.
- **Free-space path loss (Friis):** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ ÔÇö higher frequencies (shorter $\lambda$) attenuate faster.
- **Shannon capacity** limits all wireless data: $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- **Module 1 attack surface (security preview):** Eavesdropping at PHY, MAC spoofing, rogue APs at management layer ÔÇö motivates study of WPA3/802.11i in later modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Mnemonic for PHY Standards:**
> **"B-b-b-b 2.4, A-a-a-a 5, G is 2.4 + 5's trick, N is MIMO stick"**
> (802.11b = 2.4 GHz, 802.11a = 5 GHz, 802.11g = 2.4 GHz with OFDM like 802.11a, 802.11n = MIMO both bands)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WiMax
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** WiMax

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**WiMax (Worldwide Interoperability for Microwave Access)** is a family of wireless broadband communication standards based on the **IEEE 802.16** suite, designed to provide high-throughput, long-range, last-mile broadband connectivity as an alternative to cable, DSL, and fibre. The standard is maintained by the **IEEE 802.16 Working Group** on Broadband Wireless Access (BWA), with commercial interoperability certified by the **WiMax Forum**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** WiMax is positioned as a Metropolitan Area Network (MAN) technology. From a **security perspective**, it is critical because its native security sublayer (PKM) has documented weaknesses that have driven the migration toward LTE/5G. The course treats WiMax as a *case study* of layer-2 wireless security evolution.

### 1.2 Evolution of the IEEE 802.16 Standard

| Amendment | Year | Common Name | Key Capability |
|-----------|------|-------------|----------------|
| IEEE 802.16 | 2001 | ÔÇö | Single-carrier PHY, 10ÔÇô66 GHz (LOS only) |
| IEEE 802.16a | 2003 | Fixed WiMax | 2ÔÇô11 GHz, OFDM PHY, NLOS support |
| IEEE 802.16d | 2004 | 802.16-2004 | Roll-up of prior amendments, fixed/nomadic |
| IEEE 802.16e | 2005 | Mobile WiMax | OFDMA, handover, 120 km┬ĚhÔü╗┬╣ mobility |
| IEEE 802.16m | 2010 | WiMax 2 / Release 2 | ITU-R IMT-Advanced candidate, up to **1 Gbps** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box ÔÇö "The Cellular Tower for a City"**
> Imagine a city where every home needs a high-speed internet pipe. Laying fibre to each home is expensive and slow. A single tall tower (the **Base Station ÔÇô BS**) broadcasts a strong radio signal over a **radius of up to 50 km**, and each home installs a small **Subscriber Station (SS)** antenna. The tower acts as the "city gateway," talking to an ISP backbone.
> - **Fixed WiMax** = the home never moves (like a satellite dish for the home).
> - **Mobile WiMax** = the receiver can be in a moving car/phone, handed over from one tower to the next.
> - **Security** in this analogy = locking the conversation between the home antenna and the tower so that a neighbour with a rooftop antenna cannot eavesdrop or impersonate the home.

### 1.4 Defining Physical & Performance Metrics

- **Operating Frequency Bands:** 2.3 GHz, 2.5 GHz, 3.5 GHz (licensed), 5.8 GHz (unlicensed).
- **Channel Bandwidth (B):** Scalable from **1.25 MHz** to **20 MHz**.
- **Peak Data Rate:** Up to **75 Mbps** (Fixed WiMax) and up to **128 Mbps** downlink + 56 Mbps uplink (Mobile WiMax, Release 1).
- **Cell Radius:** Up to **50 km** (LOS), **5ÔÇô15 km** (NLOS, typical urban).
- **Mobility Support:** Up to **120 km┬ĚhÔü╗┬╣** (16e), **350 km┬ĚhÔü╗┬╣** (16m).
- **Duplexing:** TDD or FDD (Time/Frequency Division Duplex).

### 1.5 Why WiMax is Critical for a Security Course

WiMax is the **first mainstream MAN standard that baked authentication and encryption directly into the MAC layer** through the **Privacy and Key Management (PKM) protocol**. Studying WiMax security exposes students to the *real-world failures* of early PKMv1 and the *improvements* in PKMv2 ÔÇö a blueprint for evaluating 4G/5G security evolution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Network Reference Model (NRM)

The WiMax Forum defined a **four-network logical model** to decouple access from services:

1. **MS (Mobile Station)** ÔÇô the end-user device.
2. **ASN (Access Service Network)** ÔÇô radio access infrastructure: BS + ASN-GW (gateway that terminates the IP/control plane and routes user traffic).
3. **CSN (Connectivity Service Network)** ÔÇô core IP backbone: AAA (Authentication, Authorisation, Accounting), DHCP, Home Agent (for mobility), internet gateways.
4. **NAP (Network Access Provider)** ÔÇô entity that owns the radio access (ASN).
5. **NSP (Network Service Provider)** ÔÇô entity that provides IP services to the subscriber (CSN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why split ASN and CSN?** It allows a single NSP to roam across multiple NAPs, mirroring the 3GPP model of "visited network vs. home network" ÔÇö directly relevant for *roaming security* questions.

### 2.2 IEEE 802.16 Protocol Stack (Layered View)

| Layer | Sublayer | Function |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **Application / IP** | ÔÇö | IPv4, IPv6, Ethernet, ATM payloads |
| **MAC (Data Plane)** | **Service-Specific Convergence Sublayer (CS)** | Maps external traffic (IP, Eth) to MAC SDUs |
| **MAC (Data Plane)** | **Common Part Sublayer (CPS)** | Bandwidth allocation, framing, ARQ, scheduling |
| **MAC (Security)** | **Privacy Sublayer (PKM)** | Authentication, key management, encryption |
| **PHY** | OFDM / OFDMA / SC / SCa | Modulation, coding, RF transmission |

### 2.3 Security Sublayer Architecture (Heart of Module 1)

The Privacy Sublayer contains **two PKM versions** that coexist:

- **PKMv1** (original): RSA-based unilateral authentication, single-key DES-CBC encryption. *Insecure*.
- **PKMv2** (added in 16e): Mutual authentication, EAP-based, AES-CCM encryption, group-key support. *Robust*.

Five mandatory security functions:

1. **Authentication** ÔÇô mutual identity verification of MS and BS.
2. **Authorisation** ÔÇô checking what the MS is *allowed* to do.
3. **Key Management** ÔÇô generation, distribution, and refresh of traffic keys.
4. **Confidentiality** ÔÇô encrypting user data on the air.
5. **Replay Protection** ÔÇô using CMAC/PN (packet number) chains.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Important LaTeX Notation Rule:** Because markdown tables break on vertical bars, all absolute values and conditional expressions use the commands `\vert` or `\mid` instead of a raw `|`.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Shannon Channel Capacity** | $$C \;=\; B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) \; \text{[bits/s]}$$ | Upper bound on bit rate; $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = SNR (linear). |
| 2 | **Spectral Efficiency of M-QAM** | $$\eta \;=\; \log_2(M) \;\; \text{bits/s/Hz}$$ | $M=4\to 2$, $M=16\to 4$, $M=64\to 6$, $M=256\to 8$. |
| 3 | **Net PHY Data Rate** | $$R_{\text{net}} \;=\; \eta \cdot B \cdot r_c \cdot N_{\text{sub}}/\text{symbol}$$ | $r_c$ = coding rate (e.g., 3/4). |
| 4 | **Free-Space Path Loss (dB)** | $$L_{\text{FS}} \;=\; 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz. |
| 5 | **OFDM Symbol Duration** | $$T_{\text{sym}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{FFT}}}{B} \;=\; N_{\text{FFT}} \cdot T_s$$ | $T_s$ = sampling period; with 10 MHz / 1024 FFT, $T_{\text{sym}}\approx 102.4\;\mu\text{s}$. |
| 6 | **Number of Used Subcarriers (16e)** | $$N_{\text{used}} \;=\; 128 \;\left(\tfrac{B}{1.25\,\text{MHz}}\right)$$ | Includes 60 subcarriers per OFDMA symbol slot for 5 MHz. |
| 7 | **AES-CCM Counter Increment** | $$\text{Block Counter} \;=\; A \;\vert\vert \; i \;\vert\mid \text{flag}$$ | A = nonce (13 B), $i$ = 16-bit counter. |
| 8 | **RSA Key Strength (bits)** | $$\log_2\!\text{secure} \;\ge\; 2048 \text{ bits (2025 NIST floor)}$$ | PKMv1 used 1024-bit Ôćĺ broken by 2010. |
| 9 | **Reed-Solomon Coding Gain** | $$G_c \;=\; 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r_c}\right) \text{ (approx, dB)}$$ | Trade-off between redundancy and energy per bit. |
| 10 | **Link Budget Equation** | $$P_{\text{tx}} + G_{\text{tx}} - L_{\text{FS}} - L_{\text{other}} + G_{\text{rx}} \;\ge\; P_{\text{rx, min}}$$ | Solves for maximum cell radius. |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Rural Broadband:** WiMax is still deployed in **rural India (BSNL, MTNL pilots)**, Africa, and Latin America because its 50 km cell radius beats cellular in sparse regions.
- **Backhaul for Wi-Fi Hotspots:** Acts as a metro-scale backhaul to remote Wi-Fi clusters.
- **Disaster Recovery:** Quickly deployable cellular-style connectivity.
- **Security Research Reference:** WiMax PKMv1 weaknesses (no mutual auth, static DES keys) became *textbook examples* in IEEE 802.11i/WPA2 and 3GPP AKA design.

### 2.6 Threat Model for WiMax (What the Defender Protects Against)

1. **Eavesdropping** on the wireless frame (mitigated by AES-CCM in PKMv2).
2. **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** during authorisation (mitigated by mutual EAP).
3. **Replay attacks** during key exchange (mitigated by CMAC sequence numbers).
4. **Rogue BS / impersonation** (mitigated by BS certificate verification in PKMv2).
5. **Denial of Service** via management-frame flooding (mitigated by CMAC integrity on management frames).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum WiMax Cell Radius from Link Budget

**Problem (a typical 7-mark KTU analytical question):** A WiMax BS transmits at **EIRP = 36 dBm** with a 14-dBi antenna, the MS has a receive antenna gain of 8 dBi, the receiver sensitivity is **Ôłĺ92 dBm** at a 64-QAM rate, and the operating frequency is **3.5 GHz**. Assuming free-space path loss plus 10 dB of other losses (cabling, foliage, building penetration), determine the maximum cell radius.

**Step 1 ÔÇö Convert EIRP to absolute transmit power at the MS front end:**

The received power at the MS is governed by the link budget identity:

$$P_{\text{rx}} \;=\; P_{\text{tx,EIRP}} + G_{\text{rx}} - L_{\text{FS}} - L_{\text{other}}$$

We require $P_{\text{rx}} \geq P_{\text{rx, min}}$ to maintain the link.

**Step 2 ÔÇö Solve for the maximum tolerable path loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{FS, max}} &\;=\; P_{\text{tx,EIRP}} + G_{\text{rx}} - L_{\text{other}} - P_{\text{rx, min}} \\
&\;=\; 36\;\text{dBm} + 8\;\text{dBi} - 10\;\text{dB} - (-92\;\text{dBm}) \\
&\;=\; 36 + 8 - 10 + 92 \\
&\;=\; 126\;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Invert the free-space path-loss equation to find distance $d$ (km):**

$$L_{\text{FS}} \;=\; 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$$

$$\begin{aligned}
20\log_{10}(d) &\;=\; L_{\text{FS}} - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.45 \\
20\log_{10}(d) &\;=\; 126 - 20\log_{10}(3500) - 32.45 \\
20\log_{10}(3500) &\;=\; 20 \times 3.5441 \;=\; 70.88 \\
20\log_{10}(d) &\;=\; 126 - 70.88 - 32.45 \;=\; 22.67 \\
\log_{10}(d) &\;=\; 1.1335 \\
d &\;=\; 10^{1.1335} \;\approx\; 13.6\;\text{km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Apply NLOS correction factor:** in real urban WiMax deployments, NLOS adds 15ÔÇô25 dB penalty, shrinking the radius to **2ÔÇô5 km**. This is why fixed WiMax is marketed for *rural* and mobile WiMax for *urban* cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU 2024 scheme ESE problems test both the formula and the *units conversion*. Marks are typically lost when students confuse MHz with GHz inside the $20\log_{10}(f)$ term. Always state $f$ in **MHz** in the FSPL constant 32.45 version.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Net PHY Data Rate for a 64-QAM Mobile WiMax Carrier

**Problem:** A 5 MHz carrier uses 1024-FFT OFDMA, 64-QAM, and PUSC permutation with coding rate **3/4**, **frame duration 5 ms**, and **48 data subcarriers per slot**. Find the peak downlink data rate.

**Step 1 ÔÇö Subcarriers per OFDM symbol:**

For 5 MHz in 16e, $N_{\text{FFT}} = 512$ (commonly used), with **N\_used = 420** (roughly). PUSC groups subcarriers into tiles; for this example, use the simplified figure:

$$N_{\text{data}} \;=\; 48 \;\text{(per slot symbol)}$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Bits per OFDM symbol (64-QAM):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{bits per subcarrier} &\;=\; \log_2(64) \;=\; 6 \\
\text{bits per slot symbol} &\;=\; 48 \times 6 \;=\; 288\;\text{bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Apply coding rate 3/4:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{coded bits per slot symbol} &\;=\; 288 / (3/4) \;\text{(raw)} \\
\text{net bits per slot symbol} &\;=\; 288 \times 0.75 \;=\; 216\;\text{bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Symbols per second and slots per frame:**

With 5 ms frame and 48 OFDM symbols per frame (standard PUSC):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{symbols/s} &\;=\; 48 / 0.005 \;=\; 9600\;\text{symbols/s} \\
R_{\text{peak, DL}} &\;=\; 216 \times 9600 \;\approx\; 2.07\;\text{Mbps per slot}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying by the number of slots per subchannel (e.g., 30 slots/symbol for full PUSC) yields a sector peak of Ôëł **46 Mbps** ÔÇö which matches the published Mobile WiMax Release-1 spec.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: PKMv2 Authentication Message Count for an EAP-TLS Exchange

**Question (Part-A style, 3 marks):** How many EAP-TLS messages are exchanged between MS, BS, and AAA to complete a *mutual* authentication under PKMv2?

**Step 1 ÔÇö List the message flow:**

1. MS Ôćĺ BS: **PKM-REQ Auth-Info (X.509 cert request)**
2. BS Ôćĺ MS: **PKM-RSP Auth-Info (BS's X.509 cert + AAAs' cert chain)**
3. MS Ôćĺ BS Ôćĺ AAA: **EAP-Response / Identity**
4. AAA Ôćĺ MS (relayed by BS): **EAP-Request / TLS-Start**
5. MS Ôćö AAA: **EAP-TLS ClientHello**
6. AAA Ôćö MS: **EAP-TLS ServerHello, Certificate, CertificateRequest, ServerHelloDone**
7. MS Ôćö AAA: **EAP-TLS Certificate, ClientKeyExchange, CertificateVerify, Finished**
8. AAA Ôćĺ MS: **EAP-TLS Finished**
9. AAA Ôćĺ MS (relayed): **EAP-Success** + **MSK (Master Session Key)**
10. MS Ôćĺ BS: **PKM-REQ SA-TEK-Challenge (encrypted with MSK)**
11. BS Ôćĺ MS: **PKM-RSP SA-TEK-Response (AK = Authorization Key derived from MSK)**
12. MS Ôćĺ BS: **PKM-REQ Key-Request (TEK = Traffic Encryption Key from AK)**
13. BS Ôćĺ MS: **PKM-RSP Key-Reply (TEK + TEK lifetime)**

**Step 2 ÔÇö Count and categorise:**

$$\text{Total messages} \;=\; 13$$

Categorisation: 4 PKM-management messages + 9 EAP-TLS protocol messages. This is the *gold standard* against which KTU examiners evaluate students' understanding of "mutual vs. unilateral" authentication.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: PKMv1 vs. PKMv2 (KTU's Favourite Comparison)

| Property | PKMv1 (802.16-2004) | PKMv2 (802.16e) | Consequence |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Authentication | Unilateral (BS Ôćĺ MS only) | **Mutual (EAP-based)** | PKMv1 vulnerable to MitM |
| Encryption Algorithm | DES-CBC (56-bit) | **AES-CCM (128-bit)** | PKMv1 broken in hours on a GPU |
| Key Refresh | Static AK | **Re-authentication + key hierarchy** | PKMv1 long-lived key Ôçĺ easier cracking |
| Integrity | None on data plane | **CMAC on data + management** | PKMv1 cannot detect bit-flipping |
| Handover Security | None | **HO-Key pre-distribution + 3-way handshake** | PKMv1 cannot support fast handover |
| Replay Protection | None | **Packet Number (PN) counter** | PKMv1 susceptible to replay |
| Group Keying | Not supported | **Multicast & broadcast service keys** | PKMv1 cannot serve IPTV securely |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Replay-Protection Counter in AES-CCM

The PN (Packet Number) field in the Generic MAC Header is **48 bits** long. To avoid wrap-around in a 5 ms frame, the same key may be reused until PN reaches $2^{48}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum frames at key lifetime} &\;=\; 2^{48} \;=\; 2.81 \times 10^{14} \\
\text{Time to exhaust at 100 fps} &\;=\; \dfrac{2^{48}}{100} \;\approx\; 89\;\text{years}
\end{aligned}$$

This proves that the 48-bit PN is **practically non-exhausting**; a wrap-around attack is infeasible.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WiMax Network Reference Model (NRM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MS1[MOBILE STATION MS]
    MS2[SUBSCRIBER STATION SS]
    BS1[BASE STATION BS]
    ASN1[ASN GATEWAY ASN GW]
    AAA1[AAA SERVER RADIUS]
    HA1[HOME AGENT MOBILE IP]
    DHCP1[DHCP SERVER]
    CSN1[CSN INTERNET GATEWAY]
    NAP1[NAP DOMAIN]
    NSP1[NSP DOMAIN]

    MS1 -->|Radio Air IF UW| BS1
    MS2 -->|Radio Air IF UW| BS1
    BS1 -->|R3 IP tunnel| ASN1
    ASN1 -->|R4 AAA RADIUS| AAA1
    ASN1 -->|R5 mobile IP| HA1
    ASN1 -->|R6 IP assignment| DHCP1
    AAA1 -->|R7 inter AAA| CSN1
    HA1 -->|R8 internet| CSN1
    DHCP1 -->|R9 internet| CSN1
    NAP1 --- BS1
    NAP1 --- ASN1
    NSP1 --- AAA1
    NSP1 --- HA1
    NSP1 --- CSN1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid lines are *data/control planes*. The dashed grouping boxes mark the **NAP** (radio owner) and the **NSP** (service provider). R3ÔÇôR9 are the standardised WiMax Forum reference points.

---

### 4.2 PKMv2 Authentication & Key-Management Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([MS Initial Ranging])
    req1[MS sends PKM REQ Auth Info Cert Request]
    resp1[BS replies PKM RSP Auth Info BS X509 Cert]
    eap1[MS sends EAP Response Identity]
    eap2[AAA sends EAP Request TLS Start]
    eap3[MS sends EAP TLS Client Hello]
    eap4[AAA sends EAP TLS Server Hello Cert CertReq Server Hello Done]
    eap5[MS sends EAP TLS Cert Client Key Exchange Cert Verify Finished]
    eap6[AAA sends EAP TLS Finished]
    eap7[AAA sends EAP Success with MSK]
    pkm1[MS derives PMK from MSK]
    pkm2[MS sends PKM REQ SA TEK Challenge encrypted]
    pkm3[BS sends PKM RSP SA TEK Response with AK]
    pkm4[MS sends PKM REQ Key Request]
    pkm5[BS sends PKM RSP Key Reply with TEK and lifetime]
    data([Secure Data Plane AES CCM Active])
    end1([Connection Established])

    start --> req1 --> resp1 --> eap1 --> eap2 --> eap3 --> eap4 --> eap5 --> eap6 --> eap7 --> pkm1 --> pkm2 --> pkm3 --> pkm4 --> pkm5 --> data --> end1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step Highlight ÔÇö Why derive PMK locally from MSK?**
> The MSK never leaves the **AAA server's RADIUS reply**. Both MS and AAA know MSK. The BS receives *only the AK derived from PMK*, so even a compromised BS cannot decrypt past sessions ÔÇö a crucial *forward-secrecy* property.

---

### 4.3 Security Sublayer Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PHY_LAYER[PHYSICAL LAYER OFDM OFDMA]
    MAC_CPS[MAC COMMON PART SUBLAYER]
    MAC_CS[MAC CONVERGENCE SUBLAYER CS]
    SEC_AUTH[AUTHENTICATION MODULE EAP TLS RSA]
    SEC_AKM[AUTHORIZATION AND KEY MANAGEMENT PKM]
    SEC_ENC[ENCRYPTION ENGINE AES CCM DES CBC]
    SEC_INT[INTEGRITY ENGINE CMAC]
    KEY_HIER[KEY HIERARCHY AK TEK KEK GKEK]
    EXT_IP[IP UPPER LAYERS]

    PHY_LAYER --> MAC_CPS
    MAC_CPS --> SEC_AUTH
    SEC_AUTH --> SEC_AKM
    SEC_AKM --> SEC_ENC
    SEC_AKM --> SEC_INT
    SEC_AKM --> KEY_HIER
    SEC_ENC --> MAC_CS
    SEC_INT --> MAC_CS
    MAC_CS --> EXT_IP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dotted box is the **Privacy Sublayer** that this course focuses on. Notice the parallel paths of *Encryption* and *Integrity* ÔÇö they share the **Key Hierarchy** but use distinct subkeys (TEK vs. CMAC key) to maintain cryptographic isolation.

---

### 4.4 OFDMA Frame Layout (Time-Frequency Grid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FRAME[OFDMA FRAME 5 MS]
    DL[DL SUBFRAME BS TO MS]
    UL[UL SUBFRAME MS TO BS]
    TTG[TTG GAP]
    RTG[RTG GAP]
    PREAMBLE[PREAMBLE FCH DL MAP UL MAP]
    DL_BURST1[DL BURST 1 QPSK]
    DL_BURST2[DL BURST 2 16 QAM]
    DL_BURST3[DL BURST 3 64 QAM]
    UL_BURST1[UL BURST 1 HANDOVER SS]
    UL_BURST2[UL BURST 2 NORMAL SS]

    FRAME --> PREAMBLE
    PREAMBLE --> DL
    DL --> DL_BURST1
    DL --> DL_BURST2
    DL --> DL_BURST3
    DL --> TTG
    TTG --> UL
    UL --> UL_BURST1
    UL --> UL_BURST2
    UL --> RTG
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ÔÇö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ÔÇö Dec 2023]** *Define WiMax. Differentiate between Fixed WiMax (802.16d) and Mobile WiMax (802.16e) in terms of PHY, mobility, and peak data rate.*

**Model Answer (10ÔÇô12 lines):**

WiMax (Worldwide Interoperability for Microwave Access) is a wireless MAN technology standardised by IEEE 802.16 and certified by the WiMax Forum, providing broadband access up to 50 km.

| Aspect | Fixed WiMax (16d) | Mobile WiMax (16e) |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| PHY | Single-carrier OFDM 256-FFT | Scalable OFDMA 128/512/1024/2048-FFT |
| Mobility | None / Nomadic | Up to **120 km┬ĚhÔü╗┬╣** with hard/soft handover |
| Peak Rate | ~**75 Mbps** (20 MHz, 64-QAM) | ~**128 Mbps DL / 56 Mbps UL** |
| Duplex | FDD or H-FDD | Primarily TDD |
| Security | PKMv1 (DES-CBC) | **PKMv2 (AES-CCM + EAP)** |
| Cell Radius | Up to 50 km (LOS) | 2ÔÇô5 km (urban NLOS) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ÔÇö June 2024]** *What is PKM? Why is PKMv2 preferred over PKMv1 in modern WiMax deployments?*

**Model Answer:**

**PKM (Privacy and Key Management)** is the protocol at the heart of the IEEE 802.16 MAC Security Sublayer. It performs three functions: (i) authentication of the Subscriber/Mobile Station, (ii) authorisation to access the network, and (iii) traffic-key (TEK) distribution.

PKMv2 is preferred because:

1. **Mutual authentication** via EAP-TLS prevents a rogue BS from impersonation.
2. **AES-CCM (128-bit)** replaces weak DES-CBC (56-bit), making brute-force infeasible.
3. **Integrity protection** (CMAC) on both data and management frames prevents bit-flipping and replay.
4. **Group keying** enables secure multicast/broadcast (IPTV, push-to-talk).
5. **Handover key pre-distribution** allows fast, secure handovers.
6. **Key hierarchy** with AK Ôćĺ KEK Ôćĺ TEK supports periodic re-keying.

---

### 5.2 Part B ÔÇö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers **either** the full Q-A or full Q-B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ÔÇö *WiMax Network Architecture and Security Sublayer*

> **[KTU University Exam ÔÇö Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Explain the WiMax Network Reference Model (NRM) with a neat block diagram. List the standard reference points and explain the role of ASN and CSN.  (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Section a):**

The WiMax NRM, as defined by the WiMax Forum's **Network Architecture (Stage 2) document**, decomposes the network into four logical entities:

1. **MS (Mobile/Subscriber Station):** The end device.
2. **ASN (Access Service Network):** Owns the radio (BS) and the ASN-GW. Implements Layer-2 forwarding, mobility anchoring, and radio-resource management. An ASN may be shared by multiple NSPs.
3. **CSN (Connectivity Service Network):** Hosts AAA, DHCP, Home Agent, billing, and the Internet gateway. Provides the IP services the subscriber pays for.
4. **NAP/NSP:** The business-roles (Network Access Provider / Network Service Provider) ÔÇö the former owns radio, the latter owns the service.

**Reference Points (Use these in your diagram):**

- **R1:** MS Ôćö BS (air interface, IEEE 802.16 PHY/MAC).
- **R2:** MS Ôćö CSN (IP-layer auth, DHCP, HA).
- **R3:** BS Ôćö ASN-GW (intra-ASN, e.g., GRE/MPLS).
- **R4:** ASN-GW Ôćö AAA (RADIUS/Diameter).
- **R5:** ASN-GW Ôćö HA (Mobile IP signalling).
- **R6:** ASN-GW Ôćö DHCP.
- **R7:** AAA Ôćö AAA (roaming, RADIUS proxies).
- **R8/R9:** HA / CSN Ôćö Internet.

**Why this split is important for security:** It enables a **roaming architecture** in which a visited NSP can authenticate the MS via its home NSP's AAA across R7, using secure Diameter/TLS tunnels ÔÇö analogous to 3GPP S6a.

**(b)** *With a functional block diagram, describe the IEEE 802.16 Security Sublayer. Explain the role of PKMv1 and PKMv2 protocols, highlighting the cryptographic algorithms used in each.  (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Section b):**

The Security (Privacy) Sublayer sits between the MAC CPS and PHY. It comprises:

- **Authentication Module** ÔÇô RSA (PKMv1) or **EAP-TLS/EAP-AKA** (PKMv2).
- **Authorisation & Key-Management Module** ÔÇô PKM protocol state machine.
- **Encryption Engine** ÔÇô DES-CBC (PKMv1) or **AES-CCM** (PKMv2).
- **Integrity Engine** ÔÇô *Absent in PKMv1*; **CMAC with AES-128** in PKMv2.
- **Key Hierarchy** ÔÇô AK (PKMv1) expanded to **PMK Ôćĺ AK Ôćĺ KEK Ôćĺ TEK + GKEK** in PKMv2.

| Parameter | PKMv1 | PKMv2 |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Auth | RSA X.509 (one-way) | EAP (mutual) |
| Key lengths | 1024-bit RSA, 56-bit DES | 2048-bit RSA, 128-bit AES |
| Cipher | DES-CBC | **AES-CCM** |
| Integrity | None | **AES-CMAC** |
| Handover | Not defined | **HO-optimisation keys** |
| Multicast | Not supported | **GKEK + GSAK** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ÔÇö Pitfall #1:** Many students write *"PKM is for encryption"* and stop there. PKM is a *protocol*; encryption is *one* of its outputs. To score above 7/7 in Part B, you must explicitly name the **algorithm and key length** for each phase, and mention the **key hierarchy** (AK Ôćĺ KEK Ôćĺ TEK).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ÔÇö *WiMax Vulnerabilities and PKMv2 Authentication Flow*

> **[KTU University Exam ÔÇö Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Identify and explain the major security vulnerabilities of the original IEEE 802.16-2004 standard. Why did these vulnerabilities force the move to PKMv2 in 16e?  (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Section a):**

The original 802.16-2004 (PKMv1) had **six critical vulnerabilities**:

1. **Unilateral Authentication:** Only the BS authenticates to the MS. The MS *trusts* whatever BS is in range ÔÇö enabling a **rogue BS / evil-twin attack** that hands out fake keys.
2. **DES-CBC (56-bit) Encryption:** Brute-forced on commodity GPUs in under 4 hours. Once cracked, **all user traffic in the cell is exposed**.
3. **No Integrity Protection:** A bit-flipper can flip ciphertext bits in DES-CBC; without a MAC, the attack is undetected and can force re-keying.
4. **No Replay Protection:** A recorded PKM message can be replayed within its lifetime ÔÇö the BS may issue another TEK to an attacker.
5. **Static Authorisation Key (AK):** Issued once and used for the entire subscriber lifetime. *Compromise = total compromise of the subscription*, with no forward secrecy.
6. **No Handover Security:** When a Mobile WiMax 16e-compatible device roams, the 16d key material is unusable. The standards committee had to retrofit handover into 16e.

These issues combined to **break confidentiality, integrity, and availability**. The IEEE response in **802.16e (2005)** was the **PKMv2 protocol** with mutual authentication, AES-CCM, CMAC integrity, periodic re-keying, and handover keys.

**(b)** *Illustrate, with a neat sequence/message-flow diagram, the complete PKMv2 EAP-TLS authentication between MS, BS, and AAA. Clearly indicate the role of the MSK, PMK, AK, and TEK in this flow.  (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Section b):**

**Message Sequence (also see diagram in Section 4.2):**

| Step | Source Ôćĺ Destination | Message | Purpose |
|------|----------------------|---------|---------|
| 1 | MS Ôćĺ BS | PKM-REQ Auth-Info | Request BS certificate |
| 2 | BS Ôćĺ MS | PKM-RSP Auth-Info | Deliver BS X.509 cert |
| 3 | MS Ôćĺ BS Ôćĺ AAA | EAP-Resp/Identity | Identify subscriber |
| 4 | AAA Ôćĺ MS | EAP-Req/TLS-Start | Begin TLS handshake |
| 5ÔÇô8 | MS Ôćö AAA | EAP-TLS (ClientHello, ServerHello, Cert, ÔÇŽ) | Mutual auth via X.509 |
| 9 | AAA Ôćĺ MS (relayed) | EAP-Success + **MSK** | Convey Master Session Key |
| 10 | MS / BS locally | **PMK = PRF-128(MSK, "PMK", MAC_MS vert MAC_BS)** | Generate Pairwise Master Key |
| 11 | MS Ôćĺ BS | PKM-REQ SA-TEK-Challenge | Negotiate SA |
| 12 | BS Ôćĺ MS | PKM-RSP SA-TEK-Response with **AK** | Issue Authorisation Key |
| 13 | MS Ôćĺ BS | PKM-REQ Key-Request | Request TEK |
| 14 | BS Ôćĺ MS | PKM-RSP Key-Reply with **TEK** | Issue Traffic Encryption Key |

**Key Hierarchy (each is *derived*, not transmitted, except MSK):**

$$\text{MSK} \;\xrightarrow{\text{PRF-128}}\; \text{PMK} \;\xrightarrow{\text{PRF-128}}\; \text{AK} \;\xrightarrow{\text{PRF}}\; \text{KEK} \;\;\text{and}\;\; \text{TEK}$$

- **MSK (Master Session Key):** 64 bytes, sent via RADIUS to BS; never sent over the air.
- **PMK (Pairwise Master Key):** Derived locally by MS and BS, *never* sent on the wire.
- **AK (Authorisation Key):** 160-bit lifetime-bound; identifiers the SA.
- **KEK (Key Encryption Key):** 128-bit, encrypts the TEK during Key-Reply.
- **TEK (Traffic Encryption Key):** 128-bit AES-CCM key, lifetime 30 min ÔÇô 12 h.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ÔÇö Pitfall #2:** A common 1-mark loss is conflating **PMK and MSK**. The MSK is the *raw* EAP output (RFC 5216); the PMK is the *802.16-specific* derivation. Always state the PRF-128 function in the derivation line.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Block ÔÇö Memorise Before Exam**

- [x] **WiMax** = IEEE 802.16-based MAN wireless broadband, ranges up to **50 km LOS**.
- [x] **802.16d** = Fixed WiMax; **802.16e** = Mobile WiMax (adds OFDMA, handover, PKMv2).
- [x] **802.16m** = WiMax Release-2, **IMT-Advanced** candidate, up to **1 Gbps**.
- [x] **Protocol Stack:** CS (convergence) Ôćĺ CPS (common part) Ôćĺ **Privacy Sublayer (PKM)** Ôćĺ PHY (OFDM/OFDMA).
- [x] **Reference Points:** R1 (air), R2 (IP), R3 (BSÔÇôASN-GW), R4 (AAA), R5 (HA), R6 (DHCP), R7 (roaming), R8/R9 (Internet).
- [x] **PKMv1 weaknesses:** unilateral auth, DES-CBC (56-bit), no integrity, static AK, no handover security.
- [x] **PKMv2 improvements:** mutual EAP, AES-CCM (128-bit), CMAC integrity, periodic re-key, GKEK for multicast, HO keys.
- [x] **Key Hierarchy:** MSK Ôćĺ PMK Ôćĺ AK Ôćĺ KEK + TEK (each derived via PRF-128).
- [x] **AES-CCM** = Counter-mode encryption + CBC-MAC integrity in one pass; uses **48-bit PN** for replay protection.
- [x] **Common QAM mapping to remember:** 4-QAM (QPSK) = 2 bits, 16-QAM = 4 bits, 64-QAM = 6 bits, 256-QAM = 8 bits.
- [x] **Free-Space Path Loss equation** (with $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz): $L_{\text{FS}} = 20\log d + 20\log f + 32.45$ dB.
- [x] **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ ÔÇö sets the *theoretical* upper bound on data rate.
- [x] **WiMax channel bandwidths:** 1.25, 5, 7, 8.75, 10, 20 MHz (scalable OFDMA).
- [x] **Frequency bands:** 2.3 / 2.5 / 3.5 / 5.8 GHz (3.5 GHz most common globally).
- [x] **ASN vs. CSN:** ASN owns radio (BS+ASN-GW); CSN owns services (AAA, HA, Internet).
- [x] **EAP-TLS message count for mutual auth in PKMv2** Ôëł **13 messages** (4 PKM + 9 EAP).
- [x] **Cipher upgrade reason:** DES is broken; AES-128 is NIST-approved until **2030+** with proper key management.
- [x] **Exam mantra:** Always state *which* key is *where* ÔÇö examiners love the phrase *"MSK is sent via RADIUS; PMK is never transmitted"*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Local Loop (WLL) and Migration Path to UMTS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wireless Local Loop (WLL) and Migration Path to UMTS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Wireless Local Loop (WLL) and Migration Path to UMTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wireless Local Loop (WLL) ÔÇö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wireless Local Loop (WLL)** is a system that uses a **wireless radio link** as the substitute for the **copper-wire "last mile"** connection between a subscriber's premises and the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) or Public Land Mobile Network (PLMN) exchange. It is also referred to as **Fixed Wireless Access (FWA)** or **Radio in the Loop (RITL)**.

Formally, WLL is defined by the ITU-R as a system that provides **fixed telecommunications services** (voice, data, fax) to subscribers using **radio transmission** between the user terminal and the network infrastructure, where the terminal location is **permanently fixed** at the time of installation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (KTU Highlight):** The "last mile" or "local loop" historically refers to the **final segment of the telecommunication network** spanning from the central office (CO) to the customer premises. WLL replaces this segment with a **wireless air-interface link**, typically operating in the **450 MHz, 900 MHz, 1800 MHz, 1900 MHz, 2.1 GHz, 2.3 GHz, 2.5 GHz, 3.5 GHz, or 5.8 GHz** bands, depending on regional regulator allocation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ÔÇö Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **traditional landline telephone** in your house. Normally, a **thick copper wire** runs from a telephone pole (or underground duct) all the way to a large building called the **telephone exchange** in your city. This physical wire is called the **local loop**. Now imagine if that copper wire **suddenly vanished** and your phone was magically connected to the exchange through **invisible radio waves** travelling through the air, much like how a Wi-Fi router at home connects your laptop to the Internet without a cable. **That invisible radio replacement for the copper wire is exactly the Wireless Local Loop.**

In other words:

- **Old way:** Customer Ôćĺ Copper Cable Ôćĺ Telephone Exchange
- **WLL way:** Customer Ôćĺ Antenna + Radio Box Ôćĺ Base Station Ôćĺ Telephone Exchange

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Exam Tip):** If a **Wi-Fi router** is a "wireless local loop" between your laptop and your home Internet, then a **WLL system** is a "wireless local loop" between your entire house/office and the **public telephone network**. The only difference is **scale, range, and regulatory spectrum licensing**.

### 1.3 Migration Path to UMTS ÔÇö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UMTS (Universal Mobile Telecommunications System)** is the **3G (Third Generation)** mobile communication standard developed by **3GPP (3rd Generation Partnership Project)**. It is designed to succeed the **2G GSM** family and deliver **higher data rates, packet-switched services, and global roaming**. The **migration path** is the structured, step-by-step evolution of network infrastructure, radio access technology, and core network elements from **2G Ôćĺ 2.5G Ôćĺ 2.75G Ôćĺ 3G**, allowing operators to **reuse existing investments** while progressively adding capacity and services.

The migration path typically follows:

**GSM (2G) Ôćĺ GPRS (2.5G) Ôćĺ EDGE (2.75G) Ôćĺ UMTS (3G) Ôćĺ HSDPA/HSUPA (3.5G)**

### 1.4 Intuition for the Migration Path

Think of the migration as **upgrading an old highway**:

- The **2G GSM highway** is a **two-lane road** built in the 1990s ÔÇö great for slow voice calls but jams up with data traffic.
- **GPRS** adds a **shoulder lane** that some cars (data packets) can use during off-peak.
- **EDGE** widens the road to **three lanes** and paves it smoother.
- **UMTS** tears down the old road and builds a **brand-new, multi-lane expressway (WCDMA)** with on-ramps for high-speed Internet.
- **HSDPA/HSUPA** is the **expressway upgrade with turbo-lanes** for video streaming and downloads.

The genius of the migration is that **operators do not scrap the old infrastructure**; they **reuse towers, antenna sites, backhaul, and core network elements** wherever possible, gradually overlaying new technology on top of the old.

### 1.5 The "Why" of WLL and UMTS Migration

| Driver | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Last-Mile Bottleneck** | Laying copper/fiber in rural, hilly, or dense urban areas is **slow, expensive, and disruptive** |
| **Rapid Service Deployment** | WLL provides **telephony in days** instead of months for new subscribers |
| **Cost-Effectiveness** | One BTS replaces **kilometres of copper** cable deployment |
| **Bandwidth Demand** | 2G GSM cannot support **mobile Internet, video, and multimedia** ÔÇö 3G UMTS solves this |
| **Global Roaming** | UMTS offers a **single global standard** unlike the fragmented 2G world |
| **Spectrum Efficiency** | WCDMA gives **3ÔÇô5├Ś better capacity** than GSM for the same bandwidth |
