# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 WLL System Architecture ÔÇö Detailed Breakdown

A WLL system is composed of **four primary functional blocks**. Each has a specific role in replacing the copper local loop with a radio path.

### Block 1: Core Network (PSTN / PLMN Backbone)

- This is the **central switching infrastructure** of the service provider ÔÇö the same one that handles wired subscribers.
- It performs **call routing, billing, subscriber authentication, and interconnection** with other carrier networks.
- **WLL is transparent to the core network**: the core treats the WLL subscriber identically to a wired subscriber.

### Block 2: Wireless Local Loop Controller (WLC) / Base Station Controller (BSC)

- Acts as the **central intelligence unit** of the WLL access network.
- Manages **multiple Base Transceiver Stations (BTS)** simultaneously.
- Handles **radio resource allocation, handover signalling (if any), subscriber authentication, encryption key distribution, and traffic concentration** back to the core.
- In GSM-based WLL, this is the **BSC**; in proprietary WLL systems, it is called **WLC** or **RAS (Radio Access Switch)**.

### Block 3: Base Transceiver Station (BTS) / Radio Port

- The **air-interface termination point** of the network.
- Contains **transmitters, receivers, antennas, and signal processing hardware**.
- Provides the **wireless coverage footprint** ÔÇö typically a cell of **1 km to 50 km radius** depending on terrain, antenna height, and power class.
- Communicates with the WLC over **terrestrial links** (microwave, fiber, or leased E1/T1 lines).

### Block 4: Subscriber Unit (SU) / Customer Premises Equipment (CPE) / Fixed Subscriber Station

- Located at the **customer's premises** (house or office).
- Comprises an **outdoor antenna (yagi, panel, or dish)**, **modem/radio unit**, and **indoor interface** (RJ-11 telephone jack, Ethernet port, or both).
- **Fixed in location** ÔÇö the customer cannot roam with it.
- The antenna is **directional** (pointed at the BTS) to maximise gain and reduce interference.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Architecture Highlight:** The chain **PSTN Ôćĺ WLC Ôćĺ BTS Ôćĺ Air-Interface Ôćĺ SU** is the **mandatory answer pattern** for any "Explain WLL architecture" question. Examiners award marks for naming **all four blocks** and stating their **function** in sequence.

## 2.2 Classification of WLL Systems

### 2.2.1 By User Mobility Class

| WLL Type | Mobility | Typical Use Case | Subscriber Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed WLL** | Zero ÔÇö permanently installed | Rural telephony, replacing copper | Outdoor directional antenna + fixed indoor terminal |
| **Nomadic WLL** | Terminal can be unplugged and reconnected at another fixed point within the network | Temporary offices, second homes | Portable indoor unit with built-in antenna |
| **Mobile WLL** | Full mobility within coverage area (functionally identical to a cellular phone) | Vehicle-mounted or handheld terminals | Mobile handset or vehicle-mounted modem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks for a "classification of WLL", examiners expect the three-class split (Fixed / Nomadic / Mobile) and **at least one distinguishing parameter for each class**.

### 2.2.2 By Underlying Radio Technology

| Technology | Standard Body | Frequency Band | Multiple Access | Modulation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DECT** | ETSI | 1880ÔÇô1900 MHz | TDMA/TDD | GFSK |
| **PHS** | ARIB (Japan) | 1895ÔÇô1918 MHz | TDMA/TDD | ¤Ç/4-DQPSK |
| **GSM-based WLL** | ETSI / 3GPP | 900 / 1800 MHz | TDMA/FDMA | GMSK |
| **CDMA-based WLL** | TIA / 3GPP2 | 450 / 800 / 1900 MHz | CDMA | QPSK / BPSK |
| **LMDS / MMDS** | ITU / FCC | 2ÔÇô40 GHz (LMDS), 2.1ÔÇô2.7 GHz (MMDS) | TDMA / FDMA / CDMA | QPSK / 16-QAM / 64-QAM |
| **WiMAX-based WLL** | IEEE 802.16 | 2.3 / 3.5 / 5.8 GHz | OFDMA | OFDM with QPSKÔÇô64QAM |

## 2.3 Advantages and Disadvantages of WLL

### Advantages

1. **Rapid Deployment** ÔÇö A cell site can be activated in weeks versus months for trenching and laying cable.
2. **Lower Civil Works Cost** ÔÇö Avoids the cost of digging, ducting, and right-of-way permits.
3. **Reach in Difficult Terrain** ÔÇö Hills, rivers, forests, and islands are bypassed by the air interface.
4. **Scalable Capacity** ÔÇö Additional subscribers are added by **assigning new time slots or codes**, not by pulling new wires.
5. **Easy Upgrades** ÔÇö A software/hardware change at the BTS upgrades all subscribers.
6. **Lower Maintenance Cost** ÔÇö No physical cable to repair due to rodent damage, theft, or corrosion.

### Disadvantages

1. **Line-of-Sight (LoS) Requirement** ÔÇö Most WLL systems need a clear LoS between BTS and SU.
2. **Rain Fade / Foliage Loss** ÔÇö High-frequency systems (LMDS, mmWave) suffer signal degradation in rain or dense vegetation.
3. **Limited Bandwidth per SU** ÔÇö Shared cell capacity reduces per-user throughput.
4. **Spectrum Licensing** ÔÇö Requires **regulatory allocation and licensing fees**.
5. **Security Concerns** ÔÇö Radio waves are **broadcast**; without encryption, eavesdropping is trivial.
6. **Interference** ÔÇö Co-channel and adjacent-channel interference limits frequency reuse.

## 2.4 The Migration Path: From GSM to UMTS

### 2.4.1 Stage 1 ÔÇö GSM (2G)

- **Generation:** 2G
- **Multiple Access:** TDMA / FDMA
- **Channel Bandwidth:** 200 kHz per carrier
- **Timeslots per carrier:** 8
- **Modulation:** GMSK (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying)
- **Peak Data Rate:** **9.6 kbps** (circuit-switched data)
- **Switching Domain:** Circuit-switched
- **Limitation:** Designed for **voice**; data is an afterthought.

### 2.4.2 Stage 2 ÔÇö GPRS (General Packet Radio Service) ÔÇö 2.5G

- **Generation:** 2.5G
- **Multiple Access:** TDMA / FDMA (reuses GSM physical layer)
- **Modulation:** GMSK
- **Peak Data Rate (theoretical):** **115 kbps** (8 timeslots ├Ś 14.4 kbps)
- **Switching Domain:** **Packet-switched** overlay onto GSM
- **New Network Nodes:** **SGSN (Serving GPRS Support Node)** and **GGSN (Gateway GPRS Support Node)**
- **Limitation:** Uses the same GMSK modulation, so the air-interface cannot deliver higher spectral efficiency per timeslot.

### 2.4.3 Stage 3 ÔÇö EDGE (Enhanced Data rates for GSM Evolution) ÔÇö 2.75G

- **Generation:** 2.75G
- **Multiple Access:** TDMA / FDMA
- **Modulation:** **8-PSK (in addition to GMSK)** ÔÇö 3 bits per symbol versus 1 bit per symbol
- **Peak Data Rate (theoretical):** **384 kbps** (8 timeslots ├Ś 48 kbps)
- **Switching Domain:** Packet-switched (extension of GPRS)
- **Limitation:** Still uses GSM's 200 kHz carrier; cannot reach UMTS-class capacity.

### 2.4.4 Stage 4 ÔÇö UMTS (Universal Mobile Telecommunications System) ÔÇö 3G

- **Generation:** 3G
- **Multiple Access:** **WCDMA (Wideband Code Division Multiple Access)**
- **Channel Bandwidth:** **5 MHz** per carrier
- **Chip Rate:** **3.84 Mcps** (Mega chips per second)
- **Modulation:** QPSK (uplink), QPSK / 16-QAM (downlink)
- **Peak Data Rate:** **2 Mbps** (with HSDPA, up to **14.4 Mbps**)
- **Switching Domain:** Both circuit-switched (voice) and packet-switched (data)
- **Core Network Evolution:** Adds **MSC, SGSN, GGSN, and introduces RNC + Node B** in the access network.

### 2.4.5 Stage 5 ÔÇö HSDPA / HSUPA (3.5G)

- **HSDPA (High-Speed Downlink Packet Access):** Downlink peak **14.4 Mbps** using 16-QAM.
- **HSUPA (High-Speed Uplink Packet Access):** Uplink peak **5.76 Mbps**.
- **HSPA+:** Introduces **64-QAM** and **MIMO**, raising the downlink peak to **42 Mbps** (and theoretically 84 Mbps with MIMO).

## 2.5 UMTS Architecture

UMTS is logically divided into three major domains:

### Domain 1 ÔÇö User Equipment (UE)

- **Mobile Equipment (ME)** ÔÇö the physical handset, modem, or data card.
- **UMTS Subscriber Identity Module (USIM)** ÔÇö a smart card storing subscriber identity, authentication keys, and the IMSI.

### Domain 2 ÔÇö UTRAN (UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network)

- **Node B** ÔÇö equivalent of a GSM BTS; performs **air-interface transmission/reception, spreading/despreading, and error correction**.
- **Radio Network Controller (RNC)** ÔÇö equivalent of a GSM BSC; controls **multiple Node Bs, handles radio resource management, handover, and macro-diversity combining**.

### Domain 3 ÔÇö Core Network (CN)

- **MSC (Mobile Switching Centre)** ÔÇö circuit-switched voice.
- **SGSN (Serving GPRS Support Node)** ÔÇö packet-switched mobility and session management.
- **GGSN (Gateway GPRS Support Node)** ÔÇö gateway to external packet data networks (Internet, corporate LAN).
- **HLR / VLR / AuC** ÔÇö subscriber database, location database, and authentication centre.

### Formula Table 1 ÔÇö Capacity and Throughput

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Upper bound on error-free data rate over bandwidth $B$ | bits/sec |
| 2 | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B}$ | Useful bit rate per unit bandwidth | bits/sec/Hz |
| 3 | WCDMA Processing Gain | $\text{PG} = \dfrac{R_c}{R_b}$ | Spreading chip rate / information bit rate | dimensionless (linear) |
| 4 | Processing Gain in dB | $\text{PG}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{R_c}{R_b}\right)$ | Same as #3 in decibels | dB |
| 5 | Required $E_b / N_0$ for BER | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{\text{SNR}}{\eta}$ | Energy per bit to noise spectral density | dimensionless (linear) |
| 6 | CDMA Cell Capacity (Polgar) | $M = 1 + \dfrac{W/R}{E_b/N_0} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\alpha \cdot \nu}$ | Max users per cell ÔÇö $W$ chip rate, $R$ voice rate, $\alpha$ voice activity, $\nu$ cell-sector gain | users |
| 7 | GPRS Theoretical Peak Rate | $R_{\text{GPRS}} = N_{\text{TS}} \times 14.4 \text{ kbps}$ | $N_{\text{TS}}$ = number of allocated timeslots (max 8) | kbps |
| 8 | EDGE Theoretical Peak Rate | $R_{\text{EDGE}} = N_{\text{TS}} \times 48 \text{ kbps}$ | 8-PSK gives 3 bits/symbol vs 1 for GMSK | kbps |

| 10 | HSDPA Peak Rate | $R_{\text{HSDPA}} = 14.4 \text{ Mbps}$ | 16-QAM, 15 codes, 3.84 Mcps | Mbps |
| 11 | Spreading Factor (SF) | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{R_c}{R_{\text{sym}}}$ | Symbols per chip; SF $\in [4, 512]$ for uplink | chips/symbol |

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 13 | Free-Space Path Loss | $L_{\text{FS}} = 32.45 + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})$ | Friis free-space model, $f$ in MHz, $d$ in km | dB |
| 14 | Received Power | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{\text{FS}} - L_{\text{other}}$ | Standard link budget | dBm |

If a derivation in an exam requires absolute values, **always write `\vert x \vert`** in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe parser.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **WLL today** is the foundation of **rural broadband** (especially in India, Africa, and Southeast Asia) under initiatives like **BharatNet, NOFN, and Digital India**, where wireless backhaul and fixed wireless CPEs connect **gram panchayats** to the optical-fiber core.
- **UMTS migration** underpins every 3G/4G/5G network worldwide ÔÇö without the GPRS/EDGE stepping stones, the leap from GSM to LTE/5G would have required **complete network replacement** and bankrupted operators.
- **Security in WLL and UMTS** (the focus of this course PECCT862) addresses the **unique vulnerabilities** introduced by the **broadcast nature of radio**: eavesdropping, IMSI catching, man-in-the-middle attacks, and the cryptographic mechanisms (MILENAGE, KASUMI, SNOW 3G) that defend them.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation ÔÇö WCDMA Processing Gain in dB

> **Given Data (Standard UMTS FDD parameters):**
> - WCDMA chip rate: $R_c = 3.84 \times 10^{6}$ chips/sec
> - Voice user bit rate (AMR 12.2 kbps): $R_b = 12.2 \times 10^{3}$ bits/sec
> - Spreading factor: $\text{SF} = 256$ (typical for voice)

**Step 1 ÔÇö State the definition of Processing Gain.**
Processing gain is defined as the ratio of the spread-spectrum chip rate to the user information bit rate.

$$\text{PG} = \frac{R_c}{R_b}$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\text{PG} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^{6}}{12.2 \times 10^{3}}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Perform the division.**

$$\text{PG} = \frac{3.84}{12.2} \times 10^{3} = 0.31475 \times 10^{3} = 314.75$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö State the result in linear form.**

$$\text{PG} \approx 315 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Step 5 ÔÇö Convert to decibels.**

$$\text{PG}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(314.75)$$

**Step 6 ÔÇö Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$\log_{10}(314.75) = \log_{10}(3.1475) + 2 = 0.4980 + 2 = 2.4980$$

**Step 7 ÔÇö Multiply by 10.**

$$\text{PG}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \times 2.4980 = 24.98 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 8 ÔÇö State the final answer.**

> **Result:** The WCDMA processing gain for a 12.2 kbps AMR voice user is **PG Ôëł 315 (linear) = 24.98 dB Ôëł 25 dB**. This is the **spreading gain** that allows WCDMA to push narrowband voice signals below the noise floor, giving the system its celebrated **interference-limited** behaviour.

## 3.2 Derivation ÔÇö Theoretical CDMA Cell Capacity (Polgar's Formula)

The Polgar capacity formula for a single-cell CDMA system is the **most important WCDMA capacity equation** in any KTU exam. The following is the **complete step-by-step derivation**, including the role of **voice activity factor, sectorisation gain, and required $E_b/N_0$**.

> **Notation:**
> - $M$ = number of simultaneous users per cell
> - $W$ = spread bandwidth (chip rate) in Hz
> - $R$ = information bit rate per user in bps
> - $(E_b/N_0)_{\text{req}}$ = bit-energy-to-noise ratio required for the target BER
> - $\alpha$ = voice activity factor (typical 0.4 to 0.5)
> - $\nu$ = sectorisation gain (1 for omnidirectional, 2.5 for 3-sector, 5 for 6-sector)

**Step 1 ÔÇö Start with the received power balance at the base station.**
In a single isolated cell, the total interference at the BTS is the sum of received powers from all $M$ active users.

$$I_{\text{total}} = (M - 1) \cdot P_r + \eta$$

where $P_r$ is the received power per user and $\eta$ is thermal noise (often neglected in capacity-limited analysis).

**Step 2 ÔÇö Define the bit-energy-to-noise-density ratio for one user.**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{P_r / R}{(M-1) P_r / W} = \frac{W / R}{(M - 1)}$$

This equation uses the identity $N_0 = I_{\text{total}} / W$ (interference power spread over the chip bandwidth). Substituting $M - 1 \approx M$ for large $M$:

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{W / R}{M}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Solve for M (the na├»ve capacity).**

$$M = \frac{W / R}{E_b / N_0}$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Introduce the voice activity factor $\alpha$.**
In a real voice conversation, a subscriber is **silent for ~50ÔÇô60% of the time**. During silence, the transmitter is either turned off or drastically reduced in power. The interference is therefore **multiplied by $\alpha$** on average, allowing more users to share the cell.

$$M = \frac{W / R}{E_b / N_0} \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha}$$

**Step 5 ÔÇö Introduce the sectorisation gain $\nu$.**
By replacing the omnidirectional antenna with a **3-sector or 6-sector antenna**, interference is reduced to roughly $1/\nu$ of the original because each sector sees only a fraction of the interferers. The capacity is then multiplied by $\nu$.

$$M = 1 + \frac{W / R}{E_b / N_0} \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{\nu}$$

> **Note:** The "1+" accounts for the fact that the reference user also contributes to its own self-interference; the "+1" is the additive correction for the *non-negligible* case of small $M$.

**Step 6 ÔÇö Substitute standard UMTS FDD values.**
- $W = 3.84 \times 10^{6}$ chips/sec
- $R = 9.6 \times 10^{3}$ bps (GSM-equivalent voice) or $12.2 \times 10^{3}$ bps (AMR)
- $E_b/N_0 = 7$ dB $\approx 5.01$ linear (typical for voice, 1% BER after Viterbi)
- $\alpha = 0.5$ (50% voice activity)
- $\nu = 2.5$ (3-sector antenna)

**Step 7 ÔÇö Plug into the formula (using $R = 9.6$ kbps first).**

$$M = 1 + \frac{3.84 \times 10^{6} / 9.6 \times 10^{3}}{5.01} \cdot \frac{1}{0.5} \cdot \frac{1}{2.5}$$

**Step 8 ÔÇö Compute the processing gain ratio.**

$$\frac{W}{R} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^{6}}{9.6 \times 10^{3}} = 400$$

**Step 9 ÔÇö Divide by $E_b/N_0$ (linear).**

$$\frac{400}{5.01} = 79.84$$

**Step 10 ÔÇö Multiply by $1/\alpha$.**

$$79.84 \times \frac{1}{0.5} = 79.84 \times 2 = 159.68$$

**Step 11 ÔÇö Multiply by $1/\nu$.**

$$159.68 \times \frac{1}{2.5} = 63.87$$

**Step 12 ÔÇö Add 1.**

$$M = 1 + 63.87 = 64.87$$

**Step 13 ÔÇö State the result.**

> **Result:** A single 3-sector UMTS FDD cell can support **approximately 64 simultaneous 9.6 kbps voice users** in a 5 MHz carrier under standard assumptions. In practice, this becomes the **pole capacity** that is derated by **loading factor, neighbour-cell interference, and pilot pollution** to give the **operational capacity** of **40ÔÇô45 users per sector** or **120ÔÇô135 users per 3-sector cell**.

## 3.3 Derivation ÔÇö GPRS/EDGE Timeslot Throughput

**Step 1 ÔÇö Recall the GSM timeslot structure.**
A GSM radio frame is 4.615 ms long and contains **8 timeslots** (TS0ÔÇôTS7). Each timeslot carries **156.25 bits** in one burst period, using GMSK (1 bit/symbol).

**Step 2 ÔÇö Compute the gross bit rate per timeslot.**

$$R_{\text{TS}} = \frac{156.25 \text{ bits}}{0.577 \text{ ms (per TS)}} = 270.83 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Subtract GSM signalling overhead (Ôëł 1/3 of gross rate).**
After deducting **FEC, framing, and signalling overhead**, the **net data rate per timeslot in GMSK** is approximately **14.4 kbps**.

**Step 4 ÔÇö Calculate the maximum GPRS rate over 8 timeslots.**

$$R_{\text{GPRS, max}} = 8 \times 14.4 = 115.2 \text{ kbps}$$

This matches the standard 3GPP TS 03.64 figure.

**Step 5 ÔÇö Recalculate for EDGE using 8-PSK.**
8-PSK transmits **3 bits per symbol** versus 1 bit/symbol for GMSK. Therefore the net rate per timeslot is **3 ├Ś 14.4 = 43.2 kbps**, with some coding overhead rounding it to ~48 kbps per timeslot in standard rate.

$$R_{\text{EDGE, max}} = 8 \times 48 = 384 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 6 ÔÇö Verify the modulation gain.**

$$\text{Gain} = \frac{384}{115.2} = 3.33 \times$$

> **Result:** EDGE delivers a **3.33├Ś peak-rate improvement over GPRS** purely through the 8-PSK modulation upgrade, with **no change in the underlying 200 kHz carrier bandwidth or timeslot structure**. This is the key engineering insight that allowed EDGE to be deployed as a **software upgrade** on existing GSM base stations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ÔÇö Migration Step Decision Criteria

The following matrix summarises the **trigger conditions and business rationale** for an operator to migrate from one stage to the next. This is a high-yield item for KTU viva and design questions.

| Migration Step | Trigger Condition | Technology Change | Required CAPEX | User Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GSM Ôćĺ GPRS | Subscriber demand for always-on data, MMS, WAP | Add SGSN + GGSN; software upgrade at BSC | Low | Transparent; pay-per-packet billing |
| GPRS Ôćĺ EDGE | Insufficient data throughput; EDGE handsets penetrate market | Swap BTS transceiver cards; enable 8-PSK | LowÔÇôMedium | Higher throughput, same coverage |
| EDGE Ôćĺ UMTS | Need for broadband data, video calling, mobile TV | New 5 MHz carrier; new Node B; new RNC | High | Step-change in user experience |
| UMTS Ôćĺ HSDPA | Demand for downlink-heavy services (YouTube, app stores) | Software upgrade at Node B; new scheduler | Low | 7├Ś downlink speedup |
| HSDPA Ôćĺ HSPA+ | Operators need LTE-like speeds without LTE spectrum | Enable 64-QAM, MIMO; software upgrade | Low | 3├Ś additional speedup |
