# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ÔÇö WLL System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end functional flow** of a WLL system, from the PSTN core down to the customer premises. The wireless air interface is highlighted as a dashed radio link to distinguish it from wired backhaul.

```mermaid
graph TB
    PSTN[PSTN or PLMN Core Network]
    WLC[Wireless Local Loop Controller WLC or BSC]
    BTS1[BTS Cell Sector Alpha]
    BTS2[BTS Cell Sector Beta]
    BTS3[BTS Cell Sector Gamma]
    SU1[Subscriber Unit House A]
    SU2[Subscriber Unit House B]
    SU3[Subscriber Unit Office C]
    SU4[Subscriber Unit Office D]
    SU5[Subscriber Unit Rural E]
    SU6[Subscriber Unit Rural F]

    PSTN -- E1 or fiber backhaul --> WLC
    WLC -- Abis or microwave --> BTS1
    WLC -- Abis or microwave --> BTS2
    WLC -- Abis or microwave --> BTS3
    BTS1 -. 900 MHz air interface .-> SU1
    BTS1 -. 900 MHz air interface .-> SU2
    BTS2 -. 1800 MHz air interface .-> SU3
    BTS2 -. 1800 MHz air interface .-> SU4
    BTS3 -. 2100 MHz air interface .-> SU5
    BTS3 -. 2100 MHz air interface .-> SU6
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = **wired** backhaul (E1/T1, fiber, or PtP microwave).
- **Dashed arrows** = **wireless** air-interface link ÔÇö the actual "WLL" replacement for the copper drop.
- Each BTS can serve **dozens to hundreds** of SUs within its radio footprint.
- The WLC is the **single point of intelligence**; failure of one BTS does not collapse the system.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ÔÇö End-to-End Migration Path: 2G Ôćĺ 5G

This diagram shows the **full migration lineage** from 2G GSM all the way to 4G LTE-A. The 2G Ôćĺ 3G segment is the core focus of this module; the 4G/5G endpoints are shown for context.

```mermaid
graph LR
    GSM[GSM 2G - 9.6 kbps]
    HSCSD[HSCSD 2G plus - 57.6 kbps]
    GPRS[GPRS 2.5G - 115 kbps]
    EDGE[EDGE 2.75G - 384 kbps]
    UMTS[UMTS 3G R99 - 2 Mbps]
    HSDPA[HSDPA 3.5G - 14.4 Mbps]
    HSUPA[HSUPA 3.5G - 5.76 Mbps UL]
    HSPA[HSPA plus - 42 Mbps]
    LTE[LTE 3.9G - 100 Mbps]
    LTEA[LTE Advanced 4G - 1 Gbps]
    NR5G[5G NR - 10 Gbps]

    GSM --> HSCSD
    GSM --> GPRS
    GPRS --> EDGE
    EDGE --> UMTS
    UMTS --> HSDPA
    UMTS --> HSUPA
    HSDPA --> HSPA
    HSUPA --> HSPA
    HSPA --> LTE
    LTE --> LTEA
    LTEA --> NR5G
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **upward diagonal** in data rates reflects the **progressive spectrum-efficiency gains** delivered by each generation.
- **Branching** (e.g., HSCSD from GSM, HSDPA + HSUPA from UMTS) reflects that operators often **adopted multiple parallel upgrades** in the field.
- The **3G turning point (UMTS)** is the **architectural break**: all prior stages used **TDMA/FDMA on a 200 kHz carrier**; UMTS introduces a **brand-new 5 MHz WCDMA air interface** and a new core-network overlay.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 ÔÇö UMTS Network Architecture (3GPP Release 99)

This block diagram shows the **functional split** of the UMTS system into UE, UTRAN, and Core Network, along with the **standard interfaces** (Uu, IuB, IuR, IuCS, IuPS).

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph UE[User Equipment Domain]
        ME[Mobile Equipment ME]
        USIM[USIM Smart Card]
    end

    subgraph UTRAN[UTRAN Domain]
        NB1[Node B Site Alpha]
        NB2[Node B Site Beta]
        RNC[Radio Network Controller RNC]
    end

    subgraph CN[Core Network Domain]
        MSC[MSC VLR for Circuit Switched]
        SGSN[SGSN for Packet Switched]
        GGSN[GGSN Gateway to Internet]
        HLR[HLR Home Location Register]
        AUC[AuC Authentication Centre]
    end

    EXT[External Networks PSTN Internet]

    ME -- Uu air interface --> NB1
    ME -- Uu air interface --> NB2
    USIM -.inserted into.-> ME
    NB1 -- IuB --> RNC
    NB2 -- IuB --> RNC
    RNC -- IuCS --> MSC
    RNC -- IuPS --> SGSN
    MSC -- signaling --> HLR
    SGSN -- signaling --> HLR
    HLR -- key derivation --> AUC
    SGSN -- Gn --> GGSN
    MSC -- connects to --> EXT
    GGSN -- connects to --> EXT
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **UE** = handset + USIM. The USIM holds the **IMSI, Ki, OP/OPC, and algorithm identifiers** that drive UMTS security (covered in later modules of PECCT862).
- **UTRAN** = Node B (radio) + RNC (intelligence). The RNC is the **first point of cryptographic enforcement** for user-plane data and signalling.
- **Core Network** = MSC (voice) + SGSN (packet) + GGSN (gateway) + HLR/AuC (subscriber database).
- The **dashed link** between USIM and ME indicates **physical insertion** of the smart card, not a network protocol.
- The **solid arrow from HLR to AuC** represents **internal key-management signalling** for generating authentication vectors (AVs).

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 ÔÇö Migration Strategy Trade-off Matrix

This is a **decision tree** showing when an operator should **choose each migration step** based on traffic type and demand.

```mermaid
graph TB
    START[Operator has deployed GSM network]

    START --> Q1{Dominant traffic is voice only}
    Q1 -- Yes --> STAY2G[Stay on GSM 2G no migration needed]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is there a low-cost data demand for SMS MMS WAP}

    Q2 -- Yes --> GPRSNODE[Deploy GPRS 2.5G packet overlay add SGSN GGSN]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is broadband data or video required}

    Q3 -- No --> EDGENODE[Upgrade to EDGE 2.75G software only 8-PSK]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Operator has 3G spectrum license}

    Q4 -- No --> STAYG[Stay on EDGE wait for spectrum]
    Q4 -- Yes --> UMTSNODE[Deploy UMTS 3G new 5 MHz WCDMA carrier]

    UMTSNODE --> Q5{Downlink heavy traffic YouTube streaming}
    Q5 -- Yes --> HSDPANODE[Add HSDPA software upgrade at Node B]
    Q5 -- No --> MAINTAIN[Maintain UMTS R99 circuit plus packet]

    GPRSNODE --> EDGENODE
    EDGENODE --> STAYG
    HSDPANODE --> MAINTAIN
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The diagram is a **decision-support flowchart** that an operator's planning team would use to justify CAPEX to the board.
- Note that **GPRS, EDGE, and HSDPA are software upgrades** ÔÇö they require **minimal new hardware** and are the **cheapest migration steps**.
- The **UMTS migration is the most expensive** because it requires a new **5 MHz spectrum block, new Node B hardware, and a new RNC**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) ÔÇö *[KTU University Exam ÔÇö Dec 2023, Model Paper]*

> **"Define Wireless Local Loop (WLL). List any four advantages of WLL over the conventional wired local loop."**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Wireless Local Loop (WLL) is a system that provides telecommunication services to subscribers using a **radio link** between the customer's premises and the service provider's core network, **replacing the copper-wire drop** of the conventional local loop. It is also known as **Fixed Wireless Access (FWA)**.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Rapid deployment** ÔÇö A WLL BTS can be commissioned in weeks without trenching.
2. **Lower civil-works cost** ÔÇö Avoids the high cost of laying and maintaining copper cables, especially in difficult terrain (hills, rivers, islands).
3. **Scalable capacity** ÔÇö New subscribers are added by **assigning additional radio resources** (time slots or codes) at the BTS, without pulling new wires to each house.
4. **Easy upgrades** ÔÇö A modulation change (e.g., GMSK to 8-PSK in EDGE) at the BTS instantly upgrades all subscribers.

> **Marks Distribution Hint (KTU Pattern):** 1 mark for the correct definition, 0.5 marks each for the four advantages = **1 + 2 = 3 marks**.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) ÔÇö *[KTU University Exam ÔÇö June 2024, Model Paper]*

> **"What is meant by the 'migration path to UMTS'? Name the intermediate steps between GSM and UMTS."**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The "migration path to UMTS" is the **planned, phased evolution of a 2G GSM mobile network into a 3G UMTS network** through a sequence of intermediate technology upgrades. Each step reuses parts of the existing infrastructure (towers, backhaul, billing, core) while introducing new capabilities, thereby **protecting operator investment** and **minimising service disruption**.

**Intermediate steps (in order):**

1. **GSM (2G)** ÔÇö circuit-switched voice + 9.6 kbps data.
2. **GPRS (2.5G)** ÔÇö packet-switched overlay; peak 115 kbps.
3. **EDGE (2.75G)** ÔÇö 8-PSK modulation upgrade; peak 384 kbps.
4. **UMTS (3G)** ÔÇö WCDMA air interface; peak 2 Mbps.

> **Marks Distribution Hint:** 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for naming the four stages in correct order with data rates.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ÔÇö Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both choices must be attempted in full if asked, but the student answers **only one** of the two alternatives.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) ÔÇö *[KTU University Exam ÔÇö Dec 2023]*

#### Part (a) ÔÇö 7 Marks

> **"Explain the architecture of a Wireless Local Loop (WLL) system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block."**

**Model Answer:**

A WLL system consists of **four functional blocks** connected in a chain from the service-provider core to the customer premises:

**Block 1 ÔÇö PSTN / Core Network:**
The central switching system of the operator. It provides **call routing, billing, subscriber authentication, and interconnection** with other carrier networks. The WLL access network is **transparent** to the core ÔÇö it treats the WLL subscriber identically to a wired subscriber.

**Block 2 ÔÇö Wireless Local Loop Controller (WLC) / BSC:**
The intelligence unit of the WLL access network. It manages **multiple BTSs**, performs **radio resource allocation, subscriber authentication, encryption key distribution, and traffic concentration** back to the core. In GSM-based WLL, this is the **BSC**.

**Block 3 ÔÇö Base Transceiver Station (BTS) / Radio Port:**
The air-interface termination point. It contains **transmitters, receivers, antennas, and signal-processing hardware**, and provides the **wireless coverage footprint** (typical cell radius 1ÔÇô50 km). It communicates with the WLC over a **terrestrial link** (E1/T1, fiber, or PtP microwave).

**Block 4 ÔÇö Subscriber Unit (SU) / Customer Premises Equipment (CPE):**
Located at the customer's premises. It comprises an **outdoor directional antenna** (yagi, panel, or dish), a **radio/modem unit**, and an **indoor interface** (RJ-11 telephone jack and/or Ethernet port). The SU is **fixed in location** at the time of installation.

**Block Diagram:** (see Mermaid Diagram 1 in Section 4)

> **Marks Split (KTU Examiner Key):**
> - Naming the four blocks: 2 marks.
> - Explaining the function of each block: 4 marks (1 mark each).
> - Neat block diagram with arrows: 1 mark.
> - **Total = 7 marks.**

---

#### Part (b) ÔÇö 7 Marks

> **"Describe the step-by-step migration from GSM to UMTS. For each step, state the modulation scheme and peak data rate."**

**Model Answer:**

The migration from GSM (2G) to UMTS (3G) is a four-step evolution that allows operators to **reuse infrastructure** and **introduce higher data rates progressively**.

| Step | Standard | Modulation | Peak Data Rate | Key Change |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GSM (2G) | **GMSK** | **9.6 kbps** | Voice + low-speed circuit-switched data |
| 2 | GPRS (2.5G) | **GMSK** (reused) | **115 kbps** | Add **packet-switching** core (SGSN, GGSN); reuse GSM air interface |
| 3 | EDGE (2.75G) | **8-PSK** (in addition to GMSK) | **384 kbps** | Software upgrade at BTS; 3 bits per symbol |
| 4 | UMTS (3G) | **QPSK (UL), QPSK/16-QAM (DL)** | **2 Mbps** | New **5 MHz WCDMA** air interface; new Node B and RNC |

**Key insights for the examiner:**

- **GPRS** is a **packet-core upgrade** ÔÇö no change to the air-interface modulation.
- **EDGE** is a **modulation upgrade** at the BTS ÔÇö minimal hardware change.
- **UMTS** is an **air-interface revolution** ÔÇö the operator must procure a new 5 MHz spectrum block, install new Node Bs, and deploy a new RNC.
- The migration protects CAPEX because the **PSTN core, billing system, and antenna masts** are reused at every step.

> **Marks Split (KTU Examiner Key):**
> - Table of four stages with modulation and data rate: 4 marks.
> - Explanation of *why* each step is needed and *what* changes: 2 marks.
> - Conclusion (infrastructure reuse): 1 mark.
> - **Total = 7 marks.**

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) ÔÇö *[KTU University Exam ÔÇö June 2024]*

#### Part (a) ÔÇö 7 Marks

> **"Explain the UMTS network architecture as specified by 3GPP. Differentiate between UTRAN and the Core Network."**

**Model Answer:**

3GPP Release 99 specifies the UMTS architecture as **three functional domains**:

**Domain 1 ÔÇö User Equipment (UE):**
- **Mobile Equipment (ME)** ÔÇö the physical handset, modem, or data card.
- **USIM (UMTS Subscriber Identity Module)** ÔÇö a smart card storing the **IMSI, the long-term secret key Ki, OP/OPC values, and the algorithm identifiers (MILENAGE, KASUMI, SNOW 3G)**.

**Domain 2 ÔÇö UTRAN (UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network):**
- **Node B** ÔÇö the base transceiver station. Performs **air-interface transmission/reception, spreading/despreading, physical-layer error correction, and inner-loop power control**.
- **Radio Network Controller (RNC)** ÔÇö the access-network intelligence. Manages **multiple Node Bs, performs radio resource management, handover, macro-diversity combining, and outer-loop power control**. Equivalent to the GSM BSC.

**Domain 3 ÔÇö Core Network (CN):**
- **MSC (Mobile Switching Centre)** + VLR ÔÇö handles **circuit-switched voice calls**.
- **SGSN (Serving GPRS Support Node)** ÔÇö handles **packet-switched session management, mobility, and authentication** for packet users.
- **GGSN (Gateway GPRS Support Node)** ÔÇö **gateway** to external packet data networks such as the public Internet and corporate intranets.
- **HLR (Home Location Register) + AuC (Authentication Centre)** ÔÇö **subscriber database** and **cryptographic key generator** for UMTS AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement).

**Differences between UTRAN and Core Network:**

| Parameter | UTRAN | Core Network |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Radio access | Switching + routing |
| **Elements** | Node B, RNC | MSC, SGSN, GGSN, HLR, AuC |
| **Domain** | Radio access network | Backbone transport |
| **Interface to UE** | Uu (air interface) | None ÔÇö talks to UTRAN only |
| **Key responsibility** | Radio resource management | Subscriber management, call/session control, billing |
| **Speed of operation** | Real-time (millisecond scheduling) | Slower (call/session timescale) |

> **Marks Split (KTU Examiner Key):**
> - Correct identification of three domains: 1.5 marks.
> - Naming the elements in each domain: 3 marks.
> - One-line description of each element: 1.5 marks.
> - Difference table UTRAN vs CN: 1 mark.
> - **Total = 7 marks.**

---

#### Part (b) ÔÇö 7 Marks

> **"Discuss WCDMA technology used in UMTS. List any five key WCDMA parameters and explain the concept of processing gain."**

**Model Answer:**

**WCDMA (Wideband Code Division Multiple Access)** is the **air-interface technology** standardised by 3GPP for UMTS frequency-division-duplex (FDD) mode. Unlike GSM (which uses TDMA/FDMA on a 200 kHz carrier), WCDMA spreads each user's signal over a wide **5 MHz carrier bandwidth** using a unique **pseudo-random spreading code** at a chip rate of **3.84 Mcps**. Multiple users share the same 5 MHz carrier simultaneously, distinguished only by their codes.

**Five Key WCDMA Parameters:**

| # | Parameter | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Carrier Bandwidth** | **5 MHz** | ~25├Ś wider than GSM's 200 kHz ÔÇö supports higher data rates |
| 2 | **Chip Rate** | **3.84 Mcps** | 3,840,000 chips per second; defines the spreading bandwidth |
| 3 | **Spreading Factor (SF)** | **4 to 512** (UL) | SF = chip rate / symbol rate; higher SF Ôćĺ lower data rate but more processing gain |
| 4 | **Frame Length** | **10 ms** (15 slots/frame, 2560 chips/slot) | Defines the transmission time interval (TTI) granularity |
| 5 | **Modulation** | **QPSK (UL), QPSK / 16-QAM (DL)** | Quadrature Phase Shift Keying for spectral efficiency |

**Concept of Processing Gain (PG):**

Processing gain is the **spreading gain** that WCDMA achieves by spreading each user's narrowband signal over a wide bandwidth.

$$\text{PG} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{\text{Chip rate}}{\text{User bit rate}}$$

For a voice user at 12.2 kbps with chip rate 3.84 Mcps:

$$\text{PG} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{12.2 \times 10^3} \approx 315 \quad \text{(linear)} = 24.98 \text{ dB}$$

**Why it matters:** The processing gain of 25 dB pushes the voice signal **25 dB below the noise floor** after despreading. This is the **fundamental reason** WCDMA is called an **interference-limited** system: the system's capacity is governed by **how much interference it can tolerate**, not by thermal noise.

> **Marks Split (KTU Examiner Key):**
> - WCDMA concept explanation: 1.5 marks.
> - Table of five parameters: 2.5 marks (0.5 each).
> - Processing gain concept + formula: 1.5 marks.
> - Numerical example: 1 mark.
> - "Why it matters" insight: 0.5 marks.
> - **Total = 7 marks.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in WLL / UMTS Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing BTS and Node B** ÔÇö They are *not* identical. Node B (UMTS) and BTS (GSM) both terminate the air interface, but Node B also performs **spreading/despreading and inner-loop power control** that the GSM BTS does *not*. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** if the student simply equates the two.
>
> 2. **Wrong data rate units** ÔÇö Peak data rates must always be quoted with the correct unit: **9.6 kbps for GSM, 115 kbps for GPRS, 384 kbps for EDGE, 2 Mbps for UMTS, 14.4 Mbps for HSDPA**. Writing "9.6 Mbps for GSM" is a **sure-shot 1-mark penalty**.
>
> 3. **Confusing 2G with 3G switching** ÔÇö GSM is **circuit-switched**, GPRS introduces **packet-switched**, UMTS has **both**. Saying "GPRS is circuit-switched" loses 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "+1" in Polgar's formula** ÔÇö The correct capacity formula is $M = 1 + \frac{W/R}{E_b/N_0} \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha \nu}$. Dropping the "+1" gives the *approximate* answer; including it is the **exam-safe** answer.
>
> 5. **Listing GSM data rate as 9.6 kbps but not specifying it is "circuit-switched"** ÔÇö Examiners look for the keyword *"circuit-switched"*; a bare number is **incomplete** and may cost 0.5 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing up the FDD and TDD modes of UMTS** ÔÇö UMTS has two duplex modes: **FDD (WCDMA)** and **TDD (TD-CDMA)**. The 3.84 Mcps chip rate applies to **FDD only**. Writing this in a TDD context loses 0.5 marks.
>
> 7. **Spelling/punctuation in "UMTS"** ÔÇö Always capitalise as **UMTS**, not "Umts" or "umts".

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision bullets ÔÇö read these 15 minutes before walking into the exam hall.**

### Core WLL Concepts
- WLL = **wireless replacement** of the copper local loop; also called **Fixed Wireless Access (FWA)**.
- Four-block architecture: **PSTN Ôćĺ WLC Ôćĺ BTS Ôćĺ SU**.
- Three mobility classes: **Fixed, Nomadic, Mobile WLL**.
- LoS is required for most WLL deployments; **high-frequency (LMDS/mmWave) systems suffer rain fade**.

### Migration Path
- **GSM (2G)** Ôćĺ GMSK Ôćĺ 9.6 kbps Ôćĺ circuit-switched.
- **GPRS (2.5G)** Ôćĺ GMSK Ôćĺ 115 kbps Ôćĺ packet-switched overlay (SGSN + GGSN).
- **EDGE (2.75G)** Ôćĺ 8-PSK Ôćĺ 384 kbps Ôćĺ modulation upgrade.
- **UMTS (3G)** Ôćĺ WCDMA Ôćĺ 2 Mbps Ôćĺ **architectural break** (5 MHz carrier).
- **HSDPA (3.5G)** Ôćĺ 16-QAM Ôćĺ 14.4 Mbps.
- **HSPA+ (3.5G+)** Ôćĺ 64-QAM + MIMO Ôćĺ 42 Mbps.
- **LTE (3.9G)** Ôćĺ OFDMA Ôćĺ 100 Mbps.
- **LTE-A (4G)** Ôćĺ 1 Gbps.

### UMTS Architecture
- Three domains: **UE, UTRAN, Core Network**.
- UE = ME + USIM.
- UTRAN = **Node B + RNC**.
- Core Network = **MSC + SGSN + GGSN + HLR + AuC**.

### WCDMA Parameters
- Chip rate = **3.84 Mcps**.
- Carrier BW = **5 MHz**.
- Frame length = **10 ms** (15 slots ├Ś 2560 chips).
- SF range = **4 to 512**.
- Modulation = **QPSK (UL), QPSK/16-QAM (DL)**.

### Key Formulas (Memorise These)
- $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ ÔÇö Shannon capacity.
- $\text{PG} = R_c / R_b$ ÔÇö processing gain.
- $\text{PG}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(R_c / R_b)$ ÔÇö processing gain in dB.
- $M = 1 + \dfrac{W/R}{(E_b/N_0) \cdot \alpha \cdot \nu}$ ÔÇö Polgar CDMA cell capacity.
- $R_{\text{GPRS}} = 8 \times 14.4 = 115.2$ kbps.
- $R_{\text{EDGE}} = 8 \times 48 = 384$ kbps.

### Security Hooks (for PECCT862)
- USIM holds the **IMSI, Ki, OP/OPC, MILENAGE, KASUMI, SNOW 3G** ÔÇö UMTS security primitives.
- WLL air interface is **broadcast** ÔÇö eavesdropping, **IMSI catching**, and **man-in-the-middle attacks** are inherent risks.
- UMTS introduced **mutual authentication** (network authenticates to the user, not just the user to the network) and **integrity protection** of signalling ÔÇö a major upgrade over GSM's one-way authentication.

> **End of Topic ÔÇö Wireless Local Loop (WLL) and Migration Path to UMTS**
> *(Module 1, PECCT862 ÔÇö Security in Wireless Networks, KTU 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UMTS Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** UMTS Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**UMTS (Universal Mobile Telecommunications System)** is the **3rd Generation (3G)** mobile communication system standardized by the **3GPP (3rd Generation Partnership Project)**. It is one of the radio access standards adopted by the **ITU (International Telecommunication Union)** under the **IMT-2000 (International Mobile Telecommunications-2000)** family. UMTS employs **Wideband Code Division Multiple Access (W-CDMA)** as its terrestrial air-interface radio access technology, designed to deliver higher data rates, global roaming, multimedia support, and Quality of Service (QoS) differentiation over 2G/GSM networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> UMTS is a **packet-switched + circuit-switched hybrid** 3G architecture, unlike the purely circuit-switched GSM. It supports a minimum bandwidth of **5 MHz** with a chip rate of **3.84 Mcps**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-lane toll booth system** on a national highway:
- In **GSM (2G)**, only one narrow lane exists, and each car (caller) gets a dedicated time slot. It's organized but slow and rigid.
- In **UMTS (3G)**, the entire highway is open, and each car is given a **unique colored sticker (spreading code)**. All cars travel simultaneously, but the toll collector can separate them by color. This is **W-CDMA**.

So UMTS is essentially: *"Everyone talks at the same time, on the same frequency, but each call has a unique code so receivers can pull out their own conversation."*

## 1.3 Key Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| **Chip Rate** | **3.84 Mcps** (Mega chips per second) |
| **Carrier Bandwidth** | **5 MHz** |
| **Uplink Frequency** | **1885 ÔÇô 2025 MHz** |
| **Downlink Frequency** | **2110 ÔÇô 2200 MHz** |
| **Frame Duration** | **10 ms** |
| **Slots per Frame** | **15** |
| **Max Data Rate (theoretical)** | **2 Mbps** (indoor/small cells) |
| **Min Data Rate (mandatory)** | **144 kbps** (vehicular) |
| **Spreading Factor (SF) Range** | **4 to 256** (uplink), **4 to 512** (downlink) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3.84 Mcps and 5 MHz?**
> The chip rate of 3.84 Mcps occupies a bandwidth of roughly 5 MHz (with roll-off filters). This gives a processing gain that makes W-CDMA robust against multipath fading.

## 1.4 Evolution Context (Predecessor to UMTS)

UMTS was designed as the **3G successor to GSM (2G)**. The architectural evolution is:
- **1G** Ôćĺ Analog (AMPS, NMT) Ôćĺ Voice only
- **2G** Ôćĺ Digital (GSM) Ôćĺ Voice + SMS (9.6 kbps)
- **2.5G** Ôćĺ GPRS / EDGE Ôćĺ Packet data (up to 384 kbps)
- **3G** Ôćĺ **UMTS (W-CDMA)** Ôćĺ Broadband mobile (up to 2 Mbps)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 UMTS Network Architecture ÔÇö The Three Domains

UMTS is logically divided into **three major domains**, each with a distinct responsibility:

### Domain 1: User Equipment (UE)
The mobile device + its **SIM card (USIM in UMTS)**. The UE contains:
- **ME (Mobile Equipment)** ÔÇö physical handset
- **USIM (UMTS Subscriber Identity Module)** ÔÇö holds subscriber identity, keys, and authentication credentials

### Domain 2: UTRAN (UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network)
The radio access part of UMTS. Composed of:
- **Node B** ÔÇö the base station (analogous to BTS in GSM). Handles physical-layer transmission/reception over the **Uu air interface**.
- **RNC (Radio Network Controller)** ÔÇö controls multiple Node Bs. Manages radio resource allocation, handover, and power control (analogous to BSC in GSM).

### Domain 3: Core Network (CN)
The backbone that routes calls and data. Includes:
- **MSC / VLR** ÔÇö circuit-switched voice
- **SGSN (Serving GPRS Support Node)** ÔÇö packet-switched data anchor
- **GGSN (Gateway GPRS Support Node)** ÔÇö gateway to external packet networks (Internet)
- **HLR / AuC** ÔÇö subscriber database + authentication center

## 2.2 Why W-CDMA? ÔÇö The Core "Why"

- **CDMA principle**: All users share the **same 5 MHz band simultaneously**, separated by **unique orthogonal/quasi-orthogonal codes**.
- **Spread Spectrum**: Each bit is multiplied by a high-rate pseudo-random code (chip sequence), spreading its bandwidth.
- **Processing Gain** = spreading factor Ôćĺ improves resistance to interference and eavesdropping (a security bonus).
- **Soft Handover**: UE can connect to **multiple Node Bs simultaneously** during handover Ôćĺ no "ping-pong" effect seen in GSM.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Chip Rate ($R_c$)** | $R_c = 3.84 \times 10^6$ | chips/sec (Mcps) |
| **Data Rate ($R_b$)** | $R_b = \frac{R_c}{SF}$ | bits/sec |
| **Spreading Factor (SF)** | $SF = \frac{R_c}{R_b}$ | Integer, 4ÔÇô512 |
| **Processing Gain (PG)** | $PG = 10 \log_{10}(SF)$ | dB |
| **Frame Duration** | $T_{frame} = 15 \text{ slots} \times 2560 \text{ chips} \times T_{chip}$ | 10 ms |
| **Chip Duration** | $T_{chip} = \frac{1}{R_c} \approx 0.26 \,\mu s$ | microseconds |
| **Bandwidth** | $BW \approx 5 \text{ MHz}$ | MHz |
| **Bit Energy to Noise Density** | $\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{SF \cdot S}{(N + I)}$ | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility of these equations:**
> The $R_b = R_c / SF$ relationship is what allows UMTS to **adapt data rates on-the-fly** per user. A user far from Node B gets a **high SF** (low data rate, robust link); a user close gets a **low SF** (high data rate, peak throughput).

## 2.4 UMTS Operating Modes

UMTS supports **two duplex modes**:
1. **FDD (Frequency Division Duplex)** ÔÇö Uplink and downlink on **separate frequency bands** (most common).
2. **TDD (Time Division Duplex)** ÔÇö Uplink and downlink on the **same frequency**, separated in time (used for unpaired spectrum, e.g., indoor hotspots).

## 2.5 Quality of Service (QoS) Classes in UMTS

UMTS introduced **four standardized QoS traffic classes**, a major leap from GSM:

| QoS Class | Use Case | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Conversational** | Voice, video call | Low delay, symmetric |
| **Streaming** | Video on demand | Low delay, asymmetric |
| **Interactive** | Web browsing, gaming | Low error rate, request-response |
| **Background** | Email, FTP | No delay guarantee, best-effort |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Data Rate from Spreading Factor

**Given:**
- Chip rate $R_c = 3.84 \times 10^6$ chips/sec
- Spreading factor $SF = 4$

**Find:** Data rate $R_b$ in kbps.

### Step 1 ÔÇö State the fundamental relationship

In W-CDMA, each user data bit is multiplied by $SF$ chips. Therefore, the number of bits transmitted per second equals the chip rate divided by the number of chips per bit.

$$
R_b = \frac{R_c}{SF}
$$

### Step 2 ÔÇö Substitute the known values

We have $R_c = 3.84 \times 10^6$ chips/sec and $SF = 4$:

$$
R_b = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{4}
$$

### Step 3 ÔÇö Perform the division

$$
3.84 \times 10^6 \div 4 = 0.96 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec}
$$

### Step 4 ÔÇö Convert to kilobits per second (kbps)

Since $1 \text{ Mbps} = 1000 \text{ kbps}$:

$$
R_b = 0.96 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 960 \text{ kbps}
$$

### Step 5 ÔÇö Conclude

$$
\boxed{R_b = 960 \text{ kbps when } SF = 4}
$$

> **Conversion logic recap:** Chip rate carries the unit "chips per second"; dividing by the dimensionless count $SF$ yields "bits per second." Conversion to kbps is purely a unit prefix change.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Processing Gain in dB

**Given:** $SF = 256$

**Find:** Processing gain $PG$ in dB.

### Step 1 ÔÇö State the processing gain formula

Processing gain expresses the spreading factor on a logarithmic (dB) scale, defined as ten times the base-10 logarithm of the spreading factor:

$$
PG = 10 \log_{10}(SF)
$$

### Step 2 ÔÇö Substitute the spreading factor

We have $SF = 256$. Insert this value into the formula:

$$
PG = 10 \log_{10}(256)
$$

### Step 3 ÔÇö Evaluate the logarithm

Since $256 = 2^8$, the base-10 logarithm can be written as:

$$
\log_{10}(256) = 8 \log_{10}(2)
$$

Using $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$:

$$
\log_{10}(256) = 8 \times 0.30103 = 2.40824
$$

### Step 4 ÔÇö Multiply by 10

$$
PG = 10 \times 2.40824 = 24.0824 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 5 ÔÇö Conclude

$$
\boxed{PG \approx 24.08 \text{ dB when } SF = 256}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This 24 dB gain means the spread signal can be **24 dB below the noise floor** and still be recovered ÔÇö a key reason W-CDMA is robust against jamming and interference.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Computing the Frame Duration

**Given:** A UMTS frame has 15 slots, each slot contains 2560 chips, and $T_{chip} = \frac{1}{R_c} = \frac{1}{3.84 \times 10^6}$ seconds.

**Find:** Total frame duration.

### Step 1 ÔÇö Compute the slot duration

Each slot has 2560 chips. The duration of one slot is:

$$
T_{slot} = 2560 \times T_{chip} = 2560 \times \frac{1}{3.84 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{slot} = \frac{2560}{3.84 \times 10^6} \approx 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.6667 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 2 ÔÇö Compute the frame duration

The frame contains 15 slots, so the frame duration is:

$$
T_{frame} = 15 \times T_{slot} = 15 \times 0.6667 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{frame} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 ÔÇö Conclude

$$
\boxed{T_{frame} = 10 \text{ ms (as standardized by 3GPP)}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: GSM vs. UMTS

| Parameter | GSM (2G) | UMTS (3G) |
|---|---|---|
| Multiple Access | TDMA / FDMA | **W-CDMA** |
| Carrier Bandwidth | 200 kHz | **5 MHz** |
| Switching | Circuit-switched | **Circuit + Packet** |
| Data Rate (max) | 9.6 kbps (GSM), 384 kbps (EDGE) | **2 Mbps** |
| Handover | Hard handover | **Soft / Softer handover** |
| QoS Classes | None | **4 standardized classes** |
| Air Interface | Um | **Uu** |
| Subscriber Identity | SIM | **USIM (with stronger crypto)** |
| Chip Rate | N/A | **3.84 Mcps** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UMTS High-Level Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER_EQUIPMENT
        UE[UE - User Equipment]
        USIM[USIM Card]
    end

    subgraph UTRAN["UTRAN (UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network)"]
        NB1[Node B 1]
        NB2[Node B 2]
        RNC[Radio Network Controller RNC]
    end

    subgraph CORE_NETWORK["Core Network (CN)"]
        MSC[MSC / VLR]
        SGSN[SGSN]
        GGSN[GGSN]
        HLR[HLR / AuC]
    end

    EXT[External Networks - Internet / PSTN]

    UE -- "Uu Air Interface" --> NB1
    UE -- "Uu Air Interface" --> NB2
    NB1 -- "Iub Interface" --> RNC
    NB2 -- "Iub Interface" --> RNC
    RNC -- "Iu-CS Interface" --> MSC
    RNC -- "Iu-PS Interface" --> SGSN
    MSC --> HLR
    SGSN --> HLR
    SGSN -- "Gn Interface" --> GGSN
    GGSN --> EXT
    MSC --> EXT

    USIM -. "Embedded in" .- UE
```

> **Note:** The **Uu** interface is the radio (air) link between the UE and Node B. The **Iub** is between Node B and RNC. The **Iu** is between UTRAN and CN (split into Iu-CS for circuit-switched and Iu-PS for packet-switched traffic).

---

## 4.2 W-CDMA Spreading & Despreading Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Data Bit - rate Rb] --> B[Multiplier]
    C[Spreading Code - rate Rc, 3.84 Mcps] --> B
    B --> D[Spread Signal - 5 MHz bandwidth]
    D --> E[Channel - with noise and interference]
    E --> F[Receiver Multiplier]
    G[Same Spreading Code - synchronized] --> F
    F --> H[Despread Signal - narrow band]
    H --> I[Original User Data Bit - recovered]
```

> **Key idea:** At the transmitter, the low-rate user bit is multiplied by a high-rate pseudo-random code, spreading its spectrum. At the receiver, multiplying by the same synchronized code "despreads" the desired signal back to a narrow band, while leaving other users' signals spread (appearing as noise).

---

## 4.3 UMTS Protocol Stack Layers (User Plane)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer - Voice, Video, Web]
    L2[IP Layer - Network protocols]
    L3[PDCP - Packet Data Convergence Protocol]
    L4[RLC - Radio Link Control]
    L5[MAC - Medium Access Control]
    L6[Physical Layer - W-CDMA, 3.84 Mcps]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Define UMTS. List any two advantages of UMTS over GSM.

**Model Answer:**
**UMTS (Universal Mobile Telecommunications System)** is a 3rd Generation (3G) mobile communication standard developed by the **3GPP**, using **W-CDMA** as its air interface, standardized under IMT-2000.

**Two advantages over GSM:**
1. **Higher data rates** ÔÇö up to **2 Mbps** (versus 9.6 kbps in basic GSM), enabling multimedia and broadband services.
2. **Soft handover** ÔÇö UE maintains simultaneous connection to multiple Node Bs, eliminating call drops during handover (a hard-handover issue in GSM).
3. *(Optional 3rd)* QoS differentiation with four traffic classes for voice, video, web, and background data.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô May 2024]`
**Q:** What is the chip rate of W-CDMA in UMTS? Why is the spreading factor important?

**Model Answer:**
The chip rate of W-CDMA in UMTS is **3.84 Mcps (Mega chips per second)**.

**Importance of the Spreading Factor (SF):**
- Determines the **data rate per user** via $R_b = R_c / SF$.
- Higher SF Ôćĺ lower data rate but **better resistance to interference** (more processing gain in dB).
- Allows the system to **dynamically allocate capacity** between users ÔÇö users close to Node B get low SF (high data rate); users at cell edge get high SF (robust link).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> Per KTU ESE pattern, **answer ANY ONE** of the following full questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **UMTS network architecture**. Clearly identify the three domains and the key interfaces between them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The UMTS network architecture is logically partitioned into **three domains**:

**1. User Equipment (UE):** The mobile station consisting of the **Mobile Equipment (ME)** and the **UMTS Subscriber Identity Module (USIM)**. The USIM securely stores the subscriber's identity (IMSI) and authentication keys (K, OP/OPc), and runs the MILENAGE algorithm set for mutual authentication with the network.

**2. UTRAN (UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network):** Comprises:
- **Node B** ÔÇö performs physical-layer functions: modulation, channel coding, spreading, power control, and soft handover combining.
- **RNC (Radio Network Controller)** ÔÇö manages multiple Node Bs, performs radio resource management (RRM), admission control, congestion control, and macrodiversity combining.

**3. Core Network (CN):** The switching and routing backbone containing:
- **MSC/VLR** ÔÇö handles circuit-switched voice calls.
- **SGSN** ÔÇö handles packet-switched session management and mobility for PS domain.
- **GGSN** ÔÇö provides interworking with external IP networks (Internet).
- **HLR/AuC** ÔÇö central subscriber database and authentication center.

**Key Interfaces (refer to diagram in Section 4.1):**
- **Uu** ÔÇö Air interface between UE and Node B (W-CDMA).
- **Iub** ÔÇö Between Node B and RNC.
- **Iu-CS** ÔÇö Between RNC and MSC (circuit-switched traffic).
- **Iu-PS** ÔÇö Between RNC and SGSN (packet-switched traffic).
- **Gn** ÔÇö Between SGSN and GGSN (within CN).

UMTS is therefore a **layered architecture** that cleanly separates the radio access (UTRAN) from the core switching (CN), enabling independent evolution of both.

---

**(b)** Explain the **W-CDMA multiple access technique** used in UMTS. How does it differ from the TDMA used in GSM? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**W-CDMA (Wideband Code Division Multiple Access):**
- All users transmit **simultaneously on the same 5 MHz frequency band**.
- Each user is assigned a **unique pseudo-random spreading code** (of length $SF$ chips per data bit).
- The user's data is multiplied by this code, spreading the spectrum. Receiver uses the **same synchronized code** to despread the desired signal, while other users' signals remain spread (treated as noise).
- The chip rate is **3.84 Mcps**.

**Differences from GSM's TDMA:**

| Feature | GSM (TDMA/FDMA) | UMTS (W-CDMA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Time slots** | Users share 8 time slots per carrier | No time slots ÔÇö all users transmit simultaneously |
| **Frequency planning** | Strict frequency reuse pattern required | **Frequency reuse = 1** (universal frequency reuse) |
| **Handover** | Hard handover (break-before-make) | **Soft handover** (make-before-break) |
| **Data rate adaptation** | Fixed per slot | **Variable via Spreading Factor** |
| **Capacity** | Hard-limited by slots | **Soft capacity** ÔÇö interference-limited |
| **Interference handling** | Time separation | **Code separation + power control** |

W-CDMA's soft capacity means an operator can **slightly oversubscribe** the cell as long as interference stays manageable ÔÇö a fundamentally more flexible model.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define the **Spreading Factor (SF)** and derive the relationship between **chip rate, data rate, and SF**. A UMTS user is assigned $SF = 64$. Compute the resulting user data rate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The **Spreading Factor (SF)** is the number of chips used to represent one user data bit in a W-CDMA system. It is the ratio of the chip rate to the user data rate:

$$
SF = \frac{R_c}{R_b}
$$

**Derivation:** In W-CDMA, each user bit of duration $T_b$ is multiplied by a code sequence of $SF$ chips, each chip of duration $T_c$. So:

$$
T_b = SF \times T_c
$$

Taking reciprocals (rate = 1 / duration):

$$
\frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{1}{SF \times T_c} = \frac{1}{SF} \times \frac{1}{T_c}
$$

Identifying $R_b = 1/T_b$ and $R_c = 1/T_c$:

$$
R_b = \frac{R_c}{SF} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad R_c = SF \times R_b
$$

**Numerical Computation:** Given $SF = 64$ and $R_c = 3.84 \times 10^6$ chips/sec:

$$
R_b = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{64} = 60{,}000 \text{ bps} = 60 \text{ kbps}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_b = 60 \text{ kbps when } SF = 64}
$$

---

**(b)** Discuss the **four QoS traffic classes** standardized in UMTS. Why is QoS support a major improvement over GSM? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

UMTS standardized **four QoS traffic classes** in 3GPP Release 99:

**1. Conversational Class**
- Real-time, low-latency, symmetric traffic.
- Examples: **voice call (VoIP), video telephony**.
- Strict delay budget (~100 ms end-to-end).

**2. Streaming Class**
- Real-time, asymmetric, one-way traffic.
- Example: **video streaming, live audio broadcast**.
- Tolerates higher delay than conversational.

**3. Interactive Class**
- Request-response, low error rate required.
- Examples: **web browsing (HTTP), database queries, online gaming**.
- Preserves payload integrity through retransmission.

**4. Background Class**
- Best-effort, no delay guarantee.
- Examples: **email (SMTP), FTP downloads, file sync**.
- Lowest priority, sent when network is idle.

**Why QoS is a major improvement over GSM:**
GSM was designed purely for **circuit-switched voice** with a single fixed 9.6 kbps channel ÔÇö it had no concept of differentiating traffic types. UMTS QoS allows:
- **Simultaneous multimedia sessions** on one device (voice + video + web).
- **Operator monetization** via tiered service classes.
- **Efficient radio resource use** ÔÇö high-bandwidth video gets high data rate, low-bandwidth email uses minimal resources.
- **Guaranteed experience** for delay-sensitive apps (VoIP, gaming).

This is a direct consequence of UMTS being a **packet-switched + circuit-switched hybrid**, with bearer services defined per QoS class.

---

## 5.3 ÔÜá´ŞĆ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in UMTS questions ÔÇö and where you lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing chip rate with data rate.** Chip rate is fixed at **3.84 Mcps**; data rate varies with $SF$. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units.** Final answers must be in **kbps or Mbps** ÔÇö leaving "3.84 ├Ś 10ÔüÂ bps" costs partial marks.
> 3. **Not labeling interfaces.** Students often draw UTRAN and CN boxes but forget to label **Uu, Iub, Iu-CS, Iu-PS** ÔÇö these are **must-mention** for full marks.
> 4. **Mixing GSM and UMTS terminology.** Saying "BTS" instead of "Node B," or "SIM" instead of "USIM," is a clear sign of incomplete reading and loses 0.5ÔÇô1 mark per occurrence.
> 5. **In QoS questions, omitting real-world examples** (VoIP, HTTP, SMTP). Examiners award marks for application-level insight, not just definitions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ôťů **UMTS = 3G** system standardized by **3GPP**, using **W-CDMA** air interface.
- Ôťů **Chip rate = 3.84 Mcps**, **Bandwidth = 5 MHz**, **Frame = 10 ms (15 slots)**.
- Ôťů **Three domains**: UE, UTRAN (Node B + RNC), CN (MSC, SGSN, GGSN, HLR/AuC).
- Ôťů **Key interfaces**: Uu (air), Iub (Node BÔÇôRNC), Iu-CS, Iu-PS, Gn.
- Ôťů **Core equation**: $R_b = R_c / SF$ Ôćĺ $SF = R_c / R_b$.
- Ôťů **Processing Gain** in dB: $PG = 10 \log_{10}(SF)$.
- Ôťů **SF range**: 4 to 256 (uplink), 4 to 512 (downlink).
- Ôťů **Duplex modes**: **FDD** (paired spectrum) and **TDD** (unpaired).
- Ôťů **Four QoS classes**: Conversational, Streaming, Interactive, Background.
- Ôťů **Handover**: **Soft handover** (simultaneous multi-Node B) ÔÇö major GSM improvement.
- Ôťů **Frequency reuse = 1** (universal reuse) ÔÇö no tight frequency planning needed.
- Ôťů **USIM** replaces SIM with stronger cryptographic algorithms (MILENAGE, Kasumi).
- Ôťů **Soft capacity** ÔÇö interference-limited, not slot-limited like GSM.
- Ôťů UMTS supports data rates: **144 kbps (vehicular)**, **384 kbps (pedestrian)**, **2 Mbps (indoor)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Air Interface

The **Air Interface** (also called the **Radio Interface** or **Um/Uu Interface**) is the **physical and logical boundary** that defines how wireless signals are transmitted and received over the air between a **Mobile Station (MS) / User Equipment (UE)** and the **Base Transceiver Station (BTS) / Node B / gNodeB** in a cellular network. It encompasses the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)**, **Data Link Layer (Layer 2 ÔÇö MAC sublayer)**, and parts of **Layer 3** of the OSI model as it applies to the wireless link.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Air Interface specifies:
- **Frequency bands** of operation (e.g., 700 MHz, 2.1 GHz, 3.5 GHz, 26 GHz mmWave)
- **Multiple Access Schemes** (FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, OFDMA)
- **Modulation and Coding Schemes (MCS)**
- **Channel structures** (logical, transport, physical)
- **Security primitives** such as ciphering, integrity protection, and authentication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Air Interface is the *most vulnerable* attack surface in any wireless network because the medium is **shared, broadcast, and open**. All wireless security mechanisms (A3/A8 in GSM, MILENAGE in 4G/5G, NEA/NIA in 5G NR) operate *on top of* the air interface.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ÔÇö The "Radio Highway" Intuition

Imagine the **electromagnetic spectrum** as a vast, invisible **highway in the sky**. Thousands of cars (mobile users) want to travel from point A (their device) to point B (the base station tower). However, unlike a real highway, no one owns a lane and there are no road signs visible to intruders.

- The **Air Interface** is the **set of traffic rules** that decides *which car goes in which lane, at what speed, with what kind of engine, and how passengers are protected from highway robbers.*
- **Multiple Access Techniques** are the lane discipline (one car per lane, or time-sliced shared lanes).
- **Security mechanisms at the Air Interface** are like **armored vehicles, encrypted cargo, and a bouncer at the toll booth** ÔÇö they ensure that even though the highway is public, the contents and identities remain private.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** "Air Interface" in 2G is called the **Um interface**; in 3G it is the **Uu interface**; in 4G LTE it remains **Uu**; in 5G NR it is also referred to as **NR Uu**. These names are essentially synonymous for the KTU examination context.

## 1.3 Key Entities of the Air Interface

| Entity | Full Form | Role |
|---|---|---|
| MS / UE | Mobile Station / User Equipment | End user device |
| BTS / NodeB / gNB | Base Transceiver Station | Base station antenna site |
| BSC / RNC | Base Station / Radio Network Controller | 2G/3G controller |
| EPC / 5GC | Evolved Packet Core / 5G Core | Backhaul core network |
| SIM / USIM | Subscriber Identity Module | Stores subscriber credentials |
| AuC / HSS / UDM | Authentication Center | Stores secret keys (Ki, K) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants / Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Speed of light: **$c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s**
> - Free-space path loss exponent (urban): **n = 3 to 4**
> - GSM frame period: **4.615 ms**
> - LTE subframe: **1 ms**; LTE radio frame: **10 ms**
> - 5G NR subframe: **1 ms**; slot: variable (14 OFDM symbols)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Layered Architecture of the Air Interface

The Air Interface is organized into three protocol planes:

1. **User Plane (U-Plane):** Carries actual user data (voice, video, IP packets).
2. **Control Plane (C-Plane):** Carries signaling ÔÇö paging, authentication, radio resource control (RRC).
3. **Security Plane (cross-cutting):** Provides confidentiality (ciphering) and integrity protection.

### 2.1.1 Protocol Stack Summary

| Layer | 2G (GSM) | 3G (UMTS) | 4G (LTE) | 5G (NR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Physical (L1) | TDMA/FDMA | WCDMA | OFDMA/SC-FDMA | OFDMA/DFT-s-OFDM |
| MAC (L2) | TDMA frames | WCDMA slots | Scheduled grants | Flexible slot/symbol |
| RLC (L2) | LAPDm | RLC | RLC (AM/UM/TM) | RLC (AM/UM/TM) |
| RRC (L3) | GSM-RRC | RRC | RRC | RRC |
| Security | A3/A5/A8 | MILENAGE / KASUMI | EPS-AKA / SNOW 3G / ZUC | 5G-AKA / EAP-AKA' / NEA1-3 / NIA1-3 |

## 2.2 Multiple Access Techniques ÔÇö The Heart of the Air Interface

The Air Interface must allow **many simultaneous users** to share a finite spectrum. The four fundamental multiple access paradigms are:

### 2.2.1 FDMA ÔÇö Frequency Division Multiple Access
- Each user gets a **unique frequency band** (e.g., 200 kHz in GSM).
- **Analogy:** Each caller is given a different radio station frequency.
- **Pros:** Simple. **Cons:** Inflexible, requires guard bands.

### 2.2.2 TDMA ÔÇö Time Division Multiple Access
- Users share the **same frequency** but transmit in **different time slots**.
- GSM uses a hybrid **FDMA + TDMA** (8 time slots per 200 kHz carrier).
- **Analogy:** Eight people sharing one telephone but allowed to speak only in their assigned 1/8th of a second window.

### 2.2.3 CDMA ÔÇö Code Division Multiple Access
- All users transmit simultaneously on the **same frequency** but are separated by **orthogonal spreading codes** (e.g., Walsh-Hadamard codes).
- Used in **IS-95** and **UMTS (WCDMA)**.
- **Analogy:** Many people speaking different languages in the same room ÔÇö a listener who knows only one language hears only that conversation.

### 2.2.4 OFDMA ÔÇö Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access
- The available bandwidth is divided into **many narrow orthogonal subcarriers** (15 kHz in LTE, variable in 5G NR).
- Subcarriers are grouped into **Resource Blocks (RBs)** and assigned dynamically.
- Used in **LTE downlink** and **5G NR** (downlink and uplink).
- **Analogy:** A piano with 1200 keys; each note is a subcarrier, and the system hands a chord to each user.

### 2.2.5 SDMA / MIMO / Beamforming
- **Spatial Division Multiple Access** uses directional antennas or multiple antennas to separate users in **space**.
- **MIMO (Multiple Input Multiple Output)** uses multiple antennas at both transmitter and receiver for spatial multiplexing and diversity.

## 2.3 Air Interface Security ÔÇö The KTU High-Yield Perspective

Since this is a *Security in Wireless Networks* course, the **security mechanisms layered over the Air Interface** are paramount:

| Generation | Ciphering Algorithm | Integrity Algorithm | Key Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2G (GSM) | A5/1, A5/3 (KASUMI) | None | 64-bit |
| 3G (UMTS) | KASUMI (F8) | KASUMI (F9) | 128-bit |
| 4G (LTE) | SNOW 3G / AES / ZUC | SNOW 3G / AES / ZUC | 128-bit |
| 5G (NR) | NEA1 (SNOW 3G), NEA2 (AES), NEA3 (ZUC) | NIA1, NIA2, NIA3 | 128-bit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Security Highlight:** 2G GSM had **no integrity protection** on the air interface ÔÇö this is the root cause of well-known attacks like **IMSI catching** and **false base station (IMSI-catcher / Stingray)** attacks. 4G and 5G introduced **mutual authentication** and **integrity-protected signaling** to close these gaps.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU University Exams for Wireless Network Security courses.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_c = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Carrier frequency from wavelength |
| 2 | $L_{fs}(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | Free-space path loss |
| 3 | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon channel capacity (bps) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{(D/R)^n}{i_0}$ | Carrier-to-Interference ratio (n = path loss exponent) |
| 5 | $N = i_0^{2/n}$ | Co-channel reuse ratio / cluster size |
| 6 | $SIR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\dfrac{(\sqrt{3N})^n}{6}\right]$ | Signal-to-Interference in dB |
| 7 | $BW_{coh} \approx \dfrac{c}{2v}$ | Coherence bandwidth (approximate, for fast fading) |
| 8 | $T_{slot}^{GSM} = \dfrac{4.615\,ms}{8} = 0.577\,ms$ | GSM time slot duration |
| 9 | $N_{subcarriers}^{LTE} = \dfrac{BW_{MHz}}{0.18}$ (approx) | Number of LTE subcarriers |
| 10 | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{\sum \log_2(1 + SINR_k)}{BW}$ | Spectral efficiency (bps/Hz) |

**Key boundary conditions / KTU memory hooks:**
- Reuse factor **N = 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13...** (N must satisfy $N = a^2 + ab + b^2$ where $a, b$ are non-negative integers).
- GSM uplink band: **890ÔÇô915 MHz**; downlink: **935ÔÇô960 MHz**.
- LTE max bandwidth: **20 MHz**; 5G NR FR1 max: **100 MHz** (sub-6 GHz); FR2: **400 MHz** (mmWave).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cluster Size and Co-Channel Interference

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation question**.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a cellular system with:
- Hexagonal cells of radius $R$
- Co-channel cells at distance $D$ from the center of the serving cell
- Path loss exponent $n$ (typically 3.5 in urban environment)
- First tier of co-channel interferers: **6 cells** (the six nearest co-channel neighbors)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ÔÇö Define Co-channel Reuse Ratio $q$.**

The co-channel reuse ratio is defined as the ratio of the distance between co-channel cell centers to the cell radius:

$$q = \dfrac{D}{R}$$

For a hexagonal cellular layout, the relationship between $q$ and the cluster size $N$ is given by:

$$q = \sqrt{3N}$$

**Derivation of the hexagonal relationship:**

In a hexagonal grid, if you shift by $i$ cells along one hexagonal axis and $j$ cells along the other, the geometric distance is:

$$D = R \cdot \sqrt{3(i^2 + ij + j^2)}$$

Since cluster size $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$, we get $D = R\sqrt{3N}$, hence $q = \sqrt{3N}$.

**Step 2 ÔÇö Express received signal power using the path loss model.**

Assume a power law path loss model:

$$P_r = P_t \cdot K \cdot d^{-n}$$

where $K$ is a constant and $n$ is the path loss exponent.

**Step 3 ÔÇö Express the Carrier-to-Interference Ratio (CIR or $C/I$).**

For a mobile at distance $d_0$ from its serving base station and at distance $D_k$ from the $k$-th co-channel base station:

$$\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{P_t \cdot K \cdot d_0^{-n}}{\sum_{k=1}^{6} P_t \cdot K \cdot D_k^{-n}} = \dfrac{d_0^{-n}}{\sum_{k=1}^{6} D_k^{-n}}$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Apply the worst-case assumption.**

To find the *minimum* acceptable $C/I$, assume the mobile is at the cell edge, $d_0 = R$, and all six interferers are at approximately the same distance $D$. Then:

$$\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{R^{-n}}{6 \cdot D^{-n}} = \dfrac{1}{6} \left(\dfrac{D}{R}\right)^n = \dfrac{q^n}{6}$$

Substituting $q = \sqrt{3N}$:

$$\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{3N})^n}{6} = \dfrac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6}$$

**Step 5 ÔÇö Convert to dB form (final KTU answer form):**

$$\left(\dfrac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\dfrac{(\sqrt{3N})^n}{6}\right]$$

**Step 6 ÔÇö Numerical sanity check.**

For $N = 7$ and $n = 3.5$:

$$\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{21})^{3.5}}{6} = \dfrac{21^{1.75}}{6} = \dfrac{145.5}{6} \approx 24.25$$

In dB: $10 \log_{10}(24.25) \approx 13.85$ dB. This is the **minimum tolerable $C/I$** in many analog systems (e.g., AMPS required Ôëą 18 dB, GSM requires Ôëą 9 dB, LTE/5G require Ôëą 0ÔÇô5 dB with advanced receivers).

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Capacity at the Air Interface

**Step 1 ÔÇö Start with Shannon's Hartley theorem:**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bps]}$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Express SNR in dB if required:**

If $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$, then $S/N = 10^{SNR_{dB}/10}$.

**Step 3 ÔÇö For LTE carrier of 20 MHz with SNR = 20 dB (good urban):**

$$S/N = 10^{20/10} = 100$$

$$C = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(101) = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot 6.658 \approx 133.2 \text{ Mbps}$$

This is the *raw theoretical* maximum; in practice, LTE delivers ~75ÔÇô100 Mbps for a 20 MHz carrier (4├Ś4 MIMO).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ÔÇö Mapping Attack Ôćĺ Air Interface Vulnerability

| Attack | Air Interface Vulnerability | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| Eavesdropping | Broadcast radio medium | Ciphering (NEA2/AES) |
| IMSI Catching | 2G IMSI transmitted in clear | 5G SUPI/SUCI + SUPIR masking |
| Jamming | Unlicensed/shared bands | Frequency hopping, DSSS |
| Man-in-the-Middle (False BS) | Lack of mutual auth in 2G | 4G/5G mutual authentication |
| Replay Attack | Captured signaling | Fresh sequence numbers + integrity |
| Denial of Service | Uncontrolled access | RACH barring, access class barring |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Air Interface Protocol Stack (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CoreNet["CORE NETWORK / 5GC / EPC"]
        nodeCore[Subscriber Database and Authentication Server]
    end

    subgraph BaseStation["BASE STATION gNB / NodeB / BTS"]
        nodeRRC[RRC Layer - Radio Resource Control]
        nodePDCP[PDCP Layer - Ciphering and Integrity]
        nodeRLC[RLC Layer - Segmentation and ARQ]
        nodeMAC[MAC Layer - Scheduling and HARQ]
        nodePHY[PHY Layer - Modulation and OFDMA]
    end

    subgraph AirInt["AIR INTERFACE Uu / Um - RADIO LINK"]
        nodeRF[Radio Waves - Electromagnetic Spectrum]
    end

    subgraph UE["USER EQUIPMENT - Mobile Phone"]
        nodeUERRC[UE RRC]
        nodeUEPDCP[UE PDCP]
        nodeUERLC[UE RLC]
        nodeUEMAC[UE MAC]
        nodeUEPHY[UE PHY]
    end

    nodeCore <--> nodeRRC
    nodeRRC --> nodePDCP
    nodePDCP --> nodeRLC
    nodeRLC --> nodeMAC
    nodeMAC --> nodePHY
    nodePHY <--> nodeRF
    nodeRF <--> nodeUEPHY
    nodeUEPHY --> nodeUEMAC
    nodeUEMAC --> nodeUERLC
    nodeUERLC --> nodeUEPDCP
    nodeUEPDCP --> nodeUERRC
    nodeUERRC <--> nodeApp[User Application and SIM/USIM]
```

## 4.2 Multiple Access Techniques Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart[Spectrum Sharing Problem] --> nodeFDMA[FDMA - Split by Frequency]
    nodeStart --> nodeTDMA[TDMA - Split by Time]
    nodeStart --> nodeCDMA[CDMA - Split by Code]
    nodeStart --> nodeOFDMA[OFDMA - Split by Subcarrier]
    nodeStart --> nodeSDMA[SDMA - Split by Space]

    nodeFDMA --> nodeGen2G[Used in 1G AMPS and 2G GSM uplink]
    nodeTDMA --> nodeGen2GTDMA[Used in 2G GSM and DECT]
    nodeCDMA --> nodeGen3G[Used in 3G UMTS WCDMA and CDMA2000]
    nodeOFDMA --> nodeGen4G5G[Used in 4G LTE and 5G NR]
    nodeSDMA --> nodeGen5GAdv[Used in 5G NR Massive MIMO and Beamforming]
```

## 4.3 Air Interface Security Attack Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeAttacker[Adversary] --> nodePassive[Passive Attacks]
    nodeAttacker --> nodeActive[Active Attacks]

    nodePassive --> nodeEaves[Eavesdropping on Ciphered Traffic]
    nodePassive --> nodeTraffic[Traffic Analysis]
    nodePassive --> nodeLoc[Location Tracking via IMSI or SUPI]

    nodeActive --> nodeJam[Jamming the Air Interface]
    nodeActive --> nodeMitM[False Base Station MITM]
    nodeActive --> nodeReplay[Replay of Authenticated Signaling]
    nodeActive --> nodeDoS[DoS via RACH Flooding]

    nodeEaves --> nodeDef1[Defense - Strong Ciphering NEA2 or NEA3]
    nodeTraffic --> nodeDef2[Defense - Padding and Tunneling]
    nodeLoc --> nodeDef3[Defense - SUCI Subscription Concealed Identifier]
    nodeJam --> nodeDef4[Defense - Frequency Hopping and DSSS]
    nodeMitM --> nodeDef5[Defense - Mutual Authentication 5G AKA]
    nodeReplay --> nodeDef6[Defense - Sequence Numbers and Integrity NIA]
    nodeDoS --> nodeDef7[Defense - Access Class Barring and RACH Throttling]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram of an Air Interface Transceiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeData[User Data Bits] --> nodeSourceEnc[Source Encoder - Voice or Video]
    nodeSourceEnc --> nodeChannelEnc[Channel Encoder - Turbo or LDPC]
    nodeChannelEnc --> nodeInterleaver[Interleaver - Burst Error Protection]
    nodeInterleaver --> nodeCipher[Ciphering Block - NEA2 AES 128]
    nodeCipher --> nodeMod[Modulator - QPSK or 16 QAM or 64 QAM]
    nodeMod --> nodeOFDM[OFDM Modulator - IFFT and Cyclic Prefix]
    nodeOFDM --> nodeDAC[DAC and RF Front End]
    nodeDAC --> nodeAntennaTx[Tx Antenna - Radiates Electromagnetic Wave]
    nodeAntennaTx --> nodeChannel[Wireless Channel - Multipath and Fading]
    nodeChannel --> nodeAntennaRx[Rx Antenna]
    nodeAntennaRx --> nodeADC[ADC and RF Front End]
    nodeADC --> nodeOFDMRx[OFDM Demodulator - FFT]
    nodeOFDMRx --> nodeDemod[Demodulator - Soft Decisions]
    nodeDemod --> nodeDecipher[Deciphering Block - NEA2 AES 128]
    nodeDecipher --> nodeDeinter[Deinterleaver]
    nodeDeinter --> nodeChannelDec[Channel Decoder - LDPC or Polar]
    nodeChannelDec --> nodeSourceDec[Source Decoder]
    nodeSourceDec --> nodeDataOut[Recovered User Data]
```
