# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ÔÇö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define the term "Air Interface" in a wireless network. List any three security vulnerabilities specific to the air interface.
**[KTU University Exam ÔÇô June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Air Interface** is the radio communication link between the Mobile Station (MS/UE) and the Base Transceiver Station (BTS/gNB) that defines the physical transmission parameters (frequency, modulation, access scheme) and protocol layers (PHY, MAC, RLC, RRC).

**Three security vulnerabilities:**
1. **Eavesdropping** ÔÇö radio waves can be passively intercepted by any receiver within range using a software-defined radio.
2. **Jamming** ÔÇö an attacker can transmit high-power noise on the operating band to disrupt communication.
3. **Impersonation / False Base Station** ÔÇö lack of mutual authentication in 2G allows an adversary to set up a rogue BTS and lure the MS (e.g., Stingray / IMSI catcher).

### Question 2. Compare FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, and OFDMA in terms of the dimension of separation and typical wireless standard where each is used.
**[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Technique | Separation Dimension | Typical Standard |
|---|---|---|
| FDMA | Frequency | 1G AMPS |
| TDMA | Time | 2G GSM (combined with FDMA) |
| CDMA | Code (spreading) | 3G UMTS, IS-95 |
| OFDMA | Orthogonal subcarriers | 4G LTE, 5G NR |

---

## 5.2 Part B ÔÇö 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **protocol stack of the LTE Air Interface** and clearly state the role of the **PDCP layer** in providing security services. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Carrier-to-Interference ratio** for a hexagonal cellular system with cluster size $N$ and path loss exponent $n$. Hence compute the minimum $C/I$ (in dB) for $N=7$, $n=4$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) LTE Air Interface Protocol Stack ÔÇö Explanation (7 marks)**

The LTE Air Interface (Uu) is organized into Layer 1 (Physical), Layer 2 (MAC, RLC, PDCP), and Layer 3 (RRC). The block diagram in Section 4.1 (above) is the answer.

The **PDCP (Packet Data Convergence Protocol)** layer is responsible for:
1. **Header compression** (ROHC) ÔÇö reduces IP overhead.
2. **Ciphering** ÔÇö applies **EPS encryption algorithms** (EEA1 = SNOW 3G, EEA2 = AES-128-CTR, EEA3 = ZUC) using the key $K_{UP}$ for user plane.
3. **Integrity protection** ÔÇö applies **EIA1 / EIA2 / EIA3** algorithms using key $K_{RRC}$ for control plane.
4. **In-sequence delivery** and **duplicate removal** during handover.

**(b) $C/I$ Derivation (7 marks)**

From the derivation in Section 3.1:

$$\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{3N})^n}{6}$$

Substituting $N = 7$ and $n = 4$:

$$\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{21})^{4}}{6} = \dfrac{441}{6} = 73.5$$

In decibels:

$$\left(\dfrac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) \approx 18.66 \text{ dB}$$

**Answer:** Minimum $C/I \approx 18.66$ dB for $N=7$, $n=4$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **multiple access techniques** used in 2G, 3G, 4G, and 5G air interfaces with suitable diagrams. Discuss why **OFDMA** was preferred over CDMA in 4G. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **security architecture of the 5G NR Air Interface**, including SUPI/SUCI concealment, NEA/NIA algorithms, and the 5G-AKA authentication flow. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Evolution of Multiple Access (7 marks)**

- **2G GSM:** Hybrid **FDMA + TDMA** ÔÇö 200 kHz carriers each carrying 8 time slots; uses Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying (GMSK) modulation.
- **3G UMTS:** **WCDMA** ÔÇö 5 MHz carrier bandwidth, users separated by orthogonal spreading codes (chip rate 3.84 Mcps).
- **4G LTE:** **OFDMA** in downlink and **SC-FDMA** in uplink ÔÇö 180 kHz Resource Blocks consisting of 12 subcarriers of 15 kHz each.
- **5G NR:** **Flexible OFDMA** with numerologies ($\mu = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$); subcarrier spacing scales as $15 \times 2^\mu$ kHz; slot duration scales as $1/2^\mu$ ms.

**Why OFDMA over CDMA in 4G:**
1. **Robustness against multipath fading** through cyclic prefix.
2. **Higher spectral efficiency** in frequency-selective channels.
3. **Easier MIMO integration** (each subcarrier can be processed independently).
4. **Flexible bandwidth allocation** via dynamic RB scheduling.
5. **Eliminates the near-far problem** inherent in CDMA.

**(b) 5G NR Air Interface Security (7 marks)**

1. **SUPI / SUCI Concealment:** The permanent subscriber identifier **SUPI** (analogous to IMSI) is concealed as **SUCI** using **ECIES (Elliptic Curve Integrated Encryption Scheme)** with the home network's public key, preventing IMSI catching attacks.
2. **Ciphering Algorithms (NEA):** NEA1 (SNOW 3G), NEA2 (AES-128-CTR), NEA3 (ZUC) ÔÇö applied at the PDCP layer with 128-bit keys.
3. **Integrity Algorithms (NIA):** NIA1, NIA2, NIA3 ÔÇö applied to RRC signaling and user plane (optional).
4. **5G-AKA Authentication:** Mutual authentication using **HE AVs (Home Environment Authentication Vectors)** derived from the shared secret key **K** stored in USIM and UDM/ARPF. The UE computes RES* and the network verifies HRES* / XRES*.
5. **Additional protection:** Serving network name hashing, anti-bidding-down protection between EPS and 5GS.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "air interface" with "core network interface."** The air interface is only between UE and the RAN; the S1/N2/N1 interfaces are core interfaces.
> 2. **Do NOT write "GSM uses CDMA"** ÔÇö it is **FDMA + TDMA**. This is a very common mistake costing 1ÔÇô2 marks.
> 3. **In the cluster size derivation, students often forget** to include the factor of 6 for the first-tier interferers. Always state "we assume 6 first-tier co-channel cells" explicitly.
> 4. **In 5G security questions, do not omit SUCI** ÔÇö it is the single most important 5G security upgrade over 4G and examiners expect it.
> 5. **Always convert the final $C/I$ answer to dB** in derivations; the examiner's marking scheme usually awards 1 mark for the dB conversion.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ÔÇö Air Interface (Module 1)**

- Ôťů **Air Interface** = radio link between UE/MS and base station; names: **Um (2G)**, **Uu (3G/4G/5G)**.
- Ôťů **Four multiple access dimensions:** Frequency (FDMA), Time (TDMA), Code (CDMA), Subcarrier (OFDMA); plus **Space (SDMA/MIMO)**.
- Ôťů **GSM** = FDMA + TDMA + GMSK; **UMTS** = WCDMA; **LTE** = OFDMA (DL) + SC-FDMA (UL); **5G NR** = flexible OFDMA with multiple numerologies.
- Ôťů **Key path loss formula:** $L_{fs}(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$.
- Ôťů **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- Ôťů **Co-channel $C/I$:** $C/I = (\sqrt{3N})^n / 6$ in linear; convert to dB using $10\log_{10}$.
- Ôťů **Cluster size N** must satisfy $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ for integers $i, j$.
- Ôťů **2G weaknesses:** No mutual authentication, no integrity protection, IMSI in clear Ôćĺ enables **IMSI catchers**.
- Ôťů **5G security upgrades over 4G:** SUCI concealment, mandatory integrity on user plane (optional), 5G-AKA mutual auth, anti-bidding-down protection.
- Ôťů **Standard cipher suites:** NEA1 (SNOW 3G), NEA2 (AES-128), NEA3 (ZUC); integrity: NIA1, NIA2, NIA3.
- Ôťů **GSM frame = 4.615 ms** split into 8 slots of 0.577 ms each.
- Ôťů **LTE resource block = 180 kHz** (12 subcarriers ├Ś 15 kHz); 5G NR numerology $\mu$ controls subcarrier spacing.
- Ôťů **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is used in every path loss calculation.
- Ôťů **Remember to draw the protocol stack diagram** for any 7-mark question on air interface architecture.
- Ôťů **Mandatory end with dB conversion** in any $C/I$ derivation problem.
- Ôťů **KTU favorite 14-mark question structure:** "Explain with block diagram + derive $C/I$" or "Compare 2G/3G/4G/5G air interface security."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3GPP Network Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** 3GPP Network Architecture # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **3GPP (3rd Generation Partnership Project)** is a global, collaborative telecommunications standards body formed in **1998** that develops and maintains protocols for mobile networks. It standardizes the end-to-end system architecture ÔÇö covering the **User Equipment (UE)**, the **Radio Access Network (RAN)**, and the **Core Network (CN)** ÔÇö for generations from **2G (GSM)** through **5G (NR)** and beyond. The 3GPP network architecture is formally defined as a layered, logical separation of: - **Access Stratum (AS)** ÔÇö handles radio-specific functions between UE and RAN node. - **Non-Access Stratum (NAS)** ÔÇö handles signaling between UE and Core Network (mobility, session, security management). - **IP Multimedia Subsystem (IMS)** ÔÇö provides voice/multimedia services over IP. > [!NOTE] > For KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT862), the focus is on how the 3GPP architecture is **secured** ÔÇö i.e., the **Security Architecture** comprising **Authentication**, **Ciphering**, and **Integrity Protection** mechanisms across the **UE Ôćö gNB/eNB Ôćö Core** chain. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!TIP] > **Analogy: A Highly Secured International Airport** > > - **User Equipment (UE)** = A passenger carrying a **passport (USIM card)** that contains secret identity keys. > - **Radio Access Network (gNB / eNB)** = The **security checkpoint** that scans your boarding pass, verifies your identity, and creates a temporary secure lane. > - **Core Network (5GC / EPC)** = The **airport authority's central system** that decides which flight you can board, allocates the secure tunnel (bearer/PDU session), and monitors every move. > - **Authentication & Key Agreement (AKA)** = The **border control officer** checking your passport against the issuing country's database. > > Just like no passenger enters a plane without a multi-stage verification, **no UE communicates with the network without passing 3GPP's layered security checks**. ## 1.3 Core Building Blocks (At a Glance) | Logical Entity | Full Form | Generation | Primary Role | |---|---|---|---| | **UE** | User Equipment | All | Mobile device + USIM (Universal Subscriber Identity Module) | | **gNB / ng-eNB** | Next-Generation Node B | 5G NR / 5G EPC-interworking | Radio access node | | **eNB** | Evolved Node B | 4G LTE | Radio access node | | **AMF** | Access and Mobility Management Function | 5G | NAS signaling, mobility, access control | | **SMF** | Session Management Function | 5G | PDU session, IP allocation, policy | | **UPF** | User Plane Function | 5G | Packet routing/forwarding, QoS | | **AUSF** | Authentication Server Function | 5G | Authenticates the UE | | **UDM / UDR** | Unified Data Management / Repository | 5G | Subscriber credentials, K key storage | | **MME** | Mobility Management Entity | 4G | NAS signaling + mobility + bearer control | | **HSS** | Home Subscriber Server | 4G/5G | Master subscriber database | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Evolution of 3GPP Network Architecture (Generation Map) > [!NOTE] > Every generation **inherits and strengthens** the security posture of its predecessor. 5G introduces **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** with HTTP/2-based service interfaces ÔÇö a fundamental shift from 4G's point-to-point EPC. ### 2.1.1 2G ÔÇö GSM (GPRS Edge) - Circuit-switched voice; GPRS added packet data. - **Security flaw:** only **one-way authentication** (network authenticates user, not vice versa) Ôćĺ vulnerable to **IMSI catching / false BTS**. ### 2.1.2 3G ÔÇö UMTS - Introduced **mutual authentication (AKA)** via **Milenage / f1ÔÇôf5** algorithm sets in the **USIM**. - Separated **CS domain** and **PS domain** in the core (MSC/VLR, SGSN, GGSN). ### 2.1.3 4G ÔÇö LTE / EPC - **All-IP flat architecture**: - **Control plane:** MME, HSS, PCF (was PCRF), HLR - **User plane:** SGW, PGW - **EPC = Evolved Packet Core** - Reference point architecture (S1, S5, S6a, S11, SGi, ÔÇŽ) ### 2.1.4 5G ÔÇö NR / 5GC - **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** ÔÇö all control-plane network functions expose services over **HTTP/2 + JSON (OpenAPI 3.0.3)**. - **Network Slicing**, **SUPI/SUCI privacy**, **SEAF (Security Anchor Function)**, **AUSF/UDM/ARPF** redesign. - Reference point **N1 (UEÔćöAMF)**, **N2 (gNBÔćöAMF)**, **N3 (gNBÔćöUPF)**, **N4 (SMFÔćöUPF)**, **N5 (PCFÔćöAF)**, etc. ## 2.2 Reference Points and Interfaces (High-Yield for KTU) | Interface | Logical Endpoints | Plane | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **Uu** | UE Ôćö eNB / gNB | Radio | Air-interface signaling + traffic | | **S1-U** | eNB Ôćö SGW | User | Per-bearer tunneling (GTP-U) | | **S1-MME** | eNB Ôćö MME | Control | S1AP signaling | | **N1** | UE Ôćö AMF | Control (NAS) | NAS messaging (5G) | | **N2** | gNB Ôćö AMF | Control (NGAP) | NG Application Protocol | | **N3** | gNB Ôćö UPF | User | PDU session tunnel (5G) | | **X2** | eNB Ôćö eNB | Control + User | Handover signaling/data | | **Xn** | gNB Ôćö gNB | Control + User | 5G handover | ## 2.3 The 3GPP Security Architecture (5 Pillars) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 syllabus strongly emphasizes the **5 security feature domains** defined in **TS 33.501** and **TS 33.102**: > 1. **Network Access Security (I)** ÔÇö secures access via 3GPP credentials. > 2. **Network Domain Security (II)** ÔÇö protects signaling between core nodes (NDS/IP, TLS, SEG). > 3. **User Domain Security (III)** ÔÇö secures access to mobile equipment/USIM. > 4. **Application Domain Security (IV)** ÔÇö end-to-end app-level security. > 5. **Visibility & Configurability of Security (V)** ÔÇö informs user about security. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Security Keys & Vectors) | Symbol | Meaning | Derivation / Source | Length (bits) | |---|---|---|---| | $K$ | Long-term secret in USIM & HSS/UDM | Pre-shared at provisioning | 128 | | $OP$ / $OP_c$ | Operator variant algorithm constant | XOR with K for key diversification | 128 | | $RAND$ | Random challenge | Generated by HSS/AUSF | 128 | | $XRES$ | Expected response from UE | f2(K, RAND) ÔÇö Milenage | 64ÔÇô128 | | $CK$ | Cipher Key | f3(K, RAND) | 128 | | $IK$ | Integrity Key | f4(K, RAND) | 128 | | $AK$ | Anonymity Key | f5(K, RAND) | 48 | | $AUTN$ | Authentication Token | SQN ÔŐĽ AK ÔÇľ AMF ÔÇľ MAC | 128 | | $K_{ASME}$ | Key in 4G serving network | KDF(CK, IK, SN id, ÔÇŽ) | 256 | | $K_{SEAF}$ | Anchor key in 5G | KDF(K, SN name, ÔÇŽ) | 256 | | $K_{AUSF}$ | AUSF-specific key | KDF(K, ÔÇŽ) | 256 | | $K_{NASint}$ / $K_{NASenc}$ | NAS integrity/ciphering | KDF(K_ASME / K_SEAF, ÔÇŽ) | 128 / 128 | | $K_{RRCint}$ / $K_{RRCenc}$ | RRC signaling keys | KDF(K_eNB / K_gNB, ÔÇŽ) | 128 / 128 | | $K_{UPenc}$ / $K_{UPint}$ | User-plane ciphering/integrity | KDF(K_eNB / K_gNB, ÔÇŽ) | 128 / 128 | | $SUPI$ | Subscription Permanent Identifier (IMSI in 4G) | Stored in USIM | 15 digits | | $SUCI$ | Subscription Concealed Identifier | ECIES(public key, SUPI) | Variable | > [!NOTE] > For 4G: $K_{ASME}$ is the **root serving-network key**. > For 5G: $K_{SEAF}$ is the **anchor key** delivered to the AMF (via $SEAF$ inside the AMF). ## 2.5 The Authentication Vector (AV) Sets > [!IMPORTANT] > A **4G Authentication Vector** is a 5-tuple: $\langle RAND, AUTN, XRES, K_{ASME} \rangle$ > A **5G Authentication Vector (AV)** generated by UDM/ARPF: $\langle RAND, AUTN, HXRES^*, K_{SEAF} \rangle$ (resynchronization uses $HRES^*$ and $HXRES^*$ hashes for faster EAP-AKA'). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Telecom Operators:** use 3GPP architecture to deploy nationwide LTE/5G networks. - **Security Auditors:** assess the AKA handshake using formal verification (Tamarin, ProVerif). - **IoT & Cellular-V2X:** use lightweight 3GPP variants (EC-GSM-IoT, NB-IoT, LTE-M). - **Penetration Testers:** evaluate **IMSI-catcher / rogue gNB** attacks against $SUPI \to SUCI$ concealment. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Although 3GPP architecture is primarily **architectural**, the **cryptographic key-derivation process** is mathematically rigorous. Below is the **complete key hierarchy derivation** for both **4G (LTE/EPC)** and **5G (NR/5GC)** ÔÇö the most exam-relevant analytical topic. ## 3.1 The Milenage Algorithm Set (f1ÔÇôf5) ÔÇö Foundation > [!IMPORTANT] > The Milenage algorithm is the **standardized algorithm set** (3GPP TS 35.205/206) used in UMTS/LTE AKA. It is built on **AES-128 (Rijndael)** as the kernel and provides **five mutually-derived functions f1, f2, f3, f4, f5** that share computation overhead. Given inputs: - $K$ (128-bit subscriber secret) - $RAND$ (128-bit random challenge) - $OP_c$ (operator variant constant, $OP_c = OP \oplus E[OP]_K$) **Step 1 ÔÇö Internal intermediate rotation constants $c_1, \dots, c_5$:** $$ c_1 = \texttt{0x00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000} $$ $$ c_2 = \texttt{0x00000000 00000000 00000000 00000001} $$ $$ c_3 = \texttt{0x00000000 00000000 00000000 00000002} $$ $$ c_4 = \texttt{0x00000000 00000000 00000000 00000004} $$ $$ c_5 = \texttt{0x00000000 00000000 00000000 00000008} $$ **Step 2 ÔÇö Compute AES round intermediate $T_i$ for each function:** $$ T_i = E_K[RAND \oplus c_i] \qquad i \in \{1,2,3,4,5\} $$ where $E_K[\cdot]$ is AES-128 encryption with the **128-bit key $K$**. **Step 3 ÔÇö Derive the five outputs:** $$ XRES = T_2[1..n_{2}] \quad \text{(64-bit expected response in 4G, 128-bit in some variants)} $$ $$ CK = T_3[0..127] \quad \text{(128-bit Cipher Key)} $$ $$ IK = T_4[0..127] \quad \text{(128-bit Integrity Key)} $$ $$ AK = T_5[0..47] \quad \text{(48-bit Anonymity Key)} $$ **Step 4 ÔÇö Compute MAC-A (used in AUTN):** $$ \text{MAC} = f_1(K, RAND, SQN, AMF) = T_1[0..63] \oplus OP_c $$ **Step 5 ÔÇö Assemble AUTN:** $$ AUTN = (SQN \oplus AK) \;\|\; AMF \;\|\; MAC $$ > [!NOTE] > **UE verification:** UE receives $AUTN$, extracts $MAC$, recomputes $f_1$ locally, and if $MAC_{local} = MAC_{received}$, the network is authenticated (defeats **false BTS**). ## 3.2 4G Key Hierarchy Derivation (LTE/EPC) ### 3.2.1 From CK/IK to $K_{ASME}$ (Anchor Key) Per **3GPP TS 33.401**, $K_{ASME}$ is derived using the Key Derivation Function (KDF) defined in **Annex A** ÔÇö based on HMAC-SHA-256. $$ K_{ASME} = \text{KDF}(CK, IK, \text{SQN} \oplus AK, SN\_ID) $$ > **Where:** > - $CK$, $IK$ from Milenage ($f_3, f_4$) > - $SN\_ID$ = Serving Network Identity (PLMN ID of the visited MME) > - $\text{SQN} \oplus AK$ ties the key to the specific authentication session In expanded HMAC-SHA-256 form (the underlying primitive used by 3GPP KDF): $$ K_{ASME} = \text{HMAC-SHA-256}(S, \text{FC} \,\|\, P_0 \,\|\, L_0 \,\|\, P_1 \,\|\, L_1 \,\|\, \dots) $$ where $S = CK \Vert IK$ (256-bit key), FC = 0x10, and $P_i$ are the input parameters. ### 3.2.2 From $K_{ASME}$ to NAS and AS keys $$ K_{NASint} = \text{KDF}(K_{ASME}, \text{FC} = 0x02, \text{``NAS\_int''}, \text{ALG\_ID}, 0x01) $$ $$ K_{NASenc} = \text{KDF}(K_{ASME}, \text{FC} = 0x03, \text{``NAS\_enc''}, \text{ALG\_ID}, 0x01) $$ ### 3.2.3 From $K_{ASME}$ to $K_{eNB}$ $$ K_{eNB} = \text{KDF}(K_{ASME}, \text{FC} = 0x11, \text{NCC} = 0) $$ ### 3.2.4 From $K_{eNB}$ to RRC and UP keys $$ K_{RRCint} = \text{KDF}(K_{eNB}, \text{FC} = 0x04, \text{``RRC\_int''}, \text{ALG\_ID}, 0x01) $$ $$ K_{RRCenc} = \text{KDF}(K_{eNB}, \text{FC} = 0x05, \text{``RRC\_enc''}, \text{ALG\_ID}, 0x01) $$ $$ K_{UPenc} = \text{KDF}(K_{eNB}, \text{FC} = 0x06, \text{``UP\_enc''}, \text{ALG\_ID}, 0x01) $$ $$ K_{UPint} = \text{KDF}(K_{eNB}, \text{FC} = 0x07, \text{``UP\_int''}, \text{ALG\_ID}, 0x01) $$ ## 3.3 5G Key Hierarchy Derivation (NR/5GC) > [!IMPORTANT] > 5G modifies the hierarchy: $K_{SEAF}$ is delivered to AMF; $K_{gNB}$ is then derived at AMF and sent to gNB during N2 handover / context setup. ### 3.3.1 From $K$ to $K_{AUSF}$ and $K_{SEAF}$ During **EAP-AKA'** or **5G-AKA**, the ARPF (in UDM) generates the AV and computes: $$ K_{AUSF} = \text{KDF}(K, \text{FC} = 0x6A, \text{``5G$\$$AKMA''}, \text{SQN} \oplus AK, \dots) $$ The AUSF computes the anchor key: $$ K_{SEAF} = \text{KDF}(K_{AUSF}, \text{FC} = 0x6C, \text{SN\_name}) $$ ### 3.3.2 From $K_{SEAF}$ (via $K_{AMF}$) to $K_{NAS}$, $K_{gNB}$ $$ K_{AMF} = \text{KDF}(K_{SEAF}, \text{FC} = 0x6D, \text{``AMF''}, \text{SUPI, ÔÇŽ}) $$ $$ K_{NASint} = \text{KDF}(K_{AMF}, \text{FC} = 0x69, \text{``NAS-int''}, \text{ALG}) $$ $$ K_{NASenc} = \text{KDF}(K_{AMF}, \text{FC} = 0x6B, \text{``NAS-enc''}, \text{ALG}) $$ $$ K_{gNB} = \text{KDF}(K_{AMF}, \text{FC} = 0x6E, \text{``gNB''}, \text{ABBA, count}) $$ ### 3.3.3 $K_{gNB}$ to RRC and UP keys Identical pattern to 4G, with FC values 0x04ÔÇô0x07 but applied to $K_{gNB}$. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Mini-Example (Conceptual) > [!TIP] > Let $K = \texttt{0x4041ÔÇŽ4040}$ (128-bit), $RAND = \texttt{0x00ÔÇŽ00}$ (all zeros ÔÇö *not secure, used for pedagogy only*). > > Then for $i=1$: $T_1 = E_K[RAND \oplus c_1] = E_K[\texttt{0x00ÔÇŽ00}] = E_K[\texttt{0}]$. > > Since AES-128 with $K$ is a deterministic bijection, $T_1$ is some known 128-bit value. $MAC = T_1[0..63] \oplus OP_c$. > > In a real deployment, the operator $K$ is **never** transmitted ÔÇö it is provisioned physically in the USIM and the AuC/HSS. ## 3.5 Key-Length Boundary Conditions | Hierarchy | Min Length | Max Length | Use | |---|---|---|---| | $K$ | 128 | 128 | Long-term secret | | $CK$, $IK$ | 128 | 128 | Confidentiality + integrity root | | $K_{ASME}$ / $K_{SEAF}$ | 256 | 256 | Serving network anchor | | $K_{gNB}$ / $K_{eNB}$ | 128 | 256 (5G supports 256-bit) | Radio node root | | $K_{NAS*}$, $K_{RRC*}$, $K_{UP*}$ | 128 | 128 | Algorithm-specific keys | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## SUCI = ECIES SUPI"] d["SUCI over the air
concealed identity"] e["AMF forwards to UDM"] f["UDM decrypts using HN private key"] a --> c b --> c c --> d d --> e e --> f ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this diagram matters for KTU 2024:** > - Section 4.1 shows the **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** ÔÇö the defining feature of 5G. > - Section 4.2 is the **classic 4G architecture** ÔÇö still the backbone of most Indian telecom. > - Section 4.3 visualizes the **key derivation chain** that protects the entire system. > - Section 4.4 shows the **IMSI-catcher defense** (SUPI Ôćĺ SUCI) introduced in 5G. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions are modeled on the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: > - **Part A:** 3-mark short questions > - **Part B:** 14-mark module internal-choice (Part (a) = 7m, Part (b) = 7m) > - CO mapping follows PECCT862 syllabus; Bloom's Level: **L3 (Apply) to L5 (Evaluate)**. --- ## 5.1 Part A ÔÇö 3 Mark Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô May 2024]` **Differentiate between the 3GPP and 3GPP2 standardization bodies. Which cellular generations does 3GPP cover?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - 3GPP is the **3rd Generation Partnership Project** that standardizes **GSM/GPRS (2G), UMTS (3G), LTE/LTE-A (4G), and 5G NR** ÔÇö founded 1998. - 3GPP2 was a parallel body for **cdma2000**-based networks (now largely dissolved/integrated into 3GPP). - 3GPP uses **GSM/UMTS/LTE family**; 3GPP2 used **CDMA family** (1xRTT, EV-DO). ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023]` **What is the role of the USIM in the 3GPP security architecture?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The **USIM (Universal Subscriber Identity Module)** is the tamper-resistant smart card inside the UE. - It stores the **long-term secret key $K$** and the **operator constant $OP/OP_c$**. - It executes the **Milenage algorithms $f_1$ÔÇô$f_5$** to compute $RES$, $CK$, $IK$ during authentication. - It is the **root of trust** ÔÇö without it, no UE can authenticate to the 3GPP network. --- ## 5.2 Part B ÔÇö 14 Mark Module Internal Choice ### Ôťů Question A `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Model Paper 2024, Module 1]` > **(a)** Explain the **3GPP reference network architecture for 5G NR** with a neat block diagram. List the major network functions and their roles. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Describe the **5G-AKA authentication and key agreement procedure** between UE, AMF, AUSF, and UDM, with the message sequence and the security keys derived at each stage. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution ÔÇö Part (a): 5G 3GPP Reference Architecture** **Step 1 ÔÇö Three logical domains (1 mark):** The 5G system is divided into: 1. **User Equipment (UE)** ÔÇö mobile device + USIM. 2. **5G Radio Access Network (NG-RAN)** ÔÇö set of **gNBs/ng-eNBs**. 3. **5G Core Network (5GC)** ÔÇö set of network functions (NFs) following **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)**. **Step 2 ÔÇö Network Functions in 5GC (3 marks):** | NF | Function | |---|---| | **AMF** | NAS signaling, registration, mobility, access authentication relay. | | **SMF** | PDU session establishment, IP allocation, charging, policy enforcement. | | **UPF** | Packet routing/forwarding, QoS handling, anchoring, interworking with DN. | | **AUSF** | Authenticates the UE, supports EAP-AKA' / 5G-AKA. | | **UDM** | Stores subscription data, generates Authentication Vectors. | | **ARPF** | Anchor of the long-term key $K$ inside UDM. | | **PCF** | Policy rules, charging rules, network slicing policy. | | **NRF** | NF service discovery and registration. | | **NEF** | Exposes 3GPP services to external networks (API gateway). | | **SEPP** | Security Edge Protection Proxy ÔÇö protects inter-PLMN signaling (NDS). | **Step 3 ÔÇö Key Interfaces (2 marks):** - **N1:** UE Ôćö AMF (NAS over Uu). - **N2:** gNB Ôćö AMF (NGAP). - **N3:** gNB Ôćö UPF (GTP-U). - **N4:** SMF Ôćö UPF. - **N5:** PCF Ôćö AF. - **N6:** UPF Ôćö Data Network. **Step 4 ÔÇö Distinguishing SBA feature (1 mark):** In SBA, all control-plane NFs consume/expose services over a common **service bus (HTTP/2 + JSON)**. AMF, SMF, AUSF, etc., register their services with the NRF and discover each other dynamically ÔÇö a major architectural shift from 4G's static S1-MME / S6a interfaces. --- #### **Model Solution ÔÇö Part (b): 5G-AKA Procedure** **Step 1 ÔÇö Authentication request initiation (1 mark):** - UE sends its concealed identifier **SUCI** (or SUPI) and the **Serving Network Name (SN-name)** in the *Registration Request* message to the AMF. - The AMF selects an **AUSF** (using NRF discovery) and forwards the authentication request via the **N12 (AMF Ôćö AUSF)** reference point. **Step 2 ÔÇö Vector generation at UDM/ARPF (2 marks):** - The **UDM/ARPF** retrieves the subscriber's long-term key $K$. - It generates a random **$RAND$** and computes Milenage outputs: - $XRES = f_2(K, RAND)$ - $CK = f_3(K, RAND)$ - $IK = f_4(K, RAND)$ - $AK = f_5(K, RAND)$ - $AUTN = (SQN \oplus AK) \Vert AMF \Vert MAC$ - It forms the **5G AV**: $\langle RAND, AUTN, HXRES^*, K_{SEAF} \rangle$. - UDM sends the AV to the AUSF over **N13**. **Step 3 ÔÇö AUSF processing (1 mark):** - The AUSF verifies $AUTN$ and computes $K_{AUSF}$ and $K_{SEAF}$ using the KDF. - $RES^* = XRES^*$ is sent to the AMF for the **comparison** step. - The AUSF returns $\{AUTN, RAND, HXRES^*\}$ to AMF Ôćĺ AMF forwards to UE in *Authentication Request*. **Step 4 ÔÇö UE response (2 marks):** - The USIM verifies $MAC$ inside $AUTN$ (network authentication ÔÇö defeats false gNB). - The UE computes $RES^* = \text{KDF}(CK, IK, ÔÇŽ, SN\_name, RAND)$ using the new 5G KDF. - UE returns $RES^*$ in *Authentication Response* to AMF. **Step 5 ÔÇö AMF verification and anchor key delivery (1 mark):** - AMF compares received $RES^*$ with expected $HXRES^*$ (or $XRES^*$). - If they match, the AMF computes $K_{AMF} = \text{KDF}(K_{SEAF}, FC=0x6D, \text{``AMF''}, \text{SUPI}, ÔÇŽ)$. - AMF then derives $K_{NASint}$, $K_{NASenc}$ for protecting NAS, and $K_{gNB}$ for AS protection. --- ### Ôťů Question B `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Supplementary 2024, Module 1]` > **(a)** Compare the **4G LTE/EPC architecture** with the **5G NR/5GC architecture** in terms of core components, interfaces, and security enhancements. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With a clear derivation, explain the **LTE key hierarchy** from the long-term secret $K$ down to the user-plane and RRC ciphering/integrity keys. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution ÔÇö Part (a): 4G vs 5G Architecture Comparison** **Step 1 ÔÇö Core components (3 marks):** | Layer | 4G LTE / EPC | 5G NR / 5GC | |---|---|---| | Radio access | **eNB** | **gNB**, ng-eNB | | Mobility/Session | **MME** (one NF for both) | Split into **AMF** + **SMF** | | Subscriber data | **HSS** | **UDM + UDR + ARPF** | | Authentication | MME + HSS | Dedicated **AUSF** | | User plane | **SGW + PGW** (two UPs) | Unified **UPF** (scalable, multiple) | | Policy | **PCRF** | **PCF** | | Architecture style | **Point-to-point** (Diameter/S1AP) | **Service-Based (SBA)** over HTTP/2 | | Slicing | Not native | **Network Slicing** (NSSAI) | | Subscriber ID | IMSI in clear Ôćĺ IMSI catcher risk | **SUPI** concealed as **SUCI** (ECIES) | **Step 2 ÔÇö Interface comparison (2 marks):** - 4G: S1-MME, S1-U, S6a (Diameter), S5/S8, S11, SGi. - 5G: N1, N2 (NGAP), N3 (GTP-U), N4, N5, N6, N7, N12, N13 ÔÇö and **service-based interfaces** like *Nudm*, *Nausf*, *Namf*, *Nsmf* etc. for SBA. **Step 3 ÔÇö Security enhancements (2 marks):** 1. **Subscriber privacy** via SUCI (ECIES public-key encryption of SUPI) ÔÇö fixes IMSI-catcher. 2. **Unified Authentication Framework** (EAP-AKA', 5G-AKA) with $K_{SEAF}$ anchor separation. 3. **SEPP** for inter-PLMN NDS/IP protection (N32) ÔÇö replaces 4G's manual SEG/Diameter security. 4. **Network slicing** brings **slice-specific security domains**. 5. **Service-based exposure** requires **OAuth 2.0 / mTLS** between NFs (NNRF, NEF). --- #### **Model Solution ÔÇö Part (b): LTE Key Hierarchy Derivation** **Step 1 ÔÇö Root secret in USIM and HSS (1 mark):** - $K$ (128-bit) is provisioned into the USIM and the HSS AuC at subscription time. - Operator constant $OP_c$ is derived as $OP_c = OP \oplus E_K[OP]$. **Step 2 ÔÇö Authentication Vector generation at HSS (2 marks):** For each AV, HSS computes: - $XRES = f_2(K, RAND)$ - $CK = f_3(K, RAND)$ - $IK = f_4(K, RAND)$ - $AK = f_5(K, RAND)$ - $AUTN = (SQN \oplus AK) \Vert AMF \Vert MAC$ The AV tuple $\langle RAND, AUTN, XRES, K_{ASME} \rangle$ is sent to MME. **Step 3 ÔÇö MME derives $K_{ASME}$ (1 mark):** - MME computes $K_{ASME} = \text{KDF}(CK, IK, SQN \oplus AK, SN\_ID)$ where SN\_ID = PLMN of MME. **Step 4 ÔÇö UE derives matching keys (1 mark):** - The USIM computes $CK$, $IK$ and returns them to the ME; the ME independently derives $K_{ASME}$. **Step 5 ÔÇö NAS and AS key derivation (2 marks):** - $K_{NASint} = \text{KDF}(K_{ASME}, FC=0x02)$ - $K_{NASenc} = \text{KDF}(K_{ASME}, FC=0x03)$ - $K_{eNB} = \text{KDF}(K_{ASME}, FC=0x11)$ - $K_{RRCint} = \text{KDF}(K_{eNB}, FC=0x04)$, $K_{RRCenc} = \text{KDF}(K_{eNB}, FC=0x05)$ - $K_{UPenc} = \text{KDF}(K_{eNB}, FC=0x06)$, $K_{UPint} = \text{KDF}(K_{eNB}, FC=0x07)$ > **Why the FC bytes matter:** They bind the derived key to a **specific algorithm/purpose** ÔÇö preventing cross-protocol key reuse attacks (CR-KU, KRACK-class issues). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ÔÇö Common Mark Deduction Areas** > 1. **Confusing CK/IK with $K_{ASME}$/$K_{SEAF}$** ÔÇö students often write $K_{ASME}$ as the *root* key. It is **NOT** the root ÔÇö $K$ is the root. > 2. **Writing "the network authenticates the user" only** ÔÇö full marks require stating **mutual authentication** (3G+) and **SUCI concealment** (5G). > 3. **Skipping the FC byte (0x02, 0x03, etc.)** in KDF derivations ÔÇö examiners *will* deduct 1 mark. > 4. **Mixing UDM/ARPF responsibilities** ÔÇö ARPF is *inside* UDM and is the holder of $K$. > 5. **Forgetting SEPP** when discussing 5G inter-PLMN security. > 6. **Writing S6a as "IP-based"** ÔÇö it is **Diameter**-based over SCTP. > 7. **Using IMSI in 5G diagrams** ÔÇö it is **SUPI** (concealed as SUCI on air). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap ÔÇö Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (pin this!)** - Ôťů **3GPP** = standards body for GSM, UMTS, LTE, 5G (NOT 3GPP2 / WiMAX). - Ôťů Architecture = **UE + RAN (eNB/gNB) + Core (EPC/5GC)**. - Ôťů **USIM** stores $K$ and $OP_c$; **HSS/UDM** mirror them in the AuC/ARPF. - Ôťů **Milenage** = $f_1$ (MAC) Ôćĺ $f_2$ (XRES) Ôćĺ $f_3$ (CK) Ôćĺ $f_4$ (IK) Ôćĺ $f_5$ (AK), built on AES-128. - Ôťů **AUTN** = $(SQN \oplus AK) \Vert AMF \Vert MAC$. - Ôťů **4G hierarchy:** $K \to \{CK, IK\} \to K_{ASME} \to K_{eNB} \to \{K_{NAS*}, K_{RRC*}, K_{UP*}\}$. - Ôťů **5G hierarchy:** $K \to \{CK, IK\} \to K_{AUSF} \to K_{SEAF} \to K_{AMF} \to K_{gNB} \to \{K_{NAS*}, K_{RRC*}, K_{UP*}\}$. - Ôťů **KDF** = HMAC-SHA-256-based with FC bytes binding key to purpose. - Ôťů **Mutual authentication** introduced in 3G; **SUPIÔćĺSUCI** privacy in 5G. - Ôťů **SBA** (HTTP/2 + JSON) is 5G's defining control-plane paradigm; NRF = discovery. - Ôťů **SEPP** = inter-PLMN security (replaces 4G's manual SEG). - Ôťů **N1, N2, N3** = core 5G interfaces (NAS, NGAP, GTP-U). - Ôťů **5 security domains:** Network Access, Network Domain, User Domain, Application Domain, Visibility & Configurability (TS 33.501). - Ôťů **Network slicing** = NSSAI-driven logical networks sharing physical infrastructure. - Ôťů **FC byte values to memorize:** `0x02` NAS-int, `0x03` NAS-enc, `0x11` $K_{eNB}$, `0x04ÔÇô0x07` RRC/UP, `0x6A` $K_{AUSF}$, `0x6C` $K_{SEAF}$, `0x6D` $K_{AMF}$, `0x6E` $K_{gNB}$. - Ôťů **S1-AP / NGAP** = signaling between RAN and MME/AMF. - Ôťů **Diameter** (4G S6a) vs **HTTP/2** (5G SBA) ÔÇö fundamental protocol shift. - Ôťů **SUCI** = `ECIES(public_HN, SUPI)` ÔÇö ECC-based, profile A or profile B.

topics.title: CDMA2000 Overview: Radio and Network Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CDMA2000 Overview: Radio and Network Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CDMA2000** is a family of 3rd Generation (3G) mobile telecommunications standards that use **Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)** channel access to transmit voice, data, and signaling between mobile stations (MS) and the radio access network (RAN). It is a standardized successor to IS-95 (cdmaOne) and is part of the **IMT-2000** family ratified by the International Telecommunication Union (ITU). CDMA2000 is developed and maintained by **3GPP2** (3rd Generation Partnership Project 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** CDMA2000 is a *multi-carrier* CDMA system supporting both 1X (single carrier, 1.2288 Mcps) and **3X (multi-carrier, 3 ├Ś 1.2288 Mcps = 3.6864 Mcps)** deployments. The chip rate of the forward/reverse link is **1.2288 Mcps** (Mega chips per second).

The CDMA2000 family includes several revisions:

| Revision | Marketing Name | Peak Data Rate (DL/UL) | Bandwidth |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|-----------|
| CDMA2000 1X | 2.5G / 3G-1X | 153 kbps / 153 kbps | 1.25 MHz |
| CDMA2000 1xEV-DO Rev. 0 | 3G DO | 2.4 Mbps / 153 kbps | 1.25 MHz |
| CDMA2000 1xEV-DO Rev. A | 3G DO-A | 3.1 Mbps / 1.8 Mbps | 1.25 MHz |
| CDMA2000 1xEV-DO Rev. B | 3G DO-B | 14.7 Mbps / 4.9 Mbps | 5 MHz (multi-carrier) |
| CDMA2000 1xEV-DV | 3G DV | 3.1 Mbps / 1.8 Mbps | 1.25 MHz |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a huge **cocktail party** (the wireless cell) where 50 people (mobile users) want to talk to the host (Base Station) **simultaneously**, in the same room, in the same language.

- **FDMA** (1G) is like giving each person a separate room. Expensive and limited.
- **TDMA** (2G/GSM) is like letting each person speak for exactly 1 minute, one after another in the same room.
- **CDMA** (CDMA2000) is like letting **all 50 people speak at the same time in the same room**, but each person speaks a different language (a unique **spreading code**). The host understands every language; everyone else hears only noise.

> [!NOTE]
> The "different language" is the **orthogonal Walsh code** (in the forward link) or the **PN (Pseudo-Noise) long code** (in the reverse link). The host correlates with the right code to recover that user's signal.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Chip Rate (Rc):** **1.2288 Mcps** (1.2288 ├Ś 10ÔüÂ chips per second) for 1X
> - **Bandwidth (W):** **1.25 MHz** (spreading bandwidth)
> - **Frame Duration (Voice):** **20 ms**
> - **Pilot Channel Power:** typically **-7 dB to -10 dB** relative to total cell power
> - **Soft Handoff Threshold (T_ADD):** typically **-13 dB** Ec/Io
> - **Processing Gain (PG):** **10 ├Ś logÔéüÔéÇ(W/R)** in dB; for 9.6 kbps voice Ôćĺ PG Ôëł **21 dB**
> - **PN Short Code Period:** **2┬╣ÔüÁ = 32768 chips** (Ôëł 26.67 ms)
> - **PN Long Code Period:** **2Ôü┤┬▓ Ôłĺ 1 chips** (Ôëł 41 days)
> - **Walsh Code Length (1X Forward):** **64 chips** (64 orthogonal codes)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why CDMA2000? ÔÇö The Core Operational Logic

CDMA2000 was designed to overcome the **capacity, data rate, and security** limitations of 2G (IS-95). The "why" rests on three pillars:

1. **Higher Spectral Efficiency** ÔÇö Many users share the same 1.25 MHz band using orthogonal codes.
2. **Soft Handoff (Make-before-Break)** ÔÇö The MS connects to multiple sectors simultaneously, eliminating ping-pong drops and improving security against forced-downgrade attacks.
3. **Built-in Privacy** ÔÇö Spreading inherently obscures the signal; only a receiver with the correct code can decode, providing **physical-layer security**.

## 2.2 The "How" ÔÇö Operational Steps in CDMA2000

### A. Forward Link (Base Station Ôćĺ Mobile)
1. **Voice/Data is vocoded** (e.g., QCELP 8k/13k, EVRC).
2. **Convolutional/Turbo encoding** is applied (rate 1/2, 1/3, 1/4).
3. **Interleaving** is applied to disperse burst errors.
4. The signal is **spread** by a 64-chip **Walsh code** (channelization).
5. The signal is **scrambled** by a **PN Short Code** (cell/sector identification via Q- and I-branch PN sequences).
6. **QPSK modulation** is used on the forward link.

### B. Reverse Link (Mobile Ôćĺ Base Station)
1. The signal is **spread** by a 64-chip Walsh code (channelization).
2. The signal is **scrambled** by the **PN Long Code** (unique to the user ÔÇö provides privacy and user identification).
3. **HPSK (Hybrid Phase Shift Keying)** is used to reduce peak-to-average ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why different codes on Fwd vs Rev?**
> On the **forward link**, the cell needs to separate its own channels Ôćĺ orthogonal Walsh is best.
> On the **reverse link**, all mobiles arrive asynchronously at the BS Ôćĺ orthogonal codes cannot be maintained, so a quasi-orthogonal long code (PN) is used.

## 2.3 Radio Components (Layer 1 & 2 Entities)

| Component | Full Name | Function |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| MS | Mobile Station | User Equipment (UE) ÔÇö phone, modem, data card |
| BTS | Base Transceiver Station | Radio transmitter/receiver, modulates/demodulates |
| BSC | Base Station Controller | Manages radio resources, handovers, power control |
| PCF | Packet Control Function | Bridges RAN and PDSN for packet data sessions |
| AN | Access Network | Generic term for BTS + BSC |
| Pilot Channel | F-Pilot | Unmodulated beacon for sync, handoff, Ec/Io measurement |
| Sync Channel | F-Sync | Transmits system time, PN offset, paging channel data rate |
| Paging Channel | F-PCH | One-to-many signaling (incoming call, SMS, system parameters) |
| Traffic Channel | F-TCH / R-TCH | Carries user voice/data |
| Access Channel | R-ACH | Mobile-originated signaling before traffic is granted |

## 2.4 Network Components (Core Network Entities)

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| MSC | Mobile Switching Center ÔÇö circuit-switched voice call control |
| VLR | Visitor Location Register ÔÇö temporary subscriber profile (visited network) |
| HLR | Home Location Register ÔÇö permanent subscriber profile |
| AC | Authentication Center ÔÇö stores A-key, SSD (Shared Secret Data) |
| PDSN | Packet Data Serving Node ÔÇö IP gateway, PPP termination, MIP support |
| FA | Foreign Agent ÔÇö Mobile IP care-of address in visited network |
| HA | Home Agent ÔÇö Mobile IP anchor in home network |
| AAA | Authentication, Authorization, Accounting server (RADIUS/Diameter) |
| AN-AAA | Access Network AAA ÔÇö authenticates MS over the air interface |
| SC/MMS | Service Center / Multimedia Message Service Center |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SSD (Shared Secret Data)** is a 128-bit value derived from the A-key and is the cornerstone of CDMA2000 air-interface security. It is never transmitted over the air ÔÇö a critical security property.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All absolute-value bars are rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Use Case |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | Processing Gain (linear) | $PG = \dfrac{W}{R} = \dfrac{R_c}{R_b}$ | dimensionless | Anti-jam margin |
| 2 | Processing Gain (dB) | $PG_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{R}\right)$ | ~21 dB for 9.6 kbps voice | Link budget |
| 3 | Spreading Factor | $SF = \dfrac{R_c}{R_b} = \dfrac{1.2288 \times 10^6}{R_b}$ | 128 (for 9.6 kbps) | Code design |
| 4 | Number of Orthogonal Codes | $N_W = 2^n$ for n-chip Walsh | 64 (for 1X) | Channelization |
| 5 | Single-Cell Capacity (no voice activity) | $M = \dfrac{W/R}{E_b/N_0} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1+\alpha}$ | ~30ÔÇô40 users (8 kbps voice) | Capacity planning |
| 6 | Voice Activity Factor | $G_v = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$ | ╬▒ Ôëł 0.4 (43% activity) | Reverse link loading |
| 7 | Loading Factor (Reverse) | $\eta_L = 1 - (1 + \text{Load}) \cdot e^{-\text{Load}}$ | ╬Ě < 0.75 (target) | Pole capacity |
| 8 | Bit Energy / Spectral Noise Density | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{S/N \cdot W}{R}$ | 7 dB (target) | Link quality |
| 9 | PN Short Code Period | $T_{PN,s} = \dfrac{2^{15}}{R_c}$ | 26.67 ms | Sector identification |
| 10 | Frame Duration | $T_f = 20$ ms (voice) | 20 ms | Vocoder alignment |
| 11 | Walsh Code Generation (Hadamard) | $H_{2n} = \begin{bmatrix} H_n & H_n \\ H_n & -H_n \end{bmatrix}$ | recursive | Forward link |
| 12 | Path Loss (free space) | $PL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ | dB | Cell radius |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Planning Engineers** use the capacity formula to dimension cell-site equipment.
- **Security Engineers** focus on SSD, A-key, and CAVE (Cellular Authentication and Voice Encryption) algorithm integrity.
- **RF Engineers** apply processing gain in link-budget analysis to compute maximum tolerable jamming.
- **Core Network Engineers** design PDSN/AAA integration for CDMA2000 EV-DO Rev. A/B data services.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Processing Gain and Capacity of a Single CDMA2000 Cell

### Step 1 ÔÇö Start with the CDMA channel model
Assume K users transmit simultaneously in the same bandwidth W. The received signal-to-noise ratio for user k (treating other KÔłĺ1 users as interference) is:

$$
SNR_k = \frac{S_k}{N_0 W + \sum\limits_{i \neq k}^{K} S_i}
$$

### Step 2 ÔÇö Perfect power control assumption
With perfect power control, all signals are received at equal power S. Then:

$$
\sum_{i \neq k}^{K} S_i = (K - 1) S
$$

### Step 3 ÔÇö Rewrite the equation

$$
SNR_k = \frac{S}{N_0 W + (K-1)S}
$$

### Step 4 ÔÇö Convert to bit energy per noise density
The relation $E_b = S/R$ gives:

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{S}{R \cdot N_0} = \frac{S}{N_0 W} \cdot \frac{W}{R} = \frac{S}{N_0 W} \cdot PG
$$

### Step 5 ÔÇö Rearrange for $S/(N_0 W)$

$$
\frac{S}{N_0 W} = \frac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \frac{1}{PG} = \frac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \frac{R}{W}
$$

### Step 6 ÔÇö Substitute back into Step 3

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \frac{R}{W} = \frac{1}{\frac{N_0 W}{S} + (K-1)} = \frac{1}{\frac{W/R}{E_b/N_0} + (K-1)}
$$

### Step 7 ÔÇö Solve for K (single-cell capacity, hard limit)

$$
(K - 1) = \frac{W/R}{E_b/N_0} - \frac{W/R}{E_b/N_0} \cdot \frac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \frac{R}{W}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
K = 1 + \frac{W/R}{E_b/N_0} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{E_b/N_0 \cdot R/W}{1}\right) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K \approx \frac{W/R}{E_b/N_0} + 1
$$

For large K, the +1 is negligible. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{K_{\max} \;\approx\; \frac{W/R}{E_b/N_0}}
$$

### Step 8 ÔÇö Apply numerical values
- W = 1.2288 ├Ś 10ÔüÂ Hz
- R = 9600 bps (8 kbps vocoder)
- Target $E_b/N_0 = 7$ dB = 5.01 (linear)

$$
K_{\max} = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^6}{9600 \times 5.01} = \frac{128}{5.01} \approx 25.55
$$

With voice activity factor Gv = 1/╬▒ = 2.5 (╬▒ = 0.4):

$$
K_{\text{practical}} \approx K_{\max} \cdot G_v = 25.55 \times 2.5 \approx 63.87 \;\Rightarrow\; 63 \text{ users/cell}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A single 1.25 MHz CDMA2000 1X sector supports approximately **63 simultaneous voice users** (without sectorization, no soft handoff overhead). Real deployment yields **30ÔÇô40 users** per sector.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hadamard / Walsh Code Generation

The recursive Hadamard matrix defines all 64 orthogonal Walsh codes used in CDMA2000 forward link:

$$
H_1 = \begin{bmatrix} +1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
H_2 = \begin{bmatrix} +1 & +1 \\ +1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
H_{2n} = \begin{bmatrix} H_n & H_n \\ H_n & -H_n \end{bmatrix}
$$

For n = 6 (64 ├Ś 64 matrix), expanding HÔéćÔéä:

$$
H_{64} = \begin{bmatrix} H_{32} & H_{32} \\ H_{32} & -H_{32} \end{bmatrix}, \quad H_{32} = \begin{bmatrix} H_{16} & H_{16} \\ H_{16} & -H_{16} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Walsh Code Index 0 (Pilot Channel):** $\;\;W_0 = [+1, +1, +1, \ldots, +1]_{64}$ ÔÇö all +1s; this is the unmodulated pilot.

**Walsh Code Index 32 (Sync Channel):** $\;\;W_32 = [H_{32}, -H_{32}]$ ÔÇö first 32 chips +1, last 32 chips Ôłĺ1.

**Walsh Codes 1ÔÇô7, 33ÔÇô63:** allocated to paging, broadcast, and traffic channels.

> [!NOTE]
> The orthogonality property $\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_k[i] \cdot W_m[i] = 0$ for $k \neq m$ is what allows zero-interference channelization on the forward link.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PN Long Code ÔÇö User Identity Generation

The **PN Long Code** uniquely identifies each mobile. It is a 42-stage maximal-length sequence:

$$
C_{\text{long}}(t) = \bigoplus_{i=0}^{41} g_i \cdot c_i(t)
$$

where $c_i(t)$ is the ith shift-register output and $g_i$ is the mask bit (0 or 1). The mask consists of:

- **ESN (Electronic Serial Number)** ÔÇö 32-bit unique hardware ID of the MS
- **System-specific offset**

For user with ESN = E (32 bits) on system SID N (15 bits) and pilot PN offset P (9 bits):

$$
\text{Mask} = \{E, \overline{E}, N, N \oplus P, \text{reserved}\}
$$

The Long Code is **user-specific** and is the foundation of CDMA2000 air-interface privacy.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ÔÇö Security Mapping of CDMA2000 Components

| Layer | Component | Security Function | Threat Mitigated |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| Air | PN Long Code | User scrambling (privacy) | Eavesdropping |
| Air | Walsh Code | Channel separation | Cross-talk |
| Air | Power Control | Anti-fade, anti-jamming | Jamming, near-far |
| Air | CAVE algorithm | SSD-based authentication | Subscriber cloning |
| Air | Private/Public Long Code Mask | Encryption key | Plaintext intercept |
| Network | SSD | Shared secret with AC | Replay attacks |
| Network | A-key | Rooted in secret on MS | Impersonation |
| Network | AAA (RADIUS) | IP-layer auth | Unauthorized access |
| Network | IPsec (PDSNÔÇôHA) | Tunnel encryption | MITM, sniffing |
| Network | HA | Mobile IP binding | Redirection attacks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CDMA2000 1X Network Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TB
    MS[Mobile Station MS]
    BTS[Base Transceiver Station BTS]
    BSC[Base Station Controller BSC]
    PCF[Packet Control Function PCF]
    PDSN[Packet Data Serving Node PDSN]
    AAA[AAA Server]
    AN_AAA[AN-AAA Access Network AAA]
    HA[Home Agent HA]
    MSC[Mobile Switching Center MSC]
    VLR[Visitor Location Register VLR]
    HLR[Home Location Register HLR]
    AC[Authentication Center AC]
    PSTN[Public Switched Telephone Network PSTN]
    INTERNET[Internet Public IP Network]

    MS -- Um air interface --> BTS
    BTS -- Abis] --> BSC
    BSC -- A1 circuit voice --> MSC
    BSC -- A8/A9 packet data --> PCF
    PCF -- A10/A11 --> PDSN
    PDSN -- RADIUS --> AAA
    BSC -- A12/A13 --> AN_AAA
    PDSN -- Mobile IP --> HA
    HA -- IP tunnel --> INTERNET
    MSC -- IS-41 --> VLR
    MSC -- IS-41 --> HLR
    HLR --- AC
    MSC -- SS7 --> PSTN
```

## 4.2 CDMA2000 Forward Link Channel Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FL["Forward Link 1.25 MHz"]
        P[F-Pilot Walsh 0]
        S[F-Sync Walsh 32]
        PG1[F-Paging 1 Walsh 1]
        PG2[F-Paging 2 Walsh 2]
        BCAST[F-BCCH Broadcast]
        QC[F-Quick Paging]
        TC1[F-Traffic 1 Walsh N]
        TC2[F-Traffic 2 Walsh M]
    end
    QPSK[QPSK Modulation]
    AMP[Power Amplifier]
    ANT[Antenna]
    P --> QPSK
    S --> QPSK
    PG1 --> QPSK
    PG2 --> QPSK
    BCAST --> QPSK
    QC --> QPSK
    TC1 --> QPSK
    TC2 --> QPSK
    QPSK --> AMP --> ANT
```

## 4.3 CDMA2000 Reverse Link Channel Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RL["Reverse Link 1.25 MHz"]
        AC[R-Access Channel]
        TC1[R-Traffic Channel 1]
        TC2[R-Traffic Channel 2]
    end
    WL[Walsh Spreading 64 chip]
    PN[PN Long Code Scrambling User specific]
    HPSK[Hybrid PSK Modulation]
    AMP[Power Amplifier]
    ANT[Mobile Antenna]
    AC --> WL --> PN --> HPSK --> AMP --> ANT
    TC1 --> WL
    TC2 --> WL
```

## 4.4 CDMA2000 Spreading Process (Forward Link)

```mermaid
graph TB
    DATA[User Data e.g. 9.6 kbps]
    ENC[Convolutional Encoder rate 1/2]
    INT[Interleaver 24x16]
    WL[Walsh Code 64 chip channelization]
    PNS[PN Short Code Scramble cell ID]
    QPSK[QPSK Modulator]
    TX[RF Transmit]

    DATA --> ENC --> INT --> WL --> PNS --> QPSK --> TX
```

## 4.5 CDMA2000 Authentication & Security (CAVE-based)

```mermaid
graph LR
    AC[Authentication Center SSD stored]
    HLR[HLR]
    MSC[MSC VLR]
    BTS[BSC BTS]
    MS[Mobile Station]
    RAND[RAND Challenge 32 bits]
    AUTHR[AUTHR Response]
    SSD[Shared Secret Data 128 bits]
    CAVE[CAVE Algorithm in MS and AC]

    HLR --> SSD
    AC --- CAVE
    MSC --> RAND
    RAND --> BTS
    BTS --> MS
    MS --- CAVE
    CAVE --> AUTHR
    AUTHR --> BTS
    BTS --> MSC
    MSC --> AC
    AC --- CAVE
    CAVE --> AUTH_COMPARE{AUTHR match?}
    AUTH_COMPARE -->|Yes| REG[Register MS as authenticated]
    AUTH_COMPARE -->|No| REJ[Reject and report fraud]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ÔÇö 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2022]`
**Q: Define CDMA2000. List any two improvements it offers over IS-95 (cdmaOne).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
CDMA2000 is a 3G mobile telecommunications standard developed by 3GPP2 that uses **Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)** with a chip rate of **1.2288 Mcps** in a **1.25 MHz** bandwidth, as part of the IMT-2000 family.

Two improvements over IS-95:
1. **Higher data rates** ÔÇö supports 153 kbps packet data (1X) and up to 3.1 Mbps (EV-DO Rev. A) versus 14.4 kbps in IS-95.
2. **Better security** ÔÇö uses SSD-based CAVE authentication, private/public Long Code masks for encryption, and a 128-bit SSD never sent over the air.

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two improvements.)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Jun 2023]`
**Q: What is the role of the PN Long Code in CDMA2000 reverse link?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The PN Long Code is a **42-stage maximal-length sequence** ($2^{42}-1$ chips) used to scramble the reverse-link signal of each mobile station. It serves two purposes:
1. **User identification** ÔÇö the long-code mask is derived from the MS's unique ESN, making every user's transmission uniquely identifiable.
2. **Privacy** ÔÇö without knowing the long-code mask, an eavesdropper cannot decode the user's data, providing physical-layer security.

(1 mark for stating the definition, 2 marks for the two purposes.)

## 5.2 PART B ÔÇö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô May 2024]`

**Q (a) (7 marks):** With a neat block diagram, explain the forward link channel structure of CDMA2000 1X. State the role of the Pilot, Sync, Paging, and Traffic channels.

**Model Solution:**

**Forward Link Channel Structure (4 marks):**

The CDMA2000 1X forward link occupies **1.25 MHz** bandwidth with a chip rate of **1.2288 Mcps**. It uses **64-chip Walsh codes** for channelization and **PN short codes** (I/Q branches, period $2^{15} = 32768$ chips) for cell/sector identification.

| Channel | Walsh Index | Function |
|---------|-------------|----------|
| F-Pilot | WÔéÇ (all +1s) | Continuous unmodulated beacon; provides time/frequency reference, handoff measurement (Ec/Io) |
| F-Sync | WÔéâÔéé | Transmits system time, PN offset, paging channel data rate, system ID |
| F-Paging | WÔéü ÔÇô WÔéç | One-to-many signaling: incoming calls, SMS, system parameters |
| F-BCCH | (configured) | Broadcast control channel for system parameters |
| F-Quick Paging | (configured) | Short indicator to wake up MS ÔÇö battery saving |
| F-Traffic | W_N (assigned) | User voice or packet data |

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

User Data Ôćĺ Convolutional Encoder (rate 1/2) Ôćĺ Interleaver (24 ├Ś 16) Ôćĺ Walsh Spreading (64-chip) Ôćĺ PN Short-Code Scrambling (Q and I) Ôćĺ QPSK Modulation Ôćĺ Power Amplifier Ôćĺ Antenna.

The pilot channel carries no data ÔÇö it is a continuous all-+1s sequence on Walsh 0. The base station assigns a unique 64-chip Walsh code to each user; orthogonality ensures zero cross-channel interference in flat-fading conditions.

---

**Q (b) (7 marks):** A CDMA2000 1X sector operates with bandwidth W = 1.2288 MHz, supports 9.6 kbps voice users with target Eb/N0 = 7 dB, and voice activity factor Gv = 2.5. Compute the **single-cell pole capacity** and the **practical user capacity** of the sector.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ÔÇö Processing Gain (1 mark):**

$$
PG = \frac{W}{R} = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^6}{9600} = 128
$$

$$
PG_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(128) \approx 21.07 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Convert Eb/N0 to linear (1 mark):**

$$
(E_b/N_0)_{\text{lin}} = 10^{7/10} = 10^{0.7} \approx 5.012
$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Pole capacity (no voice activity) (2 marks):**

$$
K_{\text{pole}} = \frac{PG}{E_b/N_0} = \frac{128}{5.012} \approx 25.55 \;\Rightarrow\; \lfloor 25 \rfloor \text{ users}
$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Practical capacity (with voice activity) (2 marks):**

$$
K_{\text{practical}} = K_{\text{pole}} \times G_v = 25.55 \times 2.5 \approx 63.87 \;\Rightarrow\; 63 \text{ users}
$$

**Step 5 ÔÇö Loading check (1 mark):**

Reverse-link loading at K = 63:

$$
\eta_L = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + K \cdot G_v^{-1} \cdot (E_b/N_0)^{-1}} \cdot \frac{1}{PG}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\eta_L = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 25.55} = 1 - \frac{1}{26.55} = 1 - 0.0377 = 0.9623
$$

**Result:** The sector is **overloaded** (╬Ě > 0.75 target). Practical deployment limit is around **30ÔÇô40 users per sector** to maintain ╬Ě Ôëł 0.6ÔÇô0.75.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ÔÇö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert 7 dB to linear (students often write 7 instead of 5.012).
> 2. Confusing W = 1.2288 MHz with W = 1.25 MHz (the chip rate is 1.2288 Mcps, the channel bandwidth is 1.25 MHz ÔÇö use 1.2288 ├Ś 10ÔüÂ for the spread bandwidth).
> 3. Applying the voice activity factor to the pole capacity incorrectly (multiply, do not divide).
> 4. Skipping the loading-factor check and losing the final 1 mark.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô May 2024]`

**Q (a) (7 marks):** Explain the role of the **PN Short Code** and **PN Long Code** in CDMA2000. How do they differ in chip rate, period, and purpose?

**Model Solution:**

| Property | PN Short Code | PN Long Code |
|----------|---------------|--------------|
| Generator polynomial | $g_1(x) = x^{15} + x^{13} + x^{9} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{5} + 1$ (I-branch) | $g_2(x) = x^{42} + x^{35} + x^{33} + x^{31} + \ldots + x^{3} + x^{1} + 1$ |
| Length | $2^{15} = 32768$ chips | $2^{42} - 1 \approx 4.4 \times 10^{12}$ chips |
| Period (time) | 26.67 ms | $\approx 41$ days |
| Chip rate | 1.2288 Mcps | 1.2288 Mcps |
| Purpose | Sector/cell identification (sector address = PN offset 0ÔÇô511) | User identification + privacy (mask = ESN + system offsets) |
| Used in | Forward link (Q- and I-branch) ÔÇö pilot, sync, paging, traffic | Reverse link ÔÇö every MS uses its own mask |
| Repeats | Every 26.67 ms (one short-code period per pilot PN) | Practically never repeats within a session |

**Roles (7 marks):**

The **PN Short Code** (4 marks) is used on the forward link. It identifies the cell/sector. There are 512 possible PN offsets (PilotPN Ôłł {0..511}), assigned in a reuse pattern of typically 3-sector cells. Every channel transmitted by the BS is scrambled by the same short code so that the MS, after synchronizing to the pilot, can correlate against the right PN offset and demodulate the cell's signals. The pilot itself is the strongest short-code-bearing signal and is used for handoff decisions (Ec/Io measurement).

The **PN Long Code** (3 marks) is used on the reverse link. It uniquely identifies each mobile. The long-code mask is built from the mobile's **ESN (32 bits)** and system identifiers. The MS XORs the data with its long-code sequence, producing a user-specific scrambled stream. The base station, knowing the long-code mask of each registered MS, can de-scramble. To any other receiver, the signal appears as noise ÔÇö providing physical-layer privacy. The long code also serves as the basis for the **Private Long Code Mask** used in the CAVE algorithm for voice encryption.

---

**Q (b) (7 marks):** Describe the **CAVE-based authentication procedure** in CDMA2000. State the role of **A-key** and **SSD**.

**Model Solution:**

**Overview (1 mark):**
CDMA2000 uses the **CAVE (Cellular Authentication and Voice Encryption)** algorithm to authenticate subscribers and generate encryption parameters. CAVE runs in both the MS and the **Authentication Center (AC)**.

**A-key (2 marks):**
- A 64-bit **master secret** stored permanently in the MS's semi-permanent memory and known only to the AC.
- Never transmitted over the air.
- Provisioned at the time of subscription, either manually (over the air is forbidden) or via a secure factory process.
- Used to derive the **Shared Secret Data (SSD)**.

**SSD (2 marks):**
- 128-bit value, divided into **SSD-A (64 bits)** for authentication and **SSD-B (64 bits)** for voice/signal-message encryption.
- Generated by running CAVE on A-key + RANDSSD.
- Stored in the MS's temporary memory and in the AC.
- Re-generated periodically (SSD_UPDATE procedure) to limit exposure.

**Authentication Procedure (2 marks):**
1. The AC/AC generates a 32-bit random number **RAND**.
2. RAND is sent to the MS via the paging or access channel.
3. The MS runs CAVE on RAND and SSD-A Ôćĺ produces **AUTHR (18 bits)**.
4. AUTHR is sent back to the network.
5. The network runs the same CAVE computation and compares its AUTHR with the received AUTHR.
6. Match Ôćĺ MS is authenticated and registered.
7. Additionally, CAVE produces **COUNT** (call counter) and a key for **Private Long Code Mask** for voice encryption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ÔÇö Authentication Pitfalls:**
> 1. Many students write that **A-key is sent over the air** ÔÇö this is the single most common error. The A-key is **never transmitted**; only SSD is regenerated from it.
> 2. Confusing SSD-A and SSD-B roles: SSD-A Ôćĺ authentication, SSD-B Ôćĺ encryption.
> 3. Forgetting that CAVE also generates the **Private Long Code Mask** used for voice privacy.
> 4. Not mentioning the **SSD_UPDATE** procedure ÔÇö loses a mark for omitting key lifecycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Bullets ÔÇö Memorize These:**

- [ ] CDMA2000 = **1.2288 Mcps** chip rate, **1.25 MHz** bandwidth, developed by **3GPP2**, family of IMT-2000.
- [ ] CDMA2000 family: **1X** (153 kbps), **EV-DO Rev. 0/A/B**, **EV-DV** ÔÇö each revision multiplies data rate.
- [ ] **Forward link** uses **64-chip Walsh** for channelization + **PN Short Code** for cell ID.
- [ ] **Reverse link** uses **64-chip Walsh** for channelization + **PN Long Code** for user ID.
- [ ] Forward link modulation = **QPSK**; Reverse link modulation = **HPSK** (lower PAPR).
- [ ] **Processing Gain** = $W/R$ (linear) or $10 \log_{10}(W/R)$ dB; for 9.6 kbps voice Ôëł **21 dB**.
- [ ] Single-cell capacity (no activity) Ôëł $W/R \div (E_b/N_0)$ Ôćĺ ~25 users; with voice activity 2.5├Ś Ôćĺ ~63.
- [ ] Practical capacity ~ **30ÔÇô40 users per sector** to keep loading factor < 0.75.
- [ ] **Walsh 0** = Pilot; **Walsh 32** = Sync; Walsh 1ÔÇô7 = Paging; remaining = Traffic/BCCH.
- [ ] **Pilot PN offset** identifies the sector (0ÔÇô511). The MS measures **Ec/Io** for handoff.
- [ ] **PN Short Code period** = $2^{15}/1.2288\text{Mcps}$ = **26.67 ms**.
- [ ] **PN Long Code period** = $2^{42}-1$ chips Ôëł **41 days**; mask = ESN + system offsets.
- [ ] **A-key** = 64-bit master secret; **never** transmitted over the air.
- [ ] **SSD** = 128-bit; **SSD-A** = authentication, **SSD-B** = voice/signal encryption.
- [ ] **CAVE** = the algorithm that produces AUTHR, COUNT, and Private Long Code Mask.
- [ ] Network entities: **MS, BTS, BSC, PCF, PDSN, AAA, AN-AAA, HA, MSC, VLR, HLR, AC**.
- [ ] **PDSN** = IP gateway; **HA** = Mobile IP anchor; **AAA** = RADIUS/Diameter authentication.
- [ ] **Soft handoff** = make-before-break using two pilots simultaneously (improves security & QoS).
- [ ] **Power control** at 800 Hz update rate ÔÇö fundamental to CDMA capacity.
- [ ] Voice activity factor typically **╬▒ = 0.4** (user talks 40% of the time), giving 2.5├Ś capacity gain.
- [ ] **Frame duration** = 20 ms (voice); 26.67 ms = 1 PN short code period.
- [ ] **T_ADD** (pilot add threshold) = **-13 dB** Ec/Io typical; **T_DROP** = -15 dB.
- [ ] **Frame Error Rate (FER)** target ÔëĄ **1%** for voice after VSE (variable-rate vocoder).
- [ ] **Security triad** in CDMA2000 = *Confidentiality* (Private Long Code) + *Integrity* (CRC over traffic) + *Authentication* (CAVE/SSD).

---

**End of Module 1 Notes ÔÇö CDMA2000 Overview: Radio and Network Components**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Structure, 4G Features and Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Structure, 4G Features and Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Network Structure in Wireless (Cellular) Communications

### Formal Academic Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Structure (Cellular Network Topology):** A hierarchical arrangement of geographically distributed cells, each serviced by a Base Station (BTS/NodeB/eNodeB), interconnected through a backbone of switching centers (BSC/RNC, MSC, SGSN, GGSN) and the evolved packet core (EPC in 4G) to provide seamless, mobile, broadband wireless connectivity to end users (User Equipment ÔÇö UE).

The cellular architecture is a **frequency-reuse system** that divides a large service area (e.g., a city or country) into smaller hexagonal/irregular cells, each with its own base station. Adjacent cells use different frequency sub-bands to minimize co-channel interference (CCI), while non-adjacent cells may reuse the same frequencies.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ÔÇö The Beehive Postal Service ­čÉŁ**
> Imagine a giant honeycomb covering an entire country. Each small hexagonal cell is like a *neighborhood post office* (Base Station). When you (a mobile user) move from one neighborhood to another, your "mail carrier" (network) hands over your connection from one post office to the next seamlessly ÔÇö this is **handover**. The regional offices (BSC, RNC) coordinate the local post offices, and the central post office (MSC, EPC) connects you to the outside world (Internet/PSTN). This is exactly how a cellular network operates!

### Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)
- **Cell Radius:** Typically **0.1 km (picocell)** to **35 km (macrocell)**.
- **Frequency Reuse Factor (FRF):** Defined as $N = \frac{i^2 + ij + j^2}{1}$, where $i, j$ are shift parameters.
- **Handover (Handoff):** The process of transferring an active call/data session from one cell to another without interruption.
- **Standard Generations:** **1G (AMPS)**, **2G (GSM/GPRS)**, **3G (UMTS/CDMA2000)**, **4G (LTE/LTE-A)**, **5G (NR)**.

---

## 1.2 What is 4G? ÔÇö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4G (Fourth Generation Wireless):** Defined by the ITU-R under **IMT-Advanced** specification. It is an **all-IP packet-switched network** supporting peak data rates of **100 Mbps (high mobility)** and **1 Gbps (low mobility/pedestrian)**, with end-to-end QoS, low latency (Ôëł **10 ms** in radio access network), and seamless mobility across heterogeneous networks (2G/3G/Wi-Fi/LTE).

The two dominant 4G standards are:
- **3GPP LTE (Long Term Evolution)** / **LTE-Advanced** ÔÇö the global mainstream.
- **IEEE 802.16m (WiMAX 2)** ÔÇö less commercially successful.

### Conceptual Analogy ÔÇö 4G
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ÔÇö The Universal Data Highway ­čŤú´ŞĆ**
> Earlier generations (1GÔÇô3G) were like a *mixed road* where voice cars and data trucks shared the same bumpy lanes. **4G** is a brand-new **dedicated multi-lane superhighway built exclusively for data (IP packets)**, where voice is just another type of "data truck" carried via **VoLTE (Voice over LTE)**. Everything ÔÇö calls, video, IoT ÔÇö travels as IP packets on this unified highway.

### 1.3 Challenges of 4G Networks
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key 4G Challenges:** (1) **Security vulnerabilities** of all-IP architecture, (2) **Handover latency** across heterogeneous networks, (3) **Spectrum scarcity & cost**, (4) **QoS guarantees** for diverse traffic, (5) **Interference management** in OFDMA, (6) **Energy efficiency** of mobile devices, (7) **MIMO complexity** at UE side, (8) **Coverage holes** at cell edges.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Network Structure of a 4G (LTE) System

The 4G LTE architecture is composed of three major subsystems:

### A. User Equipment (UE)
- Mobile device (smartphone, tablet, dongle).
- Contains a **SIM card (USIM in 4G)** and the LTE modem.
- Assigned a unique **IMSI** (International Mobile Subscriber Identity) and temporary **GUTI** (Globally Unique Temporary Identifier) for security.

### B. Evolved UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network (E-UTRAN)
- Consists only of **eNodeB (evolved Node B)** base stations.
- **No centralized controller** (unlike 3G's RNC) ÔÇö radio resource management is **distributed** at each eNodeB.
- The X2 interface connects neighboring eNodeBs for fast handover.

### C. Evolved Packet Core (EPC) ÔÇö The All-IP Backbone
The EPC replaces the legacy circuit-switched core and contains:
- **MME (Mobility Management Entity):** Handles signaling, authentication, tracking area updates, bearer management.
- **SGW (Serving Gateway):** Routes and forwards user data packets; acts as a local mobility anchor.
- **PGW (PDN Gateway / Packet Data Network Gateway):** Provides connectivity from UE to external packet networks (Internet); assigns IP addresses, enforces policy, and performs packet filtering.
- **HSS (Home Subscriber Server):** Central database of subscriber information (similar to 2G/3G HLR + AuC).
- **PCRF (Policy and Charging Rules Function):** Manages QoS policies and charging rules.

### 2.2 Structured Logic ÔÇö Step-by-Step Operation

1. **UE Power-On & Attach:** UE sends an *Attach Request* to the eNodeB Ôćĺ forwarded to MME.
2. **Authentication & Security:** MME contacts HSS; performs **AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement)** protocol using symmetric keys stored in USIM and HSS.
3. **Default Bearer Establishment:** MME sets up a default EPS (Evolved Packet System) bearer between UE and PGW, assigning an IP address.
4. **Data Transfer:** User's IP packets travel: **UE Ôćĺ eNodeB Ôćĺ SGW Ôćĺ PGW Ôćĺ Internet**. Reverse path on the uplink.
5. **Handover:** When UE moves to a new eNodeB, signaling occurs over the **X2 interface** (or S1 if X2 fails); SGW/PGW remain the anchor point.
6. **Tracking Area Update (TAU):** Periodic location update to MME; lighter than 3G's Location/Routing Area Update.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a KTU exam-essential summary ÔÇö memorize it.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Value |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Frequency Reuse Factor (FRF) | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Co-channel Reuse Ratio (Q) | $Q = \sqrt{3N}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR) | $\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^n}{i_0}$ | dB |
| 4 | Shannon Channel Capacity (4G bound) | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/sec |
| 5 | 4G Peak Data Rate (Low mobility) | $R_{\text{peak}} = 1 \text{ Gbps}$ | Mbps |
| 6 | 4G Peak Data Rate (High mobility) | $R_{\text{peak}} = 100 \text{ Mbps}$ | Mbps |
| 7 | OFDMA Subcarrier Spacing (LTE) | $\Delta f = 15 \text{ kHz}$ | kHz |
| 8 | LTE Latency (User Plane) | $T_{\text{lat}} \approx 5 \text{ ms}$ | ms |
| 9 | LTE Latency (Control Plane) | $T_{\text{lat}} \approx 50{-}100 \text{ ms}$ | ms |
| 10 | LTE Max Bandwidth | $BW_{\max} = 20 \text{ MHz}$ (rel-8); **100 MHz** (LTE-A via CA) | MHz |
| 11 | Handover Margin | $H_m = \text{Pr}_{\text{threshold}} - \text{Pr}_{\text{min}}$ | dB |
| 12 | Handover Probability | $P_h = f(v, T_{\text{cell}})$ | ÔÇö |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Frequency Reuse Factor **N** directly determines network capacity. Operators in dense urban areas (e.g., Kochi, Mumbai) deploy **N = 1 (universal frequency reuse)** with aggressive interference-mitigation techniques (ICIC, CoMP). Rural areas use **N = 3 or 7** for lower CCI.

### 2.4 The 4G "Why" and "How"
- **Why all-IP?** Because packet switching is more efficient for bursty data; voice becomes "just another app" using VoLTE/IMS.
- **How is 1 Gbps achieved?** Through **OFDMA** (efficient spectrum use), **MIMO** (spatial multiplexing), **Carrier Aggregation (CA)** (wider bandwidth), and **higher-order modulation (64-QAM, 256-QAM in LTE-A)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Co-Channel Interference (CCI) in Cellular Networks

The dominant interference in cellular systems is **Co-Channel Interference (CCI)** from base stations in co-channel cells. We derive the **Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR)** step-by-step.

### Step 1 ÔÇö Define the Received Signal Power
Using the **log-distance path loss model**, the received signal power at the mobile from the serving base station located at distance $D$ is:

$$P_r = P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}$$

where:
- $P_t$ = transmitted power (W)
- $K$ = constant accounting for antenna gain, wavelength
- $D$ = distance between UE and serving BS
- $n$ = path loss exponent (typically $n = 3{-}4$ for urban)

### Step 2 ÔÇö Sum the Co-Channel Interference
Assume there are $i_0$ co-channel interfering cells, each at distance $D_i$ from the UE. The total interference power is:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{i_0} P_t \cdot K \cdot D_i^{-n}$$

### Step 3 ÔÇö Form the SIR Ratio
Taking the ratio of received signal to total interference:

$$\text{SIR} = \frac{P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}}{\sum_{i=1}^{i_0} P_t \cdot K \cdot D_i^{-n}}$$

### Step 4 ÔÇö Simplify
The $P_t$ and $K$ cancel out (assuming equal transmit power across all BS):

$$\text{SIR} = \frac{D^{-n}}{\sum_{i=1}^{i_0} D_i^{-n}}$$

### Step 5 ÔÇö Approximation for Large Cell Clusters
For a hexagonal cluster of size $N$, all co-channel cells are at approximately the same distance $D_i \approx D_c$ (the co-channel distance). Then:

$$\text{SIR} \approx \frac{D^{-n}}{i_0 \cdot D_c^{-n}} = \frac{1}{i_0} \cdot \left(\frac{D_c}{D}\right)^{n}$$

### Step 6 ÔÇö Express in Co-Channel Reuse Ratio
Define $Q = \dfrac{D_c}{D} = \sqrt{3N}$, so:

$$\boxed{\text{SIR} = \frac{1}{i_0} \cdot (Q)^{n} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{i_0}}$$

### Step 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)
**Problem:** A 4G LTE system uses a cluster size **N = 7** with **$i_0 = 6$** co-channel interferers. The path loss exponent is **n = 4**. Calculate the SIR in dB and check if it meets the minimum QoS threshold of **18 dB**.

**Step 1:** Compute $Q$:

$$Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583$$

**Step 2:** Compute $Q^n$:

$$Q^4 = (4.583)^4$$

Computing step by step:

$$4.583^2 = 21.0$$

$$4.583^4 = (21.0)^2 = 441.0$$

**Step 3:** Compute SIR (linear):

$$\text{SIR} = \frac{441.0}{6} = 73.5$$

**Step 4:** Convert to dB:

$$\text{SIR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) \approx 18.66 \text{ dB}$$

**Conclusion:** The SIR is **Ôëł 18.66 dB**, which **satisfies** the 18 dB threshold with a small margin of **0.66 dB**. Ôťů

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why LTE operators prefer **N = 1** (universal reuse) ÔÇö they use **advanced receivers, ICIC (Inter-Cell Interference Coordination), and CoMP (Coordinated Multi-Point)** to overcome CCI rather than using larger clusters, which waste spectrum.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Generations (KTU High-Yield)

| Feature | 1G (AMPS) | 2G (GSM) | 3G (UMTS) | 4G (LTE/LTE-A) |
|---------|-----------|----------|-----------|----------------|
| Multiple Access | FDMA | TDMA + FDMA | CDMA / WCDMA | **OFDMA / SC-FDMA** |
| Switching | Circuit | Circuit (+GPRS packet) | Packet + Circuit | **All-IP Packet** |
| Peak Data Rate | 2.4 kbps | 64 kbps ÔÇô 384 kbps | 2 Mbps | **100 Mbps ÔÇô 1 Gbps** |
| Latency | N/A | ~500 ms | ~100 ms | **~10 ms** |
| Voice Tech | Analog | Circuit Voice | Circuit / VoIP | **VoLTE (IMS)** |
| Core Network | AMPS | NSS | UTRAN + CN | **EPC (Evolved Packet Core)** |
| Security | None (analog) | A5/1, A5/2 (weak) | KASUMI | **EPS-AKA, SNOW 3G, ZUC** |
| Handover | Hard | Hard | Soft / Softer | **X2-based fast handover** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram: 4G LTE Network Architecture (E-UTRAN + EPC)

Below is a high-fidelity **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping the major components of a 4G LTE network. This is the most important diagram for the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UE["UE\nUser Equipment\nMobile Phone, Tablet, IoT"]
    eNB1["eNodeB 1\nBase Station A\nRadio Access"]
    eNB2["eNodeB 2\nBase Station B\nRadio Access"]
    MME["MME\nMobility Management\nEntity"]
    SGW["SGW\nServing Gateway\nLocal Anchor"]
    PGW["PGW\nPDN Gateway\nIP Address Allocator"]
    HSS["HSS\nHome Subscriber Server\nDB of Subscribers"]
    PCRF["PCRF\nPolicy and Charging\nRules Function"]
    INT["INTERNET\nExternal IP Networks"]
    IMS["IMS\nIP Multimedia Subsystem\nVoLTE Call Server"]

    UE -- Uu Interface\nRadio Air Interface --> eNB1
    UE -. Handover X2 .-> eNB2
    eNB1 -- S1-U\nUser Plane --> SGW
    eNB2 -- S1-U\nUser Plane --> SGW
    eNB1 -- S1-MME\nControl Plane --> MME
    eNB2 -- S1-MME\nControl Plane --> MME
    eNB1 -. X2 Interface\nHandover .- eNB2
    MME -- Diameter / S6a --> HSS
    MME -- S11 --> SGW
    SGW -- S5 / S8 --> PGW
    PGW -- SGi --> INT
    PGW -- Rx Interface --> PCRF
    PCRF -- Gx Interface --> PGW
    MME -- Diameter --> IMS
    PGW -- VoLTE Bearer --> IMS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram (KTU Exam Tip):** Memorize the **three logical planes** ÔÇö (1) **User Plane** (UE Ôćĺ eNodeB Ôćĺ SGW Ôćĺ PGW Ôćĺ Internet), (2) **Control Plane** (UE Ôćĺ eNodeB Ôćĺ MME Ôćĺ HSS/PCRF), and (3) **Handover Plane** (eNodeB Ôćö eNodeB via **X2 interface**).

## 4.2 Diagram: 4G Protocol Stack ÔÇö User & Control Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UP["USER PLANE (Data)"]
        direction TB
        U1["Application Layer\nHTTP, RTP, VoLTE"]
        U2["Transport Layer\nTCP / UDP"]
        U3["Internet Layer\nIP"]
        U4["PDCP\nPacket Data Convergence"]
        U5["RLC\nRadio Link Control"]
        U6["MAC\nMedium Access Control"]
        U7["PHY\nPhysical Layer OFDMA"]
    end
    subgraph CP["CONTROL PLANE (Signaling)"]
        direction TB
        C1["NAS\nNon-Access Stratum"]
        C2["RRC\nRadio Resource Control"]
        C3["PDCP\nAlso Encrypts"]
        C4["RLC"]
        C5["MAC"]
        C6["PHY"]
    end
    U1 --> U2 --> U3 --> U4 --> U5 --> U6 --> U7
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Handover Sequence in 4G (X2-based)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant UE as UE
    participant SeNB as Source eNodeB
    participant TeNB as Target eNodeB
    participant MME as MME
    participant SGW as Serving Gateway
    Note over UE,SeNB: UE measures RSRP from neighbors
    UE->>SeNB: Measurement Report
    SeNB->>SeNB: Handover Decision
    SeNB->>TeNB: Handover Request (X2)
    TeNB->>SeNB: Handover Request Ack
    SeNB->>UE: RRC Connection Reconfiguration
    UE->>TeNB: Random Access Preamble
    TeNB->>UE: RA Response + Timing Advance
    UE->>TeNB: RRC Connection Reconfiguration Complete
    TeNB->>MME: Path Switch Request
    MME->>SGW: Modify Bearer Request
    SGW->>TeNB: Modify Bearer Response
    MME->>TeNB: Path Switch Request Ack
    Note over UE,TeNB: Data path is now UE -> TeNB -> SGW -> PGW
```

## 4.4 Diagram: 4G Security Architecture (EPS-AKA)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UE["UE + USIM\nStores K and IMSI"]
    MME["MME\nAuthentication Server"]
    HSS["HSS\nAuC Database\nStores K per subscriber"]
    ENB["eNodeB\nCiphering / Integrity"]

    UE -- 1. Attach Request (IMSI) --> MME
    MME -- 2. Authentication Data Request --> HSS
    HSS -- 3. Authentication Vector (AV) --> MME
    MME -- 4. Authentication Challenge (AUTN, RAND) --> UE
    UE -- 5. Auth Response (RES) --> MME
    MME -- 6. Verify RES = XRES --> MME
    MME -- 7. NAS Security Mode Command --> UE
    UE -- 8. NAS Security Mode Complete --> MME
    MME -- 9. AS Security to eNodeB --> ENB
    ENB -- 10. AS Security Mode Command --> UE
    UE -- 11. AS Security Mode Complete --> ENB
    Note["Result: Integrity & Ciphering keys K_NAS_int, K_NAS_enc, K_RRC_int, K_UP_enc are derived"]
```

---
