# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A ÔÇö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ÔÇô May 2024]
**List any three key features of 4G networks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **All-IP packet-switched architecture** ÔÇö voice and data both carried as IP packets via EPC.
2. **High peak data rate** ÔÇö up to **100 Mbps** in high-mobility and **1 Gbps** in low-mobility.
3. **Low latency** ÔÇö radio access network latency as low as **~10 ms** (suitable for real-time gaming, VoLTE).
4. *(Optional 4th)* Seamless **heterogeneous handover** (LTE Ôćö Wi-Fi Ôćö 3G) and **OFDMA + MIMO** for spectral efficiency.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023]
**What is the role of eNodeB in 4G LTE architecture?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **eNodeB (evolved Node B)** is the sole base-station element in the 4G E-UTRAN.
- It performs **radio resource management (RRM)**, **scheduling**, **ciphering/integrity protection** of user data, and **IP header compression**.
- It interfaces with the UE over the **Uu air interface** and with the EPC (MME/SGW) over the **S1 interface**, and with neighboring eNodeBs over the **X2 interface** for fast handovers.

---

## PART B ÔÇö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ÔÇö Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) ÔÇö OPTION 1
**[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023 Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a 4G LTE network** showing all major components (UE, eNodeB, MME, SGW, PGW, HSS, PCRF). Describe the **user plane** and **control plane** paths. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **co-channel interference ratio (SIR)** for a hexagonal cellular system and compute the SIR in dB for a cluster size **N = 3**, path loss exponent **n = 4**, with **$i_0 = 6$** co-channel interferers. Comment on whether the result is acceptable for 4G voice traffic (threshold = 15 dB). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(a) ÔÇö Architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1 ÔÇö UE (1 mark):**
The User Equipment is the mobile device containing the **USIM** (Universal Subscriber Identity Module). It is identified by **IMSI** and assigned a temporary **GUTI** for security. It hosts the LTE protocol stack from PHY up to NAS.

**Step 2 ÔÇö eNodeB / E-UTRAN (1 mark):**
The **eNodeB** is the only network element in E-UTRAN. Unlike 3G, there is no RNC ÔÇö all radio functions (scheduling, HARQ, RRM) are handled **locally** at the eNodeB. This reduces latency. eNodeBs communicate over the **X2 interface** for handover and ICIC.

**Step 3 ÔÇö MME (1 mark):**
The **Mobility Management Entity** is the key control-plane node. It handles:
- NAS signaling (Attach, TAU, Service Request)
- Authentication via **EPS-AKA** with HSS
- Bearer establishment and modification
- Idle-state mobility tracking

**Step 4 ÔÇö SGW (1 mark):**
The **Serving Gateway** is the local user-plane anchor. All IP packets from/to the eNodeB pass through the SGW. During handover, the SGW remains the anchor, reducing core-network signaling.

**Step 5 ÔÇö PGW (1 mark):**
The **PDN Gateway** is the **exit point to the Internet**. It allocates IP addresses (typically IPv4 or IPv6), enforces **policy rules**, performs **packet filtering**, and is the anchor for **inter-3GPP mobility**.

**Step 6 ÔÇö HSS + PCRF (1 mark):**
- **HSS** (Home Subscriber Server) = master database of subscriber credentials, location, and service profiles (combined HLR + AuC of 2G/3G).
- **PCRF** (Policy and Charging Rules Function) = decides QoS and charging rules for each bearer; communicates with PGW via the **Gx interface**.

**Step 7 ÔÇö User Plane vs Control Plane Path (1 mark):**
- **User Plane:** UE Ôćĺ eNodeB Ôćĺ SGW Ôćĺ PGW Ôćĺ Internet (carries user IP packets, no signaling).
- **Control Plane:** UE Ôćĺ eNodeB Ôćĺ MME Ôćĺ HSS/PCRF (carries NAS signaling, security setup, bearer control).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference:** Section 4.1 diagram above provides the visual for this answer.

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(b) ÔÇö SIR Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 ÔÇö Define the model (1 mark):**
Using the log-distance path loss model, received power is $P_r = P_t K D^{-n}$ where $D$ is the BS-to-UE distance, $n$ is path loss exponent.

**Step 2 ÔÇö Sum co-channel interference (1 mark):**
Total interference from $i_0$ co-channel cells at distances $D_i$:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{i_0} P_t K D_i^{-n}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Form SIR (1 mark):**

$$\text{SIR} = \frac{D^{-n}}{\sum_{i=1}^{i_0} D_i^{-n}}$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Approximation for hexagonal cluster (1 mark):**
All $i_0$ co-channel cells are at approximately equal distance $D_c = Q \cdot D$, where $Q = \sqrt{3N}$ is the co-channel reuse ratio.

$$\text{SIR} \approx \frac{1}{i_0} \left(\frac{D_c}{D}\right)^{n} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{i_0}$$

**Step 5 ÔÇö Numerical substitution (1 mark):**
With $N = 3$, $n = 4$, $i_0 = 6$:

$$Q = \sqrt{3 \times 3} = \sqrt{9} = 3$$

$$Q^n = 3^4 = 81$$

**Step 6 ÔÇö Compute SIR (linear) (1 mark):**

$$\text{SIR} = \frac{81}{6} = 13.5$$

**Step 7 ÔÇö Convert to dB and conclude (1 mark):**

$$\text{SIR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(13.5) \approx 11.30 \text{ dB}$$

**Conclusion:** The computed SIR is **11.30 dB**, which is **below** the 15 dB threshold required for 4G voice traffic. Hence, **N = 3 is not acceptable** for high-quality VoLTE service; the operator should use **N Ôëą 7** or enable **ICIC/CoMP** to mitigate CCI. ÔŁî

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) ÔÇö OPTION 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam ÔÇô June 2024 Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **key features and challenges of 4G networks** in detail. How does 4G overcome the limitations of 3G? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a diagram, explain the **EPS-AKA authentication procedure** in 4G. Discuss why the temporary identity **GUTI** is used in place of IMSI for normal signaling. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(a) ÔÇö 4G Features & Challenges (7 marks)

**Key Features of 4G (4 marks):**
1. **All-IP Network:** Unlike 3G's hybrid circuit + packet core, 4G uses a **single packet-switched EPC**, simplifying architecture and reducing CAPEX/OPEX. Voice is delivered via **VoLTE / IMS**.
2. **Higher Data Rates:** **100 Mbps (high mobility)** and **1 Gbps (low mobility)** via OFDMA, MIMO, carrier aggregation, and 64-QAM/256-QAM modulation.
3. **Lower Latency:** **~5 ms** user-plane and **~50 ms** control-plane ÔÇö enabling real-time gaming, industrial automation, and tactile Internet.
4. **OFDMA & SC-FDMA:** OFDMA on downlink (high spectral efficiency) and SC-FDMA on uplink (lower PAPR Ôćĺ better battery life).
5. **MIMO & Beamforming:** Multi-antenna techniques multiply capacity without additional spectrum.
6. **Heterogeneous Networks (HetNets):** Seamless integration with 3G, Wi-Fi, and small cells.

**Challenges of 4G (3 marks):**
1. **Security Risks of All-IP:** All-IP architecture is exposed to Internet threats (DoS, MITM, IP spoofing). The 4G security perimeter is the USIM; once the long-term key K is compromised, the network collapses.
2. **Handover Latency in HetNets:** Vertical handovers (4G Ôćö Wi-Fi) can be slow; X2-based handovers may fail across vendor boundaries.
3. **Coverage Holes & Cell-Edge Performance:** Higher frequencies (e.g., 3.5 GHz) suffer from path loss; relays and small cells are needed.
4. **Spectrum Scarcity & Cost:** Auction prices for LTE bands are extremely high; refarming 2G/3G bands is politically complex.
5. **Energy Efficiency:** Continuous radio monitoring drains UE battery; **DRX/eDRX** mechanisms partially address this.
6. **QoS Complexity:** Multiple bearers (default + dedicated) with different QCI (QoS Class Identifiers) need careful policy management.

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(b) ÔÇö EPS-AKA & GUTI (7 marks)

**EPS-AKA Procedure (5 marks):**
1. UE sends **Attach Request** with **IMSI** (or GUTI) to MME.
2. MME fetches **Authentication Vectors (AV = RAND || AUTN || XRES || K_ASME)** from HSS/AuC.
3. MME sends **RAND and AUTN** to UE.
4. The USIM in UE verifies AUTN (using its copy of K) and computes **RES = f2(K, RAND)** and derives **K_ASME**.
5. UE sends **RES** back to MME; MME verifies **RES = XRES**.
6. Mutual authentication is complete. Both sides derive **K_NAS_int, K_NAS_enc** (control-plane keys) and **K_RRC_int, K_RRC_enc, K_UP_enc** (user-plane keys).
7. **NAS Security Mode Command** and **AS Security Mode Command** activate ciphering and integrity protection.

**Why GUTI is used (2 marks):**
- The **IMSI** is a **permanent, globally unique identity** of the subscriber. If sent in plaintext on every signaling message, an attacker can **eavesdrop on the air interface** and **track the user's location** (IMSI catchers / Stingrays exploit this in 2G/3G).
- The **GUTI (Globally Unique Temporary Identifier)** is **periodically reassigned** by the MME and is only meaningful within a limited tracking area. It is used for all routine signaling (TAU, Service Request, paging) to **protect subscriber identity and location privacy**.
- The IMSI is only sent in cleartext during the **initial Attach** when GUTI is unknown, after which GUTI is allocated and used thereafter.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse the planes** (user vs. control) ÔÇö be explicit. A common mistake is mixing up **S1-U** (user plane to SGW) with **S1-MME** (control plane to MME). Marks are lost in part (a) for this.
> - In numerical SIR problems, students often **forget to convert to dB** at the end. Even a correct linear value without dB conversion loses 1 mark.
> - In EPS-AKA, students frequently **omit the mutual authentication** aspect ÔÇö both UE and network authenticate each other; it's not one-way.
> - When explaining 4G challenges, do **not** list only technical challenges; also mention **economic / regulatory** ones (spectrum auction costs).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ­čôŁ

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ÔÇö KTU Module 1: Network Structure, 4G Features & Challenges**

- ÔşÉ **Cellular Network Hierarchy:** UE Ôćĺ eNodeB Ôćĺ EPC (MME/SGW/PGW/HSS/PCRF) Ôćĺ Internet.
- ÔşÉ **Three Planes:** **User plane** (data), **Control plane** (signaling), **Handover plane** (X2).
- ÔşÉ **Interfaces:** Uu (UEÔÇôeNodeB), S1-MME, S1-U, S5/S8 (SGWÔÇôPGW), S6a (MMEÔÇôHSS), X2 (eNodeBÔÇôeNodeB), SGi (PGWÔÇôInternet).
- ÔşÉ **4G Peak Rates:** **100 Mbps** (mobile) and **1 Gbps** (stationary). **Latency: ~5ÔÇô10 ms.**
- ÔşÉ **Multiple Access:** **OFDMA downlink**, **SC-FDMA uplink** (low PAPR).
- ÔşÉ **MIMO + Carrier Aggregation + 256-QAM** are the 4G capacity boosters.
- ÔşÉ **FRF Formula:** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$; Co-channel Reuse Ratio: $Q = \sqrt{3N}$.
- ÔşÉ **SIR Formula:** $\text{SIR} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{i_0}$; threshold for 4G Ôëł 15ÔÇô18 dB.
- ÔşÉ **4G Challenges:** All-IP security, handover latency, spectrum cost, cell-edge performance, energy efficiency, QoS complexity.
- ÔşÉ **EPS-AKA:** Mutual authentication, derives **K_ASME**, then K_NAS_int, K_NAS_enc, K_RRC_int, K_UP_enc.
- ÔşÉ **GUTI:** Temporary identity for privacy; IMSI used only at initial Attach.
- ÔşÉ **eNodeB = D-RNC:** All RRM functions are distributed; no centralized controller in 4G.
- ÔşÉ **VoLTE = Voice over IMS:** Replaces 2G/3G circuit-switched voice; runs entirely on the LTE packet core.

topics.title: Technology Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Technology Path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Technology Path (in Wireless Networks)** refers to the chronological trajectory of wireless communication standards, protocols, and architectures from first-generation (1G) analog cellular systems to current and emerging sixth-generation (6G) intelligent networks. It documents the migration of modulation schemes, multiple-access techniques, switching paradigms, and ÔÇö most critically from a security standpoint ÔÇö the cryptographic and authentication mechanisms layered onto each generation.

In the context of **PECCT862 (Security in Wireless Networks)**, the *Technology Path* is not merely a historical timeline. It is a **threat-surface evolution map**: every leap in spectral efficiency, capacity, or service class (voice Ôćĺ data Ôćĺ multimedia Ôćĺ IoT/URLLC) introduces a new attack vector, a new trust boundary, and a new set of cryptographic primitives that defenders must master.

The four orthogonal dimensions of any wireless technology path are:

1. **Air Interface** ÔÇö modulation, coding, multiple access (FDMA / TDMA / CDMA / OFDMA / NOMA).
2. **Core Network Architecture** ÔÇö circuit-switched Ôćĺ packet-switched Ôćĺ all-IP Ôćĺ cloud-native / SBA (Service-Based Architecture).
3. **Security Stack** ÔÇö from *plaintext A-key* (1G/AMPS) to *5G-AKA / SUCI / SUPI* and quantum-safe key derivation.
4. **Service Class** ÔÇö voice (1G/2G) Ôćĺ messaging & low-rate data (2.5G/3G) Ôćĺ mobile broadband (4G) Ôćĺ eMBB / mMTC / URLLC (5G) Ôćĺ AI-native sensing (6G).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ÔÇö The "Generational Family Tree"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of the wireless technology path as the evolution of **postal mail in a country**.
> - **1G** is a *town crier shouting down a lane* (analog, anyone can listen).
> - **2G** is *sealed envelopes with a wax stamp* (digital ciphering begins).
> - **3G** is a *registered post with signature on delivery* (mutual authentication).
> - **4G** is *a courier company that encrypts every parcel and tracks it online* (all-IP, IPSec/TLS).
> - **5G** is *a digital notary using blockchain-anchored identity* (SUPI/SUCI, network slicing).
> - **6G** will be an *AI courier that predicts and reroutes attacks in real time* (AI-native security, quantum-safe).

Each generation **inherits** the strengths of its predecessor and **patches** the discovered weaknesses ÔÇö but every patch also creates a new seam for adversaries to exploit.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Meaning | First Appeared |
|------|---------|----------------|
| **AMPS** | Advanced Mobile Phone System | 1G (1983) |
| **GSM** | Global System for Mobile Communications | 2G (1991) |
| **UMTS** | Universal Mobile Telecommunications System | 3G (2001) |
| **LTE** | Long Term Evolution | 4G (2008) |
| **NR** | New Radio (5G air interface) | 5G (2019) |
| **AKA** | Authentication and Key Agreement | 3G onward |
| **SUPI/SUCI** | Subscription Permanent Identifier / Concealed Identifier | 5G |
| **SBA** | Service-Based Architecture | 5G Core |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic:** *"A Guru Uses Little Nerves Safely"* ÔÇö **A**MPS, **G**SM, **U**MTS, **L**TE, **N**R, **S**BA. Memorize this sequence; almost every 14-mark question in Module 1 has appeared in some form around this order.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generation-by-Generation Technical Dissection

### 2.1.1 Generation 1G (Analog Cellular, ~1980s)

- **Multiple Access:** FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access).
- **Modulation:** Analog FM (Frequency Modulation).
- **Switching:** Circuit-switched core.
- **Security:** *Essentially none.* Conversations were broadcast in clear on a tunable RF channel. The infamous **IMSI-catcher (stingray)** attack is a direct descendant of this weakness.
- **Standards:** AMPS (USA), NMT (Nordic), TACS (UK), C-450 (Germany).

### 2.1.2 Generation 2G ÔÇö *The Digital Dawn* (GSM Era, 1991)

- **Multiple Access:** TDMA + FDMA hybrid.
- **Modulation:** GMSK (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying).
- **Security Primitives Introduced:**
  - **A3** ÔÇö subscriber authentication (challenge-response using a 128-bit **Ki**).
  - **A5/1, A5/2** ÔÇö stream ciphers for over-the-air confidentiality.
  - **A8** ÔÇö session key **Kc** generation.
- **Known Vulnerabilities:**
  - **A5/2** broken in real time (2003, GoldbergÔÇôWagner).
  - **A5/1** broken via rainbow tables (Kr├ĄmerÔÇôWeis, ~2009).
  - **COMP128** hash of Ki cracked in seconds on a laptop.
- **KTU Hook:** The A5 family is **the** classic 14-mark question on stream-cipher weakness.

### 2.1.3 Generation 2.5G / 2.75G ÔÇö GPRS & EDGE

- **GPRS (General Packet Radio Service):** first always-on packet data; theoretical **115 kbps**.
- **EDGE (Enhanced Data rates for GSM Evolution):** introduces 8-PSK; theoretical **384 kbps**.
- **Security delta:** GPRS introduces **GEA1/GEA2/GEA3** (GPRS Encryption Algorithms). GEA1 and GEA2 were classified but publicly broken by 2011.

### 2.1.4 Generation 3G ÔÇö UMTS / IMT-2000 (2001)

- **Multiple Access:** **W-CDMA** (Wideband Code Division Multiple Access) ÔÇö 5 MHz carrier.
- **Switching:** hybrid; ultimately evolved to all-IP core.
- **Security Architecture (3GPP TS 33.102):**
  - **MILENAGE** algorithm set (f1ÔÇôf5).
  - **AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement)** with mutual authentication ÔÇö defeats the **false-base-station** attack that plagued GSM.
  - **CK / IK** (Ciphering Key / Integrity Key) ÔÇö confidentiality **and** integrity, the first time both were mandated.
- **KTU Hook:** "Why does 3G defeat the IMSI catcher but 5G goes further?" ÔÇö Answer requires explaining **SUPI Ôćĺ SUCI concealment**.

### 2.1.5 Generation 3.5G / 3.9G ÔÇö HSPA & LTE

- **HSPA / HSPA+:** up to **42 Mbps** downlink via 16-QAM/64-QAM.
- **LTE (Long Term Evolution, 3GPP Release 8):**
  - **OFDMA** in downlink, **SC-FDMA** in uplink (lower PAPR Ôćĺ better battery).
  - Variable bandwidth: **1.4 / 3 / 5 / 10 / 15 / 20 MHz**.
  - All-IP core (EPC ÔÇö Evolved Packet Core).
  - Security built around **EPS-AKA** and the **SNOW 3G / ZUC / AES** cipher suite.

### 2.1.6 Generation 4G / IMT-Advanced (2010s)

- **Peak Data Rate:** **1 Gbps** (low mobility), **100 Mbps** (high mobility) ÔÇö per ITU-R M.1822.
- **Latency Target:** **10 ms** one-way (radio access).
- **Security Primitives:** EPS-AKA, NAS (Non-Access Stratum) and AS (Access Stratum) security mode commands, **PDCP** ciphering with SNOW 3G or AES-128.

### 2.1.7 Generation 5G / IMT-2020 (2019 onward)

- **Three Service Classes:**
  - **eMBB** ÔÇö enhanced Mobile BroadBand (peak **20 Gbps** downlink).
  - **mMTC** ÔÇö massive Machine-Type Communications (10ÔüÂ devices/km┬▓).
  - **URLLC** ÔÇö Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications (**1 ms** air-interface latency, 99.999 % reliability).
- **Architectural Shift ÔÇö SBA (Service-Based Architecture):** network functions (AMF, SMF, UPF, AUSF, UDM) communicate over HTTP/2 + **TLS 1.3**.
- **Security Innovations:**
  - **SUPI / SUCI** ÔÇö the IMSI is never sent in clear. SUCI = ECIES-encrypted form of SUPI under the home network's public key.
  - **5G-AKA** and **EAP-AKAÔÇ▓** ÔÇö formal proofs of security via the **ProVerif / Tamarin** models.
  - **Network slicing** isolation per tenant.
  - **SEAF (Security Anchor Function)** in the visited network ÔÇö avoids forwarding AVs across operators.

### 2.1.8 Generation 6G (2030 Horizon)

- **Vision:** AI-native, sub-THz (100ÔÇô300 GHz), integrated sensing & communication (ISAC), quantum-safe key distribution.
- **Security Posture:** zero-touch trust, post-quantum cryptography (PQC ÔÇö CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium), digital twin of the radio channel.

## 2.2 The Migration Strategy ÔÇö Defense-in-Depth Across Generations

Every generational handoff has **three simultaneous migrations** occurring:

1. **Air-interface migration** ÔÇö coexistence of legacy frames (e.g., 5G NSA still anchors to 4G LTE for control plane).
2. **Core-network migration** ÔÇö e.g., 5G NSA uses EPC, 5G SA uses 5GC.
3. **Security-stack migration** ÔÇö backward compatibility is *forced* by regulators, so old weak ciphers (A5/1) often linger long after being broken.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam standard** and have appeared (in equivalent form) in KTU model papers. Master the units and boundary conditions ÔÇö that is where the marks live.

| # | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Engineering Utility | Units |
|---|---------|-----------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $C$ = Shannon capacity, $B$ = bandwidth, SNR = signal-to-noise ratio | Theoretical upper bound on spectral efficiency | bits/s |
| 2 | $\text{SE} = \dfrac{R_b}{B} = \log_2(M)$ | $R_b$ = bit rate, $B$ = bandwidth, $M$ = constellation size | Spectral efficiency of $M$-ary modulation | bits/s/Hz |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Throughput}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{Throughput}_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | $\eta$ = efficiency ratio | Real-world system performance vs. theoretical | % |
| 4 | $\text{Path Loss (free space)} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | $d$ = distance (km), $f$ = frequency (MHz) | dB loss over a line-of-sight link | dB |
| 5 | $\text{Cell Capacity} = \dfrac{N_{\text{ch}} \cdot \text{Erlangs per user}}{N_{\text{users}}}$ | $N_{\text{ch}}$ = number of channels | Erlang B table usage | Erlangs |
| 6 | $N_{\text{max devices}} = \dfrac{B}{\text{BW}_{\text{per device}}}$ for $mMTC$ | $B$ = total bandwidth | 5G massive-IoT dimensioning | devices |
| 7 | $\text{Latency (URLLC)} \le 1 \text{ ms}$ | air-interface one-way | 5G URLLC budget | ms |
| 8 | $\text{Reliability (URLLC)} = 1 - 10^{-5}$ | 99.999 % packet success | Industrial-IoT grade | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Key length} \ge 128$ bits for AES; $\ge 256$ bits for 5G SUPI concealment | NIST SP 800-131A | Cryptographic floor | bits |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** When asked *"Compare 4G and 5G security"*, always anchor to the **three guarantees**: (i) subscriber identity confidentiality (SUCI), (ii) mutual authentication (5G-AKA), and (iii) service-based isolation. Vague answers score ÔëĄ 4 / 14; anchored answers routinely score 11ÔÇô13 / 14.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ÔÇö Shannon Capacity Across Generations

We derive the *theoretical* maximum data rate for each generation's representative bandwidth and operating SNR, then compare to the *achieved* peak rate. This is the most common 14-mark analytical question.

> **Assumptions (declared for board-credit):**
> - Operating SNR for outdoor macrocell Ôëł **10 dB** (linear = 10).
> - Operating SNR for indoor small cell Ôëł **20 dB** (linear = 100).

### 3.1.1 Worked Example: 4G LTE

Given: $B = 20 \text{ MHz}$, $\text{SNR} = 10$ (linear).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Shannon}} &= B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\
&= 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 10) \\
&= 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(11)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute $\log_2(11)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_2(11) &= \dfrac{\log_{10}(11)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\
&= \dfrac{1.04139}{0.30103} \\
&\approx 3.4594 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Shannon}} &= 20 \times 10^{6} \times 3.4594 \\
&= 69.188 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comparison with LTE peak (Ôëł 100 Mbps in 20 MHz, Category 4):** Shannon gives Ôëł **69 Mbps**; LTE uses higher-order QAM and MIMO to *exceed* Shannon for a single SISO link by exploiting multi-stream spatial multiplexing. This is the heart of the "Shannon is per-link" caveat.

### 3.1.2 Worked Example: 5G NR (eMBB)

Given: $B = 100 \text{ MHz}$ (FR1), $\text{SNR} = 100$ (indoor hotspot).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Shannon}} &= 100 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 100) \\
&= 100 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(101)
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_2(101) &= \dfrac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\
&= \dfrac{2.00432}{0.30103} \\
&\approx 6.6582 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Shannon}} &= 100 \times 10^{6} \times 6.6582 \\
&= 665.82 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Insight:** With **4├Ś4 MIMO** and 256-QAM, 5G NR in 100 MHz actually achieves Ôëł **1.5 ÔÇô 2 Gbps** in practice ÔÇö exceeding the SISO Shannon bound by a factor of Ôëł 3 via spatial multiplexing gain.

### 3.1.3 Comparative Table ÔÇö Generations vs. Shannon

| Generation | Bandwidth $B$ | SNR (lin.) | Shannon Cap. $C$ | Practical Peak | $\eta$ |
|------------|---------------|-----------|------------------|----------------|--------|
| 2G GSM | 200 kHz | 10 | $\approx 691$ kbps | 9.6 kbps (GPRS slot) | Ôëł 1.4 % |
| 3G W-CDMA | 5 MHz | 10 | $\approx 17.3$ Mbps | 384 kbps (R99) | Ôëł 2.2 % |
| 4G LTE (SISO) | 20 MHz | 10 | $\approx 69$ Mbps | 100 Mbps (Cat-4) | 145 % (via MIMO/QAM) |
| 5G NR (SISO) | 100 MHz | 100 | $\approx 666$ Mbps | 1.5 Gbps (4├Ś4 MIMO) | 225 % |
| 5G NR (mmWave) | 800 MHz | 100 | $\approx 5.3$ Gbps | 7 Gbps (FR2 + 8├Ś8) | 132 % |

> **Engineering note:** "$\eta > 100\%$" is *not* a violation of Shannon; it reflects exploitation of spatial degrees of freedom (MIMO) and richer constellations that the basic formula does not capture.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ÔÇö Security Strength (Bit-Security vs. Cipher)

The "**effective security**" of a generation can be quantified as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{eff}} &= \min\bigl(K_{\text{cipher}},\, K_{\text{auth}}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_{\text{cipher}}$ is the effective key strength (bits) and $K_{\text{auth}}$ is the authentication-tag strength. A *broken* cipher (e.g., A5/1) has $K_{\text{cipher}} = 0$.

| Generation | Cipher | $K_{\text{cipher}}$ (bits) | $K_{\text{auth}}$ (bits) | $S_{\text{eff}}$ |
|------------|--------|---------------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| 1G AMPS | None | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2G GSM | A5/1 | $\approx 40$ (academic) Ôćĺ **0** (practical) | 0 (no integrity) | 0 |
| 3G UMTS | KASUMI (128) + f9 MAC | 128 | 128 | 128 |
| 4G LTE | SNOW 3G / AES-128 | 128 | 128 | 128 |
| 5G NR | AES-256 / SNOW 3G / ZUC | 128 ÔÇô 256 | 128 | 128 ÔÇô 256 |
| 6G (target) | Kyber-1024 + AES-256 | 256 | 256 | 256 (PQC) |

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ÔÇö URLLC Latency Budget

5G URLLC mandates a **1 ms one-way user-plane latency**. The budget decomposes as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{URLLC}} &= T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{re-tx}} + T_{\text{queue}} \le 1 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Typical split:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{tx}} &\approx 0.1 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{mini-slot OFDM at 30 kHz SCS}) \\
T_{\text{prop}} &\approx 0.03 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{10 km fibre-equivalent}) \\
T_{\text{proc}} &\approx 0.5 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{gNB L1 + L2}) \\
T_{\text{re-tx}} &\approx 0.2 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{single HARQ round}) \\
T_{\text{queue}} &\approx 0.17 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{leftover budget}) \\
\hline
\text{Total} &\approx 1.00 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often quote "1 ms" without showing the decomposition. Showing the budget tree **always** scores the 4-method marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ÔÇö 4G vs. 5G (Board-Exam Gold)

| Dimension | 4G LTE | 5G NR | Security Delta |
|-----------|--------|-------|----------------|
| Peak rate | 1 Gbps | 20 Gbps | N/A |
| Latency | 10 ms | 1 ms (URLLC) | Faster revocation |
| Identity sent | IMSI in clear | SUPI concealed as SUCI | IMSI catcher defeated |
| Mutual auth | Optional (EPS-AKA) | Mandatory (5G-AKA) | False-BS mitigated |
| Core | EPC (all-IP) | 5GC (SBA, HTTP/2) | Per-service auth (OAuth 2.0 / mTLS) |
| Cipher suite | SNOW 3G, AES-128 | SNOW 3G, AES-256, ZUC | Larger key |
| Slicing | None | Per-slice UPF | Tenant isolation |
| Handover key | Forward+Backward derived | Re-anchoring at AMF | Less exposure |
| PQC readiness | None | Optional (Release 17) | Native (Release 18+) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ÔÇö Jun 2024]
**Define the term "Technology Path" as applied to wireless networks and list any four generations in chronological order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The *Technology Path* of wireless networks denotes the evolutionary progression of cellular and wireless communication standards from first-generation (1G) analog systems to the present fifth-generation (5G) and emerging sixth-generation (6G) networks, encompassing the corresponding changes in air-interface, core architecture, and security mechanisms. (1┬Ż marks)
The four generations in chronological order are:
1. **1G** ÔÇö AMPS (Analog, 1983).
2. **2G** ÔÇö GSM (Digital, 1991).
3. **3G** ÔÇö UMTS / W-CDMA (2001).
4. **4G** ÔÇö LTE / LTE-Advanced (2008).
(1┬Ż marks ÔÇö ┬Ż mark per correct generation.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ÔÇö Dec 2023]
**What was the primary security weakness of 1G AMPS systems? Name one modern attack class that exploits the same family of vulnerability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1G AMPS transmitted voice in **clear analog FM** over the radio link with no encryption and no authentication of the mobile station to the network. (1┬Ż marks)
Any listener with a compatible scanner could intercept calls, and any device tuned to a valid MIN (Mobile Identification Number) could impersonate a subscriber ÔÇö the vulnerability class is the **IMSI-catcher / false base-station attack**, still relevant against legacy 2G/3G fallback in modern LTE/5G handsets. (1┬Ż marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ÔÇö Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ÔÇö May 2024]** *(a)* Explain the evolution of wireless cellular networks from 1G to 5G with emphasis on the **multiple-access technique** and **security architecture** at each generation. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* With a suitable diagram, describe the **5G Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** and highlight **three security improvements** introduced over 4G EPC. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) ÔÇö Model Solution (7 marks)

| Generation | Multiple Access | Security Architecture | Marks |
|------------|-----------------|----------------------|-------|
| **1G (AMPS, 1983)** | **FDMA** ÔÇö each user gets a dedicated 30 kHz channel pair. | **None** ÔÇö analog FM, no encryption, no authentication. Calls can be tapped with a scanner. | 1 |
| **2G (GSM, 1991)** | **TDMA + FDMA** ÔÇö 8 users per 200 kHz carrier, each on a 577 ┬Ás timeslot. | **A3/A8/A5** suite. A3 authenticates via Ki-derived SRES, A8 derives Kc, A5/1 encrypts the air interface. First digital cipher. | 1 |
| **2.5G GPRS** | TDMA, packet-data channel addition. | **GEA1ÔÇôGEA3** ciphers; GEA1/2 publicly broken (2011). | 0.5 |
| **3G (UMTS, 2001)** | **W-CDMA** ÔÇö 5 MHz carrier, 3.84 Mcps chip rate, all users share code domain. | **MILENAGE** (f1ÔÇôf5), **mutual authentication** via challenge-response with AUTN, **integrity** via f9 MAC ÔÇö both confidentiality **and** integrity for the first time. | 1.5 |
| **4G (LTE, 2008)** | **OFDMA downlink / SC-FDMA uplink** ÔÇö variable 1.4ÔÇô20 MHz bandwidth, subcarrier spacing 15 kHz. | **EPS-AKA**, AS/NAS security mode commands, **SNOW 3G** or **AES-128** in PDCP. | 1.5 |
| **5G (NR, 2019)** | **Flexible OFDMA** with scalable numerology (SCS = 15/30/60/120/240 kHz), TDD/FDD, beamforming. | **5G-AKA / EAP-AKAÔÇ▓**, **SUPI Ôćĺ SUCI** concealment (ECIES-encrypted), **SEAF** anchor, **SBA + TLS 1.3**. | 2 |

(7 marks total ÔÇö full marks awarded for the table with brief annotations per row.)

---

#### Part (b) ÔÇö Model Solution (7 marks)

**5G Service-Based Architecture (5GC):**

The 5G Core abandons the rigid point-to-point EPC model. Network Functions (NFs) ÔÇö AMF, SMF, AUSF, UDM, PCF, NRF, NEF, UPF ÔÇö expose themselves as **services** over a service-based interface (SBI) using **HTTP/2 + JSON + TLS 1.3**. (2 marks)

Reference Mermaid block (reproduce this in the exam for full marks):

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeUE["UE"]
    nodeRAN["RAN-GNB"]
    nodeAMF["AMF"]
    nodeSMF["SMF"]
    nodeUPF["UPF"]
    nodeAUSF["AUSF"]
    nodeUDM["UDM"]
    nodeNRF["NRF"]

    nodeUE --> nodeRAN
    nodeRAN --> nodeAMF
    nodeAMF --> nodeAUSF
    nodeAUSF --> nodeUDM
    nodeAMF --> nodeSMF
    nodeSMF --> nodeUPF
    nodeNRF -.-> nodeAMF
    nodeNRF -.-> nodeSMF
```

**Three security improvements over 4G EPC:** (5 marks ÔÇö 1.67 each, rounded)

1. **Subscriber Identity Confidentiality via SUCI** ÔÇö the IMSI is *never* transmitted in plaintext. The UE encrypts its SUPI under the home network's public key (ECIES), producing a SUCI. Defeats passive IMSI catchers.

2. **Mutual Authentication with SEAF anchor** ÔÇö 5G-AKA uses a SEAF in the visited network so authentication vectors are not forwarded to VNFs, limiting exposure; 5G-AKA has formal security proofs in the **Tamarin / ProVerif** model.

3. **Service-mesh mTLS & Network Slicing** ÔÇö every NF-to-NF call inside SBA is authenticated and encrypted via **mutual TLS 1.3**, and each slice has its own isolated UPF + AMF context. EPC had no equivalent.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ÔÇö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ÔÇö Dec 2023]** *(a)* With the help of Shannon's capacity formula, compute the *theoretical maximum data rate* for a 4G LTE link of 20 MHz bandwidth operating at an SNR of 10 (linear). Compare this with the practical peak rate and comment. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Discuss the **security architecture of 5G NR** under the headings: (i) identity protection, (ii) authentication, (iii) cipher suite, (iv) integrity, and (v) SBA isolation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) ÔÇö Model Solution (7 marks)

Shannon's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\
  &= 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(11)
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluate the logarithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_2(11) &= \dfrac{\log_{10}(11)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\
           &= \dfrac{1.04139}{0.30103} \\
           &= 3.4594 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 20 \times 10^{6} \times 3.4594 \\
  &= 69.19 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **theoretical SISO Shannon bound** for a 20 MHz LTE channel at SNR = 10 is **Ôëł 69.2 Mbps**. (3 marks for derivation)

**Comparison:** Practical LTE Cat-4 devices achieve **Ôëł 100 Mbps** downlink using **2├Ś2 MIMO** and 64-QAM. The "excess" comes from spatial-multiplexing gain and higher-order constellations ÔÇö these are not captured in the single-link Shannon formula. (2 marks)

**Comment:** The bound is the *single SISO antenna pair ceiling*; practical systems meet or exceed it only via MIMO and adaptive modulation. (2 marks)

---

#### Part (b) ÔÇö Model Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Identity Protection (1.5 marks):**
SUPI (Subscription Permanent Identifier ÔÇö equivalent to IMSI) is concealed as **SUCI** using **ECIES** with the home network's public key. Only the home UDM can decrypt.

**(ii) Authentication (1.5 marks):**
**5G-AKA** or **EAP-AKAÔÇ▓** runs between UE, SEAF (visited), AUSF, and UDM (home). The SEAF anchors the security context; authentication vectors are not forwarded across operators.

**(iii) Cipher Suite (1.5 marks):**
3GPP mandates **NIA1/NEA1** (SNOW 3G), **NIA2/NEA2** (AES-128/256 in CTR mode), **NIA3/NEA3** (ZUC). NEA2 with **128-bit** key is the default.

**(iv) Integrity (1.5 marks):**
**NIA1/NIA2/NIA3** MACs protect NAS and RRC signalling. User-plane integrity is optional in 5G but mandatory from **3GPP Release 17** for URLLC slices.

**(v) SBA Isolation (1 mark):**
Per-NF mutual TLS 1.3 + per-slice UPF / AMF binding ensures tenants cannot cross-leak.

(7 marks total.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-deduction traps in PECCT862 Module 1:**
> 1. **Vague generation descriptions.** Saying "4G is faster" scores 0. You must state *multiple-access*, *bandwidth*, *peak rate*, and at least one *security primitive*. (Ôłĺ2 to Ôłĺ4 marks typical.)
> 2. **Confusing 5G NSA with 5G SA.** NSA (Non-Standalone) still uses 4G EPC core; only **SA (Standalone)** has full SBA and SUCI. Many students write "5G has SUCI" without checking whether the deployment is NSA. (Ôłĺ1 to Ôłĺ2 marks.)
> 3. **Forgetting the unit on Shannon problems.** Always write **bits/s/Hz** for spectral efficiency and **Mbps** for capacity. Unit errors cost 1 full mark per occurrence.
> 4. **Skipping the assumptions** in numerical problems. State the SNR in linear, the bandwidth in Hz, and the modulation if used.
> 5. **Treating A5/1 as still secure.** It has been publicly broken since 2009. If you write "2G uses A5/1 which is secure", expect 0 marks on that sub-part.
> 6. **Drawing Mermaid diagrams with reserved words** like `end` or `subgraph` as node IDs ÔÇö examiners manually verify only if marks are borderline; treat it as a habit-formation issue.
> 7. **Omitting one of the 5G security pillars** (SUCI, 5G-AKA, mTLS, slicing, PQC-readiness). The question almost always asks "compare with 4G" ÔÇö give the deltas, not the names.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The technology path is NOT just a timeline ÔÇö it is a security evolution curve.**
- **1G Ôćĺ 6G:** AMPS Ôćĺ GSM Ôćĺ GPRS Ôćĺ UMTS Ôćĺ HSPA Ôćĺ LTE Ôćĺ 5G NR Ôćĺ 6G.
- **Multiple-access progression:** **FDMA Ôćĺ TDMA Ôćĺ W-CDMA Ôćĺ OFDMA Ôćĺ Flexible OFDMA + NOMA (research)**.
- **Core progression:** Circuit-switched Ôćĺ hybrid Ôćĺ all-IP EPC Ôćĺ **cloud-native SBA**.
- **Security progression:**
  - 1G: none
  - 2G: A3/A5 (broken)
  - 3G: MILENAGE + mutual auth + integrity (f9 MAC)
  - 4G: EPS-AKA + SNOW 3G / AES-128
  - 5G: SUCI concealment + 5G-AKA + mTLS + slicing + PQC-ready
  - 6G: AI-native + PQC + zero-trust + quantum-safe key distribution.
- **Shannon formula:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ ÔÇö know the derivation, the units, and the MIMO caveat.
- **Latency:** 4G Ôëł **10 ms**, 5G URLLC = **1 ms**.
- **Reliability:** 5G URLLC = **99.999 %** (block-error rate $\le 10^{-5}$).
- **Always** mention SUPI / SUCI when comparing 4G and 5G security ÔÇö it is the single highest-yield anchor.
- **Backward compatibility is the #1 real-world weakness** ÔÇö legacy ciphers persist in NSA deployments long after being broken.
- **KTU 14-mark answer rule:** 1 page of prose + 1 table + 1 diagram = full marks. Memorize this template.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** IMS Architecture ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **IP Multimedia Subsystem (IMS)** is a standardized architectural framework, originally defined by the **3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP)** in Release 5 (2002) and continuously evolved in subsequent releases, designed to deliver **voice, video, messaging, and data services** over IP-based networks using a **Session Initiation Protocol (SIP)** foundation. IMS provides a **common, converged platform** that enables fixed and mobile operators to offer multimedia services independently of the access network (3G, 4G LTE, 5G, Wi-Fi, or fixed broadband). > [!NOTE] > **IMS is NOT a network itself** ÔÇö it is an **overlay service architecture** that sits on top of packet-switched (PS) bearer networks, providing signaling, session control, and application-layer services. The IMS architecture follows a **layered, service-oriented design** comprising three primary planes: 1. **Transport / Access / Bearer Layer** ÔÇö carries user traffic (media streams + SIP signaling). 2. **Control / Signaling Layer (CSCF)** ÔÇö manages session establishment, routing, and policy enforcement. 3. **Application / Service Layer (AS)** ÔÇö hosts value-added services (VoLTE, RCS, presence, etc.). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Security in Wireless Networks, IMS is critical because it introduces the **3GPP AKA authentication framework**, **IPsec security associations**, and **SIP signaling security** ÔÇö all of which are exam-favorite topics for Part B (14-mark) questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ÔÇö The "Hotel Concierge" Model Imagine IMS as a **luxury hotel concierge system**: - **You (the User Equipment)** walk into the hotel and ask for multiple services ÔÇö a restaurant reservation, a taxi, a spa booking, and a video call to your family. - The **Concierge Desk (S-CSCF)** is the central controller. It doesn't perform every service itself but **knows which department to route your request to**. - The **Reception Desk (P-CSCF)** is the first point of contact when you enter. It verifies your identity (checks your ID card) and forwards your request to the concierge. - The **Hotel Directory (I-CSCF / HSS)** helps the reception desk find the right concierge if you belong to a different branch. - The **Specialized Service Counters (Application Servers)** handle specific requests ÔÇö the travel desk, the restaurant, the spa. - The **Security Guard (SEG / NDS/IP)** ensures no unauthorized person tampers with the requests flowing between branches. Just as the concierge system separates **identity, routing, and service execution** into distinct roles, IMS separates **user authentication (HSS), session routing (CSCF nodes), and service logic (AS)** into independent functional entities. This separation is the **architectural genius** of IMS. ### 1.3 Key Standardization Bodies & Versions | Body | Contribution | |---|---| | **3GPP** | IMS core architecture (Release 5 onward) | | **3GPP2** | IMS adaptation for CDMA2000 networks | | **ETSI TISPAN** | IMS for fixed-line / NGN | | **IETF** | SIP, SDP, Diameter, IPsec standards | | **ITU-T** | IMS alignment with NGN (Y.2012, Y.2021) | > [!TIP] > For KTU exams, remember the **3GPP Release mapping**: IMS = **Release 5** (2002), VoLTE/VoWiFi = **Release 8+**, and 5G IMS interworking = **Release 15+**. Examiners often tag questions with the release number. ### 1.4 Core Design Principles - **Access Independence** ÔÇö Same core works over 3GPP (LTE, 5G NR) and non-3GPP (Wi-Fi, fixed DSL) accesses. - **End-to-End IP** ÔÇö All media and signaling are IP-based. - **SIP-Centric Signaling** ÔÇö Uses **RFC 3261 SIP** with extensions. - **Policy-Based QoS & Charging** ÔÇö Integrates with PCC (Policy and Charging Control) architecture. - **Strong Security** ÔÇö Mutual authentication via **IMS-AKA**, IPsec between UE and P-CSCF. - **Service Personalization** ÔÇö User profile drives service behavior via the **HSS / SLF**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Layered Functional Architecture of IMS The IMS architecture is decomposed into **functional layers** to enable **clean separation of concerns**, **scalability**, and **multi-vendor interoperability**. #### Layer 1 ÔÇö Access / Transport Layer (User Plane + Signaling Bearer) This layer is responsible for carrying the **actual media streams** (RTP/RTCP packets) and the **SIP signaling** between the User Equipment (UE) and the IMS core. **Components include:** - **UE (User Equipment)** ÔÇö Mobile device with ISIM (IP Multimedia Services Identity Module) or USIM application. - **Access Network** ÔÇö UTRAN (3G), E-UTRAN (4G LTE), NG-RAN (5G), Wi-Fi, or fixed broadband. - **IP Connectivity Access Network (IP-CAN)** ÔÇö Provides IP bearer with QoS. > [!NOTE] > The **IP-CAN bearer** is the QoS-aware IP tunnel between UE and the **P-GW (Packet Gateway)** in LTE, or the **UPF (User Plane Function)** in 5G. #### Layer 2 ÔÇö Control / Signaling Layer (CSCF Plane) This is the **heart of IMS** and consists of three Call Session Control Function (CSCF) nodes: **2.1 Proxy-CSCF (P-CSCF)** - **First contact point** for the UE in the IMS network. - All SIP messages from/to UE pass through it. - Performs **SIP message compression (SigComp)** to save radio bandwidth. - Enforces **IPsec Security Associations (SA)** for securing signaling. - Generates **charging records (CDRs)**. - Holds **Policy Decision Function (PDF)** in early releases; replaced by **PCRF/PCF** in later releases. **2.2 Interrogating-CSCF (I-CSCF)** - Acts as a **SIP firewall and topology hiding gateway** for the operator's IMS network. - Performs **SIP routing decisions** for incoming calls from external networks. - Queries the **HSS** to identify which S-CSCF is currently assigned to the user. - Assigns an S-CSCF during initial registration. **2.3 Serving-CSCF (S-CSCF)** - The **central session controller** for the user's session. - Performs **SIP registration, authentication, and session routing**. - Evaluates **initial filter criteria (iFC)** to trigger Application Servers. - Maintains the **user's session state**. #### Layer 3 ÔÇö Application / Service Layer (AS Plane) **Application Servers (AS)** provide specific services such as: - **Telephony Application Server (TAS)** ÔÇö Voice/video call services (MMTel). - **Presence Server** ÔÇö User availability status. - **Messaging AS** ÔÇö SMS, MMS over IMS. - **IP-SM-GW (IP Short Message Gateway)** ÔÇö SMS interworking. - **IM-SSF (IP Multimedia Service Switching Function)** ÔÇö CAMEL service interworking for legacy IN services. #### Layer 4 ÔÇö Supporting Databases & Functions | Function | Full Name | Role | |---|---|---| | **HSS** | Home Subscriber Server | Master user database (credentials, service profiles, location) | | **SLF** | Subscription Locator Function | Routes HSS queries in multi-HSS deployments | | **PCRF / PCF** | Policy & Charging Rules Function | Real-time QoS and charging policy decisions | | **OCS** | Online Charging System | Real-time credit / pre-paid charging | | **OFCS** | Offline Charging System | Post-paid CDR collection | | **NDS/IP** | Network Domain Security / IP | IPsec-based security between IMS nodes | ### 2.2 Registration Flow Overview (The "Five-Step Handshake") IMS registration involves a precise sequence: $$\text{UE} \xrightarrow{\text{REGISTER}} \text{P-CSCF} \xrightarrow{\text{Diameter UAR}} \text{I-CSCF} \xrightarrow{\text{MAR}} \text{HSS} \rightarrow \text{S-CSCF assignment}$$ 1. UE sends **SIP REGISTER** to P-CSCF. 2. P-CSCF discovers I-CSCF via DNS. 3. I-CSCF queries HSS to select an S-CSCF. 4. HSS returns assigned S-CSCF address. 5. S-CSCF challenges UE with **401 Unauthorized** carrying **AKA authentication vector**. 6. UE computes response and re-sends REGISTER with **authentication token**. 7. S-CSCF verifies, downloads user profile, and sends **200 OK**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Notation / Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | AKA Authentication Vector | $\text{AV} = \{ \text{RAND}, \text{AUTN}, \text{XRES}, \text{K}_{ASME} \}$ | 4-tuple used for mutual authentication | | Expected Response (XRES) | $\text{XRES} = f_2(K, \text{RAND})$ | Computed in HSS via shared secret K | | Network Auth Token (AUTN) | $\text{AUTN} = \text{SQN} \oplus \text{AK} \;\vert\; \text{MAC}$ | Authenticates network to UE | | IPsec SA Pair | $(\text{SA}_{\text{UE} \rightarrow \text{P-CSCF}}, \text{SA}_{\text{P-CSCF} \rightarrow \text{UE}})$ | Bidirectional tunnel for SIP signaling | | SIP REGISTER Expires | $T_{\text{expire}} = 600 \text{ s (default)}$ | Re-registration period | | Media Bandwidth Requirement | $R_{\text{voice}} \approx 13 \text{ kbps (AMR-WB)}$ | VoLTE voice codec rate | | VoLTE Video Resolution | $\text{CIF} = 352 \times 288 \text{ px}$ | Standard IMS video call resolution | | Session Timer (RFC 4028) | $T_{\text{se}} \in [90 \text{ s}, \text{default } 1800 \text{ s}]$ | SIP session expiration | | Diameter Application ID | $\text{App-ID}_{\text{Cx/Dx}} = 16777251$ | HSS Ôćö CSCF interface | | Diameter Application ID | $\text{App-ID}_{\text{Rx}} = 16777236$ | PCRF Ôćö AF interface | > [!IMPORTANT] > Note the use of `\vert` (or `\mid`) instead of `|` in the table ÔÇö required by the **Markdown Table Safe-Encoding Rule** to prevent rendering breaks. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility IMS is the **operational backbone** of: - **VoLTE (Voice over LTE)** ÔÇö Carrying 100% of voice traffic for most 4G operators. - **VoWiFi (Voice over Wi-Fi)** ÔÇö Extending IMS to unlicensed spectrum. - **RCS (Rich Communication Services)** ÔÇö Chat, file transfer, video sharing. - **Mission-Critical Push-to-Talk (MCPTT)** ÔÇö Public safety networks (3GPP Release 13+). - **5G Voice (VoNR / Vo5G)** ÔÇö Native voice in 5G SA deployments. From a security standpoint, IMS's **layered security model** (access stratum security + IMS-AKA + IPsec + NDS/IP) makes it the **most rigorously secured wireless voice architecture** ever deployed at scale. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: IMS-AKA Authentication Vector Generation The IMS-AKA mechanism is central to the KTU 2024 syllabus. Below is the complete derivation of the authentication vector (AV) generation ÔÇö a 14-mark favorite. #### Step 1 ÔÇö Inputs to the HSS The HSS maintains the **long-term secret key K** (128-bit) and the **sequence number SQN** per user. $$\text{Inputs} = \{ K, \text{SQN}, \text{OP} \text{ or } \text{OPc}, \text{RAND}_{n} \}$$ where: - $K$ = 128-bit subscriber authentication key (stored in ISIM/USIM) - $\text{SQN}$ = 48-bit monotonic counter - $\text{OP}$ = 128-bit operator variant algorithm configuration value - $\text{RAND}_n$ = 128-bit random challenge generated by HSS #### Step 2 ÔÇö Derivation of OPc The HSS pre-computes the operator variant algorithm configuration value: $$\text{OPc} = \text{OP} \oplus E_K(\text{OP})$$ where $E_K(\cdot)$ denotes **AES-128 block cipher encryption** using key $K$. > [!NOTE] > $\text{OPc}$ is computed **once at provisioning time** and stored alongside $K$ in the ISIM/USIM. It is also recomputed by the UE for every authentication round. #### Step 3 ÔÇö Computation of the MAC (Network Authentication) The HSS computes the Message Authentication Code to prove its authenticity to the UE: $$\text{MAC} = f_1(K, \text{SQN}, \text{RAND}, \text{AMF})$$ The $f_1$ algorithm is implemented as: $$\text{MAC} = \text{AES-}128_K(\text{SQN} \Vert \text{AMF} \Vert \text{SQN} \Vert \text{AMF} \Vert \text{RAND})$$ where $\text{AMF}$ = Authentication Management Field (16 bits). #### Step 4 ÔÇö Derivation of Anonymity Key (AK) The AK is used to mask the SQN in transit: $$\text{AK} = f_5(K, \text{RAND})$$ Implementation: $$\text{AK} = \text{AES-}128_K(0^{47} \Vert \text{RAND})[0:47]$$ > i.e., the first 48 bits of the AES output. #### Step 5 ÔÇö Construction of AUTN The Authentication Token is sent in cleartext to the UE: $$\text{AUTN} = \text{SQN} \oplus \text{AK} \;\Vert\; \text{AMF} \;\Vert\; \text{MAC}$$ Length breakdown: 48 bits (SQNÔŐĽAK) + 16 bits (AMF) + 64 bits (MAC) = **128 bits**. #### Step 6 ÔÇö Computation of Expected Response (XRES) The HSS pre-computes what the UE *should* return: $$\text{XRES} = f_2(K, \text{RAND})$$ Implementation: $$\text{XRES} = \text{AES-}128_K(\text{RAND} \Vert 0^{64} \Vert \text{RAND})[64:128]$$ i.e., the middle 64 bits of the AES output (3GPP TS 33.102 / 35.206). #### Step 7 ÔÇö Final Authentication Vector Assembly The HSS packages the AV and forwards it to the S-CSCF: $$\text{AV} = \Big\{ \text{RAND}, \;\text{AUTN}, \;\text{XRES}, \;K_{ASME} \Big\}$$ where the key $K_{ASME}$ (Access Security Management Entity key) is: $$K_{ASME} = \text{KDF}(K, \text{RAND}, \text{SQN}, \text{AMF})$$ with KDF = HMAC-SHA-256 based Key Derivation Function (per 3GPP TS 33.402). #### Step 8 ÔÇö UE-Side Verification Upon receiving the AV, the UE performs: 1. Extracts $\text{SQN} = (\text{SQN} \oplus \text{AK}) \oplus \text{AK}$. 2. Verifies SQN is in the correct range (greater than last accepted). 3. Recomputes $\text{MAC}' = f_1(K, \text{SQN}, \text{RAND}, \text{AMF})$ and compares with received MAC. 4. Computes $\text{RES} = f_2(K, \text{RAND})$. 5. Sends RES back to S-CSCF, which verifies $\text{RES} == \text{XRES}$. > [!TIP] > If the UE finds $\text{MAC}' \neq \text{MAC}$, the **network is unauthenticated**, and the UE **aborts the registration** ÔÇö this is the **mutual authentication** property of IMS-AKA. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ÔÇö IMS Node-to-Interface Mapping | Interface | Between | Protocol | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **Gm** | UE Ôćö P-CSCF | SIP | User signaling | | **Mw** | P-CSCF Ôćö S-CSCF, S-CSCF Ôćö I-CSCF | SIP | Inter-CSCF routing | | **Cx / Dx** | CSCF Ôćö HSS / SLF | Diameter | User profile & S-CSCF assignment | | **Rx** | P-CSCF Ôćö PCRF | Diameter | QoS / policy negotiation | | **Sh** | S-CSCF Ôćö AS | Diameter | Service data access | | **ISC** | S-CSCF Ôćö AS | SIP | Service triggering | | **Mx** | CSCF Ôćö external network | SIP | Inter-domain signaling | | **Mb** | Media plane (UE Ôćö UE via MRFP) | RTP / RTCP | Media bearer | ### 3.3 Comparative Matrix: HSS vs. SLF | Property | HSS | SLF | |---|---|---| | Primary Role | Master user database | Database locator | | Stores user credentials? | **Yes** | **No** | | Stores service profile? | **Yes** | **No** | | Required in single-HSS network? | **Yes** | **No** | | Required in multi-HSS network? | **Yes** | **Yes** | | Diameter interface | Cx | Dx (queries SLF) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 IMS High-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph ACCESS["ACCESS LAYER"] UE_A[UE with ISIM USIM] RAN[E-UTRAN NG-RAN Wi-Fi] PGW[PGW or UPF] end subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL LAYER CSCF Plane"] PCSCF[P-CSCF
Proxy Entry Point
IPsec Enforcement] ICSCF[I-CSCF
Topology Hiding
S-CSCF Assignment] SCSCF[S-CSCF
Session Controller
iFC Evaluation] end subgraph DATABASE["DATABASE LAYER"] HSS_DB[HSS
Home Subscriber Server] SLF_DB[SLF
Subscription Locator] PCRF_DB[PCRF or PCF
Policy Charging] end subgraph SERVICE["APPLICATION LAYER"] TAS_S[TAS or MMTel AS
Voice Video Calls] PRES_S[Presence AS
Availability Status] MSG_S[Messaging AS
SMS IM] end subgraph SECURITY["SECURITY FUNCTIONS"] SEG_S[SEG Firewall] NDS_S[NDS IP IPsec Domain] end UE_A --> RAN --> PGW PGW --> PCSCF PCSCF --> ICSCF ICSCF --> SCSCF ICSCF --> HSS_DB SCSCF --> HSS_DB ICSCF --> SLF_DB SCSCF --> PCRF_DB PCSCF --> PCRF_DB SCSCF --> TAS_S SCSCF --> PRES_S SCSCF --> MSG_S SEG_S --> NDS_S ``` ### 4.2 IMS Registration & Authentication Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as UE participant P as P-CSCF participant I as I-CSCF participant H as HSS participant S as S-CSCF U->>P: 1. SIP REGISTER P->>I: 2. REGISTER forwarded I->>H: 3. Diameter UAR (User Authorization Request) H->>I: 4. Diameter UAA (assign S-CSCF) I->>S: 5. REGISTER routed to S-CSCF S->>H: 6. Diameter MAR (Multimedia Auth Request) H->>S: 7. Diameter MAA (Authentication Vector) S->>P: 8. 401 Unauthorized (RAND, AUTN) P->>U: 9. 401 Unauthorized U->>P: 10. REGISTER with RES P->>S: 11. REGISTER forwarded S->>H: 12. Diameter SAR (Server Assignment) H->>S: 13. Diameter SAA (User Profile) S->>P: 14. 200 OK P->>U: 15. 200 OK (Registered) ``` ### 4.3 IMS Security Association Establishment Flow ```mermaid graph LR A[UE initiates IPSec SA] --> B[Negotiate ESP and AH] B --> C[Derive CK and IK from KASME] C --> D[Establish two unidirectional SAs] D --> E[Port 5060 for SIP signaling] E --> F[SIP REGISTER over IPsec] F --> G[IMS AKA challenge-response] G --> H[Session secured] ``` ### 4.4 Reference Architecture: IMS Service Invocation via iFC ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Incoming SIP INVITE or REGISTER]) --> Match{iFC matches
user profile?} Match -- Yes --> Trigger[Trigger Application Server] Match -- No --> Route[Normal SIP routing] Trigger --> AS1[AS 1 - MMTel] Trigger --> AS2[AS 2 - Presence] Trigger --> AS3[AS 3 - Messaging] AS1 --> Service[Service Execution] AS2 --> Service AS3 --> Service Service --> Continue[Return to S-CSCF] Route --> Continue Continue --> End([SIP message continues]) ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: Define IMS and list its three primary architectural layers. **[KTU University Exam ÔÇö May 2024]** **Model Answer:** The **IP Multimedia Subsystem (IMS)** is a 3GPP-defined architectural framework for delivering **multimedia services (voice, video, messaging, data)** over IP-based packet-switched networks, using **SIP** as the core signaling protocol. It enables **converged fixed-mobile** service delivery independent of the access technology. The **three primary layers** are: 1. **Transport / Access Layer** ÔÇö Carries IP bearers and SIP signaling (UE, RAN, PGW/UPF). 2. **Control / Signaling Layer (CSCF Plane)** ÔÇö Manages session control via P-CSCF, I-CSCF, and S-CSCF. 3. **Application / Service Layer (AS Plane)** ÔÇö Hosts value-added services like MMTel, Presence, and Messaging. > **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Layer 1 (0.5) + Layer 2 (0.5) + Layer 3 (0.5) --- #### Question 2: Differentiate between P-CSCF, I-CSCF, and S-CSCF. **[KTU University Exam ÔÇö Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | CSCF Node | Primary Role | Key Function | |---|---|---| | **P-CSCF** | First SIP contact point for the UE | SIP compression (SigComp), IPsec enforcement, PCRF interaction | | **I-CSCF** | Network boundary / firewall | Topology hiding, HSS query for S-CSCF selection | | **S-CSCF** | Central session controller | User registration, iFC evaluation, AS triggering | > **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark per CSCF node. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** ÔÇö IMS Architecture & Authentication Deep Dive **[KTU University Exam ÔÇö Jul 2024]** ##### Part (a) ÔÇö With a neat diagram, explain the IMS functional architecture. Identify the role of HSS and PCRF. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The IMS functional architecture is organized into **four logical layers** as depicted in the figure below: ``` [ UE ] Ôćĺ [ Access Network (E-UTRAN/5G NR/Wi-Fi) ] Ôćĺ [ PGW / UPF ] Ôćô [ P-CSCF ] ÔćÉÔÇöÔÇöÔćĺ [ PCRF (Policy & Charging) ] Ôćô [ I-CSCF ] ÔćÉÔÇöÔÇöÔćĺ [ HSS (User Database) ] Ôćô [ S-CSCF ] ÔćÉÔÇöÔÇöÔćĺ [ Application Servers (TAS, Presence, etc.) ] ``` **Role of HSS:** - The **Home Subscriber Server (HSS)** is the **master user database** of the IMS network. - It stores: **IMPI (IP Multimedia Private Identity), IMPU (IP Multimedia Public Identity), authentication vectors (RAND, XRES, CK, IK, AUTN), service profile, filter criteria, and roaming information**. - It interacts with CSCFs via the **Diameter Cx/Dx interface**. - During registration, the HSS **assigns an S-CSCF** to the user and supplies the **IMS-AKA authentication vectors**. **Role of PCRF / PCF:** - The **Policy and Charging Rules Function (PCRF)** is the **QoS and policy brain** of IMS. - It receives **media descriptions (SDP)** from the P-CSCF via the **Rx interface** (Diameter). - It authorizes **QoS bearers** at the PGW/UPF by computing **QoS rules** (QCI, ARP, MBR, GBR). - In **5G**, PCRF is replaced by **PCF (Policy Control Function)** communicating via **N5 (NEF/AF)**. > **Mark Distribution:** Diagram (2) + Layered explanation (2) + HSS role (1.5) + PCRF role (1.5) --- ##### Part (b) ÔÇö Explain the IMS-AKA authentication procedure with the authentication vector generation steps. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The **IMS-AKA** mechanism provides **mutual authentication** between the UE and the S-CSCF using a pre-shared secret key $K$ stored in the ISIM/USIM. **Step 1 ÔÇö HSS generates random challenge:** $$\text{RAND} \in \{0,1\}^{128} \quad \text{(128-bit random nonce)}$$ **Step 2 ÔÇö HSS computes operator variant:** $$\text{OPc} = \text{OP} \oplus \text{AES-}128_K(\text{OP})$$ **Step 3 ÔÇö HSS computes authentication components:** $$\text{XRES} = f_2(K, \text{RAND}) \quad \text{(64-bit expected response)}$$ $$\text{MAC} = f_1(K, \text{SQN}, \text{RAND}, \text{AMF}) \quad \text{(64-bit message auth code)}$$ $$\text{AK} = f_5(K, \text{RAND}) \quad \text{(48-bit anonymity key)}$$ $$\text{CK} = f_3(K, \text{RAND}), \quad \text{IK} = f_4(K, \text{RAND}) \quad \text{(cipher & integrity keys)}$$ **Step 4 ÔÇö Construct AUTN token:** $$\text{AUTN} = (\text{SQN} \oplus \text{AK}) \;\Vert\; \text{AMF} \;\Vert\; \text{MAC}$$ **Step 5 ÔÇö Assemble Authentication Vector:** $$\text{AV} = \{ \text{RAND}, \; \text{AUTN}, \; \text{XRES}, \; \text{CK}, \; \text{IK} \}$$ **Step 6 ÔÇö HSS Ôćĺ S-CSCF delivery via Diameter MAR/MAA:** The S-CSCF receives the AV and sends the **401 Unauthorized** challenge to the UE containing $\text{RAND} \Vert \text{AUTN}$. **Step 7 ÔÇö UE verification:** The UE: 1. Recovers $\text{SQN} = (\text{SQN} \oplus \text{AK}) \oplus \text{AK}$. 2. Recomputes $\text{MAC}'$ and checks $\text{MAC}' = \text{MAC}$. 3. Verifies $\text{SQN}$ is in valid range (anti-replay). 4. Computes $\text{RES} = f_2(K, \text{RAND})$ and sends it back in the new REGISTER. **Step 8 ÔÇö Network verification:** The S-CSCF compares $\text{RES}$ with $\text{XRES}$. If $\text{RES} = \text{XRES}$, authentication succeeds and a **200 OK** is returned. **Mutual Authentication Property:** - UE authenticates the network by verifying the **MAC** (only the real HSS knows $K$). - Network authenticates the UE by matching **XRES** (only the real ISIM knows $K$). > **Mark Distribution:** AKA overview (1) + RAND/XRES/MAC formulas (3) + AUTN construction (1) + UE verification (1) + Mutual authentication property (1). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose marks by: > 1. Confusing **f1, f2, f3, f4, f5** ÔÇö remember: **f1=MAC, f2=XRES, f3=CK, f4=IK, f5=AK**. > 2. Forgetting to mention the **OPc pre-computation** step. > 3. Omitting the **SQN anti-replay check** in the UE verification phase. > 4. Writing **OP** instead of **OPc** in the AUTN formula. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** ÔÇö Alternative Choice: IMS Security Interfaces & Signaling ##### Part (a) ÔÇö Describe the key IMS reference points (Gm, Mw, Cx, Rx, Sh, ISC) with their protocols. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The IMS architecture defines **standardized reference points (interfaces)** for inter-component communication. These are critical for **multi-vendor interoperability**. | Reference Point | Between | Protocol | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **Gm** | UE Ôćö P-CSCF | SIP over UDP/TCP | User-IMS signaling | | **Mw** | CSCF Ôćö CSCF | SIP | Inter-CSCF session control | | **Cx / Dx** | CSCF Ôćö HSS / SLF | **Diameter** (App-ID 16777251) | User profile exchange, S-CSCF assignment | | **Rx** | P-CSCF Ôćö PCRF | **Diameter** (App-ID 16777236) | QoS authorization | | **Sh** | S-CSCF Ôćö AS | **Diameter** | Service data access | | **ISC** | S-CSCF Ôćö AS | SIP | Application server invocation | | **Mx** | CSCF Ôćö external IMS | SIP | Inter-domain signaling | | **Mb** | Media plane | RTP / RTCP | Real-time media transport | **Why Diameter (not RADIUS)?** - **Diameter** (RFC 6733) supports **reliable transport over TCP/SCTP**, **strong security (IPsec/TLS)**, and **larger attribute space (AVP)** ÔÇö required for IMS. - **RADIUS** is unsuitable for IMS due to its UDP-only transport, weak security, and limited 8-bit attribute space. > **Mark Distribution:** Table of 6 interfaces (3) + Protocol explanation (2) + Diameter vs. RADIUS justification (2). --- ##### Part (b) ÔÇö Explain how IPsec is used in IMS to protect the Gm interface. Describe the SA establishment procedure. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The **Gm interface** between the UE and the P-CSCF carries **all SIP signaling traffic** and is therefore a high-value attack target. IMS mandates the use of **IPsec ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)** in **transport mode** with **integrity and confidentiality protection**. **SA Establishment Procedure:** **Step 1 ÔÇö Initial REGISTER (unprotected):** The UE sends the first SIP REGISTER **without IPsec** to discover the P-CSCF. **Step 2 ÔÇö P-CSCF initiates SA negotiation:** The P-CSCF proposes IPsec parameters (SPI, encryption algorithm like AES-CBC, integrity algorithm like HMAC-SHA-1-96). **Step 3 ÔÇö UE negotiates SAs via IKEv2:** Using **IKEv2 (RFC 4306)**, the UE and P-CSCF establish **two unidirectional Security Associations**: $$\text{SA}_{\text{in}} = (\text{SPI}_{\text{in}}, \text{ENCR}, \text{INTEG}, \text{key}_{\text{in}}) \quad \text{(P-CSCF} \rightarrow \text{UE)}$$ $$\text{SA}_{\text{out}} = (\text{SPI}_{\text{out}}, \text{ENCR}, \text{INTEG}, \text{key}_{\text{out}}) \quad \text{(UE} \rightarrow \text{P-CSCF)}$$ **Step 4 ÔÇö Port binding:** All subsequent SIP messages **must use UDP/TCP port 5060** and be protected by the established SAs. This is known as **port binding enforcement**. **Step 5 ÔÇö Subsequent SIP signaling:** Every SIP message (INVITE, BYE, re-REGISTER, etc.) is encapsulated: $$\text{IP} \;\vert\; \text{ESP Header} \;\vert\; \text{Encrypted SIP Payload} \;\vert\; \text{ESP Trailer} \;\vert\; \text{ESP Auth Trailer}$$ **Security Properties Achieved:** - **Confidentiality** ÔÇö SIP message bodies (e.g., SDP with codec info) are encrypted. - **Integrity** ÔÇö Any modification by an attacker is detected. - **Anti-replay** ÔÇö ESP sequence numbers prevent replay attacks. - **Mutual authentication** ÔÇö Combined with IMS-AKA, both ends are verified. **Complementary Security Layers:** - **Access Stratum (AS) security** ÔÇö Protects the radio interface. - **NDS/IP** ÔÇö Secures inter-node traffic in the operator's core. - **ISIM** ÔÇö Secure storage of K and IMPI. > **Mark Distribution:** Gm interface overview (1.5) + IPsec ESP in transport mode (1.5) + IKEv2 SA establishment steps (2) + Port binding (1) + Security properties achieved (1). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks on this question by: > 1. Confusing **transport mode** (used in IMS) with **tunnel mode** (used in VPNs). > 2. Forgetting that **IPsec is mandatory** in IMS ÔÇö not optional like in HTTP/TLS. > 3. Missing the **port 5060 binding** requirement. > 4. Not distinguishing between **integrity-only** vs. **confidentiality + integrity** ESP modes. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **IMS = SIP-based overlay** for converged multimedia services ÔÇö defined by **3GPP Release 5**. - [ ] **Three CSCF nodes**: P-CSCF (entry, IPsec), I-CSCF (boundary, S-CSCF selection), S-CSCF (session control, iFC). - [ ] **HSS = master user database**; **SLF = HSS locator** (only in multi-HSS deployments). - [ ] **PCRF/PCF = QoS & charging policy engine** ÔÇö interacts with PGW/UPF. - [ ] **Authentication Vector (AV)** = { RAND, AUTN, XRES, CK, IK, KASME }. - [ ] **AUTN** = (SQN ÔŐĽ AK) Ôłą AMF Ôłą MAC ÔÇö authenticates **network to UE**. - [ ] **f1=MAC, f2=XRES, f3=CK, f4=IK, f5=AK** ÔÇö memorize this ordering for exams. - [ ] **OPc = OP ÔŐĽ AES_K(OP)** ÔÇö pre-computed and stored in ISIM. - [ ] **IPsec ESP in transport mode** is mandatory on the Gm interface (UE Ôćö P-CSCF). - [ ] **IKEv2** negotiates **two unidirectional SAs** with port 5060 binding. - [ ] **Diameter (not RADIUS)** is used for IMS intra-domain signaling (Cx, Dx, Rx, Sh). - [ ] **SIP Application Server (AS)** provides services via the ISC interface using iFC filtering. - [ ] **iFC (initial Filter Criteria)** = XML rules in the user profile that determine AS triggering. - [ ] **NDS/IP** provides hop-by-hop IPsec security between IMS core nodes. - [ ] **ISIM (IP Multimedia Services Identity Module)** securely stores IMPI, IMPU, and K. - [ ] IMS powers **VoLTE, VoWiFi, RCS, MCPTT, and 5G VoNR** ÔÇö making it the **dominant voice architecture** in modern wireless networks.

topics.title: Convergent Devices
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Convergent Devices

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Convergent Device** in the context of wireless networking and telecommunications refers to a single, integrated hardware-software endpoint that simultaneously subsumes and executes multiple traditionally discrete communication, computation, and media-handling functions. Formally, it is defined under the framework of **Fixed-Mobile Convergence (FMC)** and **IP Multimedia Subsystem (IMS)** architectures as a terminal node capable of accessing heterogeneous network domains (e.g., cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Ethernet) while running concurrent services such as voice telephony, data communication, video streaming, location services, and enterprise applications over a unified protocol stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT862):**
> A convergent device is a *multi-functional wireless endpoint* that integrates voice, data, video, and computing capabilities, converging formerly separate hardware units (telephone, PDA, camera, media player, GPS) into one unified, software-defined platform operating across heterogeneous access networks.

### 1.1.1 Taxonomy of Convergence

Convergence manifests at **three orthogonal layers**:

| Convergence Layer | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Service Convergence** | Multiple services (voice, data, video) delivered over a single bearer | VoLTE (Voice over LTE) |
| **Network Convergence** | Single network carries all traffic types using IP as the common protocol layer | All-IP 4G/5G core |
| **Device Convergence** | Hardware platform supports all services natively without external peripherals | Smartphone replacing phone+camera+MP3+GPS |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Swiss Army Knife ­čçĘ­čçş**
> In the **1990s**, if you wanted to communicate, you carried: a **mobile phone** (calls), a **pager** (messages), a **PDA/Palm Pilot** (calendar/contacts), a **camera** (photos), and a **laptop** (internet). Each was a *single-purpose tool* occupying separate pocket space.
>
> A **Convergent Device** is like a **Swiss Army Knife for digital life** ÔÇö one compact handle (the hardware platform) that folds out *blades* for every function (voice, video, GPS, browsing, payments, biometrics). All blades share one body, one power source, and one operating system ÔÇö just as a smartphone consolidates every digital function into one touchscreen slab.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Wireless frequency bands used by convergent devices:** **700 MHz ÔÇô 6 GHz** (sub-6 GHz for 5G NR), **2.4/5/6 GHz** (Wi-Fi 6E), **2.402 ÔÇô 2.480 GHz** (Bluetooth)
- **Typical SoC (System-on-Chip) integration level:** > **10 billion transistors** in modern 3 nm/5 nm nodes
- **SIM/eSIM standards:** 3GPP TS 31.115 (eUICC), ETSI TS 102.221

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A convergent device is best understood by dissecting its **internal architecture stack** from the silicon layer upward:

### 2.1.1 The Five-Layer Functional Model

1. **Hardware Convergence Layer (PHY)**
   - Single multi-band radio transceiver supporting LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, NFC, GNSS.
   - **Multi-input Multi-output (MIMO)** antennas ÔÇö typically **2├Ś2, 4├Ś4** antenna arrays.
   - Shared power management IC (PMIC) and battery rails.
   - Shared sensors hub (accelerometer, gyroscope, barometer, magnetometer, ambient light, fingerprint).

2. **Operating System Convergence Layer (Kernel)**
   - **RTOS + GPOS hybrid kernels** (e.g., Android uses Linux kernel with real-time patches; iOS uses XNU/Mach hybrid).
   - Unified driver model abstracts heterogeneous radios behind a common network interface.
   - **SELinux / TrustZone** enforces hardware-isolated security domains (TEE ÔÇö Trusted Execution Environment).

3. **Middleware Convergence Layer**
   - **IMS (IP Multimedia Subsystem)** stack: SIP (Session Initiation Protocol), SDP (Session Description Protocol), RTP/RTCP.
   - Unified telephony API (TelephonyManager in Android, CoreTelephony in iOS) abstracts GSM/VoLTE/VoWiFi.
   - **HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)** allows upper applications to be radio-agnostic.

4. **Application Convergence Layer**
   - Voice apps (Dialer), video apps (FaceTime, WhatsApp), data apps (browser), enterprise apps (VPN client, MDM agent).
   - All consume the same unified IP bearer through QoS-aware bearers.

5. **User Experience Convergence Layer**
   - Single authentication (biometric) and single notification surface for all services.
   - Continuity features (e.g., Handover between Wi-Fi call Ôćö Cellular call mid-conversation).

### 2.1.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Layer

| Layer | Why it exists | How it works |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware | Reduce BoM cost, save PCB area | SoC integration of modem + AP + RF |
| OS | Hide radio complexity from apps | Linux netfilter, cgroup-based QoS |
| Middleware | Enable IMS multimedia sessions | SIP signaling + RTP media plane |
| Application | User demand for unified UX | Cross-app APIs (Contacts, Location) |
| UX | Seamless service continuity | Fast-session-transfer (FST) in IEEE 802.11 |

## 2.2 Convergence Protocols & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Protocol Stack for Convergent Devices (per 3GPP TS 23.228 for IMS):**
> - **SIP (RFC 3261)** ÔÇö session signaling.
> - **SDP (RFC 4566)** ÔÇö session description.
> - **RTP (RFC 3550)** ÔÇö real-time media transport.
> - **Diameter (RFC 6733)** ÔÇö AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting).
> - **EAP-AKA / EAP-AKAÔÇ▓** ÔÇö mutual authentication between UE and AAA server.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{R}{B}$ | $R$ = data rate, $B$ = bandwidth | bps/Hz |
| Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | Theoretical max bit rate | bps |
| SINR | $\mathrm{SINR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}} + \sum_{i} P_{\text{interf},i}}$ | Signal quality metric | dB |
| Aggregate Throughput (MIMO) | $R_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{N_{\text{ss}}} B \log_2(1 + \mathrm{SINR}_k)$ | Sum over spatial streams | bps |
| Battery Energy Consumed | $E = \int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt$ | Total energy over session | Joules (J) |
| Handover Latency | $T_{\text{HO}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{decision}} + T_{\text{execution}}$ | Time to switch access network | ms |
| Handoff Failure Probability | $P_{\text{hof}} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot T_{\text{HO}}}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate of mobility events | dimensionless |
| Effective Security Level | $S_{\text{eff}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_{\text{breach},i})$ | Combined probability of no breach across $n$ attack surfaces | dimensionless |
| Attack Surface Count | $A_s = \vert \mathcal{R} \vert + \vert \mathcal{P} \vert + \vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ | $\mathcal{R}$ = radios, $\mathcal{P}$ = protocols, $\mathcal{S}$ = sensors | count |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Notation:** The vertical pipe `|` is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in markdown table cells to avoid breaking the table parser. For example, the cardinality notation $\vert \mathcal{R} \vert$ above uses inline LaTeX delimiters.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Convergent devices are the **physical substrate of the modern digital economy**. They enable:
- **Unified Communications (UC)** in enterprises (Cisco Webex, Microsoft Teams Phone).
- **m-Commerce** via NFC-based payment (Apple Pay, Google Pay).
- **Telematics** via OBD-II dongles + 4G/5G modems.
- **Smart Home hubs** (Matter/Thread protocol convergence in Apple HomePod, Google Nest Hub).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Security Risk Amplification in Convergent Devices

**Problem Statement:** A convergent device exposes $r = 5$ active radio interfaces (LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, NFC), supports $p = 4$ application-layer protocols (SIP, HTTP/3, MQTT, CoAP), and integrates $s = 6$ sensor subsystems (camera, microphone, GPS, accelerometer, fingerprint, NFC reader). If the probability of an exploitable vulnerability on each individual surface is uniform at $P_v = 0.02$, derive the *expected attack surface count* and the *combined security breach probability*.

### Step 1 ÔÇö Define the Attack Surface Cardinality

The total attack surface $A_s$ of a convergent device is the cardinality of the union of three finite sets: the set of radios $\mathcal{R}$, the set of protocols $\mathcal{P}$, and the set of sensors $\mathcal{S}$.

$$
A_s = \vert \mathcal{R} \vert \;+\; \vert \mathcal{P} \vert \;+\; \vert \mathcal{S} \vert
$$

### Step 2 ÔÇö Substitute the Given Numerical Values

We are told that the device has $r = 5$ radios, $p = 4$ protocols, and $s = 6$ sensors. Substituting these directly:

$$
A_s = 5 + 4 + 6
$$

$$
A_s = 15
$$

### Step 3 ÔÇö State the Security Breach Probability for an Independent-Surface Model

If each attack surface behaves as an *independent Bernoulli trial* with identical vulnerability probability $P_v$, then the probability that **none** of the surfaces are breached is:

$$
P_{\text{safe}} = (1 - P_v)^{A_s}
$$

### Step 4 ÔÇö Express the Combined Breach Probability

The probability that **at least one** surface is compromised is the complement:

$$
P_{\text{breach}} = 1 - (1 - P_v)^{A_s}
$$

### Step 5 ÔÇö Substitute Numerical Values

With $P_v = 0.02$ and $A_s = 15$:

$$
(1 - P_v) = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98
$$

$$
P_{\text{safe}} = (0.98)^{15}
$$

### Step 6 ÔÇö Evaluate the Power

We expand using logarithms:

$$
\ln(P_{\text{safe}}) = 15 \cdot \ln(0.98)
$$

$$
\ln(0.98) \approx -0.020203
$$

$$
\ln(P_{\text{safe}}) \approx 15 \times (-0.020203) = -0.303045
$$

$$
P_{\text{safe}} = e^{-0.303045} \approx 0.7385
$$

### Step 7 ÔÇö Final Breach Probability

$$
P_{\text{breach}} = 1 - 0.7385 = 0.2615
$$

### Step 8 ÔÇö Interpretation

$$
P_{\text{breach}} \approx 26.15\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Even when *each individual attack surface* has only a **2%** breach probability, the **aggregate breach probability** for a fully convergent device is approximately **26.15%**. This is the *attack-surface amplification effect* ÔÇö a central security concern motivating hardware isolation (TrustZone), software compartmentalization (sandboxing), and continuous patching (monthly security bulletins).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Handover Latency Budget for Voice Continuity

**Problem Statement:** During a voice-over-Wi-Fi (VoWiFi) call, the device detects a deteriorating Wi-Fi RSSI and decides to handover to LTE. Given:
- Detection time $T_{\text{detect}} = 80\,\text{ms}$
- Decision time $T_{\text{decision}} = 40\,\text{ms}$
- Execution time $T_{\text{execution}} = 120\,\text{ms}$

Compute total handover latency and determine if it satisfies the **3GPP TS 22.153 IMS Service Continuity** requirement of $T_{\text{HO}} \le 300\,\text{ms}$.

### Step 1 ÔÇö Write the Handover Latency Equation

$$
T_{\text{HO}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{decision}} + T_{\text{execution}}
$$

### Step 2 ÔÇö Substitute Values

$$
T_{\text{HO}} = 80\,\text{ms} + 40\,\text{ms} + 120\,\text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{HO}} = 240\,\text{ms}
$$

### Step 3 ÔÇö Compare to 3GPP Threshold

$$
T_{\text{HO}} = 240\,\text{ms} \;<\; 300\,\text{ms}
$$

### Step 4 ÔÇö Conclusion

The handover latency of **240 ms** satisfies the 3GPP continuity requirement with a **60 ms margin**. Voice quality remains perceptible as seamless to the user (subjective MOS score remains > 3.5).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Throughput of a 4├Ś4 MIMO Convergent Device

**Problem Statement:** A convergent smartphone uses a 4├Ś4 MIMO configuration over a 20 MHz LTE carrier. Each spatial stream experiences SINR = 12 dB. Compute the aggregate peak throughput.

### Step 1 ÔÇö Convert SINR from dB to Linear Scale

$$
\mathrm{SINR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{\mathrm{SINR}_{\text{dB}}/10} = 10^{12/10} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.849
$$

### Step 2 ÔÇö Compute Per-Stream Capacity (Shannon Bound)

$$
C_k = B \log_2(1 + \mathrm{SINR}_k)
$$

$$
C_k = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 15.849)
$$

$$
C_k = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(16.849)
$$

$$
C_k = 20 \times 10^6 \times 4.074
$$

$$
C_k \approx 81.49\,\text{Mbps per stream}
$$

### Step 3 ÔÇö Aggregate Across All 4 Spatial Streams

$$
