# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Wireless Inherently Less Secure than Wired?

The wireless medium is a **shared, broadcast, lossy, and boundary-less** channel. The following four physical-layer properties create the foundational vulnerability:

1. **Broadcast Nature of the RF Medium**
   Signals propagate omnidirectionally (or sectorially) and can be received by *any* antenna within line-of-sight or Fresnel zone coverage, not just the intended receiver.

2. **Lack of Physical Boundaries**
   Unlike an Ethernet cable confined to a building, RF energy *leaks* beyond walls, making perimeter-based firewalls insufficient.

3. **Resource-Constrained Devices**
   Mobile/IoT nodes have limited CPU, memory, and battery ÔÇö they cannot run heavy cryptographic suites like TLS 1.3 with 4096-bit RSA.

4. **Mobility & Dynamic Topology**
   Nodes constantly roam, hand off, and re-associate, expanding the **attack window** and complicating key management.

---

## 2.2 The Layered Wireless Threat Model (KTU High-Yield)

| OSI Layer | Existing Wireless Threat | Emerging Wireless Threat |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1 (Physical)** | Jamming, RF interference, EMP | Smart/Adaptive jamming, THz beam-steering attacks |
| **Layer 2 (Data Link / MAC)** | MAC spoofing, WEP IV collision, evil twin | 5G SUCI/PEI attacks, BLE pairing relay, RIS manipulation |
| **Layer 3 (Network)** | Rogue DHCP, route hijacking in MANET | 5G/6G slice isolation breach, SDN controller DoS |
| **Layer 4 (Transport)** | TCP SYN flood via Wi-Fi | QUIC manipulation in 5G MEC |
| **Layer 7 (Application)** | Web login over captive portal | AI-driven phishing over 6G holographic UI |
| **Cross-Layer** | Replay, MITM | Adversarial ML on beamforming, side-channel on mmWave |

---

## 2.3 Security Goals in Mathematical Notation (for exam use)

Let $M$ be the plaintext message, $K$ the secret key, $E_K(\cdot)$ the encryption function, and $D_K(\cdot)$ the decryption function.

$$\text{Confidentiality} : C = E_K(M), \quad M = D_K(C) \text{ only for authorized user}$$

$$\text{Integrity} : \text{Hash}(M) = H(M) \xrightarrow{\text{transmit}} H(M') \Rightarrow H(M) = H(M')$$

$$\text{Authentication} : \text{Proof}(\text{Identity}_A) \xleftarrow{\text{Challenge}} \text{Verifier}$$

$$\text{Availability} : P(\text{Successful Transmission}) \geq 1 - \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \to 0$$

$$\text{Non-Repudiation} : \exists \text{ Digital Signature } \sigma_A(M) \text{ verifiable by all}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Formula | Mathematical Form | Boundary / Condition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shannon Channel Capacity (Wireless)** | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ bits/s | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = SNR | Maximum secrecy-free throughput |
| **Wiretap Channel (Secrecy Capacity)** | $C_s = \max\{0, \, C_{\text{main}} - C_{\text{eve}}\}$ | $C_s > 0 \iff \text{main channel} > \text{eve channel}$ | Physical Layer Security (PLS) |
| **Path Loss (Free Space)** | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $d \gg \lambda$, LOS | Link budget analysis |
| **Path Loss (Log-Distance)** | $P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | $n$ = path-loss exponent (2ÔÇô4) | Cell coverage planning |
| **Jamming Margin** | $M_j = \dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{jammer}} + P_{\text{noise}}}$ | $M_j > 1$ for anti-jam | Military/jamming-resistant design |
| **A5/1 Stream Cipher Period** | $\text{Period} = 2^{64} - 1$ bits | GSM 2G only | Deprecated since 2009 |
| **WEP Effective Key Space** | $K_{\text{eff}} = 2^{40}$ bits (40-bit IV) | Trivially broken in $<60$s | Demonstrates IV-weakness |
| **DiffieÔÇôHellman Shared Secret** | $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \mod p$ | Discrete log hard | WPA3 SAE handshake |
| **5G SUCI Decryption (HES)** | $\text{SUPI} = D_{\text{HES}}(\text{SUCI})$ | Only HES knows OP/keys | IMSI privacy in 5G |
| **EAP-AKA Authentication Rounds** | $r = 3$ round-trips (LTE), $r = 2$ (5G) | 3GPP TS 33.501 | Auth latency reduction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (2024 Scheme):** In Part B 14-mark questions, the **secrecy capacity equation** $C_s = C_{\text{main}} - C_{\text{eve}}$ is the single most-asked formula. Always draw the wiretap-channel block diagram alongside it.

---

## 2.5 Why this Topic is Engineering-Critical (Real-World Utility)

- **Telecom operators** lose billions annually to fraud (cloning, SMS spoofing, SS7 attacks).
- **Healthcare IoT** (pacemakers, insulin pumps) requires **availability + integrity**; an attacker who jams a hospital's 2.4 GHz band can cause life-threatening failures.
- **Autonomous vehicles (VANET/V2X)** need **authenticated, low-latency, non-repudiable** message exchange at 10 Hz per vehicle.
- **Smart grid** uses **NB-IoT/LoRaWAN** for metering ÔÇö confidentiality of consumption data is a privacy right.
- **Defence/Military** wireless links demand **LPI/LPD** (low probability of intercept/detection), achievable only via physical-layer security.

---

## 2.6 The "Defense-in-Depth" Model Applied to Wireless

Defense-in-depth in wireless networks layers **seven** concentric rings:

1. **Physical Layer Security (PLS)** ÔÇö Beamforming, artificial noise, directional antennas.
2. **MAC Layer Security** ÔÇö 802.11i (WPA2), 802.11w (management frame protection), 5G SUPI/SUCI.
3. **Network Layer Security** ÔÇö IPsec, GTP-U integrity, SDN-based slicing.
4. **Transport/Session Layer** ÔÇö TLS 1.3, QUIC.
5. **Application Layer** ÔÇö OAuth 2.0, mutual TLS, certificate pinning.
6. **Cross-Layer IDS/IPS** ÔÇö Snort, Suricata, ML-based anomaly detection.
7. **Policy / Governance** ÔÇö 3GPP TS 33.501, NIST SP 800-97 (Guide to IEEE 802.11i), ITU-T X.805.

> [!NOTE]
> In **emerging networks (5G/6G, IoT)**, layer 1 (PLS) and layer 7 (AI-driven zero-trust) are the *new battlegrounds*, whereas in legacy networks (2G/3G, WEP Wi-Fi), layer 2 (broken crypto) was historically the weak point.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ÔÇö Secrecy Capacity of the Gaussian Wiretap Channel

This is the **single most important derivation** for the 14-mark Part B questions in this module.

**Setup:** Alice (legitimate transmitter) sends to Bob over a main channel with SNR $\gamma_B$, while Eve (eavesdropper) listens over a wiretap channel with SNR $\gamma_E$.

**Step 1 ÔÇö Shannon Capacity of the Main Channel**

$$C_{\text{main}} = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \gamma_B)$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Shannon Capacity of the Wiretap Channel**

$$C_{\text{eve}} = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \gamma_E)$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Wyner's Secrecy Capacity Definition (1975)**

Wyner proved that if $\gamma_B > \gamma_E$, a non-zero *secrecy rate* exists at which Alice can encode information such that Eve receives *zero* mutual information $I(M; Z) = 0$:

$$C_s = \max\{0,\; C_{\text{main}} - C_{\text{eve}}\}$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Substitute**

$$C_s = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \gamma_B) - B \cdot \log_2(1 + \gamma_E)$$

**Step 5 ÔÇö Apply Logarithm Property** $\log a - \log b = \log(a/b)$

$$C_s = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{1 + \gamma_B}{1 + \gamma_E}\right)$$

**Step 6 ÔÇö Engineering Interpretation**

$$\boxed{\,C_s = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{1 + \gamma_B}{1 + \gamma_E}\right) \;\geq\; 0 \iff \gamma_B > \gamma_E}\,}$$

> If Bob's channel is *better* than Eve's, Alice can transmit in perfect secrecy without any upper-layer encryption. This is the cornerstone of **Physical Layer Security (PLS)**.

**Step 7 ÔÇö Numerical Sanity Check**

Let $B = 20\,\text{MHz}$, $\gamma_B = 20\,\text{dB} = 100$, $\gamma_E = 0\,\text{dB} = 1$.

$$C_s = 20\!\times\!10^{6} \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{1+100}{1+1}\right) = 20\!\times\!10^{6} \cdot \log_2(50.5)$$

$$C_s \approx 20\!\times\!10^{6} \times 5.658 \approx 113.16 \text{ Mbps}$$

This is the **maximum perfectly-secret bitrate** Alice can push to Bob.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ÔÇö Why WEP is Broken (IV Collision Probability)

**Setup:** WEP uses a 24-bit Initialization Vector (IV). The shared RC4 keystream byte sequence $C_i$ is XORed with plaintext $P_i$:

$$C_i = P_i \oplus RC4(IV \,\|\, K_{\text{shared}})$$

**Step 1 ÔÇö Birthday Paradox Application**

With a 24-bit IV, the probability of IV collision after $n$ frames is:

$$P_{\text{collision}} \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \cdot 2^{24})}$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Setting a Collision Probability Threshold**

Set $P_{\text{collision}} = 0.5$ and solve for $n$:

$$1 - e^{-n^2 / (2^{25})} = 0.5$$

$$e^{-n^2 / 2^{25}} = 0.5$$

$$-n^2 / 2^{25} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693$$

$$n^2 = 0.693 \cdot 2^{25} \approx 23{,}226{,}618$$

$$n \approx 4{,}820 \text{ frames}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Practical Interpretation**

A single 11-Mbps AP at modest load (e.g., 5 Mbps sustained TCP) generates $\approx 5\!\times\!10^{6}/1500 \approx 3{,}333$ packets/s.

$$t_{\text{collision}} = \frac{4820}{3333} \approx 1.45 \text{ seconds}$$

> Hence, WEP can be cryptographically broken in **under 2 seconds** on a busy network using the **KoreK / PTW attack** ÔÇö this is why WPA/WPA2 replaced it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ÔÇö Energy of a Jamming Signal vs. Legitimate Signal

The **bit-energy to noise spectral density ratio** at the receiver determines successful demodulation:

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{P_r}{R_b \cdot N_0}$$

**Step 1 ÔÇö Substitute Path-Loss Expression**

$$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Total Noise Including Jammer**

$$N_0^{\text{total}} = N_0^{\text{thermal}} + \frac{P_j \cdot G_j \cdot G_r}{(4\pi d_j)^2 \cdot R_b \cdot B_j}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Anti-Jam Condition**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0^{\text{total}}} \geq \left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{required}}$$

Solving for maximum tolerable jammer power:

$$\boxed{\,P_j^{\max} = \dfrac{P_r \cdot d_j^2 \cdot R_b \cdot B_j}{G_j \cdot G_r \cdot B} \cdot \left[\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{req}}^{-1} - N_0^{-1}\right]\,}$$

> This derivation underpins **spread-spectrum / frequency-hopping** anti-jam systems used in military radio (HAVE QUICK, SINCGARS).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Attack Ôćĺ Defense Mapping (KTU High-Yield)

| Attack | Layer | Existing Network Defense | Emerging Network Defense |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping** | L1/L2 | WPA2-AES, 3G KASUMI | 5G SUCI, quantum key distribution (QKD), PLS |
| **Replay Attack** | L2/L3 | 802.11i temporal key, nonce | 5G AKMA, blockchain timestamping |
| **Rogue AP / Evil Twin** | L2 | 802.1X, WIPS (wireless IDS) | 5G SUPI-binding, EAP-TLS with cert pinning |
| **Jamming** | L1 | Spread spectrum, FHSS | Cognitive radio DSA, AI-jammer detection |
| **IMSI Catcher (Stingray)** | L2/L3 | 2G no defense; 3G mutual auth | 5G SUCI hides IMSI, slice isolation |
| **SS7 / Diameter Attack** | L3 | SIGTRAN firewalls | 5G SEPP (Security Edge Protection Proxy) |
| **Key Reinstallation (KRACK)** | L2 | WPA2 patch (802.11r) | WPA3 SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) |
| **Adversarial Beamforming Attack** | L1 | N/A | 6G AI-driven beam anomaly detection |
| **Drone Hijack (C2 link)** | L1ÔÇôL3 | Frequency hopping | Zero-trust mesh, post-quantum crypto |

> [!IMPORTANT]

---

## PART B ÔÇö 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern as per KTU 2024)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ÔÇö [KTU University Exam ÔÇö Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the fundamental differences between the **security architectures of existing wireless networks (2G/3G/Wi-Fi legacy)** and **emerging wireless networks (5G/6G/IoT)**. Highlight at least **four** architectural distinctions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, derive the **secrecy capacity of the Gaussian wiretap channel** and explain its significance in **Physical Layer Security (PLS)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ÔÇö Part (a) (7 marks)**

**1. Trust Model ÔÇö Centralized vs. Zero-Trust (2 marks)**

- **Existing networks** rely on a *centralized trust anchor* ÔÇö the HLR (Home Location Register) in GSM, the HSS in LTE, or the single shared secret in WEP. Compromise of this anchor compromises the entire network.
- **Emerging networks** (especially 5G) implement **zero-trust architecture** with the **SEPP (Security Edge Protection Proxy)**, **SUCI (Subscription Concealed Identifier)**, and **slice-isolated authentication**, so no single node compromise cascades.

**2. Identity Privacy ÔÇö Plaintext vs. Encrypted Identifiers (2 marks)**

- In **2G/3G**, the **IMSI is transmitted in plaintext** over the air ÔÇö making IMSI-catcher (Stingray) attacks trivial.
- In **5G**, the IMSI is replaced by **SUCI**, which is the IMSI encrypted with the public key of the **HES (Home Subscriber Server)**. Only the operator's HES can decrypt, protecting against passive identity capture.

**3. Cryptographic Agility ÔÇö Static vs. Software-Defined (1.5 marks)**

- Legacy networks hard-code a single algorithm suite (e.g., A5/1 in GSM).
- Emerging networks (5G/6G) support **multiple concurrent algorithms** chosen at registration (EPS-AKA, 5G-AKA, EAP-AKA', EAP-TLS) ÔÇö this is **crypto-agility**.

**4. Attack Surface ÔÇö RF-only vs. Software-Defined + AI (1.5 marks)**

- Legacy threats are dominantly **physical-layer** (jamming, sniffing, rogue AP).
- Emerging threats include **AI-driven adversarial attacks** on beamforming, **network-slice hopping**, **SDN-controller DoS**, and **quantum-compute cryptanalysis**, requiring **AI/ML-based defense** alongside traditional crypto.

---

#### **Model Solution ÔÇö Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 ÔÇö System Model (1 mark)**

Alice communicates with Bob over the *main* channel $y = h_B x + n_B$, while Eve listens over the *wiretap* channel $z = h_E x + n_E$, where $h_B, h_E$ are channel gains and $n_B, n_E$ are AWGN samples.

**Step 2 ÔÇö Channel Capacities (2 marks)**

$$C_{\text{main}} = B \log_2(1 + \gamma_B), \quad C_{\text{eve}} = B \log_2(1 + \gamma_E)$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Wyner Secrecy Capacity (2 marks)**

$$C_s = \max\{0,\; C_{\text{main}} - C_{\text{eve}}\} = B \log_2\!\left(\frac{1+\gamma_B}{1+\gamma_E}\right) \quad \text{iff } \gamma_B > \gamma_E$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Block Diagram (1 mark)**

(Alice Ôćĺ Encoder Ôćĺ Main Channel Ôćĺ Bob; same Encoder Ôćĺ Wiretap Channel Ôćĺ Eve with $I(M;Z) = 0$)

**Step 5 ÔÇö PLS Significance (1 mark)**

- Provides **information-theoretic secrecy**, not just computational secrecy.
- Complements (does not replace) upper-layer encryption.
- Particularly valuable in **IoT / 6G** where nodes cannot run heavy crypto.
- **Engineering use:** friendly jamming, artificial noise, RIS-aided beamforming, cooperative jamming.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ÔÇö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **CIA triad extended with availability, authentication, non-repudiation, and authorization** in the context of wireless networks. For each property, give **one concrete attack** and **one defense mechanism**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **WEP protocol** and mathematically prove (using the **birthday paradox**) why its **24-bit IV** is cryptographically inadequate. Show that a busy network breaks WEP in **under 2 seconds**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ÔÇö Part (a) (7 marks)**

| Property | Attack (1/2 mark) | Defense (1/2 mark) |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Eavesdropping with Wireshark | WPA2-AES / WPA3-SAE encryption |
| **Integrity** | Bit-flipping, MITM in WEP | CCMP CBC-MAC, GCM authentication tag |
| **Authentication** | Rogue AP / evil twin | 802.1X EAP-TLS, 5G AKA |
| **Availability** | Jamming, flooding | Spread spectrum, cognitive radio DSA |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Repudiation in VANET messages | ECDSA digital signatures, PKI |
| **Authorization** | Privilege escalation via rogue AP | RBAC, slice-based access in 5G |

*(6 properties ├Ś 1 mark = 6 marks + 1 mark for a coherent introduction paragraph)*

---

#### **Model Solution ÔÇö Part (b) (7 marks)**

**WEP Protocol Overview (2 marks):**

WEP uses RC4 with a 24-bit IV prepended to a 40-bit (or 104-bit) shared key. Ciphertext is:

$$C_i = P_i \oplus RC4(IV \,\|\, K)$$

CRC-32 of the plaintext is appended for integrity.

**Birthday Paradox Derivation (3 marks):**

With a 24-bit IV ($N = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$), the probability of an IV collision after $n$ frames is:

$$P(n) = 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2N)}$$

Setting $P = 0.5$ and solving for $n$:

$$n \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{N} = 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^{24}} \approx 4{,}820 \text{ frames}$$

**Time to Collision (1 mark):**

A 5 Mbps AP sends ~3,333 packets/s at 1500 B/frame:

$$t = \frac{4820}{3333} \approx 1.45 \text{ seconds}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**

A passive attacker collecting 4,820 frames (~1.5 s of traffic) obtains two ciphertexts XORed with the *same* keystream. XORing the two ciphertexts cancels the keystream and reveals $P_1 \oplus P_2$, from which plaintexts can be statistically recovered (PTW attack). Hence WEP is **trivially insecure** and was deprecated in 2003ÔÇô2004 in favor of WPA/WPA2.

---

## ÔÜá´ŞĆ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in this Module (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting Availability & Non-Repudiation** ÔÇö many students list only CIA. Always add the *wireless-specific* extensions; missing them costs 1ÔÇô2 marks per question.
> 2. **Confusing WEP with WPA** ÔÇö WEP uses **RC4 + 24-bit IV**; WPA uses **TKIP (RC4-based but with per-packet keys)**. WPA2 uses **AES-CCMP**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for any swap.
> 3. **No block diagram in derivation** ÔÇö the wiretap-channel block diagram is *mandatory* for full marks in Part B (b).
> 4. **Confusing SUPI vs. SUCI** ÔÇö SUPI = the actual permanent ID (like IMSI); SUCI = the encrypted-on-the-air version. Swap = ÔÇô1 mark.
> 5. **Writing the secrecy capacity without the $\max\{0, \cdot\}$** ÔÇö examiners consider this mathematically incomplete; always include the non-negativity clause.
> 6. **Memorizing definitions without examples** ÔÇö at least one *real attack example* (Stingray, KRACK, Evil Twin) is required per answer to score the application marks.

---

## Ôťů Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core principle:** Wireless is *inherently less secure* than wired due to the broadcast, boundary-less RF medium.
- **Security goals** = CIA + **Availability, Authentication, Non-repudiation, Authorization** (4 extensions).
- **Existing networks** = mature but with **known broken ciphers** (WEP, A5/1, A5/2).
- **Emerging networks** = secure-by-design but face **AI/quantum/slicing threats** (5G/6G, IoT, VANET, UAV, LEO satellite).
- **Shannon's channel capacity** $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$ ÔÇö base formula.
- **Secrecy capacity** $C_s = B\log_2\!\big((1+\gamma_B)/(1+\gamma_E)\big)$ ÔÇö *most important formula of this module*.
- **WEP IV collision** in **~1.5 seconds** ÔÇö proof of legacy insecurity.
- **5G SUCI** hides IMSI ÔÇö major privacy upgrade over 2G/3G/4G.
- **Defense-in-depth** = 7 layers (PLS, MAC, Network, Transport, App, Cross-Layer IDS, Policy).
- **Always draw a block diagram** for derivation questions in Part B.
- **2024 Scheme CO2:** Must demonstrate ability to *analyze, differentiate, and recommend* security for both existing and emerging wireless networks.
- **Frequent past-year tag topics:** "CIA in wireless" (Dec 2023), "WEP vs WPA2" (May 2024), "wiretap channel secrecy" (model paper 2024).
- **Always use $\max\{0, \cdot\}$** with secrecy capacity.
- **Always cite a 3GPP / IEEE / NIST standard** in your answer to gain the application-level mark (e.g., 3GPP TS 33.501, IEEE 802.11i, NIST SP 800-97).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Cryptographic Algorithms and Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Cryptographic Algorithms and Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cryptographic Algorithms ÔÇö Formal Definition

In the context of **Wireless Network Security (PECCT862)**, a **Cryptographic Algorithm** is defined as a well-defined, deterministic mathematical procedure (a sequence of rules) that transforms readable plaintext into unreadable ciphertext and back, using a controlled secret called the **key**, in order to provide confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation over insecure wireless mediums such as RF, microwave, and infrared channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> *Cryptographic algorithms are mathematical functions used to encrypt and decrypt data. They form the algorithmic foundation of every wireless security protocol (WEP, WPA2, WPA3, Bluetooth E0, Zigbee, LTE/5G-AKA, etc.).*

A **Cryptographic Protocol** is the higher-level, ordered exchange of messages between two or more wireless entities (AP Ôćö STA, UE Ôćö eNodeB, gNB Ôćö AMF) that uses one or more cryptographic algorithms to achieve a specific security objective.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Cryptography in Wireless Networks

| Pillar | What it Protects | Wireless Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Eavesdropping on the radio link | WPA3-Enterprise AES-CCMP |
| **Integrity** | Bit-flipping / replay injection | TKIP Michael MIC |
| **Authentication** | Rogue AP / impersonation | 802.1X / EAP-AKA' in 5G |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ÔÇö "The Wireless Lockbox"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ÔÇö Sending a Secret Letter Over a Park Bench:**
> Imagine you and a friend want to exchange secrets while sitting on a park bench, but the bench is in a public garden where anyone can listen.
> 
> 1. **Symmetric Key (Shared Secret):** You both own an identical physical key to a single lockbox. You lock the letter inside; only the matching key can open it. **Fast, but how do you safely pass the key across the garden?**
> 2. **Asymmetric Key (Public/Private Pair):** You give every friend a *padlock that only you can open* (your public key). They snap it shut and send the box. **Solves the key-exchange problem, but is slower.**
> 3. **Hash Function (Tamper-Evident Seal):** You press a unique wax seal on the letter. If anyone tampers with even one word, the seal breaks. **Ensures integrity.**
> 
> Wireless cryptography is the digital engineering of exactly these three ideas, scaled for billions of radio transmissions.

## 1.4 Classification of Cryptographic Algorithms

```
Cryptographic Algorithms
ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ 1. Symmetric-Key (Secret-Key)
Ôöé     ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ Block Ciphers  Ôćĺ AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish
Ôöé     ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ Stream Ciphers Ôćĺ RC4, ChaCha20, A5/1 (GSM)
ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ 2. Asymmetric-Key (Public-Key)
Ôöé     ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ RSA, ElGamal
Ôöé     ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ Elliptic Curve Ôćĺ ECC, ECDH, ECDSA
ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ 3. Hash Functions (One-Way)
Ôöé     ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ MD5 (broken), SHA-1 (deprecated)
Ôöé     ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ SHA-2 family (SHA-256/384/512), SHA-3
ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ 4. Authentication / MAC Codes
      ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ HMAC, CMAC
      ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ Digital Signatures (DSA, ECDSA, RSA-PSS)
```

## 1.5 Core Symmetric vs Asymmetric Trade-off

| Property | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
|---|---|---|
| **Keys Used** | 1 shared key | Key pair (Public + Private) |
| **Speed** | **Very Fast (~100x)** | Slow (modular exponentiation) |
| **Key Distribution** | Hard problem | Trivial (public key is public) |
| **Scalability (n users)** | n(nÔłĺ1)/2 keys | 2n keys |
| **Primary Wireless Use** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, authentication |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Modern wireless protocols (WPA3, 5G) are **hybrid cryptosystems** ÔÇö asymmetric cryptography (e.g., ECDHE) establishes a session, then symmetric cryptography (e.g., AES-256-GCM) encrypts the bulk traffic. You must state this in any 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Symmetric-Key Cryptography ÔÇö Block Ciphers in Wireless

A **block cipher** encrypts fixed-size blocks (e.g., **128 bits for AES**) of plaintext using a secret key. Block ciphers operate in standardized **modes of operation**:

| Mode | Full Name | Wireless Context |
|---|---|---|
| **ECB** | Electronic Codebook | ÔŁî Never used in wireless (patterns leak) |
| **CBC** | Cipher Block Chaining | Used in older TLS, Bluetooth Classic E0-derived |
| **CTR** | Counter | Used in WPA3-GCMP, 5G NEA1 |
| **GCM** | Galois/Counter Mode | Ôťů Modern gold standard (AES-128-GCM in WPA3) |
| **CCM** | CTR + CBC-MAC | Used in WPA2/WPA3 CCMP, Zigbee, Bluetooth LE |

### The AES Algorithm (Advanced Encryption Standard)

**AES** is the dominant symmetric cipher mandated by **NIST FIPS-197** and used in virtually every modern wireless standard (Wi-Fi 6E, 5G NR, Bluetooth 5.x).

- **Block size:** Fixed at **128 bits**
- **Key sizes:** **128, 192, or 256 bits** (AES-128, AES-192, AES-256)
- **Number of rounds:** **10, 12, or 14** respectively
- **Operations:** SubBytes (S-box substitution), ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey

$$ \text{AES}: \{0,1\}^{128} \times \{0,1\}^{k} \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{128}, \quad k \in \{128, 192, 256\} $$

## 2.2 Stream Ciphers ÔÇö Used in Legacy & Lightweight Wireless

A stream cipher generates a **keystream** $KS$ XORed with plaintext bit-by-bit:

$$ C_i = P_i \oplus KS_i \qquad P_i = C_i \oplus KS_i $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Wireless Examples:** RC4 (deprecated WEP/TKIP), ChaCha20 (modern WPA3-Personal transition), ZUC (4G/LTE confidentiality & integrity ÔÇö EEA3/EIA3).

## 2.3 Asymmetric-Key Cryptography

### RSA (RivestÔÇôShamirÔÇôAdleman)

Based on the **practical difficulty of factoring the product of two large primes**.

**Key Generation:**
1. Choose two large primes $p$ and $q$.
2. Compute modulus: $n = p \cdot q$
3. Compute Euler's totient: $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$
4. Choose public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$
5. Compute private exponent: $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$

**Operations:**
$$ C = M^{e} \bmod n \qquad M = C^{d} \bmod n $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Size:** For wireless security through 2030, RSA keys must be **Ôëą 2048 bits**. KTU examiners will penalize < 2048 in any answer.

### Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) ÔÇö Preferred for Wireless

ECC offers **equivalent security with much shorter keys**, which is critical for constrained wireless devices (IoT, RFID, sensors).

| Security Level (bits) | RSA Key Size | ECC Key Size |
|---|---|---|
| 80 | 1024 | 160 |
| 128 | 3072 | 256 |
| 192 | 7680 | 384 |
| 256 | 15360 | 512 |

**Curve equation (Weierstrass form):**
$$ y^{2} = x^{3} + ax + b \pmod{p} $$

**Core operation (point multiplication):**
$$ Q = k \cdot P $$
where $P$ is a base point and $k$ is the private scalar. Reversing this ÔÇö finding $k$ from $Q$ and $P$ ÔÇö is the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**, which is computationally infeasible for large $k$.

## 2.4 Cryptographic Hash Functions

A hash function $H$ maps arbitrary input to a fixed-size output:
$$ H: \{0,1\}^{*} \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{n} $$

**Three Security Properties (MANDATORY in KTU answers):**

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pre-image resistance** | Given $h$, hard to find $M$ s.t. $H(M)=h$ |
| **Second pre-image resistance** | Given $M_1$, hard to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ s.t. $H(M_1)=H(M_2)$ |
| **Collision resistance** | Hard to find any $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1)=H(M_2)$ |

**SHA-256 (used in Bitcoin, TLS 1.3, 5G SUCI):**
$$ H: \{0,1\}^{*} \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{256} $$

## 2.5 Message Authentication Codes (MAC) & Digital Signatures

### HMAC (Hash-based MAC)
$$ \text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\Big( (K' \oplus opad) \,\|\, H((K' \oplus ipad) \,\|\, M) \Big) $$

Used in WPA2 4-way handshake, IPsec, TLS.

### Digital Signatures (Asymmetric MAC)
$$ \sigma = \text{Sign}(d, M) = M^{d} \bmod n \quad \text{(RSA)} $$
$$ \text{Verify}(e, M, \sigma) = \text{True if } \sigma^{e} \bmod n = M $$

Provides **authentication + integrity + non-repudiation** (the key advantage over MACs).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symmetric encryption | $C = E_{K}(P)$ | $K$ shared key |
| 2 | Symmetric decryption | $P = D_{K}(C)$ | ÔÇö |
| 3 | Stream cipher | $C = P \oplus KS$ | KS = keystream |
| 4 | RSA modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | primes $p,q$ |
| 5 | RSA totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | ÔÇö |
| 6 | RSA exponent relation | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | public $e$, private $d$ |
| 7 | RSA encryption | $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ | ÔÇö |
| 8 | ECC scalar mult | $Q = k \cdot P$ | scalar $k$, point $P$ |
| 9 | DiffieÔÇôHellman shared secret | $K_{AB} = (g^{b})^{a} = (g^{a})^{b} \bmod p$ | $a,b$ private; $g,p$ public |
| 10 | Hash output | $h = H(M)$, $\vert h \vert = n$ bits | $n \in \{128, 160, 224, 256, 384, 512\}$ |
| 11 | HMAC | $\text{HMAC}(K,M)$ | keyed hash |
| 12 | AES block size | $128$ bits | fixed |
| 13 | AES key sizes | $128, 192, 256$ bits | $Nk = 4, 6, 8$ words |
| 14 | AES rounds | $Nr = 10, 12, 14$ | matches key size |

## 2.7 Real-World Wireless Utility

| Cryptographic Tool | Wireless Protocol Where It Lives |
|---|---|
| AES-128-CCM | WPA2/WPA3 Wi-Fi, Bluetooth LE, Zigbee |
| ChaCha20-Poly1305 | WPA3-Personal, TLS 1.3 over Wi-Fi |
| ECC (P-256, Curve25519) | 5G-AKA, Bluetooth LE Secure Connections |
| RSA-2048 | TLS handshakes (legacy Wi-Fi EAP-TLS) |
| SHA-256/384 | WPA3 SAE, 5G SUPI/SUCI, TLS 1.3 |
| HMAC-SHA-256 | TLS record MAC, IPsec ESP |
| ZUC | 4G LTE (China), some 5G NEA3 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example ÔÇö RSA Key Generation & Encryption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Any 7-mark sub-question on RSA must show all five steps below ÔÇö generation, encryption, decryption ÔÇö or marks are docked for missing totient/exponent verification.

**Problem:** Alice generates an RSA key pair using primes $p = 61$ and $q = 53$. Bob sends her the plaintext integer $M = 42$. Perform full RSA encryption and decryption.

### Step 1 ÔÇö Compute the modulus $n$
$$ n = p \times q = 61 \times 53 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} 61 \times 53 &= 61 \times (50 + 3) \\ &= 3050 + 183 \\ &= 3233 \end{aligned} $$

So $n = 3233$.

### Step 2 ÔÇö Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$
$$ \phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (61 - 1)(53 - 1) = 60 \times 52 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} 60 \times 52 &= 60 \times (50 + 2) \\ &= 3000 + 120 \\ &= 3120 \end{aligned} $$

So $\phi(n) = 3120$.

### Step 3 ÔÇö Choose Public Exponent $e$
We need $e$ coprime to $\phi(n) = 3120$. The KTU-favored choice (and FIPS standard) is:
$$ e = 17 $$
Verify: $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$ since $3120 = 17 \times 183 + 9$, $17 = 9 \times 1 + 8$, $9 = 8 \times 1 + 1$, $8 = 1 \times 8 + 0 \Rightarrow \gcd = 1$. Ôťô

### Step 4 ÔÇö Compute Private Exponent $d$
Solve:
$$ e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 17 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120} $$

Using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:

$$ \begin{aligned} 3120 &= 17 \times 183 + 9 \\ 17 &= 9 \times 1 + 8 \\ 9 &= 8 \times 1 + 1 \\ 8 &= 1 \times 8 + 0 \end{aligned} $$

Back-substitute:
$$ \begin{aligned} 1 &= 9 - 8 \times 1 \\ &= 9 - (17 - 9 \times 1) \times 1 \\ &= 9 \times 2 - 17 \times 1 \\ &= (3120 - 17 \times 183) \times 2 - 17 \times 1 \\ &= 3120 \times 2 - 17 \times 366 - 17 \times 1 \\ &= 3120 \times 2 - 17 \times 367 \end{aligned} $$

Therefore:
$$ d = -367 \equiv 3120 - 367 = 2753 \pmod{3120} $$

So the **private exponent** $d = 2753$.

**Public Key:** $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$
**Private Key:** $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$

### Step 5 ÔÇö Encryption (Bob Ôćĺ Alice, using Alice's public key)
$$ C = M^{e} \bmod n = 42^{17} \bmod 3233 $$

Compute stepwise using **modular exponentiation** (square-and-multiply):

$$ \begin{aligned} 42^{1} \bmod 3233 &= 42 \\ 42^{2} \bmod 3233 &= 1764 \\ 42^{4} \bmod 3233 &= 1764^{2} \bmod 3233 = 3111696 \bmod 3233 \\ &= 3111696 - 3233 \times 962 = 3111696 - 3110146 = 1550 \\ 42^{8} \bmod 3233 &= 1550^{2} \bmod 3233 = 2402500 \bmod 3233 \\ &= 2402500 - 3233 \times 743 = 2402500 - 2402119 = 381 \\ 42^{16} \bmod 3233 &= 381^{2} \bmod 3233 = 145161 \bmod 3233 = 145161 - 3233 \times 44 = 145161 - 142252 = 2909 \end{aligned} $$

Combine for $17 = 16 + 1$:
$$ \begin{aligned} 42^{17} \bmod 3233 &= 42^{16} \times 42^{1} \bmod 3233 \\ &= 2909 \times 42 \bmod 3233 \\ &= 122178 \bmod 3233 \\ &= 122178 - 3233 \times 37 = 122178 - 119621 = 2557 \end{aligned} $$

So ciphertext $C = 2557$.

### Step 6 ÔÇö Decryption (Alice recovers plaintext)
$$ M = C^{d} \bmod n = 2557^{2753} \bmod 3233 $$

By the RSA correctness theorem:
$$ M = 42 \quad \text{(Ôťô plaintext recovered)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Shortcut Allowed:** For exam time, you may state $M = 2557^{2753} \bmod 3233 = 42$ and cite the Euler's theorem guarantee $M^{ed} \equiv M^{1+k\phi(n)} \equiv M \pmod{n}$, provided you write out at least one or two intermediate square-and-multiply steps.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example ÔÇö DiffieÔÇôHellman Key Exchange

**Problem:** Prime $p = 23$, generator $g = 5$. Alice picks secret $a = 6$, Bob picks secret $b = 15$. Compute the shared secret.

### Public Parameters
$$ p = 23, \quad g = 5 $$

### Step 1 ÔÇö Alice computes her public value $A$
$$ A = g^{a} \bmod p = 5^{6} \bmod 23 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} 5^{2} &= 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23} \\ 5^{4} &\equiv 2^{2} = 4 \pmod{23} \\ 5^{6} &\equiv 5^{4} \cdot 5^{2} \equiv 4 \times 2 = 8 \pmod{23} \end{aligned} $$

So $A = 8$.

### Step 2 ÔÇö Bob computes his public value $B$
$$ B = g^{b} \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} 5^{8} &\equiv 5^{4} \cdot 5^{4} \equiv 4 \times 4 = 16 \equiv -7 \pmod{23} \\ 5^{15} &= 5^{8} \cdot 5^{4} \cdot 5^{2} \cdot 5^{1} \\ &\equiv (-7) \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \\ &= -280 \\ &\equiv -280 + 23 \times 13 = -280 + 299 = 19 \pmod{23} \end{aligned} $$

So $B = 19$.

### Step 3 ÔÇö Both compute the shared secret
Alice:
$$ K_{AB} = B^{a} \bmod p = 19^{6} \bmod 23 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} 19^{2} &= 361 = 23 \times 15 + 16 \equiv 16 \equiv -7 \pmod{23} \\ 19^{4} &\equiv (-7)^{2} = 49 \equiv 49 - 46 = 3 \pmod{23} \\ 19^{6} &\equiv 19^{4} \cdot 19^{2} \equiv 3 \times (-7) = -21 \equiv 2 \pmod{23} \end{aligned} $$

Bob:
$$ K_{AB} = A^{b} \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} 8^{2} &= 64 \equiv 64 - 46 = 18 \equiv -5 \pmod{23} \\ 8^{4} &\equiv (-5)^{2} = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23} \\ 8^{8} &\equiv 2^{2} = 4 \pmod{23} \\ 8^{15} &= 8^{8} \cdot 8^{4} \cdot 8^{2} \cdot 8^{1} \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot (-5) \cdot 8 \\ &= -320 \equiv -320 + 23 \times 14 = -320 + 322 = 2 \pmod{23} \end{aligned} $$

Both arrive at:
$$ K_{AB} = 2 \quad \checkmark $$

This shared value $K_{AB} = 2$ becomes the symmetric session key (often after a KDF) for encrypting all subsequent wireless traffic.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Classical DiffieÔÇôHellman is vulnerable to **man-in-the-middle (MITM)** attacks because neither party authenticates the other. Wireless standards fix this by using **authenticated variants** like ECDHE + digital signatures (e.g., TLS 1.3, WPA3 SAE).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ÔÇö Algorithm Selection for Wireless Scenarios

| Wireless Scenario | Best Algorithm | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Bulk Wi-Fi data encryption | **AES-128 in GCM/CCM** | Hardware acceleration (AES-NI), authenticated |
| LTE/5G control signaling | **SNOW 3G, ZUC, AES** | 3GPP standardized NEA1/NEA2/NEA3 |
| IoT sensor (8-bit MCU) | **ChaCha20 or AES-128** | Lightweight, low-power |
| Key establishment over hostile RF | **ECDH on P-256** | Short keys (256-bit) for low-bandwidth handshake |
| Firmware integrity check | **SHA-256** | 128-bit collision resistance, widely audited |
| Rogue AP detection | **ECDSA signature** | Non-repudiation on AP certificate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Symmetric Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plaintext[PLAINTEXT P] --> encryptBlock[ENCRYPTION ALGORITHM E]
    keyIn[SECRET KEY K] --> encryptBlock
    encryptBlock --> ciphertext[CIPHERTEXT C]
    ciphertext --> decryptBlock[DECRYPTION ALGORITHM D]
    keyIn2[SECRET KEY K] --> decryptBlock
    decryptBlock --> recovered[RECOVERED PLAINTEXT P]
```

## 4.2 Asymmetric (Public-Key) Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    senderMsg[PLAINTEXT M] --> encAsym[RSA or ECC ENCRYPTION]
    pubKey[RECIPIENT PUBLIC KEY e,n] --> encAsym
    encAsym --> cipherAsym[CIPHERTEXT C]
    cipherAsym --> decAsym[RSA or ECC DECRYPTION]
    privKey[RECIPIENT PRIVATE KEY d,n] --> decAsym
    decAsym --> recvPlain[RECOVERED MESSAGE M]
```

## 4.3 Hybrid Cryptosystem Flow (Used in WPA3, 5G, TLS 1.3)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Session Begins]) --> asymStep[STEP 1: ASYMMETRIC KEY EXCHANGE]
    asymStep --> ekd[ECDH or RSA Generates Shared Secret]
    ekd --> kdf[KDF DERIVES SESSION KEY]
    kdf --> symStep[STEP 2: SYMMETRIC BULK ENCRYPTION]
    symStep --> aes[AES or ChaCha20 Encrypts Data]
    aes --> mac[HMAC or AEAD Tag Provides Integrity]
    mac --> endSess([Encrypted Secure Session])
```

## 4.4 Cryptographic Protocol Stack for Wireless Networks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        a1[HTTP, MQTT, CoAP, VoIP]
    end
    subgraph SEC["SECURITY PROTOCOL LAYER"]
        s1[TLS 1.3 or DTLS 1.3]
        s2[IPsec ESP and AH]
        s3[802.1X EAP AKA]
    end
    subgraph CRYPTO["CRYPTOGRAPHIC ALGORITHM LAYER"]
        c1[AES, ChaCha20, ZUC]
        c2[ECC, RSA, DH]
        c3[SHA 256, HMAC]
    end
    subgraph PHY["WIRELESS PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        p1[Wi-Fi Radio]
        p2[5G NR Radio]
        p3[Bluetooth Radio]
    end
    APP --> SEC --> CRYPTO --> PHY
```

## 4.5 Hash Function Operational Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputMsg[INPUT MESSAGE M ANY LENGTH] --> hashFx[HASH FUNCTION H]
    hashFx --> digestOut[FIXED DIGEST H M N BITS]
    inputMsg2[INPUT MESSAGE M PRIME TAMPERED] --> hashFx2[HASH FUNCTION H]
    hashFx2 --> digestOut2[COMPLETELY DIFFERENT DIGEST]
```

## 4.6 End-to-End Wireless Security Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    client[WIRELESS CLIENT STA or UE] -->|1 ASYMMETRIC HANDSHAKE| ap[ACCESS POINT or gNB]
    ap -->|2 CERTIFICATE and SIGNATURE| client
    client -->|3 VERIFY and DERIVE SESSION KEY| client
    ap -->|3 VERIFY and DERIVE SESSION KEY| ap
    client -->|4 AES SYMMETRIC ENCRYPTION| ap
    ap -->|5 DECRYPT and FORWARD| core[NETWORK CORE or SERVER]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ÔÇö 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô May 2024]`
**Define a cryptographic hash function. List its three key security properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A cryptographic hash function $H$ is a one-way mathematical function that maps an input of arbitrary length to a fixed-size output called a *message digest* (typically 128ÔÇô512 bits).

The three key security properties are:
1. **Pre-image resistance** ÔÇö Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find $M$ such that $H(M) = h$.
2. **Second pre-image resistance** ÔÇö Given $M_1$, it is infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
3. **Collision resistance** ÔÇö It is infeasible to find any two distinct inputs $M_1 \neq M_2$ with the same hash value $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

**Example:** SHA-256 (used in WPA3 SAE and 5G SUCI).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key cryptography.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
|---|---|---|
| Keys | Single shared secret | PublicÔÇôprivate key pair |
| Speed | Fast (AES Ôëł GB/s) | Slow (modular exponentiation) |
| Key distribution | Difficult | Easy (public key is open) |
| Example | AES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC |
| Wireless use | Bulk data encryption | Authentication, key exchange |

Modern wireless systems use **hybrid cryptography** ÔÇö asymmetric for key establishment, symmetric for bulk data.

---

## Part B ÔÇö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ÔÇö Choice 1

#### `(a)` **[7 Marks]** Explain the RSA algorithm with a neat block diagram. Perform RSA encryption/decryption for $p=5$, $q=11$, $e=3$, $M=9$. Verify the recovered plaintext.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ÔÇö Key Generation:**
$$ n = p \cdot q = 5 \times 11 = 55 $$
$$ \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \times 10 = 40 $$

Given $e = 3$. Verify $\gcd(3, 40) = 1$ Ôťô.

Find $d$ such that $3d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$:
$$ 3d - 40k = 1 \Rightarrow d = 27 \quad (3 \times 27 = 81 = 40 \times 2 + 1) \checkmark $$

**Public key:** $(3, 55)$; **Private key:** $(27, 55)$.

**Step 2 ÔÇö Encryption:**
$$ C = M^{e} \bmod n = 9^{3} \bmod 55 = 729 \bmod 55 $$

$$ 729 = 55 \times 13 + 14 \Rightarrow C = 14 $$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Decryption:**
$$ M = C^{d} \bmod n = 14^{27} \bmod 55 $$

By RSA theorem: $M = 9$ Ôťô.

**Block Diagram:** (refer to Section 4.2 ÔÇö Public-Key Encryption Diagram).

**Mark split:** Key gen 3 + Encryption 2 + Decryption 2.

---

#### `(b)` **[7 Marks]** Describe the DiffieÔÇôHellman key exchange algorithm. Discuss its vulnerability and how it is mitigated in modern wireless protocols (WPA3/5G).

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm:**
1. Public parameters: prime $p$, generator $g$.
2. Alice picks secret $a$, sends $A = g^{a} \bmod p$.
3. Bob picks secret $b$, sends $B = g^{b} \bmod p$.
4. Alice computes $K = B^{a} \bmod p$. Bob computes $K = A^{b} \bmod p$.
5. Both obtain the same shared secret $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$.

**Mathematical Security:** Based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem** ÔÇö given $g$, $p$, $A$, computing $a$ is infeasible.

**Vulnerability ÔÇö Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack:** An attacker Mallory can sit between Alice and Bob, exchange independent DH values with each, and establish two separate session keys ÔÇö fully decrypting, re-encrypting, and forwarding traffic without detection.

**Mitigation in Wireless Standards:**
- **WPA3-Personal** uses **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)** ÔÇö a password-authenticated key exchange (PAKE) that performs a zero-knowledge proof to mutually authenticate before DH.
- **5G-AKA** uses **ECDH + EPS-AKA** with operator-issued authentication vectors.
- **TLS 1.3** uses **ECDHE** combined with **digital signatures** on the DH exchange to bind identities.

**Mark split:** DH steps 3 + MITM explanation 2 + Mitigation 2.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ÔÇö Choice 2

#### `(a)` **[7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of AES-128. List the four transformations performed in each round.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pt[128-BIT PLAINTEXT] --> addRK0[AddRoundKey K0]
    addRK0 --> r1[ROUND 1 to ROUND 10]
    r1 --> ct[128-BIT CIPHERTEXT]
    keyExp[KEY EXPANSION] --> addRK0
    keyExp --> r1
```

**Round Transformations (4 per round):**
1. **SubBytes** ÔÇö Byte-wise nonlinear substitution via a fixed $16 \times 16$ S-box.
2. **ShiftRows** ÔÇö Cyclic left-shift of the last three rows of the 4├Ś4 state matrix.
3. **MixColumns** ÔÇö Linear mixing of each column over $\text{GF}(2^{8})$.
4. **AddRoundKey** ÔÇö XOR of the state with the round key derived from key expansion.

**Parameters for AES-128:**
- Block size: 128 bits
- Key size: 128 bits ($Nk = 4$ words)
- Number of rounds: $Nr = 10$
- Total round keys: $Nr + 1 = 11$ (128-bit each)

**Why AES in wireless:** Native hardware support (AES-NI) in modern Wi-Fi/5G chipsets; constant-time implementation; NSA Suite-B approved.

**Mark split:** Diagram 2 + Four transformations 3 + Parameters 2.

---

#### `(b)` **[7 Marks]** Explain the HMAC construction. Why is HMAC preferred over a plain hash in wireless authentication handshakes (e.g., WPA2 4-way handshake)?

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
$$ \text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\Big( (K' \oplus opad) \,\|\, H((K' \oplus ipad) \,\|\, M) \Big) $$

where:
- $K'$ = key padded/hashed to block length
- $ipad$ = inner padding (0x36 repeated)
- $opad$ = outer padding (0x5C repeated)
- $H$ = underlying hash (e.g., SHA-256)

**Working Steps:**
1. If $|K| < B$, pad $K$ to block size $B$; if longer, hash and pad.
2. Compute $S_i = K' \oplus ipad$.
3. Compute inner hash: $H_1 = H(S_i \,\|\, M)$.
4. Compute $S_o = K' \oplus opad$.
5. Compute outer hash: $\text{HMAC} = H(S_o \,\|\, H_1)$.

**Why HMAC over plain hash in wireless handshakes:**
1. **Keyed authentication:** A plain $H(M)$ provides only integrity, not authentication. HMAC binds a secret key $K$, proving the message originated from a holder of $K$.
2. **Prevents replay/injection:** The 4-way handshake exchanges HMAC-SHA-256 of the ANonce, SNonce, and MAC addresses ÔÇö preventing rogue AP impersonation.
3. **Length-extension resistance:** HMAC is immune to length-extension attacks that plague plain $H(K \,\|\, M)$ constructions.
4. **Standardization:** Mandated by IEEE 802.11i and 3GPP for control-plane authentication.

**Mark split:** Construction 3 + Wireless justification 4.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in PECCT862 Cryptography Answers:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing MAC with digital signature** ÔÇö MAC = symmetric (no non-repudiation); Signature = asymmetric (with non-repudiation). Examiners deduct 2 marks if swapped.
> 2. **Omitting totient verification in RSA** ÔÇö Always show $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
> 3. **Calling AES a stream cipher** ÔÇö AES is a **block cipher**; modes like CTR make it act as a stream.
> 4. **Missing hybrid design explanation** ÔÇö In any 14-mark question on wireless security, *always* mention that protocols use asymmetric for key exchange + symmetric for bulk data.
> 5. **Using MD5/SHA-1** in modern answers ÔÇö these are **broken/deprecated**. Use SHA-256 or stronger.
> 6. **Forgetting AEAD** ÔÇö When discussing WPA3, state that GCM/CCM provide *Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data* (confidentiality + integrity in one operation).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cryptography** = algorithms (math); **Protocols** = ordered message exchanges using those algorithms.
- **Three services:** confidentiality, integrity, authentication (+ non-repudiation for signatures).
- **Symmetric** is fast (AES, ChaCha20) ÔÇö used for bulk wireless data.
- **Asymmetric** is slow but solves key distribution ÔÇö RSA, ECC.
- **Hybrid design** is universal in wireless (WPA3, 5G, TLS 1.3).
- **RSA** security rests on integer factorization; key size **Ôëą 2048 bits** mandatory.
- **ECC** security rests on the ECDLP; **256-bit ECC Ôëł 3072-bit RSA**.
- **DiffieÔÇôHellman** provides key agreement but **no authentication** ÔÇö fix with ECDHE + signatures or PAKE (SAE).
- **Hash functions** give integrity: SHA-256 is the modern wireless default.
- **HMAC** = keyed hash for authentication; resists length-extension attacks.
- **Digital signatures** = asymmetric MAC with non-repudiation.
- **AES-128** = 10 rounds, 128-bit block, 128-bit key; mandated by 802.11i and 3GPP.
- **Block cipher modes:** avoid ECB; prefer GCM (AEAD) for wireless.
- **Stream ciphers** XOR keystream: RC4 (broken), ChaCha20 (modern), ZUC (4G/5G NEA3).
- Always state **key sizes, parameters, and a wireless context** ÔÇö KTU rewards applied answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Game Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Game Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Game Theory** is a branch of applied mathematics and decision sciences that studies **strategic interactions** between *rational decision-makers* (called **players**), where the outcome for each player depends not only on their own actions but also on the actions chosen by others. In the context of **wireless network security**, game theory provides a rigorous mathematical framework to model the adversarial interaction between a **legitimate user / defender** and a **malicious entity / attacker** (e.g., a jammer, eavesdropper, or intruder).

A **game** in its standard form (Normal Form / Strategic Form) is formally defined as the triplet:

$$G = \{ \mathcal{N}, \{\mathcal{S}_i\}_{i \in \mathcal{N}}, \{u_i\}_{i \in \mathcal{N}} \}$$

where:
- $\mathcal{N} = \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$ is the **finite set of players**.
- $\mathcal{S}_i$ is the **strategy set** (action set) of player $i$.
- $u_i : \mathcal{S}_1 \times \mathcal{S}_2 \times \dots \times \mathcal{S}_N \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the **payoff / utility function** of player $i$, which quantifies the reward (or penalty) received by player $i$ for every possible combination of actions chosen by all players.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The objective is not to solve a single optimization problem, but to model **mutual, conflicting, or cooperative** decision-making. In wireless security, the "game" is typically played between a **Defender (D)** and an **Attacker (A)**, where the defender wishes to maximize throughput / security and the attacker wishes to minimize the same.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two friends, **Aravind** and **Bhavya**, deciding where to have dinner. If both want *Biryani*, both are happy. If one wants *Biryani* and the other wants *Dosa*, the Biryani-lover is ecstatic but the Dosa-lover is disappointed. Notice that **Aravind's satisfaction does not depend only on his own choice ÔÇö it also depends on what Bhavya chooses.** This *interdependence of outcomes* is precisely what Game Theory captures mathematically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Wireless Security:** A legitimate transmitter choosing its **transmission power** and an eavesdropper choosing its **eavesdropping strategy** are *interdependent* decisions. Game theory allows the defender to *predict* the attacker's best response and choose an optimal counter-strategy, often yielding a stable pair of strategies called the **Nash Equilibrium**.

## 1.3 Fundamental Building Blocks

| Terminology | KTU Definition | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Player** | A rational decision-making entity in the game (e.g., transmitter, jammer, legitimate user). | "Who is playing?" |
| **Strategy** $s_i$ | A complete plan of action available to player $i$. | "What can each player do?" |
| **Strategy Set** $\mathcal{S}_i$ | The complete menu of strategies available to player $i$. | "All the moves a player can pick from." |
| **Payoff / Utility** $u_i$ | A numerical score representing player $i$'s preference over outcomes. | "How happy is the player with the result?" |
| **Rational Player** | A player who always selects a strategy that maximizes their own expected payoff. | "Players never play randomly; they play to win." |
| **Common Knowledge** | All players know the game, the strategies, and that the others know too ÔÇö *ad infinitum*. | "Everyone sees the same rulebook." |
| **Perfect Information** | Every player, when making a move, knows the full history of previous moves. | "No hidden cards on the table." |
| **Complete Information** | The structure of the game (payoff functions) is known to all players. | "The rulebook is public." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (must be memorized):** Payoffs are unitless mathematical scores ÔÇö they are not measured in Watts, dBm, or bps. However, in wireless applications, payoff is often mapped to quantities like **SINR (dB)**, **throughput (bps/Hz)**, or **bit error rate (BER)** through utility functions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Games (KTU High-Yield Taxonomy)

Game theory classifies strategic interactions along several orthogonal axes. The KTU examiner frequently tests your ability to correctly identify the *type* of a game.

### 2.1.1 Based on Cooperation

**A. Non-Cooperative Game**
- Players act **independently** and pursue only their own self-interest.
- No binding agreements exist between players.
- Mathematical tool used: **Nash Equilibrium (NE)**.
- *Wireless security example:* Transmitter vs. Eavesdropper ÔÇö neither can sign a contract with the other.

**B. Cooperative Game**
- Players are allowed to form **coalitions** and make **binding agreements**.
- The focus is on how the total payoff is **distributed** among coalition members.
- Mathematical tool used: **Shapley Value, Core, Bargaining Solutions**.
- *Wireless security example:* A group of legitimate nodes forming a cooperative cluster to detect an intruder.

### 2.1.2 Based on Sum of Payoffs

**A. Zero-Sum Game**
- One player's gain is *exactly* the other player's loss.
- Formally: $u_1(s) + u_2(s) = 0$ for every strategy profile $s$.
- *Wireless security example:* Jamming game ÔÇö the jammer's success is the legitimate user's failure.

**B. Non-Zero-Sum (General-Sum) Game**
- The sum of utilities is *not* constant; both can win or both can lose.
- *Wireless security example:* An attacker intrudes; defender invests in IDS. Both spending security resources changes total welfare.

### 2.1.3 Based on Timing of Moves

**A. Static (Simultaneous-Move) Game**
- All players choose their strategies **simultaneously** without knowledge of the others' current choices.
- Represented as a **payoff matrix**.
- *Wireless security example:* Transmitter and eavesdropper pick power levels at the same instant.

**B. Dynamic (Sequential-Move) Game**
- Players move one after another, and later players observe earlier moves.
- Represented as an **extensive-form game tree**.
- *Wireless security example:* Attacker probes, defender responds, attacker re-probes.

### 2.1.4 Based on Information

**A. Complete Information Game**
- The payoff functions are **common knowledge** to all players.
- *Example:* Both nodes know the channel gain and noise power.

**B. Incomplete (Asymmetric) Information Game**
- At least one player has **private information** (a "type") unknown to others.
- Modeled as a **Bayesian Game** using Harsanyi's transformation.
- *Wireless security example:* Defender does not know whether the attacker is *rational* or *malicious*.

## 2.2 Solution Concepts ÔÇö The Heart of KTU Questions

### 2.2.1 Dominant Strategy

A strategy $s_i^* \in \mathcal{S}_i$ is **strictly dominant** for player $i$ if, for *every* strategy $s_j$ of the opponent and *every* alternative strategy $s_i' \in \mathcal{S}_i$:

$$u_i(s_i^*, s_j) > u_i(s_i', s_j) \quad \forall s_i' \neq s_i^*, \; \forall s_j \in \mathcal{S}_{-i}$$

**Intuition:** "No matter what the opponent does, this strategy gives me the highest payoff ÔÇö so I play it *unconditionally*."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A dominant strategy equilibrium is *stronger* than a Nash Equilibrium. If a dominant strategy exists for every player, then the game has a *unique* solution ÔÇö the *Dominant Strategy Equilibrium* ÔÇö which is automatically a Nash Equilibrium, but the converse is **not true**.

### 2.2.2 Nash Equilibrium (NE) ÔÇö The Most-Tested Concept

A strategy profile $s^* = (s_1^*, s_2^*, \dots, s_N^*)$ is a **Nash Equilibrium** if and only if no player can **unilaterally deviate** from $s_i^*$ and obtain a strictly higher payoff, assuming all other players stick to $s_{-i}^*$. Formally:

$$u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*) \quad \forall s_i \in \mathcal{S}_i, \; \forall i \in \mathcal{N}$$

**Intuition:** "At a Nash Equilibrium, *every* player is doing the *best they can* given what everyone else is doing. No one has a *unilateral* incentive to deviate." This is a state of *mutual best response*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence & Uniqueness (KTU Favorite):** Every finite strategic-form game has **at least one mixed-strategy Nash Equilibrium** (Nash's Theorem, 1950). However, a *pure-strategy* NE may or may not exist, and even if it exists, it may *not be unique*.

### 2.2.3 Pure vs. Mixed Strategies

- **Pure Strategy:** The player picks a *single* action with certainty.
- **Mixed Strategy:** The player picks actions according to a **probability distribution** $\sigma_i$ over $\mathcal{S}_i$. The expected payoff is computed as:

$$\mathbb{E}[u_i(\sigma_1, \sigma_2)] = \sum_{s_1 \in \mathcal{S}_1} \sum_{s_2 \in \mathcal{S}_2} u_i(s_1, s_2) \cdot \sigma_1(s_1) \cdot \sigma_2(s_2)$$

In a 2-player 2-action game, let player 2 mix between A and B with probabilities $q$ and $(1-q)$. Player 1's mixed NE strategy makes the opponent *indifferent* between their two pure actions. This gives the **Indifference Condition**:

$$u_1(A, A) \cdot q + u_1(A, B) \cdot (1-q) = u_1(B, A) \cdot q + u_1(B, B) \cdot (1-q)$$

Solving for $q$ yields the NE mixing probability of player 2.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility in Wireless Security

Game theory is **not abstract** in this course ÔÇö it is the *primary analytical tool* of the module. Specific applications include:

1. **Anti-Jamming Games:** Defender chooses power/frequency; jammer chooses jamming strategy. Solved via Stackelberg / Nash games.
2. **Physical-Layer Security:** Legitimate transmitter vs. passive eavesdropper; solved using zero-sum or Stackelberg formulations.
3. **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS):** Defender (sensor network) vs. attacker (intruder); modeled as pursuit-evasion games.
4. **Spectrum Access:** Cognitive radio users competing for idle channels; modeled as potential / congestion games.
5. **Trust / Reputation Management:** Helper nodes vs. selfish/malicious nodes; modeled as Bayesian repeated games.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Game Definition | $G = \{\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{S}, u\}$ | $\mathcal{N}$: players, $\mathcal{S}$: strategy set, $u$: payoff |
| 2 | Dominant Strategy | $u_i(s_i^*, s_j) > u_i(s_i', s_j) \;\forall s_i', s_j$ | Strict inequality ÔÇö must hold for **all** opponent moves |
| 3 | Nash Equilibrium | $u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*)$ | Non-strict ($\geq$); unilateral deviation test |
| 4 | Zero-Sum Condition | $u_1(s) + u_2(s) = 0 \;\forall s$ | Constant sum implies competitive game |
| 5 | Expected Mixed Payoff | $\mathbb{E}[u_i] = \sum_{s} u_i(s) \prod_{j} \sigma_j(s_j)$ | Sum over pure strategy profiles |
| 6 | Indifference Condition | $u_i(s_1, s_{-i}^*) \cdot p = u_i(s_2, s_{-i}^*) \cdot p$ | Solved for the mixing probability $p$ |
| 7 | Best-Response Set | $BR_i(s_{-i}) = \arg\max_{s_i \in \mathcal{S}_i} u_i(s_i, s_{-i})$ | NE = $\bigcap_{i} BR_i(s_{-i}^*)$ |
| 8 | Stackelberg Leader Payoff | $u_L(s_L, BR_F(s_L))$ | Leader anticipates follower's best response |
| 9 | Bayesian Expected Payoff | $\mathbb{E}[u_i] = \sum_{\theta_{-i}} u_i(\cdot) \cdot P(\theta_{-i})$ | Sum weighted by belief probabilities |
| 10 | Pareto Efficiency | No $s'$ such that $u_i(s') \geq u_i(s^*) \;\forall i$ with strict for some $i$ | "Cannot improve one without hurting another" |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse $\geq$ (NE condition) with $>$ (Strict Dominance). The Nash Equilibrium allows *ties* ÔÇö a player may be indifferent between staying and deviating. Using strict inequality incorrectly turns a valid NE into a non-existent one.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU Module-2 derivation standard requires you to write out every algebraic step with verbal justification. We demonstrate this rigorously below using the canonical *Prisoner's Dilemma* and a *2x2 Wireless Jamming Game*.**

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ÔÇö The Prisoner's Dilemma (Pure-Strategy NE)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Two suspects (Alice = Attacker, Bob = Defender-analogue) are interrogated separately. Each can either **Confess (C)** or **Stay Silent (S)**. The payoff matrix $(u_A, u_B)$ is:

| Alice \ Bob | **Confess (C)** | **Silent (S)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Confess (C)** | $(-3, -3)$ | $(0, -5)$ |
| **Silent (S)** | $(-5, 0)$ | $(-1, -1)$ |

Higher (less negative) numbers are better. We must find the *pure-strategy Nash Equilibrium*.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ÔÇö Identify the strategy set.**
For each player $i \in \{A, B\}$: $\mathcal{S}_i = \{C, S\}$. Total strategy profiles $= 2 \times 2 = 4$.

**Step 2 ÔÇö Apply the NE unilateral-deviation test to (C, C).**

Check Alice: given Bob plays $C$, can Alice improve by switching to $S$?

$$u_A(S, C) = -5 \quad \text{vs.} \quad u_A(C, C) = -3$$

Since $-3 > -5$, Alice does **not** deviate. Ôťô

Check Bob: given Alice plays $C$, can Bob improve by switching to $S$?

$$u_B(C, S) = -5 \quad \text{vs.} \quad u_B(C, C) = -3$$

Since $-3 > -5$, Bob does **not** deviate. Ôťô

Hence $(C, C)$ satisfies the Nash condition $u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*)$ for both $i$.

**Step 3 ÔÇö Test for Dominant Strategy (to confirm uniqueness).**

Alice's column-wise best response (reading her payoff from the matrix):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If Bob plays C: } & \max\{u_A(C,C), u_A(S,C)\} = \max\{-3, -5\} = -3 \Rightarrow C \\
\text{If Bob plays S: } & \max\{u_A(C,S), u_A(S,S)\} = \max\{0, -1\} = 0 \Rightarrow C
\end{aligned}$$

So $C$ is **strictly dominant** for Alice.

**Step 4 ÔÇö Symmetry argument.**

By the symmetry of the payoff matrix (both players face identical row/column structures), $C$ is also strictly dominant for Bob. Therefore the unique dominant-strategy equilibrium is $(C, C)$.

**Step 5 ÔÇö Pareto check (a famous paradox).**

The profile $(S, S)$ yields payoffs $(-1, -1)$, which **Pareto-dominates** $(C, C)$ yielding $(-3, -3)$. Yet $(S, S)$ is **not** an equilibrium ÔÇö either player would deviate to $C$ to grab $0$ over $-1$. This gap between *individual rationality* and *collective optimality* is the essence of the Prisoner's Dilemma.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conclusion:** The unique pure-strategy NE is $(C, C)$ with payoffs $(-3, -3)$. This is *not* Pareto-optimal ÔÇö illustrating that Nash equilibria need not be socially efficient.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ÔÇö 2x2 Wireless Jamming Game (Mixed NE)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A legitimate transmitter (Player 1 = **Defender**) and a jammer (Player 2 = **Attacker**) play the following zero-sum jamming game simultaneously. Defender chooses *Low Power (L)* or *High Power (H)*. Jammer chooses *Jam (J)* or *No-Jam (N)*. The Defender's payoff matrix is given below (Jammer's payoff is the negative of these):

| Defender \ Jammer | **Jam (J)** | **No-Jam (N)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Low (L)** | $-2$ | $4$ |
| **High (H)** | $1$ | $-1$ |

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ÔÇö Check for pure-strategy NE.**

- At $(L, J)$: Defender gets $-2$. Switching to $H$ gives $1 > -2$. Defender deviates. ÔťŚ
- At $(L, N)$: Defender gets $4$. Switching to $H$ gives $-1 < 4$. Defender stays. Ôťô Jammer at $(L, N)$ gets $-4$. Switching to $J$ gives $+2 > -4$. Jammer deviates. ÔťŚ
- At $(H, J)$: Defender gets $1$. Switching to $L$ gives $-2 < 1$. Defender stays. Ôťô Jammer at $(H, J)$ gets $-1$. Switching to $N$ gives $+1 > -1$. Jammer deviates. ÔťŚ
- At $(H, N)$: Defender gets $-1$. Switching to $L$ gives $4 > -1$. Defender deviates. ÔťŚ

**No pure-strategy NE exists.** We must find a **mixed-strategy NE**.

**Step 2 ÔÇö Set up the indifference condition for the Defender.**

Let the Jammer mix with probability $p$ for $J$ and $(1-p)$ for $N$. The Defender's expected payoffs for $L$ and $H$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[u_D(L)] &= -2 \cdot p + 4 \cdot (1-p) = 4 - 6p \\
\mathbb{E}[u_D(H)] &= 1 \cdot p + (-1) \cdot (1-p) = 2p - 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Apply the Indifference Condition** $\mathbb{E}[u_D(L)] = \mathbb{E}[u_D(H)]$.

$$4 - 6p = 2p - 1$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Solve for $p$.**

$$4 + 1 = 2p + 6p \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5 = 8p \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \frac{5}{8}$$

**Step 5 ÔÇö Set up the indifference condition for the Jammer.**

Let the Defender mix with probability $q$ for $L$ and $(1-q)$ for $H$. The Jammer's expected payoffs (note: Jammer's payoff = $-$ Defender's payoff):

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[u_J(J)] &= 2 \cdot q + (-1) \cdot (1-q) = 3q - 1 \\
\mathbb{E}[u_J(N)] &= -4 \cdot q + 1 \cdot (1-q) = 1 - 5q
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 ÔÇö Apply the Indifference Condition** $\mathbb{E}[u_J(J)] = \mathbb{E}[u_J(N)]$.

$$3q - 1 = 1 - 5q$$

**Step 7 ÔÇö Solve for $q$.**

$$3q + 5q = 1 + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 8q = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad q = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 8 ÔÇö State the mixed-strategy NE.**

$$\sigma_D^* = \left(\tfrac{1}{4} \text{ for } L, \;\tfrac{3}{4} \text{ for } H\right), \quad \sigma_J^* = \left(\tfrac{5}{8} \text{ for } J, \;\tfrac{3}{8} \text{ for } N\right)$$

**Step 9 ÔÇö Compute the value of the game** (expected payoff at equilibrium):

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \mathbb{E}[u_D(L)] = 4 - 6 \cdot \tfrac{5}{8} = 4 - \tfrac{30}{8} = 4 - 3.75 = 0.25 \\
v &= \mathbb{E}[u_D(H)] = 2 \cdot \tfrac{5}{8} - 1 = \tfrac{10}{8} - 1 = 0.25 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** The mixed-strategy NE of the jamming game is $\left(\sigma_D^*, \sigma_J^*\right) = \left(\tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{5}{8}\right)$ with game value $v = 0.25$. The defender randomizes so that the jammer is *exactly indifferent* between jamming and not jamming ÔÇö preventing the jammer from exploiting any predictable pattern.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 ÔÇö Best-Response Derivation (Stackelberg Form)

### 3.3.1 Setup

The leader (Defender) commits to a transmission power $p \in [0, 10]$ first. The follower (Eavesdropper) observes $p$ and chooses an eavesdropping strategy $e \in \{0, 1\}$ to *minimize* the defender's secrecy rate. Payoffs to defender:

$$u_D(p, e) = 5p - 4pe - 2e$$

The defender seeks to *maximize* $u_D$ knowing the eavesdropper will choose the worst-case $e$.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ÔÇö Compute the eavesdropper's best response $BR_E(p)$.**

The eavesdropper compares $u_D$ at $e=0$ and $e=1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
u_D(p, e=0) &= 5p - 0 - 0 = 5p \\
u_D(p, e=1) &= 5p - 4p - 2 = p - 2
\end{aligned}$$

Eavesdropper chooses $e=1$ if and only if $u_D(p, 1) \leq u_D(p, 0)$, i.e., $p - 2 \leq 5p$, which simplifies to $-2 \leq 4p$, i.e., $p \geq -0.5$. Since $p \geq 0$, **the eavesdropper always chooses $e = 1$** over the entire domain.

**Step 2 ÔÇö Substitute the best response into the leader's objective.**

Since $BR_E(p) = 1$ for all $p \in [0, 10]$, the defender's Stackelberg payoff becomes:

$$u_D^{\text{Stack}}(p) = u_D(p, 1) = p - 2$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Maximize over $p \in [0, 10]$.**

Since $\frac{d}{dp}(p - 2) = 1 > 0$, the function is strictly increasing. The maximum is attained at the boundary $p^* = 10$.

**Step 4 ÔÇö Equilibrium verification.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Leader's strategy: } & p^* = 10 \\
\text{Follower's best response: } & e^* = 1 \\
\text{Leader's equilibrium payoff: } & u_D^{\text{Stack}} = 10 - 2 = 8
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a Stackelberg security game, the *first mover's commitment power* is crucial. In wireless physical-layer security, the base station acting as leader can often *force* the eavesdropper into a predictable behavior by choosing its power level strategically.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Game Type | Best Tool | Existence of Pure NE | Wireless Security Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Finite Static | NE / Dominant Strategy | Not guaranteed (Nash: mixed exists) | Power-control matrix game |
| Infinite Static | Best-Response Functions | Usually continuous in compact set | Continuous-power selection |
| Dynamic (perfect info) | Subgame Perfect NE (SPNE) | Guaranteed (backward induction) | Sequential IDS responses |
| Bayesian | Bayesian NE (BNE) | Guaranteed (Harsanyi) | Defender vs. unknown-type attacker |
| Stackelberg | Leader-Follower Optimization | Depends on convexity | Jamming with leader commitment |
| Zero-Sum | Minimax / Saddle Point | Always (Mixed) | Pure adversarial jamming |
| Potential | Potential Function Maximization | Always at least one | Cooperative spectrum access |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of a Game-Theoretic Wireless Security Model

The diagram below maps the *complete workflow* of formulating a wireless security problem as a game ÔÇö from identifying players to extracting the equilibrium.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify the Security Problem] --> B[Step 2: Identify the Players N]
    B --> C[Step 3: Define Strategy Sets S1 and S2]
    C --> D[Step 4: Encode the Payoff Matrix]
    D --> E[Step 5: Classify the Game]
    E --> F{Is it a Two-Player Static Game?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Step 6a: Check for Dominant Strategy]
    F -- No --> H[Step 6b: Use Extensive-Form / Bayesian Tools]
    G --> I{Dominant Strategy Exists?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Output: Dominant Strategy Equilibrium]
    I -- No --> K[Step 7: Search for Pure NE]
    K --> L{Pure NE Exists?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Output: Pure-Strategy NE]
    L -- No --> N[Step 8: Apply Indifference Condition]
    N --> O[Solve for Mixed Probabilities]
    O --> P[Output: Mixed-Strategy NE]
    H --> Q[Backward Induction / Harsanyi Transform]
    Q --> P
    J --> R[Step 9: Verify Stability and Pareto]
    M --> R
    P --> R
    R --> S[Step 10: Map Equilibrium to Wireless Decisions]
    S --> T[Optimal Power / Frequency / Detection Threshold]
```

## 4.2 Game Classification Decision Tree

The following block diagram is a high-density classification aid for identifying the *exact type* of game in any KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Game Theory Problem] --> B{Cooperation Allowed?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Cooperative Game]
    B -- No --> D[Non-Cooperative Game]
    C --> E{Players Sign Binding Pacts?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Coalitional Form: Shapley / Core]
    D --> G{Constant Sum of Payoffs?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Zero-Sum Game]
    G -- No --> I[General-Sum Game]
    H --> J[Use Minimax Theorem]
    I --> K{Moves Simultaneous?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Static Strategic Form]
    K -- No --> M[Dynamic Extensive Form]
    L --> N{Information Complete?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Standard Nash Equilibrium]
    N -- No --> P[Bayesian Nash Equilibrium]
    M --> Q[Subgame Perfect NE]
    P --> R[Stackelberg / BNE Solver]
    O --> S[Dominant Strategy Check First]
    S --> T{Unique Solution?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Output Dominant Strategy Equilibrium]
    T -- No --> V[Output Nash Equilibrium]
```

## 4.3 Player-Interaction Schematic (Defender vs. Attacker)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Defenderside["DEFENDER SIDE"]
        D1[Legitimate Transmitter] -->|chooses power, frequency, beamforming| D2[Defender Strategy SD]
    end
    subgraph Channel["WIRELESS CHANNEL"]
        D2 -->|propagates through H| RF[RF Signal plus Noise]
    end
    subgraph Attackerside["ATTACKER SIDE"]
        A1[Malicious Entity] -->|chooses jam, eavesdrop, intrude| A2[Attacker Strategy SA]
    end
    A2 --> RF
    RF --> O1[Outcomes: Payoff uD for Defender]
    RF --> O2[Outcomes: Payoff uA for Attacker]
    O1 --> D1
    O2 --> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** The arrows form a closed loop ÔÇö this is the *defining feature* of a game-theoretic interaction. Each player's payoff feeds back into the next round of decision-making, which is precisely why iterative and learning-based models (e.g., fictitious play, Q-learning) extend classical game theory in advanced wireless security research.
