Open System is the "anyone can knock on the door" model, while Shared Key requires a "secret passphrase" to be challenged and proven ÔÇö a primitive ancestor of modern PSK authentication. ### 1.3 Core Components & Standard Metrics | Component | Standard | Function | |---|---|---| | **Supplicant** | IEEE 802.1X | Wireless client requesting access | | **Authenticator** | IEEE 802.1X | AP controlling port access | | **Authentication Server (AS)** | RFC 2865 (RADIUS) | Verifies credentials, often RADIUS or Diameter | | **EAP Method** | RFC 3748 | Defines the credential exchange format | | **PMK (Pairwise Master Key)** | IEEE 802.11i | 256-bit master key from authentication | | **PTK (Pairwise Transient Key)** | IEEE 802.11i | 384-bit session key (5 components) | | **GTK (Group Temporal Key)** | IEEE 802.11i | 256-bit broadcast/multicast key | | **MIC (Message Integrity Code)** | IEEE 802.11i | Protects against forgery (uses Michael or CCMP) | | **ANonce / SNonce** | 4-Way Handshake | 32-byte nonces from Authenticator & Supplicant | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bold-emphasized constants/metrics to memorize for KTU:** **256-bit PMK**, **384-bit PTK**, **256-bit GTK**, **128-bit CCMP-128 encryption**, **48-bit IV**, **32-byte (256-bit) ANonce/SNonce**, and **MIC length of 8 bytes** for TKIP / **16 bytes** for CCMP. ### 1.4 Why Authentication is Harder in Wireless than Wired LANs In a wired LAN, the attacker needs physical access to a switch port. In a WLAN, the medium (RF spectrum) is **broadcast, shared, and easily intercepted**. Therefore, WLAN authentication must provide: 1. **Mutual authentication** ÔÇö both AP and STA must prove identity. 2. **Resistance to replay attacks** ÔÇö captured frames must not be reusable. 3. **Resistance to dictionary attacks** ÔÇö WPA2-PSK is vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks, which WPA3-Personal addresses via **Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE)**. 4. **Forward secrecy** ÔÇö compromise of long-term keys must not compromise past sessions. 5. **No shared secret on the wire** ÔÇö credentials must never traverse the wireless medium in plaintext. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of WLAN Authentication Methods WLAN authentication methods evolve in generations. KTU 2024 expects students to be able to **compare, contrast, and chronologically order** these methods. #### A. Legacy / Pre-RSNA Methods (Pre-2004) **1. Open System Authentication** - **Mechanism:** Identity assertion only. The STA sends its MAC address; the AP replies with success if the SSID matches. - **Security level:** **ZERO authentication** ÔÇö it is a *null* mechanism. It is enabled by default on virtually all APs and is acceptable only when paired with upper-layer authentication (e.g., captive portal). - **KTU Insight:** Often tested as a trick question: "Is Open System secure?" ÔÇö Answer: *No, it provides no cryptographic authentication.* **2. Shared Key Authentication (WEP-based)** - **Mechanism:** Uses a **4-step challenge-response** handshake employing WEP encryption. - STA sends Authentication Request. - AP replies with **Challenge text** (128 bytes). - STA encrypts the challenge using the **shared WEP 40/104-bit key** and returns it. - AP decrypts and compares; success if match. - **Critical Flaw:** The challenge-response reveals two WEP-encrypted frames with the **same IV**. An attacker can recover the keystream in a few thousand frames, breaking WEP entirely. Hence, Shared Key Authentication is **deprecated and disabled by default** on modern APs. #### B. Modern RSNA Methods (Post-2004, IEEE 802.11i) **3. PSK (Pre-Shared Key) Mode ÔÇö WPA2-Personal / WPA3-Personal** - **Mechanism:** A pre-shared passphrase (8ÔÇô63 ASCII characters or 64 hex digits) is configured manually on both AP and STA. The **PBKDF2** (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2) algorithm, using **4096 iterations** of **HMAC-SHA1**, derives the **256-bit PMK** from the passphrase and SSID. - **Vulnerability (WPA2-PSK):** Susceptible to **offline dictionary attacks** ÔÇö an attacker captures the 4-way handshake and brute-forces the passphrase. - **WPA3-Personal Fix:** Replaces PSK with **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)**, a **Dragonfly Key Exchange** mechanism that provides **forward secrecy** and resistance to offline dictionary attacks. **4. Enterprise Mode (802.1X + EAP) ÔÇö WPA2-Enterprise / WPA3-Enterprise** - **Mechanism:** Per-user, per-session credentials verified by a centralized **RADIUS** (or **Diameter**) server. - **Mutual authentication** prevents rogue AP attacks. - **Per-session keys** ensure forward secrecy. - **EAP methods** define how credentials are exchanged over the EAPOL (EAP over LAN) channel. ### 2.2 The IEEE 802.1X Framework The 802.1X framework defines three logical entities and operates by controlling an *uncontrolled* vs. *controlled* port: - **Uncontrolled Port:** Allows only EAPOL frames. - **Controlled Port:** Allows normal data traffic *only after* successful authentication. The 802.1X state machine has the following states: - **Initialize** Ôćĺ **Disconnected** Ôćĺ **Connecting** Ôćĺ **Authenticating** Ôćĺ **Authenticated** (or **Held** / **Force_Unauth** / **Force_Auth** as bypass states). > [!NOTE] > **PAE (Port Access Entity):** Each entity (Supplicant, Authenticator, AS) implements a PAE ÔÇö the protocol engine that processes EAPOL frames. ### 2.3 EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol) Methods ÔÇö Deep Dive EAP is a *framework*, not a single method. KTU 2024 emphasizes the following EAP variants: | EAP Method | Credential Type | Mutual Auth? | Tunnel | Certificate Req. | KTU Exam Weight | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **EAP-MD5** | Username + hashed password (MD5) | ÔŁî No | ÔŁî No | None | **Low** (legacy) | | **EAP-TLS** | X.509 digital certificates (both sides) | Ôťů Yes | Ôťů Native (TLS itself) | Client + Server | **High** | | **EAP-TTLS** | Username + password (in TLS tunnel) | Ôťů Yes | Ôťů Yes (TLS) | Server only | **High** | | **PEAP** | Username + password (in TLS tunnel) | Ôťů Yes | Ôťů Yes (TLS) | Server only | **High** | | **EAP-LEAP** | Username + password (Cisco proprietary) | Ôťů Yes | ÔŁî No | None | Medium | | **EAP-FAST** | PAC (Protected Access Credential) | Ôťů Yes | Ôťů Yes (TLS-like) | None | Medium | | **EAP-SIM / EAP-AKA** | SIM / USIM (cellular) | Ôťů Yes | Ôťů Yes | Server (optional) | Medium | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Golden Point:** **EAP-TLS** is considered the **gold standard** for WLAN authentication because both client and server hold X.509 certificates, eliminating password-based weaknesses. However, it requires a **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** ÔÇö hence the rise of **PEAPv0/EAP-MSCHAPv2** and **EAP-TTLS** as practical alternatives that need only a **server-side certificate**. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Standard | KTU Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **PMK = PBKDF2(passphrase, SSID, 4096, 256)** | PMK = 256 bits | IEEE 802.11i | Derives master key from passphrase (WPA2-PSK) | | 2 | **PTK = PRF-384(PMK, ANonce, SNonce, MAC\_AP, MAC\_STA)** | PTK = 384 bits | IEEE 802.11i | Composed of KCK + KEK + TK + TMK1 + TMK2 | | 3 | **PTK = KCK (128) + KEK (128) + TK (128) + TMK1 (64) + TMK2 (64)** | Concatenation | IEEE 802.11i | KTU: must remember individual lengths | | 4 | **GTK = PRF-256(GMK, Group\_Nonce, MAC\_AP, MAC\_STA)** | GTK = 256 bits | IEEE 802.11i | Used for broadcast/multicast | | 5 | **MIC = Michael(Message, TK)** or **CBC-MAC(Message, TK)** | MIC = 8/16 bytes | TKIP / CCMP | Integrity protection | | 6 | **WPA2 = CCMP (AES-CCM)**, **WPA1 = TKIP (RC4 + Michael)** | AES / RC4 | IEEE 802.11i | Encryption algorithm mapping | | 7 | **SAE password element derivation (WPA3):** PWE = H(MAX(SSID\_A,SSID\_B) \vert \vert MIN(SSID\_A,SSID\_B) \vert \vert password \vert \vert MAC\_A \vert \vert MAC\_B) | H = Hash | IEEE 802.11-2016 | Dragonfly KEX (WPA3) | | 8 | **RADIUS Packet:** Access-Request / Access-Challenge / Access-Accept / Access-Reject | UDP 1812 / 1645 | RFC 2865 | AAA transport | | 9 | **EAPOL Key Frame:** KCK signs the 4-Way Handshake messages | KCK = 128 bits | IEEE 802.11i | Used to compute MIC over each handshake message | | 10 | **WEP IV:** 24-bit | IV ÔŐĽ Key = RC4 keystream | IEEE 802.11 (legacy) | KTU: 24-bit IV Ôćĺ birthday attack at 2^{12} frames | > [!NOTE] > **Substitution rule for KTU:** Whenever a formula uses an absolute value or set difference (e.g., \vert x \vert), write it as \vert x \vert or \mid x \mid in the answer sheet to avoid markdown table breaks. ### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Enterprise WLANs:** Universities, hospitals, airports use **WPA3-Enterprise + EAP-TLS** for HIPAA/GDPR compliance. - **Public Wi-Fi (Hotspots):** Often use **Open + Captive Portal + WPA3-OWE (Opportunistic Wireless Encryption)** to provide encryption *without* authentication. - **IoT Devices:** Use **WPA3-Personal SAE** because certificates are impractical; SAE provides forward secrecy even on low-power chipsets. - **Defense & Military:** Mandate **EAP-TLS with hardware tokens (smart cards)** under FIPS 140-3 and Common Criteria. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: PMK Derivation in WPA2-Personal (PBKDF2) The PMK is derived from the user-provided passphrase using **PBKDF2** as defined in **RFC 2898**. **Step 1 ÔÇö Input parameters:** - **Passphrase:** A user-chosen string, 8 to 63 ASCII characters. - **SSID:** The Service Set Identifier of the WLAN, used as the cryptographic salt. - **Iteration count:** **4096** (fixed by 802.11i). - **Output length:** 256 bits (32 bytes). **Step 2 ÔÇö Apply PBKDF2 with HMAC-SHA1:** $$\text{PMK} = \text{PBKDF2}_{\text{HMAC-SHA1}}(\text{passphrase}, \text{SSID}, 4096, 256)$$ **Step 3 ÔÇö Expanded form (intermediate 64-byte block derivation):** Let $D_i = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(\text{passphrase}, \text{SSID} \Vert \text{INT32}(i))$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ where $n = 4096$. Then: $$\text{PMK} = U_1 \oplus U_2 \oplus \ldots \oplus U_{4096}$$ where $U_1 = D_1$ and $U_i = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(\text{passphrase}, D_{i-1})$ for $i > 1$. **Step 4 ÔÇö Truncate to 256 bits:** $$\text{PMK} = \text{first 256 bits of } \text{PBKDF2 output}$$ **Step 5 ÔÇö Engineering implication:** The 4096 iterations are why WPA2-PSK brute-force is **CPU-expensive but parallelizable** on GPUs (a $200 GPU can test 10^5 passphrases/sec, hence the 8-character minimum recommendation). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The 4096 iteration count is **fixed and standardized** ÔÇö it is *not* a configurable parameter. State this verbatim to earn full marks. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: PTK Generation in 4-Way Handshake **Step 1 ÔÇö Input concatenation:** $$\text{PTK} = \text{PRF-384}\left( \text{PMK},\; \text{ANonce} \Vert \text{SNonce} \Vert \text{MAC}_{\text{AP}} \Vert \text{STA} \right)$$ where **ANonce** is the AP's nonce, **SNonce** is the client's nonce, and the MAC addresses are in canonical order (smaller MAC first to avoid ambiguity). **Step 2 ÔÇö Split the 384-bit PTK into five components:** $$\text{PTK} = \text{KCK} \Vert \text{KEK} \Vert \text{TK} \Vert \text{TMK1} \Vert \text{TMK2}$$ **Step 3 ÔÇö Component sizes and purposes:** | Sub-key | Length (bits) | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **KCK** (Key Confirmation Key) | 128 | Computes MIC over EAPOL-Key frames | | **KEK** (Key Encryption Key) | 128 | Encrypts GTK in 4-Way Handshake | | **TK** (Temporal Key) | 128 | Used for CCMP/TKIP data encryption | | **TMK1** (Tx MIC Key 1) | 64 | Reserved (not used in CCMP) | | **TMK2** (Rx MIC Key 2) | 64 | Reserved (not used in CCMP) | **Step 4 ÔÇö Verify the total:** $$128 + 128 + 128 + 64 + 64 = 512 \text{ bits?}$$ > **Correction:** PTK is **384 bits total**, not 512. The actual split is **128 + 128 + 128 = 384 bits**, and TMK1/TMK2 were **deprecated** in the final 802.11i amendment. KTU may test you on the *original* IEEE 802.11i-2004 spec which still includes TMK1/TMK2. Modern textbooks (and the 802.11-2016 standard) treat PTK as **3 ├Ś 128 = 384 bits**. $$\text{PTK}_{\text{modern}} = \text{KCK} \Vert \text{KEK} \Vert \text{TK}, \quad \vert \text{PTK} \vert = 384 \text{ bits}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: WPA3-SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) ÔÇö Dragonfly KEX WPA3-Personal uses **SAE**, a **password-authenticated key exchange (PAKE)** that resists offline dictionary attacks. The derivation is: **Step 1 ÔÇö Compute the Password Element (PWE):** $$\text{PWE} = H\!\left( \max(M_A, M_B) \Vert \min(M_A, M_B) \Vert \text{passwd} \Vert \text{MAC}_A \Vert \text{MAC}_B \right)$$ where $H$ is a hash function iterated until the result lies in the **cyclic group** subgroup, and $M_A, M_B$ are the MAC addresses of the two peers. **Step 2 ÔÇö Each peer generates a scalar and element:** - Peer A chooses random $r_A \in [1, q-1]$, computes $s_A = r_A \cdot \text{PWE}$ (scalar multiplication on an elliptic curve or in a finite field). - Peer B does the same with $r_B$. **Step 3 ÔÇö Exchange and compute shared secret:** $$K = s_A \cdot s_B \cdot \text{PWE} = r_A \cdot r_B \cdot \text{PWE}^3$$ This is the **Diffie-Hellman-style shared secret** derived from the password. Crucially, no offline attacker can test passwords without an active online interaction, hence the **resistance to offline dictionary attacks**. **Step 4 ÔÇö Derive the PMK from $K$:** $$\text{PMK} = \text{KDF}(K, \text{SSID}, \text{AMAC}, \text{BMAC})$$ **Step 5 ÔÇö Engineering meaning:** Each session generates a *fresh* PMK Ôćĺ even if one PMK is compromised, *past* sessions remain secure Ôćĺ **forward secrecy**. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Authentication Methods | Attribute | Open System | Shared Key (WEP) | WPA2-PSK | WPA3-SAE | WPA2-Enterprise (EAP-TLS) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Year Standardized** | 1997 | 1999 | 2004 | 2018 | 2004 | | **Mutual Authentication** | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | ÔÜá´ŞĆ Implicit (PSK matches) | Ôťů Yes | Ôťů Yes (cert) | | **Key Per-User** | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | ÔŁî (shared) | Ôťů (per session) | Ôťů (per session) | | **Forward Secrecy** | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | Ôťů Yes | Ôťů Yes | | **Offline Dict. Attack** | N/A | N/A | ÔÜá´ŞĆ Vulnerable | Ôťů Resistant | Ôťů Resistant | | **Replay Protection** | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | Ôťů (nonce + counter) | Ôťů | Ôťů | | **PKI Required** | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | Ôťů (client cert) | | **RADIUS Server** | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | Ôťů Required | | **KTU Exam Likelihood** | **High** | **High** | **Very High** | **Very High** | **Very High** | --- ### 3.5 The 4-Way Handshake ÔÇö Message-by-Message Analysis | Msg | From | Contains | Purpose | KCK-signed? | |---|---|---|---|---| | **1/4** | AP Ôćĺ STA | ANonce | Initiates handshake, provides AP's nonce | ÔŁî (cleartext) | | **2/4** | STA Ôćĺ AP | SNonce + MIC(PTK) | Proves STA knows PMK, derives PTK | Ôťů Yes (SNonce) | | **3/4** | AP Ôćĺ STA | ANonce + GTK (encrypted) + MIC | Delivers GTK, confirms handshake | Ôťů Yes | | **4/4** | STA Ôćĺ AP | ACK (MIC) | Confirms GTK installed, handshake complete | Ôťů Yes | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The 4-Way Handshake **does not transmit the PMK over the air**. The PMK is *known* to both sides (derived locally), and the nonces + MACs are exchanged to derive a *unique* PTK per session. This is why a passive eavesdropper who does not know the passphrase cannot compute the PTK. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 IEEE 802.1X Architecture (SupplicantÔÇôAuthenticatorÔÇôAS) ```mermaid flowchart LR STA["SUPPLICANT
Wireless Client"]:::node1 AP["AUTHENTICATOR
Access Point
Controls Port State"]:::node2 AS["AUTHENTICATION SERVER
RADIUS / Diameter
Holds Credentials DB"]:::node3 NET["PROTECTED NETWORK
Corporate LAN / Internet"]:::node4 STA -- EAPOL Frames
EAP-Request or EAP-Response --> AP AP -- RADIUS Access-Request
UDP 1812 --> AS AS -- RADIUS Access-Accept or Access-Reject --> AP AP -- EAP-Success or EAP-Failure --> STA AP -. Uncontrolled Port
EAPOL only .-> STA AP -. Controlled Port
Allowed after success .-> NET classDef node1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node4 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 WPA2-Personal 4-Way Handshake Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant STA as Supplicant STA participant AP as Authenticator AP participant AS as Authentication Server RADIUS Note over STA,AP: PMK already known on both sides
from PSK or 802.1X AP->>STA: MSG 1 of 4 - ANonce
EAPOL Key Frame STA->>STA: Derive PTK using PMK, ANonce, SNonce, MACs STA->>AP: MSG 2 of 4 - SNonce + MIC computed with KCK AP->>AP: Derive same PTK AP->>STA: MSG 3 of 4 - Encrypted GTK + MIC STA->>AP: MSG 4 of 4 - ACK with MIC Note over STA,AP: Secure channel established
Unicast TK and Group GTK active AS-->>AP: Optional Accounting Stop ``` ### 4.3 EAP Method Selection Logic (KTU Decision Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Choose EAP Method"]:::root --> B{"PKI Available?"}:::q1 B -- Yes Both Sides --> C["EAP-TLS
Highest Security"]:::node1 B -- Server Only --> D{"Tunnel Required?"}:::q2 B -- No PKI --> E{"Pre-Shared Secret?"}:::q3 D -- Yes --> F["PEAPv0 EAP-MSCHAPv2
or EAP-TTLS PAP"]:::node2 E -- Yes Long --> G["EAP-FAST
Uses PAC"]:::node3 E -- Cisco Legacy --> H["EAP-LEAP
Deprecated"]:::node4 E -- No PKI, No PSK --> I["EAP-MD5
Least Secure, No Mutual Auth"]:::node5 C --> J["Validate both client and server
X.509 certificates"]:::final F --> J G --> J H --> J I --> J classDef root fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef q1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef q2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef q3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node5 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef final fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: WPA3-SAE vs WPA2-PSK ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph W2["WPA2-PSK PATH"] W2A["Passphrase + SSID"]:::w2a --> W2B["PBKDF2 HMAC-SHA1
4096 Iterations"]:::w2b W2B --> W2C["Single Static PMK
Per Device"]:::w2c W2C --> W2D["Vulnerable to Offline
Dictionary Attack"]:::w2d end subgraph W3["WPA3-SAE PATH"] W3A["Passphrase + MAC + SSID"]:::w3a --> W3B["H Computation
PWE derivation"]:::w3b W3B --> W3C["Dragonfly KEX
Scalar Exchange"]:::w3c W3C --> W3D["Fresh PMK per Session
Forward Secrecy"]:::w3d end W2D -.Compared to.- W3D classDef w2a fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef w2b fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef w2c fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef w2d fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef w3a fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef w3b fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef w3c fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef w3d fill:#82b366,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A ÔÇö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Open System Authentication and Shared Key Authentication in IEEE 802.11. State one limitation of Shared Key Authentication.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Open System Authentication | Shared Key Authentication | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Identity assertion only ÔÇö STA sends MAC, AP replies success | ChallengeÔÇôresponse using WEP-encrypted challenge | | **Cryptographic Operation** | None (null authentication) | WEP RC4 encryption of challenge | | **Mutual Authentication** | No (AP not verified) | No (AP not verified) | | **Use Case** | Public hotspots with captive portal | **Deprecated**, disabled by default | **Limitation of Shared Key Authentication (1 mark):** The same IV is reused between the challenge and the response, exposing two keystreams under identical IVs, which allows an attacker to recover the WEP keystream via statistical attacks (e.g., FMS attack), ultimately breaking the 40/104-bit WEP key. *(Award 3 marks for full table + limitation.)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ÔÇô June 2024]** **What is the role of the RADIUS server in 802.1X-based WLAN authentication? Name two EAP methods that provide mutual authentication.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Role of RADIUS server (2 marks):** - Acts as the **Authentication Server (AS)** in the 802.1X framework, holding the credential database (username/password or X.509 certificates). - Terminates the EAP conversation with the supplicant, verifies credentials, and returns **Access-Accept** or **Access-Reject** to the Authenticator (AP) using UDP port **1812**. - Also performs **accounting** (session start/stop logs) on UDP port **1813**, useful for billing and audit trails. **Two EAP methods with mutual authentication (1 mark):** 1. **EAP-TLS** (client and server certificates). 2. **PEAPv0 / EAP-MSCHAPv2** (server certificate + user password inside TLS tunnel). *(Alternative valid answer: EAP-TTLS, EAP-FAST, EAP-SIM.)* --- ### PART B ÔÇö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Part B questions of 14 marks carry sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternative full-length questions are given below ÔÇö answer ANY ONE. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the **IEEE 802.1X port-based access control** framework with a neat diagram. Describe the roles of the three entities: Supplicant, Authenticator, and Authentication Server. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a sequence diagram, explain the **WPA2 4-Way Handshake** in detail, highlighting the role of ANonce, SNonce, PTK, and MIC. **(7 marks)** --- #### MODEL SOLUTION (a) ÔÇö 7 Marks **1. Definition (1 mark):** IEEE 802.1X is a **port-based Network Access Control (PNAC)** standard that provides an authentication mechanism for devices wishing to connect to a LAN or WLAN. It blocks all traffic on a port until successful authentication. **2. Three Logical Entities (4 marks):** - **Supplicant** (e.g., laptop, smartphone): The client device requesting access. It runs **supplicant software** (e.g., wpa_supplicant on Linux) and responds to EAP challenges via the Authenticator. - **Authenticator** (e.g., wireless AP): Controls the *uncontrolled* and *controlled* logical ports. It does not verify credentials itself ÔÇö it acts as a **pass-through proxy** between the Supplicant and the Authentication Server. - **Authentication Server** (e.g., RADIUS, Diameter): Holds the credential database. Performs the actual authentication check, typically using **EAP** (Extensible Authentication Protocol) over **RADIUS** (UDP 1812). **3. Port States (1 mark):** The Authenticator keeps the port in one of two states: - **Uncontrolled port:** Always open; allows only **EAPOL** (EAP over LAN) frames. - **Controlled port:** Closed by default; opens *only after* the AS sends **Access-Accept**. **4. EAPOL vs. RADIUS (1 mark):** Between Supplicant and Authenticator, EAP runs over **EAPOL** (LAN/WLAN-specific framing). Between Authenticator and AS, EAP runs over **RADIUS** (UDP, AAA protocol). **Diagrammatic representation (already provided in Section 4.1):** Refer to the 802.1X Architecture Mermaid diagram. *(Award 7 marks: 1 def + 4 entities + 1 ports + 1 EAPOL/RADIUS.)* --- #### MODEL SOLUTION (b) ÔÇö 7 Marks **1. Pre-condition (1 mark):** Both AP and STA already share the **PMK** ÔÇö derived locally from PSK (WPA2-Personal) or delivered by the RADIUS server during 802.1X (WPA2-Enterprise). **2. The Four Messages (5 marks):** - **Message 1 (AP Ôćĺ STA):** AP sends its **ANonce** (32-byte random value) inside an EAPOL-Key frame. This is sent in cleartext, as the ANonce itself is not secret. **Purpose:** provide the entropy needed for PTK derivation. - **Message 2 (STA Ôćĺ AP):** STA generates its own **SNonce** and derives: $$\text{PTK} = \text{PRF-384}(\text{PMK}, \text{ANonce} \Vert \text{SNonce} \Vert \text{MAC}_{\text{AP}} \Vert \text{STA})$$ The STA then computes $\text{MIC} = \text{HMAC-SHA1}(\text{KCK}, \text{EAPOL-Key frame})$ and sends back the SNonce + MIC. **Purpose:** prove to AP that STA knows the PMK (without sending it). - **Message 3 (AP Ôćĺ STA):** AP independently derives the same PTK. It then **encrypts the GTK** using the KEK, and signs the message with the MIC using the KCK. **Purpose:** install the GTK (used for broadcast/multicast). - **Message 4 (STA Ôćĺ AP):** STA acknowledges with a final MIC. **Purpose:** confirms GTK installation; the controlled port is now fully open. **3. Security Properties Achieved (1 mark):** - **Key freshness** (nonces prevent replay of old PTKs). - **Mutual key confirmation** (both parties compute the same PTK). - **Forward secrecy** (per-session PTK; compromising the PMK still does not reveal past PTKs). **Sequence diagram (refer Section 4.2).** --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ÔÇô June 2024]** **(a)** Compare **EAP-TLS**, **PEAP**, and **EAP-TTLS** in terms of credential type, certificate requirement, mutual authentication, and security strength. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **WPA3-SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)** authentication mechanism. How does it overcome the offline dictionary attack vulnerability of WPA2-PSK? **(7 marks)** --- #### MODEL SOLUTION (a) ÔÇö 7 Marks **Comparison Table (6 marks):** | Feature | EAP-TLS | PEAPv0/EAP-MSCHAPv2 | EAP-TTLS | |---|---|---|---| | **Credential Type** | X.509 certificates (client & server) | Username + password (inside TLS tunnel) | Username + password (any inner method) | | **Client Certificate** | **Required** | **Not required** | **Not required** | | **Server Certificate** | Required | Required | Required | | **Mutual Authentication** | Ôťů Yes (cert-based) | Ôťů Yes (server cert + MSCHAPv2 challenge) | Ôťů Yes (server cert + inner method) | | **PKI Overhead** | High (full PKI for all clients) | Low (only server cert) | Low (only server cert) | | **Security Strength** | **Highest** ÔÇö no password | Medium-High ÔÇö protected by TLS tunnel | Medium-High ÔÇö flexible inner methods | | **Vulnerability** | Private key theft | MSCHAPv2 hash weakness (Hashcat) | Depends on inner method (PAP = weak, CHAP = OK) | | **KTU Verdict** | Gold standard | Most deployed in enterprise | Flexible alternative to PEAP | **Concluding Statement (1 mark):** **EAP-TLS** offers the strongest security but requires full PKI deployment. **PEAP** is the most widely deployed enterprise EAP method. **EAP-TTLS** is the most flexible, supporting legacy inner methods. --- #### MODEL SOLUTION (b) ÔÇö 7 Marks **1. Background (1 mark):** WPA2-Personal uses a **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** derived via PBKDF2. An attacker can capture the 4-way handshake, take the ANonce/SNonce/MIC, and offline-brute-force the passphrase using GPU-based dictionaries (10^5ÔÇô10^6 attempts/sec). **2. SAE = Password-Authenticated Key Exchange (2 marks):** WPA3-Personal replaces PSK with **SAE**, based on the **Dragonfly Key Exchange** (RFC 7664). SAE is a **PAKE protocol** that: - Performs a **Diffie-Hellman-style key exchange** authenticated by the password. - Generates a **fresh PMK per session** (even if the password is identical across sessions). - Does **not** allow an offline attacker to test passwords because the password is *never* sent over the air and the cryptographic exchange cannot be inverted without an active online interaction. **3. The SAE Handshake Steps (3 marks):** - **Commit Phase:** Both peers derive the **Password Element (PWE)** from the password, SSID, and MAC addresses. Each peer chooses a random scalar and computes its public commitment. Both commitments are exchanged. - **Confirm Phase:** Each peer derives the shared secret $K$ and the **PMK** via a Key Derivation Function. They exchange cryptographic confirm messages to prove both derived the same $K$. **4. Mathematical Insight (1 mark):** $$\text{PWE} = H(\max(M_A, M_B) \Vert \min(M_A, M_B) \Vert \text{passwd} \Vert \text{MAC}_A \Vert \text{MAC}_B)$$ $$K = r_A \cdot r_B \cdot \text{PWE}^3 \pmod{q}$$ $$\text{PMK} = \text{KDF}(K, \text{SSID} \Vert M_A \Vert M_B)$$ **5. Why it resists offline dictionary attacks (1 mark):** An eavesdropper sees the *commit* and *confirm* exchanges but cannot compute $K$ without knowing either the password (offline brute-force would require *active* trial decryption, not just captured packets) or one of the random scalars. The security reduces to a **hard Diffie-Hellman problem** in the cyclic group ÔÇö a well-studied hard problem in cryptography. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ÔÇö Common Pitfalls (Losing Marks):** > 1. **Confusing PMK and PTK:** PMK is **256 bits** (master, long-term), PTK is **384 bits** (session-specific). Examiners deduct 1 mark if mixed. > 2. **Forgetting ANonce is sent in cleartext:** Many students wrongly say the 4-Way Handshake is encrypted. It is **not** ÔÇö only the GTK in Message 3 is encrypted using KEK. > 3. **Writing "EAP-TLS uses username and password":** It uses **X.509 certificates** for *both* client and server ÔÇö no shared secret. Examiners strictly deduct. > 4. **Stating PBKDF2 iteration count as 1000 or 2048:** It is fixed at **4096** in 802.11i. > 5. **Skipping the diagram in 802.1X questions:** A diagram (SupplicantÔÇôAuthenticatorÔÇôAS) is worth **at least 2 marks**. Always draw it. > 6. **Mixing up WEP and WPA terminology:** WEP = RC4 + 24-bit IV. WPA = TKIP + RC4. WPA2 = CCMP + AES-128. WPA3 = SAE + AES-GCMP. Examiners instantly catch this. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > Ôťů **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **WLAN authentication methods (chronological):** Open System Ôćĺ Shared Key (WEP) Ôćĺ WPA-PSK Ôćĺ WPA2-PSK Ôćĺ WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X) Ôćĺ WPA3-Personal (SAE) Ôćĺ WPA3-Enterprise (192-bit). - **Three 802.1X entities:** Supplicant (client), Authenticator (AP), Authentication Server (RADIUS). - **Port states in 802.1X:** Uncontrolled (EAPOL only) vs. Controlled (data, after success). - **PMK length = 256 bits.** PBKDF2 uses **4096 iterations** of HMAC-SHA1. - **PTK length = 384 bits** = KCK (128) + KEK (128) + TK (128). Derived via PRF-384. - **GTK length = 256 bits.** Distributed encrypted with KEK in Message 3 of 4-Way Handshake. - **EAP-TLS** = certificates on *both* sides. Gold standard. Requires full PKI. - **PEAP / EAP-TTLS** = server certificate only, tunneled password inside. - **EAP-MD5** = weak, no mutual auth, deprecated. - **WPA3-SAE** = Dragonfly PAKE, fresh PMK per session, **forward secrecy**, **resists offline dictionary attacks**. - **RADIUS ports:** Authentication = UDP **1812**, Accounting = UDP **1813**. - **MIC length:** TKIP = **8 bytes**, CCMP = **16 bytes**. - **4-Way Handshake messages:** (1) ANonce, (2) SNonce+MIC, (3) Encrypted GTK+MIC, (4) ACK+MIC. - **WEP IV = 24 bits** Ôćĺ birthday attack Ôćĺ recoverable in <2^12 frames. - **KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack)** exploits 4-Way Handshake nonce reuse ÔÇö patched via 802.11r-2016 amendments. - **WPA3-Enterprise** mandates 192-bit security suite (GCMP-256, SHA-384, ECDH-384) for sensitive environments (government, defense). - **OWE (Opportunistic Wireless Encryption)** = WPA3 enhancement for open networks, providing encryption *without* authentication. - **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Understand *why* WPA2-PSK is weak against offline attacks and *how* SAE fixes it. Be able to derive the PTK formula from memory. - **Memory anchors:** **4096 iterations, 256-bit PMK, 384-bit PTK, 4 messages, 3 entities, 2 RADIUS ports (1812/1813), 1 controlled port.**

topics.title: Cross Domain Mobility Adaptive Authentication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cross Domain Mobility Adaptive Authentication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Domain Mobility Adaptive Authentication (CDMA-Auth)** is a context-aware security paradigm in heterogeneous wireless networks where the authentication strength, mechanism, and policy dynamically adapt based on the user's mobility context (roaming across administrative/technological domains) and the assessed risk profile (device trust, location, network sensitivity, behavioral patterns).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this concept is formally defined as the convergence of two orthogonal security dimensions:

1. **Cross-Domain Mobility (CDM):** The seamless movement of a principal (user/device) between distinct administrative trust domains (e.g., Home Network Ôćĺ Visited Network, Wi-Fi Ôćĺ LTE Ôćĺ 5G NR) while preserving session continuity.
2. **Adaptive Authentication (AA):** A risk-based, context-sensitive authentication mechanism that varies the *number* and *type* of authentication factors in real-time, depending on environmental and behavioral signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECCT862 M3):** This topic maps to **CO3** ÔÇö *Design secure authentication and key management protocols for heterogeneous wireless networks*. It bridges Module 2 (wireless authentication protocols like EAP) with Module 4 (advanced 5G security).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ÔÇö The "International Airport Security" Model

Imagine a frequent flyer travelling internationally:

- **Cross-Domain Mobility** = Travelling between countries (India Ôćĺ USA Ôćĺ Japan). Each country is a *different administrative domain* with its own security agency.
- **Adaptive Authentication** = The varying level of security screening:
  - At a **domestic airport** (trusted, low-risk): a simple boarding pass suffices.
  - At an **international hub** (untrusted, high-risk): passport + visa + biometrics + baggage scan are mandatory.
  - If the passenger is on a **trusted "fast-track" list** (low risk score): expedited screening.

The traveler's "credentials" (passport) are issued once by the *Home Country* (Home Domain) but are *recognized* and *adaptively accepted* by the *Visited Country* (Foreign Domain), with the security level flexing based on real-time risk.

| Real-World Element | Wireless Security Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Country Border | Administrative Trust Domain |
| Passport | Security Token / Digital Certificate |
| Visa Policy | Inter-Domain Trust Agreement (Federation) |
| Threat Level | Contextual Risk Score |
| Security Officer | Authentication Server (AAA) |
| Boarding Pass | Session Key / Temporary Credential |

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Ready Glossary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Definitions for 3-Mark Questions:**

- **Principal:** The user or device requesting access.
- **Home Domain (HD):** The administrative domain where the principal is originally registered and credentials are stored.
- **Visited/Foreign Domain (FD):** The domain the principal is currently roaming into.
- **Trust Anchor:** A pre-established cryptographic root (CA) mutually recognized by HD and FD.
- **Context Vector ($\vec{C}$):** The tuple of environmental attributes used for risk assessment: $\vec{C} = \langle L, D, T, N, B \rangle$ where $L$=Location, $D$=Device, $T$=Time, $N$=Network, $B$=Behavior.
- **Risk Score ($R$):** A normalized value $[0, 1]$ quantifying the likelihood of compromise.
- **Step-Up Authentication:** Demand for *stronger* factors when risk increases.
- **Step-Down Authentication:** Relaxation of factors when context is demonstrably safe.

---

## 1.4 Why is This Topic Critical?

Modern wireless ecosystems are **heterogeneous** ÔÇö a single smartphone session may traverse Wi-Fi (802.11ax), 5G NR, satellite (NTN), and IoT sub-networks within an hour. Static, password-only authentication is obsolete because:

- **Attack Surface Diversification:** Each domain has unique threat vectors.
- **Privacy Regulations:** GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023 demand *proportional* authentication.
- **Zero-Trust Mandate:** "Never trust, always verify" requires continuous, adaptive validation.
- **UX Pressure:** Users reject systems that re-authenticate excessively; adaptive logic balances security and usability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars Decomposed

### Pillar A: Cross-Domain Mobility (CDM)

When a mobile node (MN) moves from Home Domain (HD) to Foreign Domain (FD), three architectural challenges emerge:

1. **Identity Continuity:** The principal's identity must be valid across both domains.
2. **Authorization Transfer:** Rights/permissions must be carried or re-evaluated.
3. **Session Preservation:** Active sessions (e.g., VoIP call, video stream) should not drop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The AAA Triad for CDM:** Authentication, Authorization, Accounting ÔÇö implemented via **RADIUS** (legacy) or **Diameter** (modern, 4G/5G). The Diameter protocol explicitly supports cross-domain via the *Diameter Routing Agent (DRA)* and *interconnect* with peer domains.

### Pillar B: Adaptive Authentication (AA)

AA operates on the **Risk-Based Authentication (RBA)** principle. The core logic:

- **Why?** Static authentication is either *too weak* (always password) or *too strong* (always biometrics + token ÔÇö user-unfriendly).
- **How?** Continuously compute a **Risk Score** $R(t)$ at time $t$. If $R(t) > \tau_{high}$ (threshold), trigger **step-up**. If $R(t) < \tau_{low}$, allow **step-down**.

---

## 2.2 The Risk Score Computation Model

The cornerstone mathematical model of adaptive authentication is the **Weighted Contextual Risk Aggregation**:

$$R = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i \cdot c_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight assigned to context factor $i$ (sum to 1, $\sum w_i = 1$)
- $s_i$ = severity/score of factor $i$ (normalized 0ÔÇô1)
- $c_i$ = confidence in the measurement of factor $i$ (0ÔÇô1, accounting for sensor reliability)
- $n$ = number of context dimensions evaluated

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $R = 0$ Ôćĺ Fully trusted context (e.g., corporate LAN, biometric+device match)
- $R = 1$ Ôćĺ Maximum risk (e.g., anonymous device, unusual geography, network anomalies)
- Decision thresholds: typically $\tau_{low} = 0.3$, $\tau_{high} = 0.7$ (NIST SP 800-63B guidelines)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Decision Matrix

| # | Equation / Metric | Formula / Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Score (Weighted) | $R = \frac{\sum w_i s_i c_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Core adaptive decision metric |
| 2 | Authentication Strength | $A = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \alpha_k \cdot F_k$ | $F_k$ = factor k strength (0ÔÇô1), $\alpha_k$=weight |
| 3 | Required vs Provided | $A_{provided} \geq A_{required}(R)$ | Step-up condition |
| 4 | Trust Transitivity (Federation) | $T(A, C) = T(A, B) \otimes T(B, C)$ | $A$ trusts $B$ trusts $C \Rightarrow A$ trusts $C$ |
| 5 | False Acceptance Rate | $FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Biometric factor accuracy |
| 6 | False Rejection Rate | $FRR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Usability cost |
| 7 | Equal Error Rate | $EER$: $FAR = FRR$ | Biometric performance benchmark |
| 8 | Handover Latency Budget | $T_{auth} \leq 50$ ms | 5G NR ultra-reliable requirement |
| 9 | Re-auth Frequency | $f_{reauth} = f(R) \propto R$ | Adaptive re-auth scheduling |
| 10 | Key Refresh Rate | $K_{refresh} = \frac{S_{budget}}{N_{sessions}}$ | Session key lifetime |

---

## 2.4 The Authentication Factor Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> **Knowledge ÔÇö Possession ÔÇö Inheritance ÔÇö Behavior** (memorize in this order; examiners love asking).

1. **Knowledge Factor (K):** Password, PIN, security question. *Weakest alone.*
2. **Possession Factor (P):** Smart card, OTP token, mobile device.
3. **Inheritance Factor (I):** Biometrics ÔÇö fingerprint, iris, face, voice.
4. **Behavior/Context Factor (B):** Typing rhythm, gait, location pattern. *Enables Zero Continuous Authentication.*

The **Adaptive Logic** maps $R$ to factor combinations:

$$A_{required}(R) = \begin{cases} K & \text{if } R < 0.3 \\ K + P & \text{if } 0.3 \leq R < 0.6 \\ K + P + I & \text{if } 0.6 \leq R < 0.8 \\ K + P + I + B & \text{if } R \geq 0.8 \end{cases}$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **5G/6G Networks** | AKMA (Authentication and Key Management for Applications) uses adaptive keys based on slice context. |
| **IoT/Edge** | Lightweight devices can't afford heavy crypto; adaptive auth conserves battery and compute. |
| **Healthcare (mHealth)** | Adaptive access to Electronic Health Records based on clinician's location, device, and urgency. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | V2X communication requires sub-50ms cross-domain handovers with cryptographic continuity. |
| **Enterprise Mobility** | BYOD policies adapt based on whether device is on corp-WiFi, VPN, or public hotspot. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Threshold for Step-Up Authentication

We want to minimize the **Total Cost of Authentication (TCA)**, which balances security loss (false negatives) against usability loss (false positives).

Let:
- $C_{compromise}$ = cost of a security breach if not stepped up.
- $C_{friction}$ = usability cost (user frustration, workflow disruption) if stepped up unnecessarily.
- $P(R)$ = probability density function of risk score $R$.

The expected cost is:

$$E[Cost] = \int_{0}^{\tau} P(R) \cdot C_{friction} \, dR + \int_{\tau}^{1} P(R) \cdot C_{compromise} \, dR$$

To find the optimal threshold $\tau^*$, differentiate with respect to $\tau$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d(E[Cost])}{d\tau} = -P(\tau) \cdot C_{friction} + P(\tau) \cdot C_{compromise} = 0$$

Solving:

$$P(\tau^*) \cdot C_{friction} = P(\tau^*) \cdot C_{compromise}$$

$$\boxed{\tau^* \text{ is found where } C_{friction} = C_{compromise} \cdot P(\text{breach} \mid R > \tau)}$$

**Practical implication:** If $C_{compromise}$ is very high (e.g., medical records), $\tau^*$ is low ÔÇö system steps up aggressively. If $C_{friction}$ is high (e.g., factory floor emergency), $\tau^*$ is higher ÔÇö fewer interruptions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cross-Domain Trust Chain in Federated Authentication

Consider three domains $A$, $B$, $C$. Domain $A$ trusts $B$ with trust value $T_{A,B} \in [0,1]$. Domain $B$ trusts $C$ with $T_{B,C}$. What is $A$'s trust in $C$?

For **multiplicative trust decay** (conservative ÔÇö recommended for security):

$$T_{A,C} = T_{A,B} \cdot T_{B,C}$$

For **weighted aggregation** (when multiple paths exist):

$$T_{A,C}^{agg} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m}(1 - T_{A,C}^{(j)})$$

**Example:** Suppose $T_{A,B} = 0.9$ and $T_{B,C} = 0.8$. Then:

$$T_{A,C} = 0.9 \times 0.8 = 0.72$$

A second path exists via $D$: $T_{A,D} = 0.7$, $T_{D,C} = 0.85$, giving $T_{A,C}^{(2)} = 0.595$.

$$T_{A,C}^{agg} = 1 - (1 - 0.72)(1 - 0.595) = 1 - (0.28)(0.405) = 1 - 0.1134 = 0.8866$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cross-Domain Authentication Protocols

| Protocol | Domain Model | Mobility Support | Adaptive? | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EAP-AKA / EAP-AKA'** | 3GPP (LTE/5G) | Strong (native handover) | No (static) | **High** ÔÇö Module 2/3 |
| **EAP-TLS** | Enterprise (802.1X) | Weak (per-AP) | No | High |
| **Diameter + SLF** | Carrier-grade | **Excellent** | Partial | High |
| **OAuth 2.0 / OIDC** | Web/Cloud | Good (token-based) | **Yes** (via claims) | High |
| **FIDO2 / WebAuthn** | Browser/Mobile | Good | **Yes** (device-bound) | High |
| **GSMA eUICC** | Global M2M | Excellent | Partial | Medium |
| **5G AKMA** | 3GPP Rel-16+ | Excellent | **Yes** (slice-aware) | **Highest** |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Adaptive Authentication Decision Flow

**Scenario:** A doctor accesses patient records from her tablet.

1. **Input Capture:** System collects $\vec{C} = \langle L=\text{Hospital WiFi}, D=\text{iPad Pro}, T=\text{09:00}, N=\text{SSID=MedNet-5G}, B=\text{typing 65 wpm normal} \rangle$.

2. **Factor Evaluation:**
   - $s_L = 0.1$ (trusted SSID), $w_L = 0.25$, $c_L = 0.95$ (GPS confirmed)
   - $s_D = 0.2$ (registered device), $w_D = 0.20$, $c_D = 1.0$ (TPM verified)
   - $s_T = 0.15$ (normal hours), $w_T = 0.10$, $c_T = 0.9$
   - $s_N = 0.1$ (encrypted), $w_N = 0.15$, $c_N = 0.9$
   - $s_B = 0.2$ (normal typing), $w_B = 0.30$, $c_B = 0.7$ (low confidence)

3. **Compute Risk Score:**

$$R = \frac{(0.25)(0.1)(0.95) + (0.20)(0.2)(1.0) + (0.10)(0.15)(0.9) + (0.15)(0.1)(0.9) + (0.30)(0.2)(0.7)}{0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.30}$$

4. **Step-by-step numerator:**
   - $(0.25)(0.1)(0.95) = 0.02375$
   - $(0.20)(0.2)(1.0) = 0.04000$
   - $(0.10)(0.15)(0.9) = 0.01350$
   - $(0.15)(0.1)(0.9) = 0.01350$
   - $(0.30)(0.2)(0.7) = 0.04200$

5. **Sum of numerator:** $0.02375 + 0.04000 + 0.01350 + 0.01350 + 0.04200 = 0.13275$

6. **Sum of weights:** $0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.30 = 1.00$

7. **Risk Score:**

$$R = \frac{0.13275}{1.00} = 0.13275 \approx 0.13$$

8. **Decision:** $R = 0.13 < \tau_{low} = 0.3$ Ôćĺ **Step-down allowed** ÔÇö single-factor (PIN) sufficient.

9. **Counter-scenario:** Same doctor at 02:00 from a cafe Wi-Fi. Now $s_L = 0.8$, $s_T = 0.7$, $s_N = 0.6$. New $R$ calculation yields $\approx 0.62$ Ôćĺ **Step-up triggered** Ôćĺ requires password + biometric + OTP.

---

## 3.5 Cross-Domain Handover: Authentication Message Exchange

For a 5G Ôćĺ Wi-Fi (untrusted) handover:

$$MN \rightarrow AP: \text{ANonce} \parallel ID_{MN}$$
$$AP \rightarrow AAA_{FD}: \text{ANonce} \parallel ID_{MN} \parallel Realm_{FD}$$
$$AAA_{FD} \rightarrow AAA_{HD}: \text{Diameter-MAR (Multimedia-Auth-Request)}$$
$$AAA_{HD} \rightarrow HSS: \text{AIR (Authentication-Information-Request)}$$
$$HSS \rightarrow AAA_{HD}: \text{AIA (Authentication-Information-Answer)} \parallel AVs$$
$$AAA_{HD} \rightarrow AAA_{FD}: \text{MAA (Multimedia-Auth-Answer)} \parallel AVs$$
$$AP \rightarrow MN: EAP-Request / AKA'-Challenge$$
$$MN \rightarrow AP: EAP-Response / AKA'-Challenge \parallel RES*$$
$$AP \rightarrow AAA_{FD}: \text{AV Verification}$$
$$AAA_{FD} \rightarrow AP: \text{Success} \parallel MSK$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Notice how Diameter propagates the AV (Authentication Vector) *across domains* ÔÇö this is the *cross-domain bridge*. In AKMA, the **A-KID** (Application Key Identifier) further enables per-application adaptive keys.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture: Cross-Domain Mobility Adaptive Authentication

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MN[Mobile Node / Principal]
    CTX[Context Collector]
    RE[Risk Engine]
    PD[Policy Decision Point]
    PE[Policy Enforcement Point]
    HD[Home Domain AAA]
    FD[Foreign Domain AAA]
    TR[Trust Registry Federation]
    IDM[Identity Provider IdP]
    KS[Key Store HSS]
    LOG[Audit and Logging Server]

    MN -->|user action| PE
    CTX -->|L D T N B vector| RE
    RE -->|Risk Score R| PD
    PD -->|Required Factor Set| PE
    PE -->|Auth Challenge| MN

    PE <-->|Diameter MAR MAA EAP| FD
    FD <-->|Federated Trust Anchor| HD
    FD <-->|SLF Lookup| TR
    HD <-->|AVs Keys| KS
    FD <-->|OIDC SAML Token| IDM

    RE -->|Telemetry| LOG
    PD -->|Decision Trace| LOG
    HD -->|Audit Log| LOG
    FD -->|Audit Log| LOG
```

---

## 4.2 Adaptive Authentication Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Request Arrives] --> B[Collect Context Vector]
    B --> C[Compute Risk Score R]
    C --> D{R less than 0.3}
    D -->|Yes| E[Step Down: Knowledge Factor Only]
    D -->|No| F{R between 0.3 and 0.6}
    F -->|Yes| G[Standard: Knowledge plus Possession]
    F -->|No| H{R between 0.6 and 0.8}
    H -->|Yes| I[Step Up: K plus P plus Inheritance]
    H -->|No| J[High Risk: Full MFA plus Behavior]
    E --> K[Grant Access with Monitoring]
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Periodic Re-Authentication Loop]
    L --> C
```

---

## 4.3 Cross-Domain Handover Sequence (5G to Wi-Fi)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MN as Mobile Node
    participant AP as Wi-Fi Access Point
    participant AAAF as Foreign Domain AAA
    participant AAAH as Home Domain AAA
    participant HSS as Home Subscriber Server

    MN->>AP: Association Request plus Identity
    AP->>AAAF: Diameter MAR plus Realm
    AAAF->>AAAH: Diameter MAR Relay
    AAAH->>HSS: Authentication Information Request
    HSS-->>AAAH: Authentication Vectors AVs
    AAAH-->>AAAF: Diameter MAA plus AVs
    AAAF-->>AP: EAP AKA Challenge
    AP->>MN: EAP Request Challenge
    MN->>AP: EAP Response RES Star
    AP->>AAAF: Verify RES Star
    AAAF-->>AP: EAP Success plus MSK
    AP-->>MN: Secure Session Established
    Note over MN,HSS: Adaptive context evaluated: device network time behavior
```

---

## 4.4 Risk Score Computation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Location GPS IP] --> M1[Normalize 0 to 1]
    D1[Device Fingerprint TPM] --> M2[Normalize 0 to 1]
    T1[Time of Access Pattern] --> M3[Normalize 0 to 1]
    N1[Network SSID Encryption] --> M4[Normalize 0 to 1]
    B1[Behavior Keystroke Gait] --> M5[Normalize 0 to 1]
    M1 --> W1[Weight wL]
    M2 --> W2[Weight wD]
    M3 --> W3[Weight wT]
    M4 --> W4[Weight wN]
    M5 --> W5[Weight wB]
    W1 --> SUM[Weighted Sum plus Confidence]
    W2 --> SUM
    W3 --> SUM
    W4 --> SUM
    W5 --> SUM
    SUM --> R[Final Risk Score R in 0 1]
    R --> DEC{Decision: Step Up Standard Step Down}
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ÔÇö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Adaptive Authentication. List its key context factors.** `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Authentication** is a dynamic, risk-based authentication mechanism where the strength and type of authentication factors are selected at runtime based on the *current context* and *assessed risk* of the access request, rather than being fixed a priori.

**Key context factors (memorize the 5-tuple):**
1. **Location (L)** ÔÇö GPS, IP geolocation, SSID/BSSID
2. **Device (D)** ÔÇö fingerprint, IMEI, TPM attestation
3. **Time (T)** ÔÇö hour of day, day-of-week patterns
4. **Network (N)** ÔÇö type (Wi-Fi/cellular/ethernet), encryption, trust
5. **Behavior (B)** ÔÇö keystroke dynamics, mouse patterns, gait

**Mark Split:** Definition (1.5) + 5 context factors (1.0) + Example (0.5).

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between cross-domain authentication and adaptive authentication.** `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô May 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cross-Domain Authentication | Adaptive Authentication |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Enable identity federation across trust boundaries | Dynamically scale auth strength with risk |
| **Trigger** | Roaming/handover to foreign domain | Change in context or risk score |
| **Core Mechanism** | Trust agreements, SLF, Diameter, federation | Risk engine, policy engine, factor selection |
| **Standards** | EAP-AKA', Diameter, SAML, OAuth 2.0 | NIST SP 800-63B, FIDO2, CAEP/RISC |
| **Output** | Token/assertion accepted by FD | Variable MFA challenge |

**Mark Split:** Tabular differentiation (2.0) + 1-line synthesis (1.0).

---

## 5.2 Part B ÔÇö Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of **Cross-Domain Mobility Authentication** in 5G/Wi-Fi interworking. With a neat diagram, describe how a Diameter-based AAA framework supports authentication continuity during a 5G-to-Wi-Fi handover. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **Context-Aware Adaptive Authentication** scheme for a healthcare mobile application. Specify:
   - The context vector and weights used.
   - The risk score computation formula.
   - The step-up/step-down decision logic with thresholds.
   - The mapping from risk score to authentication factors. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer A(a) ÔÇö 7 Marks**

**Architecture Overview (2 marks):**
The 5G-Wi-Fi interworking architecture involves the UE (User Equipment), Wi-Fi AP, Foreign Domain AAA (3GPP AAA Server or AAAF), Home Domain AAA, and HSS/UDM. The **N3IWF (Non-3GPP InterWorking Function)** plays a critical role in 5G, encapsulating EAP-AKA' over the untrusted Wi-Fi access.

**Diameter-Based Flow (3 marks):**
1. UE associates with Wi-Fi AP; AP routes EAP message to AAAF.
2. AAAF uses **Diameter S6b/SWx** interface to contact AAAH in Home PLMN.
3. AAAH authenticates user with HSS/UDM using **Diameter Cx/Dx** (5G: Nudm_UEAU).
4. Authentication Vectors (AVs: RAND, AUTN, XRES, K_ASME) flow back to AAAF.
5. AAAF completes EAP-AKA' challenge with UE; MSK derived.
6. For 5GS, K_ASME Ôćĺ K_AMF Ôćĺ N3IWF key for IPSec SA establishment.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.3.

**Continuity Mechanisms (1 mark):**
- **Proactive key derivation** (pre-compute keys for candidate target cells/APs).
- **Token caching** at AAAF to avoid full re-auth if within a trust window.
- **Fast re-authentication** (EAP-AKA' re-auth, 5G: NAI-based optimization).

---

### **Model Answer A(b) ÔÇö 7 Marks**

**Context Vector and Weights (1.5 marks):**
$\vec{C} = \langle L, D, T, N, B \rangle$ with weights $w = \langle 0.25, 0.20, 0.10, 0.15, 0.30 \rangle$.

**Risk Score Formula (2 marks):**

$$R = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot s_i \cdot c_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i}$$

**Decision Logic (1.5 marks):**
- $\tau_{low} = 0.3$, $\tau_{med} = 0.6$, $\tau_{high} = 0.8$.
- Continuous re-evaluation every session heartbeat (e.g., 60s) or upon context event.

**Factor Mapping (2 marks):**

| Risk Range | Authentication Required |
|---|---|
| $R < 0.3$ | PIN only (view-only access) |
| $0.3 \leq R < 0.6$ | Password + OTP (view + prescription) |
| $0.6 \leq R < 0.8$ | Password + OTP + Fingerprint (modify records) |
| $R \geq 0.8$ | All above + Facial recognition + behavioral re-check (admin actions) |

**Sample Calculation (Bonus for 14/14):** As worked out in Section 3.4, doctor at hospital Wi-Fi Ôćĺ $R = 0.13$ Ôćĺ Step-down (PIN). Doctor at caf├ę Ôćĺ $R \approx 0.62$ Ôćĺ Step-up (Full MFA).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ÔÇö Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam ÔÇô May 2023, Re-modeled]`

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, describe the **Adaptive Risk-Based Authentication (ARBA) framework**. Explain how the **Risk Engine**, **Policy Decision Point (PDP)**, and **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** interact. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **security and privacy challenges** of implementing Cross-Domain Mobility Authentication, with specific reference to:
   - Trust anchor establishment between domains.
   - Token replay and credential leakage across domains.
   - Privacy of context data (GDPR/DPDP Act compliance). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer B(a) ÔÇö 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Reference Section 4.1 ÔÇö note the ARBA flow.

**Component Roles (3 marks):**
- **Context Collector (PEP front-end):** Captures location, device, network, behavioral signals via sensors.
- **Risk Engine:** Normalizes signals, applies weights, computes $R$, may use ML models (e.g., anomaly detection).
- **Policy Decision Point (PDP):** Holds the rule set: "if $R$ in $[a,b]$, require factors $F_1 \dots F_k$." Returns decision to PEP.
- **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP):** Issues challenges to the user (e.g., OTP prompt, biometric request) and verifies responses.

**Interaction Flow (2 marks):**
Request Ôćĺ Context Collection Ôćĺ Risk Engine ($R$) Ôćĺ PDP (decision) Ôćĺ PEP (challenge/grant) Ôćĺ Continuous re-evaluation loop.

---

### **Model Answer B(b) ÔÇö 7 Marks**

**1. Trust Anchor Establishment (2 marks):**
- Requires bilateral/multi-lateral PKI or web-of-trust.
- Challenge: certificate revocation across domains; solved via **OCSP stapling** and **CRL distribution**.
- Example: GSMA's **Root CI** for global MNO federation.

**2. Token Replay and Credential Leakage (3 marks):**
- Risk: An MSK or JWT stolen in Domain A is replayed in Domain B.
- Mitigations:
  - **Binding tokens to (device, network, session) context** via ChannelID (OAuth).
  - **Short-lived tokens** (5ÔÇô15 min) + refresh tokens.
  - **DPoP (Demonstrating Proof-of-Possession)** in OAuth 2.0+.
  - **Token introspection** via RFC 7662.
  - Mutual TLS at the API gateway.

**3. Privacy of Context Data (2 marks):**
- Context (especially location, behavior) is personally identifiable.
- **GDPR Art. 9, DPDP Act 2023:** Explicit consent, data minimization.
- Techniques:
  - **On-device context processing** (federated learning) ÔÇö raw data never leaves device.
  - **Differential privacy** noise injection.
  - **Homomorphic encryption** for aggregate risk computation without exposing individual context.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Revisit before exam!):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Cross-Domain" with "Cross-Layer":** Cross-domain = *administrative trust boundary*. Cross-layer = *OSI layer crossing*. Examiners deduct if these are interchanged.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the Risk Engine's confidence factor $c_i$:** Many students only write $R = \sum w_i s_i$ ÔÇö full marks require the **confidence multiplier** since sensor data is noisy.
>
> 3. **Static thresholds:** Examiners expect discussion of *dynamic* thresholds (e.g., Bayesian updating, sliding-window). Mentioning only fixed $\tau = 0.5$ loses 1ÔÇô2 marks.
>
> 4. **Diameter vs. RADIUS:** Students often write "RADIUS supports cross-domain mobility" ÔÇö partial credit only. RADIUS is *legacy*; **Diameter** is the correct answer for modern 4G/5G cross-domain.
>
> 5. **EAP-AKA vs. EAP-AKA':** Always use **EAP-AKA'** (prime) for 5G ÔÇö uses **HMAC-SHA-256** instead of XOR, mitigating the original AKA's vulnerabilities.
>
> 6. **Token Binding absence:** If your answer to "token replay" doesn't mention **DPoP, mTLS, or token binding** (RFC 8471), it is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision ÔÇö Memorize This Box!**

- [x] **Core Definition:** CDMA-Auth = Cross-Domain Mobility + Adaptive (Risk-Based) Authentication.
- [x] **5 Context Factors:** L (Location), D (Device), T (Time), N (Network), B (Behavior).
- [x] **Risk Score Formula:** $R = \frac{\sum w_i s_i c_i}{\sum w_i}$, with $R \in [0,1]$.
- [x] **Threshold Brackets:** $0.3 / 0.6 / 0.8$ for step-down / standard / step-up / full MFA.
- [x] **4 Authentication Factors:** Knowledge Ôćĺ Possession Ôćĺ Inheritance Ôćĺ Behavior (weakest to strongest).
- [x] **Cross-Domain Protocol:** **Diameter** (S6b/SWx in 4G, Nudm/Nausf in 5G Service-Based Architecture).
- [x] **Authentication Vectors (AVs):** RAND, AUTN, XRES, K_ASME ÔÇö flow from HSS Ôćĺ AAAH Ôćĺ AAAF.
- [x] **Adaptive Schemes:** NIST SP 800-63B AAL levels; FIDO2/WebAuthn; OAuth 2.0 Step-Up via CAEP.
- [x] **5G AKMA:** Application-specific keys derived from K_AMF, used in cross-domain app authentication.
- [x] **Trust Transitivity:** $T_{A,C} = T_{A,B} \cdot T_{B,C}$ (multiplicative decay).
- [x] **Token Replay Mitigations:** DPoP, mTLS, short-lived JWT, token binding (RFC 8471).
- [x] **Privacy Safeguards:** Federated learning, differential privacy, on-device risk computation.
- [x] **Standards & Bodies:** NIST, 3GPP, IETF (Diameter, OAuth WG), GSMA, FIDO Alliance, ETSI.
- [x] **Real-World Stacks:** EAP-AKA' + Diameter + AKMA + OIDC + WebAuthn = the modern CDMA-Auth stack.

**Golden One-Liner for Any Question:**
> *"In modern heterogeneous wireless networks, Cross-Domain Mobility Adaptive Authentication leverages federated trust anchors and continuous contextual risk evaluation to provide seamless yet proportional security ÔÇö the authentication 'dials itself' up or down based on who you are, what you carry, where you are, and how you behave, all while preserving session continuity across administrative boundaries."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AAA Architecture and Authentication for Wireless LAN Roaming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AAA Architecture and Authentication for Wireless LAN Roaming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­čöÉ AAA Architecture and Authentication for Wireless LAN Roaming

## 1.1 Formal Definition: What is AAA?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **AAA** is the security framework that underpins every secure network access decision in a Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN). It is formally defined as a **client-server architectural model** that provides centralized management of network access control.

$$AAA = \text{Authentication} + \text{Authorization} + \text{Accounting}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AAA Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):** A logical framework that enables a network to identify users (Authentication), determine what they are allowed to do (Authorization), and track what they actually do (Accounting) ÔÇö typically implemented through protocols like **RADIUS** (RFC 2865) and **Diameter** (RFC 6733).

| Pillar | Full Name | One-Line Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Authentication | "Are you who you claim to be?" |
| **A** | Authorization | "What are you permitted to do?" |
| **A** | Accounting | "What did you actually do, and for how long?" |

## 1.2 Definition: Wireless LAN Roaming

**Wireless LAN Roaming** is the ability of a mobile wireless client (STA ÔÇö station) to maintain continuous, authenticated, and authorized network connectivity as it physically moves across the coverage area of one Access Point (AP) to another ÔÇö whether within the same administrative domain (**intra-domain roaming**) or across different administrative domains (**inter-domain roaming**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Roaming Ôëá Mobility.** Roaming is the *security continuity* aspect; mobility is the *physical hand-off* of the L1/L2 connection. A secure roaming solution guarantees that AAA state survives the handoff.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ÔÇö The "International Airport Lounge" Model

Imagine you are a **frequent flyer with a Star Alliance Gold card** traveling from Kochi to Singapore via a transit hub.

| Network Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Wireless Client (STA)** | You ÔÇö the traveler |
| **Access Point (AP)** | Airline check-in counter at each airport |
| **AAA Server (RADIUS)** | The central Star Alliance database of passenger records |
| **Authentication** | Showing your passport & biometric check at the gate |
| **Authorization** | Lounge access granted only for *Gold* tier members |
| **Accounting** | The airline records your miles, flight time, and lounge usage |
| **Roaming (Inter-domain)** | Your Indian ticket is honored at Singapore Airlines lounge because of a **bilateral roaming agreement** (federation) between two carriers |

Just as a star alliance ticket is verified against a *shared trust network*, a wireless client's credentials are verified against a *shared AAA trust domain* so that the user never re-logs in as they roam.

## 1.4 Core Components of the AAA Architecture (Logical View)

- **Network Access Server (NAS)** / **Authenticator** ÔÇö the AP or wireless controller; the "gatekeeper."
- **AAA Client** ÔÇö software embedded inside the NAS that speaks RADIUS/Diameter.
- **AAA Server** ÔÇö the policy decision point (e.g., **FreeRADIUS**, **Cisco ISE**, **Aruba ClearPass**).
- **Supplicant** ÔÇö the 802.1X client software on the mobile device.
- **Authentication Backend** ÔÇö typically an LDAP/Active Directory store of credentials.
- **Roaming Broker / Federation Proxy** ÔÇö a AAA server that bridges two administrative domains during inter-domain roaming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 802.1X framework is the *de facto* AAA enforcement mechanism for Enterprise WLANs. It separates the **Supplicant**, **Authenticator**, and **Authentication Server** into three distinct roles ÔÇö a triadic architecture you must draw correctly in the exam.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­čžá Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three A's ÔÇö Deconstructed

### 2.1.1 Authentication (Identity Verification)
- **Goal:** Bind a claimed identity to cryptographic proof.
- **Methods in WLAN:** PSK (Pre-Shared Key), 802.1X with EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol), SIM/AKA-based SIM authentication.
- **Key standard:** **IEEE 802.1X-2010** (Port-Based Network Access Control).
- **EAP Methods you must know for KTU:**
  - **EAP-MD5** ÔÇö username/password hash; weak; rarely used in WLAN.
  - **EAP-TLS** ÔÇö mutual certificate-based; strongest, but PKI overhead.
  - **EAP-TTLS** ÔÇö server cert only, client uses password inside TLS tunnel.
  - **PEAP** (Protected EAP) ÔÇö server cert + MS-CHAPv2 inside tunnel.
  - **EAP-SIM / EAP-AKA / EAP-AKA'** ÔÇö used in cellular-WLAN convergence (3GPP).
  - **EAP-FAST** ÔÇö Cisco-proprietary, uses Protected Access Credentials (PACs).

### 2.1.2 Authorization (Policy Enforcement)
- **Goal:** After authentication, decide what the user *can* access (VLAN, ACL, bandwidth, role).
- **Mechanism:** The AAA server returns **RADIUS Attributes** (e.g., Filter-Id, Tunnel-Type=VLAN, Class, Cisco-AVPair) inside the **Access-Accept** message.
- **Common attribute:** `Filter-Id = "Engineering-WLAN"` Ôćĺ maps the user to VLAN 40.

### 2.1.3 Accounting (Usage Tracking)
- **Goal:** Record session duration, bytes in/out, IP, MAC, AP name for billing, audit, and forensics.
- **RADIUS Accounting Messages:** `Accounting-Start`, `Accounting-Update` (interim), `Accounting-Stop`.
- **Typical log fields:** `Acct-Session-Id`, `Acct-Input-Octets`, `Acct-Output-Octets`, `Acct-Session-Time`, `Acct-Terminate-Cause`.

## 2.2 The RADIUS Protocol ÔÇö Packet Flow Logic

RADIUS is a **UDP-based** (ports **1812/1813**) AAA protocol. It uses a **shared secret** for message authenticity and MD5 for per-packet authenticator generation.

**Logical Flow of a RADIUS Exchange (Step-by-Step "Why"):**

1. **Why UDP?** Because AAA traffic is short, real-time, and connection-state must not be held; retransmission is handled at the application layer.
2. **Why a shared secret + Request Authenticator?** To prevent replay and spoofing of AAA messages between NAS and Server.
3. **Why MD5-based HMAC?** To bind each packet to the shared secret without sending the secret on the wire.
4. **Why Access-Challenge?** To support multi-leg authentication (e.g., PEAP tunnel negotiation, OTP prompt).
5. **Why Accounting-Interim-Update?** To enforce session timeouts and detect silent client drops.

## 2.3 RADIUS vs. Diameter ÔÇö The KTU Comparison Matrix

| Feature | **RADIUS** (RFC 2865) | **Diameter** (RFC 6733) |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | **UDP** (1812/1813) | **TCP / SCTP** (port 3868) |
| Reliability | Application-layer retransmission | Built-in using TCP/SCTP ACKs |
| Security | Shared secret + MD5 (weak) | **IPsec / TLS** mandatory; supports CMAC |
| Peering Model | Client Ôćĺ Server only | **Peer-to-peer** with capability negotiation |
| Failover | Limited | Built-in (DWR/DWA ÔÇö Device Watchdog) |
| Roaming Support | Via RADIUS proxy chains | Native via Diameter Agents (Relay, Proxy, Redirect, Translation) |
| Roaming Use Case | Enterprise WLAN, Wi-Fi hotspots | **3GPP LTE/5G inter-domain roaming**, IMS |
| Message Format | TLV (Type-Length-Value) | AVP (Attribute-Value Pair) ÔÇö 32-bit aligned |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** If a question mentions *inter-domain roaming* in 4G/5G, the expected answer is **Diameter**. If it mentions *enterprise WLAN or Wi-Fi hotspots*, the expected answer is **RADIUS**.

## 2.4 Wireless LAN Roaming ÔÇö The Two Taxonomies

### 2.4.1 Intra-Domain Roaming
- The user moves between APs managed by the **same controller / same AAA server**.
- Example: Walking through a campus with multiple APs, all under one Cisco WLC.
- **Mechanism:** Caching of the **PMK** (Pairwise Master Key) at the APs, or central pre-authentication.
- **Standards:** IEEE 802.11r (Fast BSS Transition ÔÇö FT), 802.11k (Radio Resource Mgmt), 802.11v (BSS Transition Mgmt).

### 2.4.2 Inter-Domain Roaming
- The user moves between APs managed by **different administrative domains / service providers**.
- Example: Airtel user accessing a Jio hotspot abroad.
- **Mechanism:** **Roaming Consortium**, **Passpoint┬« / Hotspot 2.0**, **EAP-AKA'** with 3GPP credentials.
- **AAA Trust Model:** Bilateral or hub-and-spoke federation through **RADIUS brokers** or **Diameter Edge Agents**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on LaTeX in tables:** The pipe symbol `|` is a reserved markdown delimiter. For absolute-value and conditional notations inside the table below, we use `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve the markdown structure.

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PTK Derivation (802.11i/RSN) | $\text{PTK} = \text{PRF-384}(\text{PMK}, \text{ÔÇťPairwise key expansionÔÇŁ}, \text{MAC}_{AA} \Vert \text{MAC}_{SA} \Vert \text{ANonce} \Vert \text{SNonce})$ | Output: KCK (16) + KEK (16) + TK (16/32) + MIC Key (8) bytes |
| 2 | PTK Length | $\text{PTK} = \text{KCK} \Vert \text{KEK} \Vert \text{TK} \Vert \text{MICK}$ | bits: $128 \mid 128 \mid 128 \mid 64$ (CCMP) |
| 3 | GTK Distribution | $\text{GTK} = \text{PRF-128}(\text{GTK}_{\text{base}}, \text{ÔÇťGroup key expansionÔÇŁ}, \text{MAC}_{AA} \Vert \text{GNonce})$ | Group key, not user-specific |
| 4 | RADIUS Packet Authenticator | $\text{Auth} = \text{MD5}(\text{Shared\_Secret} \Vert \text{Request\_Authenticator} \Vert \text{Attributes})$ | 16-byte digest |
| 5 | EAP Packet Header | $Code \vert Identifier \vert Length \vert Type$ | Code Ôłł {1=Request, 2=Response, 3=Success, 4=Failure} |
| 6 | 802.1X EAPOL-Key 4-Way | $\text{Message 1: A} \rightarrow \text{S: ANonce}$ | Authenticator commits to nonce |
| 7 | PMK Caching Lifetime | Typically $\leq 43200$ s (12 h) | Vendor-configurable |
| 8 | Fast BSS Transition Time | $\text{FT} \le 50$ ms (802.11r goal) | vs. ~300ÔÇô500 ms re-auth |
| 9 | EAP-TLS Tunnel Cipher | TLS 1.2 / 1.3 with AES-128-GCM / AES-256-GCM | Mandatory PFS via ECDHE |
| 10 | Diameter Hop-by-Hop ID | 32-bit unsigned integer | Each hop increments |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility ÔÇö Where This is Used

- **Enterprise Campus Wi-Fi:** Employees roam across floors with 802.1X + RADIUS, no re-login.
- **Public Wi-Fi Hotspots (Hotspot 2.0):** Your phone auto-joins partner networks using SIM-based EAP-AKA'.
- **5G & LTE Offload:** Operators offload cellular traffic to Wi-Fi via **ANDSF** + EAP-AKA'.
- **Hospitals, Airports, Manufacturing:** Seamless mobility for IoMT, baggage scanners, AGVs.
- **Defense / Tactical MANETs:** Secure roaming across coalition networks (subset of AAA + cross-domain cert).

# ­čž« Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is dominantly *protocol-and-architecture* based, the "derivations" below are **cryptographic protocol derivations** and **state-machine walk-throughs** ÔÇö not algebraic proofs. Each step shows the *why* and the *how* of the key/identity establishment.

## 3.1 Derivation I ÔÇö PTK (Pairwise Transient Key) Hierarchy

The PTK is the cornerstone of secure 802.11 roaming. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation per **IEEE 802.11i-2004 / 802.11-2016 ┬ž12.7.1.2**.

**Step 1 ÔÇö Pre-conditions**
- The **PMK** (Pairwise Master Key) is already known to both Authenticator (AP) and Supplicant (STA).
- Sources of PMK: (a) output of 802.1X/EAP authentication, or (b) cached PMK from prior session, or (c) PSK = PMK in WPA2-Personal.
- Both parties know each other's **MAC addresses**: $\text{MAC}_{AA}$ (Authenticator) and $\text{MAC}_{SA}$ (Supplicant).

**Step 2 ÔÇö Nonce Generation**
- Authenticator generates a fresh 32-byte **ANonce** = $N_A$.
- Supplicant generates a fresh 32-byte **SNonce** = $N_S$.
- *Why nonces?* To guarantee **forward secrecy** and prevent key reuse across sessions.

**Step 3 ÔÇö Concatenation of Inputs (in a fixed order)**
The IEEE standard mandates the exact byte order:

$$\text{Data} = \text{MAC}_{AA} \Vert \text{MAC}_{SA} \Vert \text{ANonce} \Vert \text{SNonce}$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö Apply the Pseudo-Random Function (PRF-384)**
For CCMP (AES-CCM, 128-bit), the PTK is 384 bits long:

$$\text{PTK} = \text{PRF-384}\big(\text{PMK},\; \text{ÔÇťPairwise key expansionÔÇŁ},\; \text{Data}\big)$$

**Step 5 ÔÇö Truncate the PRF Output into Sub-Keys**
The 384 bits are split into 4 logical sub-keys:

$$\text{PTK} = \underbrace{\text{KCK}}_{0\text{ÔÇô}127} \Vert \underbrace{\text{KEK}}_{128\text{ÔÇô}255} \Vert \underbrace{\text{TK}}_{256\text{ÔÇô}383} \Vert \underbrace{\text{MIC Key}}_{384\text{ÔÇô}447}$$

| Sub-Key | Length | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **KCK** (Key Confirmation Key) | 128 bits | Computes MIC of EAPOL-Key frames during the 4-way handshake |
| **KEK** (Key Encryption Key) | 128 bits | Encrypts the GTK in EAPOL-Key message 3 |
| **TK** (Temporal Key) | 128 bits | Feeds CCMP for actual data confidentiality |
| **MIC Key** (EAPOL-Key MIC Key) | 64 bits | Legacy, often folded into KCK in modern stacks |

**Step 6 ÔÇö Logical Justification of Each Sub-Key**
- **Why KCK exists separately?** So the MIC key used in control frames is *never* the same as the data-encryption key ÔÇö limits blast radius if a key is compromised.
- **Why KEK is dedicated?** Encrypting the GTK over the air requires a key *different* from the one used for user data ÔÇö defense in depth.
- **Why only 128 bits for CCMP TK?** AES-CCM operates on 128-bit blocks; longer keys provide no extra security without doubling the cost.

**Step 7 ÔÇö Example (Numerical Trace)**
Assume (for illustration):
- $\text{PMK} = \text{0x0102ÔÇŽ4044}$ (32 bytes)
- $\text{ANonce} = \text{0xAAAAÔÇŽAAAA}$ (32 bytes)
- $\text{SNonce} = \text{0xBBBBÔÇŽBBBB}$ (32 bytes)
- $\text{MAC}_{AA} = \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA}$, $\text{MAC}_{SA} = \text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB}$

$$\text{Data} = \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA} \Vert \text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB} \Vert \text{AAAAÔÇŽ} \Vert \text{BBBBÔÇŽ}$$

Then:

$$\text{PTK} = \text{PRF-384}(\text{PMK},\; \text{ÔÇťPairwise key expansionÔÇŁ},\; \text{Data})$$

The resulting 48-byte PTK is split as described. In an exam, you are **not required to compute MD5/SHA hashes by hand** ÔÇö show the *structural derivation*.

## 3.2 Derivation II ÔÇö 802.1X EAPOL-Key 4-Way Handshake (State Machine)

This is the *post-PMK* protocol that proves both parties hold the PMK and synchronizes the PTK + GTK.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Message 1:} \quad & A \rightarrow S: \;\text{EAPOL-Key}(\text{ANonce}) \\
\text{Message 2:} \quad & S \rightarrow A: \;\text{EAPOL-Key}(\text{SNonce}, \text{MIC}_{\text{KCK}}) \\
\text{Message 3:} \quad & A \rightarrow S: \;\text{EAPOL-Key}(\text{ANonce}, \text{GTK}_{\text{KEK}}, \text{MIC}_{\text{KCK}}) \\
\text{Message 4:} \quad & S \rightarrow A: \;\text{EAPOL-Key}(\text{MIC}_{\text{KCK}})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Message 1 ÔÇö Why?** AP sends ANonce so the STA can compute the PTK. No MIC yet ÔÇö the STA doesn't have KCK.
2. **Message 2 ÔÇö Why?** STA derives PTK, computes MIC over Message 2 using KCK, and sends SNonce. The AP **independently** derives PTK and **verifies** the MIC ÔÇö this *proves* the STA knew the PMK.
3. **Message 3 ÔÇö Why?** AP delivers the encrypted GTK (using KEK) and a fresh MIC; STA installs TK and GTK after verifying the MIC.
4. **Message 4 ÔÇö Why?** STA confirms key installation, completing mutual authentication. AP, upon receiving Msg 4, switches the port to **Authorized** state.

## 3.3 Derivation III ÔÇö RADIUS Accounting Session Time

The accounting record keeps:

$$\text{Acct-Session-Time} = T_{\text{stop}} - T_{\text{start}}$$

For interim updates:

$$T_{\text{elapsed}} = T_{\text{interim-update},k} - T_{\text{interim-update},k-1}$$

These are returned in the `Acct-Session-Time` AVP and used for **billing, throttling, and audit**. KTU questions often ask to "list the RADIUS accounting attributes" ÔÇö remember the `Acct-Status-Type` field takes values {Start, Stop, Interim-Update, Accounting-On, Accounting-Off}.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ÔÇö Choosing the Right EAP Method

| Criterion | EAP-TLS | EAP-TTLS | PEAP | EAP-AKA | EAP-SIM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mutual Authentication | Ôťů Cert both sides | Ôťů Server cert only | Ôťů Server cert only | Ôťů SIM-based | ÔÜá´ŞĆ Limited |
| User Password Needed | ÔŁî | Ôťů | Ôťů | ÔŁî (uses SIM) | ÔŁî |
| PKI Complexity | High | Low | Low | None (SIM) | None |
| Roaming Across Carriers | Possible | Limited | Limited | Ôťů Native | ÔÜá´ŞĆ Legacy 2G/3G |
| 5G / Wi-Fi Offload | Ôťů | ÔŁî | ÔŁî | ÔťůÔťů | ÔŁî |
| Recommended for KTU Diagram | Mandatory if asked | Often drawn as "tunnel" | Common in EduRoon | Carrier-grade | Mention as legacy |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ÔÇö Roaming Performance vs. Security

| Mechanism | Handoff Latency | Security Level | KTU Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full 802.1X Re-auth | 300ÔÇô500 ms | Highest | Cold-start, security-critical |
| PMK Caching (802.11i) | <50 ms | High (PMK still fresh) | Same-domain, returning user |
| Pre-authentication (802.11i) | ~0 ms perceived | High | Same-domain, pre-planned |
| 802.11r (FT) | **<50 ms** | High (FT key hierarchy) | VoWiFi, real-time |
| OKC (Opportunistic Key Caching) | <50 ms | Medium (vendor-specific) | Lightweight intra-domain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The PTK and the 4-Way Handshake are *re-executed* on every full re-authentication but are **bypassed** in 802.11r Fast BSS Transition using a pre-derived FT key hierarchy. Draw this distinction clearly in 14-mark answers.
