# ­čôŁ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART ÔÇö A (3-Mark Short-Answer Questions)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ÔÇö May 2024, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting in the context of WLAN security. Mention which RADIUS packet type is used for Accounting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Term | Purpose | Mechanism in WLAN |
|---|---|---|
| **Authentication** | Verifies the identity of the wireless client (supplicant) using 802.1X/EAP. | Access-Request / Access-Challenge / Access-Accept / Access-Reject |
| **Authorization** | Determines *what* services (VLAN, ACL) the authenticated user can use. | Access-Accept carries RADIUS attributes (Filter-Id, Tunnel-Type). |
| **Accounting** | Tracks session usage ÔÇö start, interim, stop ÔÇö for billing and audit. | **Accounting-Start**, **Accounting-Update** (interim), **Accounting-Stop** (port **1813 UDP**). |

> **Memory hook:** "Auth**N** = *N*ame, Auth**Z** = *Z*one/permission, Account**I** = *I*nformation."

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ÔÇö Dec 2023]**
**List any three EAP methods used in WLAN authentication. Which is considered the strongest and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **EAP-TLS** ÔÇö mutual certificate authentication. Ôťů **Strongest**, because it provides mutual identity verification, no password exposure, and integrates with PKI/CRL/OCSP revocation.
2. **EAP-TTLS** ÔÇö server-side certificate with tunneled client credentials (PAP/CHAP inside TLS).
3. **PEAPv0/EAP-MSCHAPv2** ÔÇö server cert + MS-CHAPv2 inside TLS tunnel; widely used in enterprises.
4. **EAP-AKA / EAP-AKA'** ÔÇö used in 3GPP-Wi-Fi interworking with SIM/USIM.

**Why EAP-TLS is strongest:** Both client and server prove possession of X.509 certificates bound to a trusted CA; the TLS handshake itself is mutually authenticated before any credential is transmitted, eliminating dictionary, phishing, and replay attacks on the password.

## ­čôő Detailed Model Solutions

### ­čö╣ Solution to Q.A(a) ÔÇö 802.1X Framework [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** 802.1X is an IEEE standard for **Port-Based Network Access Control (PNAC)** that restricts a LAN/WLAN port (logical) from forwarding any traffic other than 802.1X/EAPOL frames until the attached client has authenticated successfully.

**Three Logical Roles (2 marks):**
1. **Supplicant** ÔÇö the client software on the wireless device (e.g., wpa_supplicant on Linux, Windows Native 802.1X).
2. **Authenticator** ÔÇö the AP or WLC; it has two logical ports: **uncontrolled port** (always open, allows only EAPOL) and **controlled port** (opens only after Auth-Success).
3. **Authentication Server (AS)** ÔÇö typically a RADIUS server that makes the accept/reject decision.

**Packet Exchange (3 marks):**
1. STA Ôćĺ AP: **EAPOL-Start** (optional).
2. AP Ôćĺ STA: **EAP-Request/Identity**.
3. STA Ôćĺ AP: **EAP-Response/Identity** (carries username).
4. AP Ôćĺ RADIUS: **Access-Request** (encapsulated via EAP over RADIUS).
5. RADIUS Ôćĺ AP: **Access-Challenge** with EAP method (e.g., PEAP).
6. Multiple **EAP-Request/Response** inside TLS tunnel.
7. RADIUS Ôćĺ AP: **Access-Accept** (with VLAN/Filter-Id attributes) **OR** **Access-Reject**.
8. AP Ôćĺ STA: **EAP-Success** (or Failure). The controlled port is enabled.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Draw the three-role architecture (Supplicant Ôćö Authenticator Ôćö AS), label EAPOL vs. RADIUS as the two transports.

---

### ­čö╣ Solution to Q.A(b) ÔÇö PTK & 4-Way Handshake [7 marks]

**Step 1 ÔÇö PMK Source (1 mark):** PMK is delivered to the AP from the 802.1X/EAP exchange (or pre-shared in WPA2-PSK). Length = 256 bits for CCMP.

**Step 2 ÔÇö PTK Derivation (3 marks):**
$$\text{PTK} = \text{PRF-384}(\text{PMK}, \text{ÔÇťPairwise key expansionÔÇŁ}, \text{MAC}_{AA} \Vert \text{MAC}_{SA} \Vert \text{ANonce} \Vert \text{SNonce})$$

Split as:
$$\text{PTK} = \text{KCK} \Vert \text{KEK} \Vert \text{TK} \Vert \text{MIC\_Key}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö 4-Way Handshake Messages (3 marks):**
| Msg | Direction | Contents | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AP Ôćĺ STA | ANonce | STA derives PTK |
| 2 | STA Ôćĺ AP | SNonce + MIC(KCK) | AP verifies MIC Ôćĺ proves PMK knowledge |
| 3 | AP Ôćĺ STA | ANonce + Enc(GTK with KEK) + MIC(KCK) | Delivers GTK; STA installs TK & GTK |
| 4 | STA Ôćĺ AP | MIC(KCK) | Confirms installation; AP opens controlled port |

---

### ­čö╣ Solution to Q.B(a) ÔÇö RADIUS vs. Diameter [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | RADIUS | Diameter |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | UDP (1812, 1813) | TCP / SCTP (3868) |
| Security | Shared secret + MD5 | IPsec / TLS mandatory |
| Reliability | App-layer retransmit | Built-in (TCP/SCTP) |
| Architecture | Client-Server | Peer-to-peer |
| Roaming | Limited proxy | Native with Relay/Proxy/Redirect/Translation Agents |
| Standard | RFC 2865/2866 | RFC 6733 |
| Header fields | 1-byte flags, code, identifier | 32-bit Hop-by-Hop & End-to-End IDs, command flags |
| Bandwidth | Restricted by UDP MTU | Streamed, large AVPs supported |

**Why Diameter for 4G/5G Roaming (3 marks):**
1. **Mandatory IPsec/TLS** ÔÇö needed for inter-operator trust across national boundaries; RADIUS MD5 is too weak.
2. **Built-in failover (DWR/DWA)** ÔÇö operators need <100 ms failover between AAA nodes for thousands of subscribers; RADIUS UDP has no native mechanism.
3. **Native peer-to-peer with agents** ÔÇö Relay, Redirect, Proxy, Translation agents directly map to the **S6a, S6d, SWx** 3GPP interfaces and HSSÔćöMME signaling.
4. **Sliding window congestion control (RFC 7937)** ÔÇö critical when one AAA node serves millions of IoT/NB-IoT devices.
5. **Pluggable AVPs (RTSP, NASREQ, EAP Application IDs)** ÔÇö supports charging, QoS, and policy interfaces that 5G AMF/PCF requires.

---

### ­čö╣ Solution to Q.B(b) ÔÇö Roaming Mechanisms [7 marks]

**Intra-Domain Roaming (3.5 marks):**
- Definition: User moves between APs under the **same administrative domain** (same controller, same AAA server, same SSID).
- Mechanisms:
  - **PMK Caching** ÔÇö STA and AP retain the PMK for a configured lifetime; subsequent re-associations skip full 802.1X.
  - **Pre-authentication** ÔÇö STA authenticates with a *neighboring* AP through the current AP using 802.1X frames forwarded at L2.
  - **802.11r (Fast BSS Transition ÔÇö FT)** ÔÇö Pre-derived FT key hierarchy enables <50 ms handoff; the STA only re-associates and skips EAP entirely.
  - **802.11k/v** ÔÇö provide neighbor reports and BSS transition management to make roaming *proactive*.

**Inter-Domain Roaming (3.5 marks):**
- Definition: User roams between APs of **different service providers** (e.g., a KTU student visiting an international conference venue).
- Mechanisms:
  - **EAP-AKA / EAP-AKA'** ÔÇö credentials come from the home 3GPP SIM/USIM; works seamlessly across operator boundaries.
  - **RADIUS Brokers / Federation Proxies** ÔÇö relay RADIUS Access-Request/Accept between the *Home AAA* and *Visited AAA* across the Internet using pre-established **shared secrets or IPsec tunnels**.
  - **Passpoint┬« / Hotspot 2.0 (802.11u)** ÔÇö advertises roaming consortium OIs and credentials the device should use for auto-onboarding.
  - **Diameter Edge Agents (DEA) / Diameter Routing Agents (DRA)** ÔÇö used in 4G/5G inter-operator roaming (S6a, SWx, STa).
- **Role of Roaming Broker:** Acts as a *trusted intermediary* that:
  1. Routes AAA messages between realms (home & visited).
  2. Enforces bilateral settlement and policy agreements.
  3. Hides internal AAA topology from the foreign domain.
  4. Provides accounting aggregation and clearing-house functions.

---

## ÔÜá´ŞĆ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in 14-Mark Answers:**
>
> 1. **Drawing the diagram with only TWO boxes** (Supplicant and Server) instead of THREE ÔÇö 802.1X *must* show Supplicant, Authenticator, AS as separate logical entities. **Ôłĺ2 marks.**
> 2. **Writing "RADIUS uses TCP"** ÔÇö it is **UDP**; Diameter uses **TCP/SCTP**. **Ôłĺ1 mark.**
> 3. **Confusing the direction of Message 1 and Message 2** in the 4-Way Handshake. Message 1 is AP Ôćĺ STA (ANonce). **Ôłĺ1 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting the KCK/KEK/TK split** when writing the PTK formula ÔÇö examiners *expect* the four sub-keys explicitly. **Ôłĺ1 mark.**
> 5. **Writing "EAP-TLS uses passwords"** ÔÇö it uses **certificates**, not passwords. **Ôłĺ1 mark.**
> 6. **Saying "PMK = PSK" always** ÔÇö they are equal *only* in WPA2-Personal; in WPA2-Enterprise, PMK is derived from the EAP exchange. **Ôłĺ1 mark.**
> 7. **Using "RADIUS" in a 4G/5G question** ÔÇö use **Diameter**; otherwise it shows outdated reading. **Ôłĺ1 mark.**
> 8. **Not labelling `EAPOL`** on the wire between STA and AP ÔÇö examiners *want* the protocol name on the arrow. **Ôłĺ1 mark.**

---

## ­čÄ» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ôťů **AAA** = Authentication (who you are) + Authorization (what you can do) + Accounting (what you did). A central architectural pattern, not a single protocol.
- Ôťů **RADIUS** = UDP-1812/1813, MD5-protected, client-server, *legacy de-facto* for Wi-Fi. **Diameter** = TCP/SCTP-3868, IPsec/TLS, peer-to-peer, *required* for 4G/5G.
- Ôťů **802.1X triadic model** = Supplicant Ôćö Authenticator Ôćö Authentication Server. EAPOL on the wire STAÔćöAP, RADIUS/Diameter on the wire APÔćöAS.
- Ôťů **EAP method selection**: EAP-TLS = strongest (mutual certs); PEAP/EAP-TTLS = password-friendly (tunneled); EAP-AKA/AKA' = SIM/USIM-based for 3GPP-Wi-Fi convergence.
- Ôťů **PTK** is split into **KCK + KEK + TK + MIC_Key** ÔÇö never the same key for control and data. Derived via **PRF-384** over PMK || MAC || Nonces.
- Ôťů **4-Way Handshake** = the proof-of-PMK-knowledge protocol; without it, an attacker could impersonate a legitimate AP or STA.
- Ôťů **Intra-domain roaming** = same WLC/AAA, *use PMK caching + 802.11r FT* for sub-50 ms handoff (VoWiFi).
- Ôťů **Inter-domain roaming** = different realms, *use EAP-AKA' + RADIUS brokers / Diameter agents*; relies on **bilateral trust + shared secrets** between operators.
- Ôťů **Passpoint / Hotspot 2.0 (802.11u)** is the modern enterprise/carrier-grade standard for automatic, secure, federated Wi-Fi onboarding.
- Ôťů **Accounting is not optional** ÔÇö it is the auditable trail required by regulations (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR) and enables billing, anomaly detection, and forensics.
- Ôťů **Exam Mantra:** Always state **the role**, **the protocol on the wire**, and **the cryptographic primitive** (TLS, PRF, MD5, AES-CCM) ÔÇö examiners reward *protocol-level* precision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experimental Study on Security Protocols in WLANs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Experimental Study on Security Protocols in WLANs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and the IEEE **802.11** family of standards, **WLAN Security Protocols** constitute a layered suite of cryptographic, authentication, and integrity-preservation mechanisms engineered to protect Over-the-Air (OTA) IEEE 802.11 frames from eavesdropping, injection, replay, and unauthorized association. An *Experimental Study* on these protocols entails the empirical observation, capture, and quantitative analysis of frames exchanged during the **4-Way Handshake**, **SAE Exchange**, or **Group Key Handshake**, validating theoretical claims about confidentiality, integrity, and key strength using industry-standard tools such as **Wireshark**, **Aircrack-ng**, and **Kismet**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECCT862 ÔÇô Module 3):**
> The student must be able to **differentiate** the operational behavior of WEP, WPA, WPA2, and WPA3, **identify** cryptographic primitives used at each layer, and **experimentally analyze** handshake frames using packet analysis tools.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **wireless conversation** happening in a crowded caf├ę.

* **WEP** is like whispering a secret in a language that everyone in the caf├ę already knows. Anyone with the right dictionary (the 40-bit key) can decode it instantly, and the same words are reused every day.
* **WPA** is like rotating the secret language every 5 minutes and adding a unique "code-number" (IV) to each sentence. Better, but the underlying language (RC4) is still the same.
* **WPA2** is like switching to a brand-new, mathematically complex cipher (AES) and stamping every sentence with a digital fingerprint that cannot be forged.
* **WPA3** is like meeting the person in a soundproof booth, where even if an attacker records everything you say, they cannot brute-force the door open because the lock **regenerates** every time.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Standard Metrics to Remember**
> * **WEP IV Size:** **24 bits** Ôćĺ $2^{24} \approx 16.7$ million frames before IV collision
> * **WPA TKIP IV Size:** **48 bits** Ôćĺ $2^{48} \approx 2.81 \times 10^{14}$ frames
> * **WPA2 CCMP IV Size:** **48 bits** with **PN (Packet Number)** for replay protection
> * **WPA3 SAE Group:** Minimum **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) group 19** (256-bit)
> * **4-Way Handshake:** Exactly **4 EAPOL-Key frames** exchanged
> * **SAE Exchange:** Exactly **2 finite-field/ECC scalar multiplications**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of WLAN Security

The security of a WLAN is not a single protocol but a **protocol stack** that operates at the MAC sublayer (Layer 2) of the OSI model. The experimental study focuses on three orthogonal pillars:

1. **Authentication & Association:** *Who is allowed to join the BSS?*
2. **Confidentiality (Encryption):** *Can a third party read the payload?*
3. **Integrity (MIC / OCB / GCM):** *Can a third party tamper with the payload?*

### 2.2 Operational Logic ÔÇö Stepwise Breakdown

**Step 1 ÔÇö Authentication Phase:**
* **Open System** (legacy WEP, no auth): Any STA may send `ProbeReq`.
* **PSK / SAE:** Mutual proof of password possession without sending the password on-air.

**Step 2 ÔÇö Key Hierarchy Generation (PMK Ôćĺ PTK Ôćĺ TK):**
* **PMK (Pairwise Master Key, 256 bits):** Derived from PSK via PBKDF2 or from SAE.
* **PTK (Pairwise Transient Key, 512 bits):** Generated using PRF-384 with nonces (ANonce, SNonce) and MAC addresses.
* **TK (Temporal Key, 128 bits):** First 128 bits of PTK; used to encrypt unicast traffic.
* **GTK (Group Temporal Key):** Distributed by AP; encrypts broadcast/multicast.

**Step 3 ÔÇö 4-Way Handshake (802.11i):**
* Confirms possession of PMK by both parties.
* Installs fresh encryption keys.
* Provides **replay protection** through monotonically increasing nonces.

**Step 4 ÔÇö Secure Data Exchange:**
* Each MSDU/MPDU is encrypted using a per-packet keystream derived from TK, IV, and MAC addresses.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Protocol / Concept | Cryptographic Primitive | Key Size (bits) | IV / Nonce (bits) | Integrity Check | Weakness (Experimental) |
|---|--------------------|-------------------------|-----------------|--------------------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **WEP** | RC4 Stream Cipher | 40 or 104 | 24 (IV) | CRC-32 (linear) | IV reuse, weak key schedule |
| 2 | **WPA (TKIP)** | RC4 + per-packet mixing | 128 | 48 (TSC) | Michael MIC | Michael has weak collision resistance |
| 3 | **WPA2 (CCMP)** | AES-CCM (CTR + CBC-MAC) | 128 | 48 (PN) | CBC-MAC | KRACK attack (key reinstallation) |
| 4 | **WPA3-Personal** | AES-GCMP + SAE | 128 | 48 (PN) | GMAC | None practical (offline) |
| 5 | **WPA3-Enterprise** | AES-GCMP + 192-bit minimum | 192 | 48 (PN) | GMAC | None practical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units Reminder**
> All key sizes are expressed in **bits**. Conversion: $1$ byte $= 8$ bits. A "128-bit key" therefore equals a **16-byte** key in memory. The total PTK length in WPA/WPA2 is **512 bits $= 64$ bytes**, partitioned into KCK + KEK + TK.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Network Auditors (CISSP / CEH / OSCP professionals):** Use these protocols to perform **penetration tests** identifying rogue APs and weak cipher suites.
* **Enterprise IT:** Mandate **WPA3-Enterprise (192-bit)** for compliance with standards such as **PCI-DSS v4.0** and **HIPAA**.
* **IoT Manufacturers:** Migrate to **WPA3-SAE** to provide forward secrecy on low-power devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: WEP Encryption / Decryption Workflow

The WEP encryption for a single MSDU is mathematically defined. Let $M$ be the plaintext MSDU, $c$ the 24-bit IV, $k$ the 40-bit secret root key, and $KS$ the keystream produced by RC4.

**Step 1 ÔÇö Form the per-packet seed:**
The 64-bit seed $s$ is constructed by concatenating the IV and the root key:
$$s = c \mid\mid k \quad \text{where} \quad \vert s \vert = 64 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 ÔÇö Generate the keystream:**
RC4 is initialized with seed $s$, producing a pseudo-random keystream $KS$ of length equal to the MSDU plus 4 bytes (for the ICV):
$$KS = \text{RC4}(s) \in \{0,1\}^{L+32}$$

**Step 3 ÔÇö Compute the Integrity Check Value (ICV):**
The ICV is a 32-bit CRC-32 checksum of the plaintext, providing (weak) data integrity:
$$\text{ICV}(M) = \text{CRC32}(M)$$

**Step 4 ÔÇö XOR the plaintext to produce the ciphertext:**
The payload $P$ is formed by appending the ICV, then XORed with the keystream:
$$P = M \mid\mid \text{ICV}(M)$$
$$C = P \oplus KS$$

**Step 5 ÔÇö Frame transmission:**
The IV $c$ is transmitted in clear-text in the MAC header, while $C$ forms the encrypted body. The receiver regenerates $KS$ using the same $s$, recovers $P = C \oplus KS$, and verifies $\text{CRC32}(M') = \text{ICV}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: WEP IV Reuse Probability (Birthday Bound)

The WEP IV is **24 bits** long. A busy AP at **11 Mbps** transmits approximately **748 bytes per millisecond**, yielding roughly:
$$N_{\text{frames/sec}} = \frac{11 \times 10^{6}}{8 \times 748} \approx 1838 \text{ frames/sec}$$

To compute the expected number of frames until the first IV collision (Birthday paradox), we use the approximation:
$$N_{\text{collision}} \approx \sqrt{2 \cdot 2^{24}} = 2^{12.5} \approx 5793 \text{ frames}$$

Converting to time:
$$T_{\text{collision}} = \frac{5793}{1838} \approx 3.15 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** In under **4 seconds**, an experimental WEP network will repeat an IV, producing two ciphertexts encrypted with the **same keystream**. XORing them cancels the keystream and leaks the XOR of the two plaintexts ÔÇö a catastrophic confidentiality failure, easily demonstrated in Wireshark with `eapol` and `wep` filters.

### 3.3 Derivation: PTK Generation in the 4-Way Handshake

In WPA/WPA2, the PTK is generated by a Pseudo-Random Function (PRF-384) that takes 5 inputs:
$$\text{PTK} = \text{PRF}\_{384}\left(\text{PMK},\ \text{`` pairwise key expansion ''} \mid\mid \min(\text{AA},\text{SA}) \mid\mid \max(\text{AA},\text{SA}) \mid\mid \min(\text{ANonce},\text{SNonce}) \mid\mid \max(\text{ANonce},\text{SNonce})\right)$$

This 384-bit output is split as:
* **KCK (Key Confirmation Key):** 128 bits ÔÇö used to compute MIC over EAPOL frames.
* **KEK (Key Encryption Key):** 128 bits ÔÇö used to encrypt GTK during Message 3.
* **TK (Temporal Key):** 128 bits ÔÇö used for data encryption (CCMP / TKIP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because the PTK depends on the **min/max ordering** of the nonces and MAC addresses, two STAs that swap roles (STA becomes AP) will still derive the same PTK, ensuring deterministic key agreement.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Strength vs. Attack Vector

| Layer | Protocol | Attack Vector | Tool of Choice | Time to Crack (Lab) |
|-------|----------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|
| L2 Auth | WEP Open | Statistical IV collection | aircrack-ng | < 30 s |
| L2 Auth | WPA-PSK | Offline dictionary (handshake) | hashcat / aircrack-ng | hoursÔÇôdays (GPU) |
| L2 Auth | WPA2-PSK | KRACK / Offline dictionary | krackattacks-scripts | exploitation-based |
| L2 Auth | WPA3-SAE | Side-channel timing | custom script | none practical |
| L3 Above | All | Evil twin / Rogue AP | hostapd-wpe | varies |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WLAN Security Protocol Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
timeline
    title Evolution of WLAN Security Protocols
    1997 : WEP (802.11) - RC4 / CRC32
    2003 : WPA (802.11i draft) - TKIP / Michael MIC
    2004 : WPA2 (802.11i ratified) - AES-CCMP
    2018 : WPA3 (802.11-2020) - SAE / AES-GCMP
    2020 : Enhanced Open - OWE (Opportunistic Wireless Encryption)
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a WPA2 Secure Session

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sta[Station STA]
    ap[Access Point AP]
    auth[Authentication Server RADIUS optional]
    pmkgen[PMK Generator PBKDF2 or SAE]
    ptken[PTK Engine PRF 384]
    ccmp[AES CCMP Engine Encrypt / Decrypt]
    four[4 Way Handshake Module]
    gkh[Group Key Handshake]

    sta -->|Probe Request| ap
    ap -->|Probe Response| sta
    sta -->|Open Auth| ap
    ap -->|Auth Success| sta
    sta -->|Association Req| ap
    ap -->|Association Resp| sta
    sta -->|Pre Shared Password| pmkgen
    pmkgen -->|PMK 256 bits| ptken
    ap -->|ANonce| ptken
    sta -->|SNonce| ptken
    ptken -->|PTK 512 bits| four
    four -->|EAPOL Key 1 to 4| ap
    four -->|Install TK| ccmp
    ap -->|GTK encrypted| gkh
    gkh -->|Group key install| ccmp
    ccmp -->|Ciphered MPDU| sta
```

### 4.3 Experimental Capture Workflow (Wireshark / Aircrack-ng)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mon[Monitor Mode Interface wlan0mon]
    cap[airodump-ng Capture cap file]
    deauth[Deauth Burst Targeted STA]
    hs[4 Way Handshake Frames]
    dict[Dictionary File rockyou.txt]
    crack[aircrack-ng or hashcat]
    result[Key Recovery PMK or PTK]

    mon --> cap
    cap --> deauth
    deauth --> hs
    hs --> crack
    dict --> crack
    crack --> result
```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram of Encryption Engines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WEP
        w1[RC4 PRGA]
        w2[CRC32 ICV]
        w3[24 bit IV]
    end
    subgraph TKIP
        t1[RC4 PRGA per packet]
        t2[Michael MIC]
        t3[48 bit TSC]
        t4[Temporal Key Mixer]
    end
    subgraph CCMP
        c1[AES CTR Encrypt]
        c2[AES CBC MAC]
        c3[48 bit PN]
        c4[128 bit TK]
    end
    WEP --> TKIP
    TKIP --> CCMP
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ÔÇö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Differentiate between WEP and WPA in terms of IV size, integrity check, and key management.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
* **WEP** uses a **24-bit IV** and a **40/104-bit static root key**, combined with a linear **CRC-32** integrity check. There is no key management; the same key is shared manually.
* **WPA** uses a **48-bit TSC (TKIP Sequence Counter)**, a dynamically derived **128-bit Temporal Key (TK)** per session, and a stronger cryptographic MIC called **Michael**.
* Hence, WPA addresses the IV reuse and integrity forgery weaknesses of WEP.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ÔÇô June 2024]**

**Q2. What is the role of the 4-Way Handshake in IEEE 802.11i?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The 4-Way Handshake performs three functions:
1. **Mutual authentication** ÔÇö confirms both AP and STA possess the same PMK.
2. **Fresh key generation** ÔÇö produces a new PTK using nonces (ANonce, SNonce) and MAC addresses.
3. **Key installation and confirmation** ÔÇö securely delivers the GTK and confirms the TK has been installed for encryption.

---

### 5.2 Part B ÔÇö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ÔÇö Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of **WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X + AES-CCMP)** and the role of an **Authentication Server (RADIUS)**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer ÔÇö Stepwise:**

1. **Components:** Supplicant (STA), Authenticator (AP), Authentication Server (RADIUS).
2. **Control Plane:** The AP blocks all data frames until the STA completes 802.1X / EAP-TLS (or PEAP). The RADIUS server issues an **MSK (Master Session Key)**.
3. **PMK Derivation:** $\text{PMK} = \text{LMSK}$ (first 256 bits of MSK), or derived via EAP methods.
4. **Data Plane:** Once authenticated, the AP and STA execute the 4-Way Handshake to derive a PTK.
5. **Encryption Engine:** AES-CCM is invoked for every MPDU:
   * **CCM = CTR (confidentiality) + CBC-MAC (integrity)**.
   * Nonce: 48-bit PN + 104-bit AAD (MAC header fields).
6. **GTK Distribution:** AP encrypts the GTK using the KEK and delivers it in Message 3 of the 4-Way Handshake.

**(b)** An experimental setup captures **5,000 WEP frames** in **3 seconds**. Using the Birthday bound, calculate the probability of at least one IV collision. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer ÔÇö Stepwise:**

1. **IV Space:** $N = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$.
2. **Number of frames:** $n = 5000$.
3. **Birthday bound approximation:**
$$P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-n(n-1)/(2N)}$$
4. Substitute values:
$$\frac{n(n-1)}{2N} = \frac{5000 \times 4999}{2 \times 16{,}777{,}216} = \frac{24{,}995{,}000}{33{,}554{,}432} \approx 0.7448$$
5. Compute:
$$P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-0.7448} \approx 1 - 0.4751 \approx 0.525$$
6. **Conclusion:** Approximately **52.5%** probability of at least one IV collision ÔÇö confirming WEP's weakness within seconds.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Compare the **WPA3-Personal SAE Handshake** with the **WPA2-Personal 4-Way Handshake** in terms of authentication, forward secrecy, and resistance to offline dictionary attacks. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer ÔÇö Stepwise:**

| Property | WPA2-PSK (4-Way) | WPA3-Personal (SAE) |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Authentication | Pre-shared key (PSK) sent implicitly | Password authenticated via Zero-Knowledge Proof |
| Forward Secrecy | ÔŁî None (PMK is reused) | Ôťů Yes ÔÇö keys derived per session |
| Offline dictionary | ÔŁî Vulnerable | Ôťů Resistant |
| Primitive | PBKDF2 over PSK | Dragonfly / SAE using ECC or FFC |
| Replay protection | Nonce-based | Scalar exchange with anti-clogging tokens |

* In WPA2-PSK, capturing a single 4-Way Handshake allows an attacker to run a **dictionary attack** offline.
* In WPA3-SAE, the password is never sent and the resulting key changes every session, **breaking** the ability to brute force offline.

**(b)** A network administrator is asked to upgrade a 200-employee corporate office from WEP to the most secure WLAN protocol. Propose a **migration plan** with a justification. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer ÔÇö Stepwise:**

1. **Audit phase:** Use `airodump-ng` and Wireshark to inventory all APs and STA devices; identify hardware that supports **802.11ac/ax** and **WPA3-Enterprise (192-bit)**.
2. **Infrastructure:** Deploy a **RADIUS server (FreeRADIUS)** with EAP-TLS certificates issued by an internal CA.
3. **Pilot phase:** Roll out **WPA2-Enterprise (AES-CCMP)** to half the office to ensure compatibility with legacy devices.
4. **Full migration:** Enable **WPA3-Enterprise (GCMP-256)** in transition mode (WPA2/WPA3 mixed). Force **PMF (Protected Management Frames)** to mitigate deauthentication attacks.
5. **Validation:** Use `hostapd` logs, RADIUS accounting, and Wireshark captures to confirm **CCMP/GCMP** frames (EtherType `0x888E`).
6. **Justification:** WPA3-Enterprise provides 192-bit cryptographic strength, mandated forward secrecy, and complies with **PCI-DSS** and **HIPAA** for regulated industries.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ÔÇö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing IV with PN:** WEP IV is 24 bits and is sent in clear. WPA2 PN is 48 bits and is inside the CCMP header ÔÇö students often swap these in diagrams.
> 2. **Mis-stating the PTK length:** PTK is **512 bits** total in WPA/WPA2 (not 128 bits). The 128-bit Temporal Key is only a *part* of the PTK.
> 3. **Incorrect handshake count:** WPA3-Personal does **not** use a 4-Way Handshake alone ÔÇö it is preceded by an **SAE exchange** (Commit/Confirm). Skipping this earns 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Forgetting PMF:** From 2020 onward, KTU expects students to mention **Protected Management Frames (802.11w)** as part of the WPA3 stack.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> * **WEP:** 40/104-bit key + 24-bit IV; **RC4 + CRC-32**; vulnerable to IV collision in **< 5 s**.
> * **WPA (TKIP):** 128-bit TK + 48-bit TSC; **RC4 + Michael MIC**; backward compatible.
> * **WPA2 (CCMP):** 128-bit TK + 48-bit PN; **AES-CTR + CBC-MAC**; full 802.11i.
> * **WPA3 (SAE + GCMP):** 128-bit personal, 192-bit enterprise; **AES-GCMP + SAE**; **forward secrecy** + offline-dictionary resistance.
> * **4-Way Handshake:** Uses **EAPOL-Key** frames; produces PTK = **KCK + KEK + TK** (128 bits each).
> * **SAE:** Dragonfly handshake using **ECC group 19/20/21**; **resists** offline attacks.
> * **PMF (802.11w):** Mandatory in WPA3; protects **disassociation / deauth** frames.
> * **PTK inputs:** PMK + "pairwise key expansion" + min(AA,SA) + max(AA,SA) + min(ANonce,SNonce) + max(ANonce,SNonce).
> * **Experimental Tools:** `airodump-ng`, `aireplay-ng`, `aircrack-ng`, `hostapd-wpe`, `hcxtools`, Wireshark display filters `eapol`, `wlan.fc.type_subtype == 0x08`.
> * **Key Hierarchy:** PSK Ôćĺ PMK (PBKDF2) Ôćĺ PTK (PRF-384) Ôćĺ TK (128 bits) Ôćĺ per-packet keystream.
> * **KRACK:** A 2017 attack on the **4-Way Handshake re-installation** ÔÇö patched via enforcing **monotonic PN**.
> * **MFP / PMF / 802.11w:** Disassoc and Deauth frames are signed using **BIP (Broadcast Integrity Protocol)** with AES-CMAC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security of Wireless Networks: GSM, UMTS, WEP, IEEE 802.11i, Public WiFi Hotspots, Bluetooth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security of Wireless Networks: GSM, UMTS, WEP, IEEE 802.11i, Public WiFi Hotspots, Bluetooth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wireless Network Security ÔÇö Formal Definition
**Wireless Network Security** is the domain of cryptographic, authentication, and access-control mechanisms engineered to protect data confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity over radio-frequency (RF) propagation media, where the medium is intrinsically shared, broadcast in nature, and physically exposed to eavesdroppers within the Fresnel zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT862 ÔÇô Module 4):**
> *"Study of security architectures, cryptographic primitives, and known vulnerabilities of cellular generations (GSM, UMTS), WLAN standards (WEP, IEEE 802.11i), open access networks (Public WiFi Hotspots), and personal area networks (Bluetooth)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ÔÇö The "Postal System" of Wireless Security

Imagine wireless networks as a chain of **letter-carrying postal systems** evolving over decades:

- **GSM (2G)** Ôćĺ Like a sealed envelope locked with a basic, factory-set pin (the SIM). Anyone who steals the algorithm can pick the lock ÔÇö the lock is universal.
- **UMTS (3G)** Ôćĺ Like an envelope where the sender and receiver *mutually prove their identity using a one-time secret* before the mailman even leaves the office.
- **WEP** Ôćĺ Like a padlock whose combination is the user's name typed backwards ÔÇö once you see one letter, you can crack all locks on the street.
- **IEEE 802.11i (WPA2)** Ôćĺ Like a 4-step handshake where both parties negotiate a *fresh, mathematically independent key* for every single session.
- **Public WiFi Hotspots** Ôćĺ Like a caf├ę bulletin board: convenient, but anyone sitting nearby can read what you pin up unless you wrap it yourself.
- **Bluetooth** Ôćĺ Like two walkie-talkies that auto-pair via a short PIN, but if the PIN is "0000", any kid with a scanner can eavesdrop.

### 1.3 Taxonomy of the Wireless Security Landscape

| Domain | Standard | Era | Primary Threat Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cellular (2G) | GSM | 1991 | Passive eavesdropping, IMSI catching |
| Cellular (3G) | UMTS | 2001 | False Base Station (FBS) / IMSI catching |
| WLAN | WEP | 1999 | Statistical key recovery, IV collision |
| WLAN | IEEE 802.11i | 2004 | Offline dictionary, KRACK |
| Open Access | Public Hotspots | 2000+ | Evil twin, MITM, sniffing |
| PAN | Bluetooth | 1999 | Bluejacking, Bluesnarfing, BIAS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this Module Matters in KTU Examinations:**
> This module constitutes a high-weightage topic in Module 4. KTU examiners frequently ask comparative questions between GSM vs. UMTS and WEP vs. 802.11i. A clear, tabular mental model of these 6 systems is essential for scoring 14-mark answers.

### 1.4 Common Cryptographic Building Blocks (Recap)

All the systems below rely on a small family of primitives:

- **A3** ÔÇö Authentication algorithm in GSM (challengeÔÇôresponse)
- **A5/x** ÔÇö Stream cipher family for GSM air-interface encryption (A5/1, A5/2)
- **A8** ÔÇö Key-generation algorithm producing the session key $K_c$
- **KASUMI** ÔÇö Block cipher (MISTY1 derivative) used in UMTS
- **RC4** ÔÇö Stream cipher (broken in WEP)
- **AES-CCMP** ÔÇö Block-cipher mode used in 802.11i
- **E0** ÔÇö Stream cipher used in Bluetooth
- **SAFER+** ÔÇö Used in Bluetooth authentication and key generation

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) Security

GSM was the first commercial cellular standard with built-in cryptographic security. The architecture is a **client-server model** involving three entities: the **MS (Mobile Station)**, the **SIM (Subscriber Identity Module)**, and the **AuC/HLR (Authentication Center / Home Location Register)**.

#### 2.1.1 The Triplet of GSM Algorithms
1. **A3 ÔÇö Authentication Algorithm**
   - Computes **SRES** (Signed Response), a 32-bit value.
   - Inputs: 128-bit secret key $K_i$ (stored in SIM) + 128-bit random challenge **RAND**.
2. **A8 ÔÇö Key-Generation Algorithm**
   - Computes the 64-bit session cipher key $K_c$.
   - Uses the same $K_i$ and **RAND** as inputs.
3. **A5/x ÔÇö Air-Interface Cipher**
   - Stream cipher used to encrypt/decrypt traffic between MS and BTS.
   - Variants: A5/1 (export-grade), A5/2 (deliberately weak), A5/3 (KASUMI-based, 3G-compatible).

#### 2.1.2 GSM Authentication Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. BTS generates a random 128-bit number **RAND**.
2. RAND is sent to the MS over the radio link (in clear).
3. SIM computes $SRES = A3(K_i, RAND)$ and $K_c = A8(K_i, RAND)$.
4. MS sends SRES back to the network.
5. The AuC (which already knows $K_i$) recomputes the expected SRES and compares.
6. If they match, MS is authenticated; $K_c$ is now used by A5/x to encrypt the traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Flaw:** Authentication is **one-way** (network Ôćĺ MS). A *false base station* (FBS) or *IMSI catcher* (e.g., Stingray) can claim to be a legitimate BTS and request RAND from the MS. The MS *must* respond ÔÇö there is no authentication of the network side.

#### 2.1.3 GSM Security ÔÇö Mathematical Notation

$$SRES = A3(K_i, RAND)$$

$$K_c = A8(K_i, RAND)$$

$$C[i] = A5/1(K_c, \text{frame\_counter}) \oplus P[i]$$

where $C[i]$ is the $i$-th ciphertext bit and $P[i]$ is the $i$-th plaintext bit.

---

### 2.2 UMTS (Universal Mobile Telecommunications System) Security

UMTS introduces **mutual authentication** and a **sequence-number-based stream cipher**, directly addressing the false-base-station attack of GSM.

#### 2.2.1 The UMTS AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement) Protocol

UMTS uses a 5-tuple authentication vector (AV) generated by the AuC:

$$AV = (RAND \Vert XRES \Vert CK \Vert IK \Vert AUTN)$$

Where:
- **RAND** ÔÇö 128-bit random challenge
- **XRES** ÔÇö Expected Response (32ÔÇô128 bits)
- **CK** ÔÇö Confidentiality Key (128 bits)
- **IK** ÔÇö Integrity Key (128 bits)
- **AUTN** ÔÇö Authentication Token (used to authenticate the network)

#### 2.2.2 Mutual Authentication Logic

1. VLR/SGSN requests AV from HLR/AuC.
2. AuC computes:
   - $XRES = f1_K(RAND)$
   - $CK = f3_K(RAND)$
   - $IK = f4_K(RAND)$
   - $AUTN = SQN \oplus AK \Vert AMF \Vert MAC$
   - where $MAC = f1_K(SQN \Vert RAND \Vert AMF)$ and $AK = f5_K(RAND)$
3. VLR sends $(RAND, AUTN)$ to the MS (called USIM in UMTS).
4. USIM verifies $MAC$ (proving network's authenticity) and checks $SQN$ is fresh (replay protection).
5. USIM computes $RES = f1_K(RAND)$ and returns it to VLR.
6. VLR compares $RES$ with $XRES$ ÔÇö proves MS authenticity.

#### 2.2.3 UMTS Air-Interface Integrity & Confidentiality

- **Integrity (f9):** $MAC\text{-}I = f9_{IK}(\text{COUNT} \Vert \text{FRESH} \Vert \text{DIRECTION} \Vert \text{MESSAGE})$
- **Confidentiality (f8):** $C[i] = P[i] \oplus \text{Keystream}_i$, where Keystream is generated by KASUMI under $CK$.

---

### 2.3 WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy)

WEP was the original security protocol of IEEE 802.11 (1999), designed to provide "wired-equivalent" confidentiality over a wireless LAN.

#### 2.3.1 WEP Architecture

WEP uses **RC4**, a stream cipher, in a vulnerable configuration:

1. A 24-bit **IV (Initialization Vector)** is concatenated with a 40-bit or 104-bit shared secret key to form the 64-bit or 128-bit RC4 seed.
2. $RC4(IV \Vert K)$ generates a keystream $ks$.
3. $C = P \oplus ks$
4. $ICV = CRC\text{-}32(P)$ is appended for integrity.
5. Transmitted frame: $IV \Vert C \Vert ICV$

> [!NOTE]
> **WEP Frame Format:**
> `| IV (24b) | Header | Payload ÔŐĽ RC4(IVÔÇľK) | ICV (32b) |`

#### 2.3.2 Why WEP Fails ÔÇö The "Why" Behind Each Vulnerability

1. **Tiny IV Space (2┬▓Ôü┤ Ôëł 16 million):** By the birthday paradox, IV collision occurs within ~4824 frames. With a busy AP, this is **hours**.
2. **Weak Key Scheduling:** The first few bytes of the RC4 keystream reveal information about the key for certain IVs (FluhrerÔÇôMantinÔÇôShamir attack, 2001).
3. **CRC-32 is not a cryptographic MAC:** Bit-flipping attacks are trivial.
4. **No Key Management:** Static shared keys; rotation is manual and rarely done.

---

### 2.4 IEEE 802.11i (WPA2) ÔÇö The Robust Security Network

IEEE 802.11i, ratified in 2004, replaces WEP with a proper key-management framework based on **RSN (Robust Security Network)** and a **4-way handshake**.

#### 2.4.1 The 802.11i Key Hierarchy

```
                Master Key (MK / MSK)
                       |
            Pairwise Master Key (PMK)         [from 802.1X/EAP]
                       |
        Pairwise Transient Key (PTK)         [from 4-way handshake]
        |                |                |
       KCK             KEK              TK
   (Key Confirmation) (Key Encryption) (Temporal Key)
                                            |
                                  Used by CCMP / TKIP
```

#### 2.4.2 The 4-Way Handshake Logic

1. **Msg1:** AP Ôćĺ STA: ANonce
2. **Msg2:** STA Ôćĺ AP: SNonce + MIC (using KCK)
3. **AP computes PTK** = PRF(PMK, ANonce, SNonce, MAC_AP, MAC_STA)
4. **Msg3:** AP Ôćĺ STA: Install PTK + Group Key + MIC
5. **Msg4:** STA Ôćĺ AP: ACK + MIC

After Msg4, both sides install the **TK (Temporal Key)** for CCMP encryption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PTK Derivation (KCK Math):**
> $$PTK = PRF\text{-}384(PMK, \text{ÔÇťPairwise key expansionÔÇŁ}, \min(AA,SA) \Vert \max(SA,AA) \Vert \min(ANonce,SNonce) \Vert \max(ANonce,SNonce))$$

#### 2.4.3 CCMP (Counter-Mode CBC-MAC Protocol)

CCMP is mandatory in WPA2 and uses **AES-128** in two modes simultaneously:
- **CTR mode** for confidentiality
- **CBC-MAC** for integrity and authenticity

A CCMP frame structure: `| PN (48b) | Header | Encrypted Payload | MIC (8 bytes) |`

---

### 2.5 Public WiFi Hotspots

Public hotspots (caf├ęs, airports, hotels) use **captive portals** and often transmit data **unencrypted** (Open System authentication).

#### 2.5.1 Threat Model

| Threat | Description |
|---|---|
| **Passive Sniffing** | Attacker captures all traffic in monitor mode. |
| **Evil Twin** | Attacker spoofs AP's SSID, luring clients. |
| **MITM** | Attacker proxies traffic between victim and internet. |
| **Session Hijacking** | Cookies captured over HTTP can be replayed. |

#### 2.5.2 Mitigation Strategies

- **EAP-based authentication** (e.g., EAP-TTLS, PEAP) over 802.1X.
- **VPN** end-to-end tunnel by the client.
- **HTTPS-Everywhere** style browser enforcement.
- **WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise** on the AP side.

---

### 2.6 Bluetooth Security

Bluetooth security is divided into three modes: **Mode 1 (no security)**, **Mode 2 (L2CAP-level)**, and **Mode 3 (link-level)**.

#### 2.6.1 Legacy Pairing (Pre-2.1)

Uses **E1** (SAFER+ based) for authentication and **E0** (stream cipher) for encryption.
- PIN-based; PIN lengths from 8 to 128 bits.
- Vulnerable to offline brute-force if PIN is short.

#### 2.6.2 Secure Simple Pairing (SSP, Bluetooth 2.1+)

Uses **ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman)** for key exchange with four association models:
1. **Just Works** ÔÇö no user interaction (vulnerable to MITM)
2. **Numeric Comparison** ÔÇö displays 6-digit code
3. **Passkey Entry** ÔÇö one device enters a code
4. **Out of Band** ÔÇö uses NFC or another channel

#### 2.6.3 Known Bluetooth Attacks
- **Bluejacking** ÔÇö sending unsolicited messages
- **Bluesnarfing** ÔÇö unauthorized read of phonebook / data
- **Bluebugging** ÔÇö taking control of the device
- **BIAS (Bluetooth Impersonation AttackS)** ÔÇö 2020 attack on secure pairing

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Notation | Notes / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| GSM Authentication | $SRES = A3(K_i, RAND)$ | SRES is 32 bits |
| GSM Key Gen | $K_c = A8(K_i, RAND)$ | $K_c$ is 64 bits |
| WEP Cipher | $C = P \oplus RC4(IV \Vert K)$ | IV is **24 bits** |
| WEP IV Birthday Bound | $N \approx \sqrt{2^{24}} \approx 4824$ | Frames before collision |
| UMTS AV | $AV = (RAND \Vert XRES \Vert CK \Vert IK \Vert AUTN)$ | 5-tuple |
| 802.11i PTK Size | $PTK = KCK + KEK + TK + ...$ | Total 512 bits for CCMP |
| CCMP Packet Number | $PN$ | **48 bits** |
| Bluetooth E0 State | LFSR of 4 registers | $25 + 31 + 33 + 39$ bits |
| KASUMI Block Size | 64 bits | 128-bit key, 8 rounds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Every cryptographic protocol here is implemented in hardware you carry daily ÔÇö your SIM card (A3/A8), your WiFi chipset (AES-CCMP), and your Bluetooth SoC (ECDH). Understanding the security of these protocols is foundational for careers in mobile network engineering, IoT security, and penetration testing.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 WEP Statistical Attack ÔÇö Birthday Bound Derivation

The **birthday paradox** tells us the expected number of samples $N$ to find a collision among $M$ possible values is:

$$N \approx 1.177 \sqrt{M}$$

For WEP's 24-bit IV, $M = 2^{24}$. We derive the collision probability.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $p(n)$ be the probability that **no** collision occurs after $n$ IVs.

The first IV has no prior frame to collide with; the second has $\frac{1}{2^{24}}$ chance of collision with the first; the $k$-th has $\frac{k-1}{2^{24}}$ chance.

$$p(n) = \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{2^{24}}\right)$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\ln p(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \ln\left(1 - \frac{i}{2^{24}}\right)$$

For small $x$, $\ln(1 - x) \approx -x$, so:

$$\ln p(n) \approx -\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{i}{2^{24}} = -\frac{1}{2^{24}} \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = -\frac{n(n-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{24}}$$

Setting $p(n) = 0.5$ (50% collision probability) and solving for $n$:

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{n^2}{2 \cdot 2^{24}}$$

$$n^2 = 2 \cdot 2^{24} \cdot \ln 2$$

$$n = \sqrt{2 \cdot 2^{24} \cdot 0.6931}$$

$$n \approx \sqrt{1.386 \cdot 2^{24}} \approx \sqrt{23220335} \approx 4824 \text{ frames}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** With a 24-bit IV space, a **50% probability of IV reuse** occurs after only **~4824 frames**. At a typical AP throughput of 1000 frames/min, this is reached in under 8 minutes.

---

### 3.2 UMTS AKA ÔÇö Mutual Authentication Walk-through

#### Step 1: AV Generation by AuC
The AuC receives a request for an authentication vector and computes:

$$XRES = f1_K(RAND)$$

$$CK = f3_K(RAND)$$

$$IK = f4_K(RAND)$$

$$AK = f5_K(RAND)$$

$$MAC = f1_K(SQN \Vert RAND \Vert AMF)$$

$$AUTN = (SQN \oplus AK) \Vert AMF \Vert MAC$$

The tuple $(RAND, XRES, CK, IK, AUTN)$ is sent to the VLR/SGSN.

#### Step 2: USIM Verification

Upon receiving $(RAND, AUTN)$:
1. Extract $(SQN \oplus AK)$, $AMF$, $MAC$ from AUTN.
2. Recover $AK = f5_K(RAND)$ using the locally stored $K$.
3. Recover $SQN = (SQN \oplus AK) \oplus AK$.
4. Compute expected $MAC' = f1_K(SQN \Vert RAND \Vert AMF)$ and compare with received $MAC$.
5. If $MAC' = MAC$ and $SQN$ is fresh, the **network is authenticated**.
6. Compute $RES = f1_K(RAND)$ and send it to VLR.
7. VLR compares $RES$ with stored $XRES$ ÔÇö **MS is authenticated**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The functions $f1, f2, f3, f4, f5, f5^*$ are all built on KASUMI (a simplified MISTY1). USIM chips are tamper-resistant smart cards evaluated under Common Criteria EAL5+.

---

### 3.3 IEEE 802.11i ÔÇö PTK Derivation Exhaustive Walk-through

Given inputs:
- $PMK$ (256 bits, from EAP)
- $ANonce$ (AP nonce, 128 bits)
- $SNonce$ (STA nonce, 128 bits)
- $AA = MAC_{AP}$, $SA = MAC_{STA}$

The PTK is derived as follows:

$$PTK = PRF\text{-}384(PMK, \text{ÔÇťPairwise key expansionÔÇŁ}, $$

$$\min(AA,SA) \Vert \max(SA,AA) \Vert \min(ANonce,SNonce) \Vert \max(ANonce,SNonce))$$

The resulting 384 bits are split:

- $KCK$ ÔÇö 128 bits (used for MIC computation)
- $KEK$ ÔÇö 128 bits (used to encrypt GTK)
- $TK$ ÔÇö 128 bits (used by CCMP for data encryption)
- Optional 128-bit MIC key for TKIP

The PRF (Pseudo-Random Function) is built on HMAC-SHA-1:

$$PRF\text{-}384(K, A, B) = HMAC\text{-}SHA\text{-}1(K, A \Vert 0x00 \Vert B \Vert Y_i) \Vert \ldots$$

where $Y_i$ iterates over $0,1,2$ to produce the 384-bit output.

---

### 3.4 GSM-to-3G Migration Analysis Matrix

| Feature | GSM (2G) | UMTS (3G) | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Network Authentication | ÔŁî None | Ôťů Mutual via AKA | Defeats IMSI catcher |
| Algorithm | A5/1 (64-bit) | KASUMI (128-bit) | Doubled key size |
| Integrity Protection | ÔŁî None | Ôťů f9 with IK | Bit-flip attacks defeated |
| Key Length | $K_c$ = 64 bits | $CK = IK = 128$ bits | Larger search space |
| Cryptographic Functions | A3, A8, A5 | $f1, f2, f3, f4, f5, f9$ | Formalized, auditable |
| Crypto Agility | ÔŁî Fixed | Ôťů Operator can choose | Future-proof |

---

### 3.5 WEP-to-802.11i Migration Analysis Matrix

| Feature | WEP | IEEE 802.11i (WPA2) | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cipher | RC4 (stream) | AES-CCMP (block) | Eliminates statistical attacks |
| IV Size | 24 bits | 48 bits (PN) | $2.8 \times 10^{14}$ vs $1.6 \times 10^{7}$ |
| Integrity | CRC-32 (linear) | CBC-MAC (cryptographic) | Bit-flipping defeated |
| Key Management | Static shared | 4-way handshake, fresh PTK per session | Forward secrecy |
| Authentication | Open / Shared | 802.1X / EAP / PSK | Scalable enterprise auth |
| Replay Protection | None | PN monotonic counter | Replay defeated |

---

### 3.6 Bluetooth E0 Cipher ÔÇö Structural Overview

The E0 stream cipher uses **four linear feedback shift registers (LFSRs)** with lengths 25, 31, 33, and 39 bits. The total state is 128 bits.

$$LFSR_1: 25 \text{ bits}, \quad LFSR_2: 31 \text{ bits}$$
$$LFSR_3: 33 \text{ bits}, \quad LFSR_4: 39 \text{ bits}$$

The keystream is generated as:

$$z_t = x_t^{(1)} \oplus x_t^{(2)} \oplus x_t^{(3)} \oplus x_t^{(4)} \oplus c_t^{(0)}$$

where the $x_t^{(i)}$ are bits from LFSR-$i$ at time $t$, and $c_t^{(0)}$ is a carry bit from a finite-state machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway for KTU:** E0 is known to be vulnerable ÔÇö a 2005 attack recovers the key in $O(2^{70})$ operations. This motivated the introduction of **Secure Simple Pairing (SSP)** with ECDH in Bluetooth 2.1+.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GSM Authentication & Key Generation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant BTS as Base Station
    participant MS as Mobile Station
    participant SIM as SIM Card
    participant AuC as AuC/HLR

    BTS->>MS: 1. RAND (128-bit)
    Note over MS,SIM: SIM holds secret Ki
    SIM->>SIM: 2. Compute SRES = A3(Ki, RAND)
    SIM->>SIM: 3. Compute Kc = A8(Ki, RAND)
    SIM->>BTS: 4. SRES (32-bit)
    BTS->>AuC: 5. Lookup Ki by IMSI
    AuC->>AuC: 6. Expected SRES' = A3(Ki, RAND)
    AuC-->>BTS: 7. SRES' for comparison
    BTS->>BTS: 8. SRES == SRES'? Authenticated
    BTS->>MS: 9. Encrypted traffic using A5/1(Kc)
    Note right of BTS: One-way: MS cannot verify BTS
```

### 4.2 UMTS Mutual Authentication (AKA Protocol)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant VLR as VLR/SGSN
    participant USIM as USIM
    participant AuC as AuC/HLR

    VLR->>AuC: 1. Request Authentication Vector

    AuC-->>VLR: 3. AV
    VLR->>USIM: 4. (RAND, AUTN)
    USIM->>USIM: 5. Verify MAC in AUTN
    USIM->>USIM: 6. Check SQN freshness
    USIM->>VLR: 7. RES
    VLR->>VLR: 8. RES == XRES? Authenticated
    Note over VLR,USIM: Mutual authentication complete
    VLR->>USIM: 9. Encrypted + Integrity-protected traffic
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ÔÇö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ÔÇô May 2024]**
*List the three cryptographic algorithms used in GSM and state the purpose of each.*

**Model Answer:**
The three algorithms are:
1. **A3 ÔÇö Authentication Algorithm:** Generates a 32-bit Signed Response (SRES) using the secret key $K_i$ and a random challenge RAND to authenticate the mobile subscriber.
2. **A8 ÔÇö Key-Generation Algorithm:** Computes the 64-bit cipher key $K_c$ from $K_i$ and RAND.
3. **A5/x ÔÇö Air-Interface Cipher:** A stream cipher (A5/1, A5/2, A5/3) that encrypts traffic between the mobile station and base station using $K_c$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ÔÇô Dec 2023]**
*Why is WEP considered cryptographically broken even though it uses RC4?*

**Model Answer:**
WEP is broken due to:
1. **24-bit IV space** ÔÇö small enough to cause IV reuse within ~4824 frames (birthday paradox), enabling statistical key recovery.
2. **Weak IV keys (FMS attack)** ÔÇö FluhrerÔÇôMantinÔÇôShamir (2001) showed certain IVs leak key bytes in the first RC4 output bytes.
3. **CRC-32 ICV** ÔÇö not a cryptographic MAC, so integrity can be defeated by bit-flipping.
4. **Static shared keys** ÔÇö no automated key management, so all clients share the same key, amplifying attack impact.

---

### Part B ÔÇö Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In 14-mark questions on WEP/802.11i, students often:
> - **Confuse RC4 and AES-CCMP** ÔÇö RC4 is a stream cipher; AES-CCMP uses CTR + CBC-MAC modes.
> - **Forget to mention the IV/PN sizes** ÔÇö always state "24 bits" for WEP and "48 bits" for 802.11i.
> - **Skip the 4-way handshake steps** ÔÇö examiners expect a labeled sequence diagram or numbered steps.
> - **Omit vulnerabilities** ÔÇö saying "WEP is broken" without *why* (FMS, IV collision) loses 4ÔÇô5 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Security Comparison of GSM and UMTS**

**[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Model Paper 2024]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the GSM security architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss the A3, A5, and A8 algorithms and their roles in the authentication and key-agreement process. Why is GSM authentication considered one-way?

**Model Answer:**

GSM security relies on three core algorithms:

- **A3 (Authentication Algorithm):** Implements challengeÔÇôresponse authentication. Given the 128-bit secret $K_i$ stored in the SIM and a 128-bit RAND from the network, it produces a 32-bit SRES. Equation: $SRES = A3(K_i, RAND)$.

- **A8 (Key-Generation Algorithm):** Co-resident with A3 in the SIM. Computes the 64-bit session key $K_c = A8(K_i, RAND)$.

- **A5/x (Encryption Algorithm):** A stream cipher implemented in the mobile hardware (not the SIM). Encrypts the radio link using $K_c$. Variants: A5/1 (commercial), A5/2 (deliberately weak export), A5/3 (KASUMI).

**Authentication Steps:**
1. AuC generates a 128-bit RAND and sends it to the MS.
2. SIM computes SRES using A3 and the stored $K_i$.
3. MS transmits SRES to the BTS.
4. AuC recomputes the expected SRES and compares.
5. If they match, the network accepts the MS and traffic is encrypted using A5/1(Kc).

**Why One-Way:** The MS *never* verifies the network's identity. There is no challenge from the MS to the network, so an attacker with a false base station (IMSI catcher) can impersonate a legitimate BTS and force the MS to reveal SRES or commit to a session.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the UMTS Authentication and Key Agreement (AKA) protocol. How does it overcome the security weaknesses of GSM?

**Model Answer:**

UMTS AKA achieves **mutual authentication** using a 5-tuple Authentication Vector (AV):

$$AV = (RAND, XRES, CK, IK, AUTN)$$

**Functions (all KASUMI-based):**
- $f1$ ÔÇö MAC generation
- $f2$ ÔÇö RES generation (used as challenge response)
- $f3$ ÔÇö CK derivation
- $f4$ ÔÇö IK derivation
- $f5, f5^*$ ÔÇö Anonymity Key (AK) and resynchronization

**Mutual Authentication Walk-through:**
1. HLR/AuC generates AV.
2. VLR/SGSN forwards $(RAND, AUTN)$ to the USIM.
3. USIM verifies $MAC$ inside $AUTN$ using $f1$ ÔÇö **authenticates the network**.
4. USIM checks sequence number $SQN$ for freshness ÔÇö **replay protection**.
5. USIM computes $RES = f2_K(RAND)$ and returns it to VLR.
6. VLR compares $RES == XRES$ ÔÇö **authenticates the subscriber**.

**How UMTS Overcomes GSM Weaknesses:**
| Weakness in GSM | UMTS Solution |
|---|---|
| No network authentication | Mutual authentication via MAC in AUTN |
| A5/1 cipher (weak) | KASUMI / 128-bit CK |
| No integrity protection | $f9$ with IK, MAC-I on every message |
| Replay attacks possible | $SQN$ counter, freshness check |
| Static, short keys | 128-bit CK and IK, fresh per session |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): WEP vs IEEE 802.11i (WPA2)**

**[KTU University Exam ÔÇô Model Paper 2024]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the WEP protocol with its frame format. Discuss in detail the cryptographic weaknesses of WEP.

**Model Answer:**

**WEP Frame Format:**
`| IV (24b) | MAC Header | Encrypted Payload | ICV (32b) |`

**Encryption Operation:**
$$C = P \oplus RC4(IV \Vert K)$$
where:
- $P = \text{Plaintext} \Vert CRC\text{-}32(P)$
- $K = \text{40 or 104-bit shared key}$
- $IV$ is sent in clear

**Cryptographic Weaknesses:**

1. **Short IV (24 bits):** After ~4824 frames, IV reuse is statistically guaranteed (birthday paradox). When two ciphertexts share an IV, XOR-ing them cancels the keystream, yielding $(P_1 \oplus P_2)$, from which a known-plaintext attack recovers the key.

2. **FMS Attack (FluhrerÔÇôMantinÔÇôShamir, 2001):** Certain "weak IVs" cause the first bytes of the RC4 keystream to leak key bytes. Aircrack-ng and similar tools recover the key from ~4 million captured frames.

3. **CRC-32 is Linear:** $CRC\text{-}32(P \oplus \Delta) = CRC\text{-}32(P) \oplus CRC\text{-}32(\Delta)$. An attacker can flip bits in the ciphertext and patch the ICV without knowing the key ÔÇö defeating integrity.

4. **No Replay Protection:** The IV is not bound to a frame counter, so captured frames can be replayed.

5. **No Key Management:** Static, shared keys across all clients. A single compromised client reveals the key to all.

6. **Authentication is Weak:** Open System or Shared Key (which actually *leaks* keystream bytes during the challenge).

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the IEEE 802.11i (RSN/WPA2) architecture. Explain the 4-way handshake and the role of PTK, KCK, KEK, and TK.

**Model Answer:**

**802.11i Architecture:**
The 802.11i amendment introduced the **Robust Security Network (RSN)** with two protocols:
- **TKIP (interim, optional in WPA2)** ÔÇö wraps WEP with per-packet keys
- **CCMP (mandatory)** ÔÇö AES in CTR + CBC-MAC mode

**Key Hierarchy:**

```
MSK (from EAP/802.1X) Ôćĺ PMK Ôćĺ PTK
                              ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ KCK (Key Confirmation, 128b)
                              ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ KEK (Key Encryption, 128b)
                              ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ TK  (Temporal Key, 128b)
```

**4-Way Handshake Steps:**
1. **Message 1:** AP sends ANonce to STA.
2. **Message 2:** STA generates SNonce, computes PTK using PRF-384, and returns SNonce + MIC (using KCK).
3. **AP verifies MIC**, computes its own PTK, sends Message 3: GTK (encrypted under KEK) + MIC.
4. **STA installs PTK & GTK**, sends Message 4: ACK + MIC to confirm.

**Roles of Sub-Keys:**
- **KCK (Key Confirmation Key):** Computes MICs on handshake messages ÔÇö protects against forgery.
- **KEK (Key Encryption Key):** Encrypts the Group Temporal Key (GTK) when AP sends it in Msg3.
- **TK (Temporal Key):** The actual data-encryption key fed to CCMP (AES-CTR) for confidentiality and CBC-MAC for integrity.

**Why This Is Better than WEP:**
- **Fresh session keys:** A new PTK is generated for every association ÔÇö forward secrecy.
- **Strong cipher:** AES-128 in CTR + CBC-MAC.
- **Cryptographic integrity:** CBC-MAC is unforgeable; bit-flipping is detected.
- **Replay protection:** 48-bit packet number (PN) increments monotonically; old frames are dropped.
- **Scalable authentication:** 802.1X/EAP supports per-user credentials.

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GSM = One-way auth**; **UMTS = Mutual auth** using AUTN/MAC.
- GSM uses **A3, A8, A5**; UMTS uses **$f1, f2, f3, f4, f5, f9$** (all on KASUMI).
- WEP = **RC4 + 24-bit IV + CRC-32** Ôćĺ broken by **FMS + IV collision**.
- 802.11i = **AES-CCMP + 4-way handshake + 48-bit PN** Ôćĺ defeats WEP attacks.
- **PTK = KCK + KEK + TK**; derived via **PRF-384** with both nonces.
- **Public WiFi Hotspots** are inherently risky; defend with **VPN / HTTPS / EAP**.
- **Bluetooth pre-2.1** uses **E0 + E1 + PIN**; **SSP (2.1+)** uses **ECDH P-192 + AE-SAFER+**.
- Remember the **birthday bound formula** $N \approx 1.177 \sqrt{M}$ for WEP IV analysis.
- For 14-mark answers, **always include a sequence diagram or block diagram** ÔÇö KTU examiners reward visual clarity.
- **Common pitfall:** Mixing up cipher types (RC4 = stream; KASUMI and AES = block). Always state the mode of operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip (Module 4):**
> When asked to "compare" two protocols, use a **comparison table** with at least 4ÔÇô5 rows covering authentication, cipher, key size, integrity, and key management. This guarantees full marks on 14-mark comparison questions, which is the most frequently asked pattern in KTU PECCT862.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicular Ad-hoc Networks: Vulnerabilities, Challenges, Security Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT862 - SECURITY IN WIRELESS NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicular Ad-hoc Networks: Vulnerabilities, Challenges, Security Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicular Ad-hoc Network (VANET)** is a subclass of Mobile Ad-hoc Networks (MANETs) that facilitates communication among **Vehicles (V2V)**, **Roadside Units/RSUs (V2I)**, and **Infrastructure/V2X** entities using short-range wireless communication protocols, primarily operating in the **5.9 GHz Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC)** band as defined by the **IEEE 802.11p** standard and the **IEEE 1609** family (WAVE ÔÇö Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments).

### Formal Entity Components of a VANET
| Entity | Abbreviation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| On-Board Unit | **OBU** | Wireless transceiver + processor mounted inside the vehicle |
| Roadside Unit | **RSU** | Fixed infrastructure unit (e.g., traffic signal, lamp post) |
| Application Unit | **AU** | In-vehicle device running VANET applications |
| Trusted Authority | **TA / CA** | Centralized Certificate Authority issuing pseudonymous credentials |
| Domain Authority | **DA** | Regional sub-authority under a root TA |
| Domain Manager | **DM** | Manages revocation lists and trust domains |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy ÔÇö "The Highway WhatsApp Group":**
>
> Imagine every car on a highway is a smartphone in a giant, moving WhatsApp group. The "Roadside Units" are the Wi-Fi towers/hotspots along the road. Cars constantly broadcast "Basic Safety Messages" (BSM) ÔÇö *"Hey, I am Car #1234, going 80 km/h, braking in 50 meters."* Other cars receive this and react (e.g., auto-braking before the driver even sees the obstacle).
>
> **The Problem:** Anyone with a hacked radio can impersonate Car #1234 and broadcast fake emergency braking messages ÔÇö causing multi-car pile-ups. Worse, tracking the real car's identity across messages could let stalkers follow a person home. So, just like WhatsApp needs **end-to-end encryption + identity privacy**, VANETs need a *cryptographic security architecture* with **pseudonymous authentication** to keep the system safe.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Operating Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Operating Frequency Band:** **5.850 ÔÇô 5.925 GHz** (5.9 GHz DSRC, **75 MHz** spectrum, FCC allocation)
> - **Channel Bandwidth:** **10 MHz** (or 20 MHz in ETSI ITS-G5)
> - **Effective Range (V2V):** **100 ÔÇô 300 meters** (typical); up to **1 km** with optimal line-of-sight
> - **Vehicle Relative Velocity:** Up to **500 km/h** (relative)
> - **Message Frequency:** BSM broadcast at **10 Hz** (100 ms interval)
> - **Maximum Tolerable End-to-End Latency:** **< 100 ms** for safety-critical messages
> - **Maximum Tolerable Packet Loss:** **< 5%** for safety applications
> - **Pseudonym Lifetime:** Typically **120 ÔÇô 300 seconds** (rotated to preserve location privacy)

---

## 1.4 Why VANETs are Architecturally Distinct from MANETs

| Property | MANET | VANET |
|---|---|---|
| Node Mobility | Pedestrian (ÔëĄ 6 km/h) | Vehicular (ÔëĄ 250 km/h) |
| Topology Change | Slow | **Very fast** (link lifetime ~ 1 s) |
| Power Constraint | Battery-limited | **No power constraint** (vehicle battery) |
| Computational Constraint | Moderate | **High** (modern vehicle CPU) |
| Storage | Limited | **High** (GB-scale) |
| Movement Pattern | Random | **Predictable** (constrained by roads) |
| Privacy Requirement | Low | **Very High** (location privacy) |
| Network Density | Variable | **Variable but high** (urban rush hour) |
| Communication Type | Mostly unicast | **Predominantly broadcast** (beacon) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes the *trust model* and *certificate-based pseudonymity* that distinguish VANET security from generic MANET security. Expect questions framed around **why traditional MANET protocols (e.g., AODV) fail in VANETs** and how the **IEEE 1609.2 security stack** replaces them.

---

## 1.5 The Three Reference Scenarios of V2X Communication

1. **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle):** Direct car-to-car communication, no infrastructure required.
2. **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure):** Communication between cars and RSUs (e.g., traffic light priority).
3. **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything):** Umbrella term including V2V, V2I, V2P (Pedestrian), and V2N (Network/Cellular).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Security:** Each V2X scenario introduces a *different trust boundary* and *different attack surface*. V2V requires peer authentication, V2I requires RSU authentication, V2N requires cellular network trust.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of VANET Security

The IEEE 1609.2 standard defines a layered security architecture mapped to the WAVE protocol stack:

```
ÔöîÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÉ
Ôöé      Application Layer (Safety / Non-Safety)Ôöé
ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöĄ
Ôöé  IEEE 1609.2 ÔÇö Security Services            Ôöé  ÔćÉ Authentication, Encryption, Signing
ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöĄ
Ôöé  IEEE 1609.3 ÔÇö Networking Services (WSMP)   Ôöé  ÔćÉ WAVE Short Message Protocol
ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöĄ
Ôöé  IEEE 1609.4 ÔÇö Multi-Channel Operations     Ôöé  ÔćÉ CCH/SCH switching
ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöĄ
Ôöé  IEEE 802.11p ÔÇö MAC/PHY (5.9 GHz DSRC)      Ôöé  ÔćÉ OFDM, 10 MHz channels
ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöĄ
Ôöé  Physical Radio (5.9 GHz Transceiver)       Ôöé
ÔööÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöś
```

---

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of VANET Security (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2024 KTU syllabus expects students to articulate **all five** security services and provide at least one cryptographic mechanism for each.

### Pillar 1 ÔÇö **Authentication** (Message Origin Verification)
- **Goal:** Verify that a BSM genuinely originated from a legitimate, registered vehicle/OBU.
- **Mechanism:** **Digital Signatures** using **ECDSA** (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm) over the **NIST P-256** curve.
- **Why ECDSA, not RSA:** A VANET BSM is **Ôëł 400 bytes payload**, transmitted at **10 Hz**. RSA-2048 signatures are 256 bytes; ECDSA-256 signatures are only **64 bytes** ÔÇö a 4├Ś reduction in overhead. At 600 BSMs/minute, this bandwidth saving is critical.

### Pillar 2 ÔÇö **Confidentiality** (Message Privacy)
- **Goal:** Prevent eavesdropping of non-safety traffic (e.g., infotainment, payment).
- **Mechanism:** **AES-128-CCM** (Counter Mode + CBC-MAC) ÔÇö provides both encryption and integrity in one pass.
- **Note:** *Safety messages are NOT encrypted* ÔÇö they must be readable by any nearby vehicle.

### Pillar 3 ÔÇö **Integrity** (Anti-Tampering)
- **Goal:** Ensure the BSM has not been modified in transit.
- **Mechanism:** Hash-based digest (SHA-256) inside the ECDSA signature.

### Pillar 4 ÔÇö **Non-Repudiation**
- **Goal:** A sender cannot deny having sent a signed message.
- **Mechanism:** Asymmetric signature (private key kept in tamper-resistant HSM of OBU).

### Pillar 5 ÔÇö **Privacy / Anonymity** (Location Privacy)
- **Goal:** Prevent long-term tracking of a vehicle by linking multiple BSMs.
- **Mechanism:** **Pseudonymous Certificates** (short-lived, unlinkable credentials from the TA).
- **Key Property:** A vehicle holds **N pseudonyms** (typically 10,000 ÔÇô 43,800 for a year), rotated every **120 ÔÇô 300 seconds**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the most tested formulas and parameters. These appear in the 2024 Scheme Part B questions on VANET security performance.

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Communication Range | $R \leq 1000$ m (line-of-sight) | meters | 802.11p max |
| 2 | BSM Broadcast Rate | $f_{BSM} = 10$ Hz | Hz | 100 ms interval |
| 3 | Channel Bandwidth | $BW = 10$ MHz | MHz | 5.9 GHz band |
| 4 | Maximum Latency (Safety) | $L_{max} \leq 100$ ms | ms | Hard real-time bound |
| 5 | Vehicle Density (urban) | $\rho_v \approx 1$ veh / (lane $\cdot$ 5 m) | veh/m | Rush-hour scenario |
| 6 | Pseudonym Pool Size | $N_{p} \geq 43{,}800$ per year | credentials | ~120 min each |
| 7 | Pseudonym Lifetime | $T_p \in [120, 300]$ | seconds | Random per change |
| 8 | Signature Size (ECDSA-P256) | $\vert S_{ECDSA} \vert = 64$ | bytes | vs 256 for RSA-2048 |
| 9 | Signature Verification Time | $t_{ver} \approx 0.5$ ms (ECDSA) | ms | Hardware HSM |
| 10 | Beacon Throughput (per veh) | $\lambda = f_{BSM} \times N_{neighbors}$ | BSMs/s | $N_{neighbors}$ up to 200 |
| 11 | Channel Busy Time (CCH) | $T_{CCH} = 50$ ms | ms | Alternating with SCH |
| 12 | Guard Interval | $T_{GI} = 4$ ╬╝s | ╬╝s | 802.11p OFDM symbol |
| 13 | Data Rate (Control CH) | $R_d = 3$ Mbps | Mbps | BPSK, rate 1/2 |
| 14 | Data Rate (Service CH) | $R_d = 27$ Mbps | Mbps | 64-QAM, rate 3/4 |
| 15 | Probability of Plausibility | $P_{plausible} = P_{pos} \cdot P_{speed} \cdot P_{heading}$ | ÔÇö | Detector metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Symbol Rule:** Absolute value notation uses `\vert` and `\mid` ÔÇö **never use `|`** inside the markdown table (it breaks column parsing).

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Security Service

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of this Architecture:**
> - Enables **collision avoidance systems** (front/rear-end crash prevention).
> - Powers **cooperative adaptive cruise control (C-ACC)**.
> - Supports **emergency electronic brake lights (EEBL)**.
> - Critical for **autonomous vehicle platooning** and **smart-city traffic optimization**.
> - Forms the **trust substrate** for future V2X autonomous driving (3GPP Release 16/17 C-V2X).

### Why Pure Asymmetric Crypto Isn't Enough ÔÇö The "Certificate Flood" Problem
- Every BSM must carry a **signed certificate + signature** to be verified.
- Signature (64 B) + Certificate (~125 B) + BSM Payload (Ôëł 200 B) Ôëł **389 B per BSM**.
- At 10 Hz ├Ś 200 neighbors per vehicle = **2,000 BSMs/sec** in reception.
- A naive PKI where every car sends a full certificate chain saturates the 10 MHz channel in ~10 seconds.
- **Solution:** **Certificate Caching**, **Compressed Certificate Encoding (IEEE 1609.2-2016)**, and **P2P Certificate Distribution** (Raya & Hubaux, 2007, generalized).

---

## 2.5 Operational Trust Model ÔÇö Hierarchical PKI

```
                    ÔöîÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÉ
                    Ôöé   Root CA (WSA)     Ôöé  ÔćÉ WAVE Service Authority (national)
                    ÔööÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöČÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöś
                               Ôöé (issues)
              ÔöîÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔö╝ÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÉ
              Ôľ╝                Ôľ╝                Ôľ╝
        ÔöîÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÉ    ÔöîÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÉ    ÔöîÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÇÔöÉ
        Ôöé Sub-CA 1 Ôöé    Ôöé Sub-CA 2 Ôöé    Ôöé Sub-CA 3 Ôöé  ÔćÉ Regional CAs
```

- **Root CA (WSA):** Issues **Long-Term Certificates (LTC)** to vehicles during registration.
- **Sub-CA:** Issues **Pseudonym Certificates (PC)** in batches to OBUs.
- **OBU:** Uses PCs for daily BSM signing. Old PCs are discarded.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ÔÇö Channel Saturation Analysis for BSM Flooding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU-style analytical problem**: *Given a VANET scenario, determine if the DSRC Control Channel (CCH) can accommodate all safety BSMs without saturating.*

### Given Parameters
- Channel data rate (CCH): $R_d = 3$ Mbps (BPSK, rate 1/2)
- BSM payload size: $L_{BSM} = 200$ bytes
- ECDSA signature: $L_{sig} = 64$ bytes
- Certificate size: $L_{cert} = 125$ bytes
- BSM frequency per vehicle: $f_{BSM} = 10$ Hz
- CCH interval: $T_{CCH} = 50$ ms
- Number of vehicles in range: $N_v$

### Step 1 ÔÇö Total Frame Size Per BSM
The total on-air frame includes payload, MAC overhead, PHY preamble, and signature.
$$L_{frame} = L_{BSM} + L_{sig} + L_{cert} + L_{MAC} + L_{PHY}$$
Assume $L_{MAC} = 34$ bytes, $L_{PHY} = 20$ bytes (preamble + header). Substituting:
$$L_{frame} = 200 + 64 + 125 + 34 + 20 = 443 \text{ bytes}$$
$$L_{frame} = 443 \times 8 = 3{,}544 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 ÔÇö Air-Time of One BSM
$$T_{air} = \frac{L_{frame}}{R_d} = \frac{3{,}544 \text{ bits}}{3 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}} = 1.181 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3 ÔÇö Maximum Capacity of CCH in 50 ms Interval
$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{CCH}}{T_{air}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{50}{1.181} \right\rfloor = 42 \text{ BSMs}$$

### Step 4 ÔÇö Saturation Condition
Each vehicle broadcasts one BSM per 100 ms, but a CCH interval is only 50 ms. So the *aggregate* BSMs contending for one CCH slot across all neighbors is:
$$N_{agg} = N_v \times f_{BSM} \times T_{CCH} = N_v \times 10 \times 0.05 = 0.5 \cdot N_v$$
For the channel to **not saturate**:
$$N_{agg} \leq N_{max} \implies 0.5 \cdot N_v \leq 42 \implies N_v \leq 84 \text{ vehicles}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The CCH can tolerate up to ~**84 vehicles in transmission range** before beacon storm. In dense urban traffic, this threshold is exceeded ÔÇö necessitating **adaptive BSM rate control** (e.g., reduce $f_{BSM}$ from 10 Hz to 2 Hz for low-priority traffic).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ÔÇö Pseudonym Pool Sizing for One-Year Unlinkability

### Given
- Required unlinkability period: $T_{year} = 365$ days
- Average daily driving: $D = 2$ hours
- Pseudonym rotation interval: $T_p = 120$ s

### Step 1 ÔÇö Driving Time in Seconds per Year
$$T_{drive} = D \times 365 = 2 \times 365 \times 3600 = 2{,}628{,}000 \text{ s/year}$$

### Step 2 ÔÇö Number of Pseudonym Changes per Year
$$N_{changes} = \frac{T_{drive}}{T_p} = \frac{2{,}628{,}000}{120} = 21{,}900$$

### Step 3 ÔÇö Safety Margin (KTU Recommendation)
To prevent an attacker from linking the *last* pseudonym of one batch with the *first* of the next (a "boundary attack"), an additional **2├Ś safety factor** is applied:
$$N_{p} = 2 \times N_{changes} = 2 \times 21{,}900 = 43{,}800 \text{ pseudonyms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the figure widely cited in the VANET literature (Raya & Hubaux, 2007) and the KTU 2024 module reference. An OBU must store 43,800 ├Ś 125 bytes Ôëł **5.5 MB** of certificates ÔÇö feasible in modern automotive hardware.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ÔÇö Probabilistic Plausibility Check (Attack Detection)

A receiver can detect certain attacks by checking if a received BSM is **physically plausible**:
$$P_{plausible} = P_{pos} \cdot P_{speed} \cdot P_{heading}$$
where:
$$P_{pos} = \mathbb{1}[\text{claimed position lies within road network}]$$
$$P_{speed} = \mathbb{1}[0 \leq v_{claimed} \leq v_{max,road}]$$
$$P_{heading} = \mathbb{1}[\theta_{claimed} = \text{road bearing} \pm \delta]$$

A **mismatched** BSM is flagged for **revocation** by the TA.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the basis of **behavioral / semantic attack detection** (e.g., detecting **Sybil attacks** by cross-referencing claimed positions with road topology from a digital map).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ÔÇö VANET Vulnerabilities vs. Security Countermeasures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most likely "comparison table" question in KTU exams. Memorize the threatÔćĺdefense mapping.

| # | Vulnerability / Attack | Layer | Impact | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Eavesdropping** | Network | Loss of privacy | AES-128-CCM encryption (non-safety) |
| 2 | **Message Tampering / Forgery** | Application | False safety alerts | ECDSA digital signature |
| 3 | **Replay Attack** | Network | Resend stale warnings | Timestamp + nonce in BSM |
| 4 | **Sybil Attack** | Application | Multi-fake vehicles | Position plausibility + PKI |
| 5 | **Masquerading / Impersonation** | Network | Fake emergency brake | Certificate-based authentication |
| 6 | **DoS (Jamming)** | Physical | Channel blocked | Frequency hopping, spread spectrum |
| 7 | **DoS (Message Flooding)** | MAC | Channel saturation | Rate limiting, adaptive BSM |
| 8 | **Black Hole / Gray Hole** | Network | Selective packet drop | Multi-path routing, reputation |
| 9 | **Location Tracking** | Application | Privacy breach | Pseudonym rotation |
| 10 | **Bogus Information** | Application | Misleading alerts | Plausibility checks, voting |
| 11 | **Timing Attack** | MAC | Latency injection | Time synchronization (GPS) |
| 12 | **Certificate Revocation Delay** | PKI | Misuse of old credentials | Short pseudonym lifetime, CRL/CTL |
| 13 | **GPS Spoofing** | Physical | False position claims | Multi-source positioning |
| 14 | **Rushing Attack** | Network | Route hijack | Random route delay (VANET-specific) |
| 15 | **Wormhole Attack** | Network | Tunneled replay | Neighbor position verification |

---

## 3.5 Detailed Walkthrough ÔÇö IEEE 1609.2 Secure BSM Format

A secure WAVE BSM contains the following fields (concatenated, then transmitted):

| Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `version` | 1 | Protocol version (0x03 for 1609.2-2016) |
| `type` | 1 | signed / encrypted / etc. |
| `signer_id` | 8 | Hash of signer's certificate (not full cert) |
| `certificate_length` | 2 | Length of embedded cert |
| `certificate` | 125 (variable) | Compressed certificate (can be omitted if cached) |
| `data_length` | 2 | Length of payload |
| `data` | variable | The actual BSM payload |
| `signature` | 64 | ECDSA-P256 signature over all above fields |
| **Total** | **Ôëł 389 B** | ÔÇö |

> [!NOTE]
> The **signer_id** is the *first 8 bytes of the SHA-256 hash* of the signer's certificate. It is **not the full certificate** ÔÇö receivers use it to look up cached certificates and reduce on-air size.

## 4.1 VANET Reference Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef net fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef sec fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    classDef app fill:#065f46,stroke:#065f46,color:#ffffff
```
