```mermaid
    subgraph PKI["Hierarchical PKI Trust Plane"]
        rootCA[Root CA / WSA]:::sec
        subCA1[Sub CA Region 1]:::sec
        subCA2[Sub CA Region 2]:::sec
        rootCA --> subCA1
        rootCA --> subCA2
    end

    subgraph VEHICLE_A["Vehicle A (OBU)"]
        hsmA[Tamper-Resistant HSM]:::sec
        poolA[Pseudonym Pool 43800]:::sec
        beaconA[BSM Generator 10 Hz]:::app
        sigA[ECDSA Sign Module]:::sec
        hsmA --> poolA
        poolA --> sigA
        beaconA --> sigA
    end

    subgraph VEHICLE_B["Vehicle B (OBU)"]
        hsmB[Tamper-Resistant HSM]:::sec
        poolB[Pseudonym Pool 43800]:::sec
        verifyB[ECDSA Verify Module]:::sec
        plausB[Plausibility Checker]:::app
        hsmB --> poolB
        verifyB --> plausB
    end

    subgraph RSU_NODE["Roadside Unit (RSU)"]
        rsuProc[RSU Processor]:::net
        rsuCert[RSU Certificate]:::sec
        fwdApp[Forwarding Application]:::app
        rsuCert --> rsuProc
        rsuProc --> fwdApp
    end

    subgraph ATTACKER["Adversary Plane"]
        eavesdrop[Eavesdropper]:::sec
        sybil[Sybil Attacker]:::sec
        jammer[Radio Jammer]:::sec
    end

    sigA -->|Signed BSM over 5.9 GHz| verifyB
    sigA -->|Broadcast| rsuProc
    rsuProc -->|Signed WSA| fwdApp
    fwdApp -->|to Internet Cloud| verifyB
    eavesdrop -.->|Passive Sniff| sigA
    sybil -.->|Inject Fake BSM| verifyB
    jammer -.->|RF Noise| sigA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **Blue plane** is the network layer (RSU + V2X).
> - The **Brown plane** is the security layer (PKI, signatures, attackers).
> - The **Green plane** is the application layer (BSM, plausibility, forwarding).
> - The **Purple plane** is the physical layer (jamming, RF).
> - This color-coding aligns with the **defense-in-depth** principle required by the IEEE 1609.2 standard.

## 4.2 BSM Authentication & Pseudonym Rotation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant OBU as Vehicle OBU
    participant HSM as Tamper-Resistant HSM
    participant RF as 5.9 GHz Radio
    participant NEIGH as Neighbor OBU
    participant TA as Trusted Authority

    Note over OBU,HSM: Pre-Loading Phase (Once per ~3 months)
    OBU->>TA: Request pseudonym batch (over secure channel)
    TA-->>OBU: 43,800 Pseudonym Certificates
    OBU->>HSM: Store in tamper-resistant memory

    Note over OBU,RF: Driving Phase (Continuous)
    loop Every 100 ms
        OBU->>HSM: Retrieve current pseudonym (index i)
        OBU->>OBU: Compose BSM (pos, speed, heading, time)
        OBU->>HSM: Sign BSM with private key
        HSM-->>OBU: ECDSA Signature (64 bytes)
        OBU->>RF: Broadcast Signed BSM (389 bytes)
        RF-->>NEIGH: 5.9 GHz DSRC frame
    end

    Note over OBU,HSM: Pseudonym Rotation (Every 120-300 s)
    OBU->>HSM: i = (i+1) mod 43800
    HSM-->>OBU: New pseudonym

    Note over NEIGH,TA: Misbehavior Detection
    NEIGH->>NEIGH: Plausibility check failed
    NEIGH->>TA: Report misbehavior (signed)
    TA->>TA: Update Certificate Revocation List (CRL)
```

## 4.4 Certificate Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Requested : OBU requests LTC
    Requested --> Issued : Root CA issues Long-Term Cert
    Issued --> PseudonymRequest : OBU requests batch
    PseudonymRequest --> PseudonymBatch : Sub-CA issues 43,800 PCs
    PseudonymBatch --> Active : OBU in use
    Active --> Rotated : Every 120-300 s
    Rotated --> Active
    Active --> Misbehaving : Plausibility fail reported
    Misbehaving --> Revoked : TA updates CRL/CTL
    Revoked --> [*] : Certificate removed
    Active --> Expired : LTC TTL expired
    Expired --> [*]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The "Active Ôćĺ Misbehaving Ôćĺ Revoked" path is the **single most asked** state transition. Be ready to draw it from memory.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Part A]`
**Differentiate between V2V and V2I communication in VANETs. Mention one security challenge unique to V2V.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | V2V | V2I |
|---|---|---|
| Participants | Vehicle Ôćö Vehicle | Vehicle Ôćö RSU |
| Infrastructure | Not required | RSU required |
| Use Case | Collision warning | Traffic signal priority, toll |
| Trust Anchor | Peer-to-peer | RSU as semi-trusted relay |

**Unique V2V Security Challenge (1 mark):** *Authentication of rapidly changing neighbors at high relative velocity* ÔÇö there is **no time for out-of-band handshakes**, so authentication must be **single-packet, stateless, and verifiable in < 1 ms** using pre-distributed pseudonymous certificates.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Part A]`
**What is a pseudonym certificate in VANET security? Why is its short lifetime critical?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Pseudonym Certificate (PC)** is a short-lived, unlinkable public-key certificate issued by a Sub-CA to an OBU. Each PC binds a temporary public key to a **hashed identity**, not the real vehicle ID. An OBU possesses a *pool* of thousands of PCs and **rotates** the active one every **120 ÔÇô 300 seconds**.

**Why short lifetime is critical (1 mark):** *Location privacy* ÔÇö if a PC were long-lived, an adversary could link all BSMs signed with the same PC, reconstruct the vehicle's trajectory, and **track it across the city**. Short lifetime plus frequent rotation breaks the linkability chain, preventing **mass surveillance** of commuters.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ÔÇö Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU ESE convention, students answer **one** of two alternatives. Both questions are framed at the **Apply / Analyze** level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy, weighted 7+7.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Part B, Module 4]`

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **reference architecture of a VANET security system**. Identify the roles of the **Trusted Authority (TA)**, **OBU**, and **RSU**. Briefly describe the **five security services** required. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Discuss in detail the **IEEE 1609.2 secure BSM format** and the **pseudonym-based privacy mechanism** used in VANETs. Explain how **pseudonym rotation** prevents **location tracking**, and derive the **pseudonym pool size** required for a one-year unlinkability period assuming 2 hours of daily driving and a rotation interval of 120 seconds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** [Reproduce the architecture diagram from Section 4.1 ÔÇö show PKI, OBU, RSU planes and their interconnections.]

**Roles (2 marks):**
- **TA (Trusted Authority):** Issues long-term certificates (LTC) to vehicles at registration, issues pseudonym certificate batches to OBUs, maintains the **Certificate Revocation List (CRL)**.
- **OBU (On-Board Unit):** Generates BSMs, signs them using the current pseudonym's private key in a **tamper-resistant HSM**, rotates pseudonyms periodically.
- **RSU (Roadside Unit):** Acts as a semi-trusted relay between OBUs and the backbone network; can also forward multi-hop messages and issue **WAVE Service Announcements (WSA)**.

**Five Security Services (2 marks):**
1. **Authentication** ÔÇö ECDSA digital signature
2. **Confidentiality** ÔÇö AES-128-CCM
3. **Integrity** ÔÇö SHA-256 hash + signature
4. **Non-Repudiation** ÔÇö Asymmetric private key custody in HSM
5. **Privacy** ÔÇö Pseudonym rotation

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**IEEE 1609.2 BSM Format (3 marks):**
[Draw the table from Section 3.5: version, type, signer_id, certificate, data, signature ÔÇö total Ôëł 389 bytes.]

**Pseudonym Rotation & Privacy (2 marks):**
- Each OBU holds **N_p** pseudonyms.
- Every **T_p = 120 ÔÇô 300 s**, the active pseudonym index `i` increments: `i = (i+1) mod N_p`.
- Since the new certificate has **no cryptographically linkable** relationship to the old one, an observer cannot stitch trajectories across rotations.
- The **signer_id** is a 1-way hash ÔÇö even if attacker caches old signer_ids, they cannot recover the true vehicle identity.

**Derivation of Pseudonym Pool Size (2 marks):**

$$T_{drive} = 2 \text{ hr/day} \times 365 \text{ days} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} = 2{,}628{,}000 \text{ s/year}$$

$$N_{changes} = \frac{T_{drive}}{T_p} = \frac{2{,}628{,}000}{120} = 21{,}900$$

$$N_p = 2 \times N_{changes} = 43{,}800 \text{ pseudonyms}$$

The **2├Ś safety margin** prevents **boundary attacks** (where an attacker links the last pseudonym of one batch with the first of the next).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Part B, Module 4, Alt]`

**a)** Classify the major **security attacks** in VANETs according to the layer they target. For each, provide one concrete example and the standard countermeasure. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the **maximum number of vehicles** that the DSRC Control Channel (CCH) can support per transmission range without saturation, given: BSM frequency = 10 Hz, BSM payload = 200 B, signature = 64 B, certificate = 125 B, MAC overhead = 34 B, PHY overhead = 20 B, CCH data rate = 3 Mbps, and CCH interval = 50 ms. Discuss **one adaptive mitigation** if this threshold is exceeded. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Layer-wise Attack Classification (5 marks ÔÇö 1 per layer + 1 example each):**

| Layer | Attack | Example | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical | **Jamming** | Continuous RF noise on 5.9 GHz | Frequency hopping, spread spectrum |
| MAC | **DoS Flooding** | Inject 1000 BSMs/sec on CCH | Rate limiting, MAC contention control |
| Network | **Wormhole** | Tunnel BSMs across the city | Neighbor position verification, hop count limit |
| Application | **Bogus Information** | "Ice on road" fake alert | Plausibility check, multi-vehicle voting |
| Privacy | **Sybil** | Create 50 fake vehicles | PKI identity binding, position cross-check |

**Additional Critical Insight (2 marks):**
- **Replay attack** (Network layer): adversary re-transmits an old BSM to create a stale warning. Countermeasure: include **timestamp + nonce** in every BSM; receiver rejects if `t_now - t_BSM > ╬öt_max`.
- **Masquerading** (Application layer): adversary uses stolen credentials. Countermeasure: **tamper-resistant HSM** prevents key extraction.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Step 1: Total Frame Size (1 mark)**

$$L_{frame} = 200 + 64 + 125 + 34 + 20 = 443 \text{ bytes} = 3{,}544 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Air-Time of One BSM (1 mark)**

$$T_{air} = \frac{3{,}544}{3 \times 10^6} = 1.181 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Capacity per CCH Interval (1 mark)**

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{50 \text{ ms}}{1.181 \text{ ms}} \right\rfloor = 42 \text{ BSMs}$$

**Step 4: Saturation Threshold (2 marks)**

Each vehicle generates $f_{BSM} \times T_{CCH} = 10 \times 0.05 = 0.5$ BSMs per CCH interval. Total BSMs competing:

$$N_{agg} = 0.5 \cdot N_v \leq 42 \implies N_v \leq 84 \text{ vehicles}$$

**Adaptive Mitigation if $N_v > 84$ (2 marks):**

**Mechanism: Adaptive BSM Rate Control (ABRC).**
- When channel load exceeds a **Carrier Sense Threshold (CST) = 0.6**, each vehicle reduces its BSM frequency from 10 Hz to 2 Hz.
- Safety-critical event BSMs (e.g., hard brake) are **always** sent at 10 Hz.
- The reduction factor is computed as:
$$f_{adapt} = f_{BSM} \cdot \max\!\left(0.2, \, 1 - \frac{C_{load} - 0.6}{0.4}\right)$$
where $C_{load}$ is the normalized channel busy ratio.
- This **doubles the effective capacity** to ~168 vehicles while preserving safety guarantees.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Writing "MANET and VANET are the same"** ÔÇö costs **2 marks** immediately. They differ in mobility, power, and privacy model.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2├Ś safety margin** in pseudonym pool sizing ÔÇö examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing 802.11p with 802.11a/b/g/n** ÔÇö 802.11p uses **10 MHz** channels (half of 802.11a's 20 MHz) for better Doppler tolerance.
> 4. **Not mentioning HSM** when discussing key storage ÔÇö non-tamper-resistant storage is **insecure** and examiner flags it.
> 5. **Drawing the diagram without color/plane separation** ÔÇö examiner expects **clearly labeled planes** (PKI, OBU, RSU, Attacker) for full 3-mark diagram credit.
> 6. **Omitting units** in the channel saturation derivation (e.g., "ms" vs "s") ÔÇö partial loss of 1 mark.
> 7. **Using `|` for absolute value inside markdown tables** ÔÇö this is a **formatting error** that breaks the table layout in PDF rendering, and examiners may reject the answer script.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ÔÇö Module 4: VANET Security Architecture**

- [ ] **VANET** = V2V + V2I + V2X over **5.9 GHz DSRC**
- [ ] **802.11p** = PHY/MAC layer; **IEEE 1609.2** = security layer
- [ ] **Five Security Services:** Authentication, Confidentiality, Integrity, Non-Repudiation, Privacy
- [ ] **ECDSA-P256** is the signature algorithm of choice (64-byte signatures)
- [ ] **AES-128-CCM** is used for confidentiality of non-safety messages
- [ ] **Safety BSMs are NOT encrypted** (must be readable by all nearby vehicles)
- [ ] **BSM frequency = 10 Hz**; **payload Ôëł 200 bytes**; **frame Ôëł 389 bytes**
- [ ] **CCH capacity Ôëł 42 BSMs per 50 ms interval** Ôçĺ **84 vehicles max range**
- [ ] **Pseudonym rotation interval = 120 ÔÇô 300 seconds**
- [ ] **Pool size = 43,800 pseudonyms per year** (2-hour daily driving)
- [ ] **HSM (Hardware Security Module)** is mandatory in OBUs to protect private keys
- [ ] **PKI Hierarchy:** Root CA (WSA) Ôćĺ Sub-CA Ôćĺ Long-Term Cert Ôćĺ Pseudonym Cert
- [ ] **CRL/CTL** maintained by TA for revocation of misbehaving vehicles
- [ ] **Channel Saturation Mitigation:** Adaptive BSM Rate Control (ABRC)
- [ ] **Key Attacks:** Jamming, DoS, Wormhole, Sybil, Eavesdropping, Replay, Bogus Information, Location Tracking
- [ ] **Plausibility Check** is the semantic-level defense against Sybil and Bogus Information attacks
- [ ] **Sign-on latency budget for safety BSMs: < 100 ms** end-to-end
- [ ] **Remember** the difference between **signer_id** (8-byte hash) and **certificate** (full cert) for IEEE 1609.2
- [ ] **GPS spoofing** is a physical-layer threat; mitigated by **multi-source positioning** (e.g., LiDAR + cellular triangulation)
- [ ] **V2V is a single-packet authentication scenario** ÔÇö no time for handshakes
- [ ] **Privacy is preserved cryptographically**, not by hiding the radio (the radio is broadcast!)
